### [1] [Contrastive Multi-Modal Hypergraph Reasoning for 3D Crowd Mesh Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13854)
*Minghao Sun,Chongyang Xu,Yitao Xie,Buzhen Huang,Kun Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal contrastive hypergraph framework for 3D crowd reconstruction that fuses RGB, geometry, and poses with a pelvis depth anchor and higher-order hypergraph reasoning to achieve SOTA on Panoptic and GigaCrowd.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods rely on a single modality and reconstruct subjects in isolation, leading to depth ambiguity and poor handling under occlusion. There is a need to exploit multi-modal cues and higher-order group structure to resolve ambiguities in crowded scenes.

Method: CoMHR: 1) robust node representations by fusing RGB features, geometric priors, and occlusion-aware incomplete poses; 2) pelvis depth indicator as a global spatial anchor to align features with a metric-scale-agnostic depth ordering; 3) shared-topology hypergraph modeling higher-order crowd dynamics; 4) hypergraph-based contrastive learning to enhance intra-modal discriminability and enforce cross-modal orthogonality; 5) information propagation to infer missing data under occlusion.

Result: Experiments on Panoptic and GigaCrowd show state-of-the-art performance; code and pretrained models are available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The proposed CoMHR effectively integrates heterogeneous cues and higher-order relations to improve 3D crowd reconstruction under severe occlusion, validated by strong results on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Multi-person 3D reconstruction is pivotal for real-world interaction analysis, yet remains challenging due to severe occlusions and depth ambiguity. Current approaches typically rely on single-modality inputs, which inherently lack geometric guidance. Furthermore, these methods often reconstruct subjects in isolation, neglecting the collective group context essential for resolving ambiguities in crowded scenes. To address these limitations, we propose Contrastive Multi-modal Hypergraph Reasoning to synergize semantic, geometric, and pose cues for crowd reconstruction. We first initialize robust node representations by combining RGB features, geometric priors, and occlusion-aware incomplete poses. Additionally, we introduce a pelvis depth indicator as a global spatial anchor, aligning visual features with a metric-scale-agnostic depth ordering. Subsequently, we construct a shared-topology hypergraph that moves beyond pairwise constraints to model higher-order crowd dynamics. To improve feature fusion, we design a hypergraph-based contrastive learning scheme that jointly enhances intra-modal discriminability and enforces cross-modal orthogonality. This mechanism enables the network to propagate global context effectively, allowing it to infer missing information even under severe occlusion. Extensive experiments on the Panoptic and GigaCrowd benchmarks confirm that our method achieves new state-of-the-art performance. Code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/SunMH-try/CoMHR.

</details>


### [2] [Few Channels Draw The Whole Picture: Revealing Massive Activations in Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13974)
*Evelyn Turri,Davide Bucciarelli,Sara Sarto,Lorenzo Baraldi,Marcella Cornia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A sparse set of massive activation channels in Diffusion Transformers acts as a transferable, prompt-conditioned carrier subspace that controls and localizes image semantics, enabling semantic transport and prompt interpolation without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how prompts shape image semantics in diffusion-based text-to-image models by identifying and characterizing the role of massive activations.

Method: Identify top-activated channels across DiT hidden states; perform a disruption probe by zeroing massive vs low-statistic channels; restrict image-stream tokens to massive channels and cluster to reveal spatial structure; transfer massive activations between prompts to test semantic transport; demonstrate text- and image-conditioned semantic transport.

Result: Massive channels are functionally critical (zeroing them degrades quality); low-statistic channels are less impactful; spatially, they align with subjects and salient regions; transferred activations bias the output toward source prompt while preserving target content, enabling localized semantic interpolation.

Conclusion: Massive activations constitute a sparse, prompt-conditioned carrier subspace that organizes semantic information in DiTs, offering a mechanism for controllable, training-free prompt or subject-driven generation.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) and related flow-based architectures are now among the strongest text-to-image generators, yet the internal mechanisms through which prompts shape image semantics remain poorly understood. In this work, we study massive activations: a small subset of hidden-state channels whose responses are consistently much larger than the rest. We show that, despite their sparsity, these few channels effectively draw the whole picture, in three complementary senses. First, they are functionally critical: a controlled disruption probe that zeroes the massive channels causes a sharp collapse in generation quality, while disrupting an equally-sized set of low-statistic channels has marginal effect. Second, they are spatially organized: restricting image-stream tokens to massive channels and clustering them yields coherent partitions that closely align with the main subject and salient regions, exposing a structured spatial code hidden inside an apparently outlier-like subspace. Third, they are transferable: transporting massive activations from one prompt-conditioned trajectory into another, shifts the final image toward the source prompt while preserving substantial content from the target, producing localized semantic interpolation rather than unstructured pixel blending. We exploit this property in two use cases: text-conditioned and image-conditioned semantic transport, where massive activations transport enables prompt interpolation and subject-driven generation without any additional training. Together, these results recast massive activations not as activation anomalies, but as a sparse prompt-conditioned carrier subspace that organizes and controls semantic information in modern DiT models.

</details>


### [3] [Unified Pix Token And Word Token Generative Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14028)
*Haun Leung,ZiNan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel model unifies pixel tokens with word tokens in a generative language model to enhance fine-grained visual understanding, featuring per-pixel embeddings, color folding, global conditional attention approximation, and unsupervised image pretraining; shows strong performance in small models with limited data and aligns with scaling laws.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of ViT-based encoders (e.g., difficulty recognizing small text/numbers) by integrating vision and language tokens and enabling fine-grained, image-aware language modeling through unsupervised pretraining and scalable design.

Method: Introduce a unified token architecture that assigns a token to every image pixel (pix token) and combines it with word tokens inside a generative LM; incorporate per-pixel embeddings, color folding, global conditional attention approximation; train with image unsupervised pretraining to learn joint visual-language representations.

Result: Empirical results show competitive or strong performance with small models and limited data; supports the scaling law by predicting improved performance with increased model size and data.

Conclusion: The proposed pix–word unified model offers improved fine-grained visual understanding and scalable performance, indicating viability of unsupervised pretraining and token-level integration for vision–language modeling.

Abstract: Since the emergence of Vision Transformer (ViT), it has been widely used in generative language model and generative visual model. Especially in the current state-of-art open source multimodal models, ViT obtained by CLIP or SigLIP method serves as the vision encoder backbone to help them acquire visual understanding capabilities. But this method leads to limitations in visual understanding for details, such as difficulty in recognizing small text or numbers in images. To address these issues, we propose a new model to unify pix token and word token into the generative language model. The new model also features with each pix of image having its own token embedding, color folding, global conditional attention approximation and image unsupervised pretraining. We conducted image unsupervised pretraining experiments using our new model to explore its potential. The experimental results show that it has good performance even in small model and with limited training data. We believe our model also conforms to the scaling law, as long as model parameters and training data increased, its performance will continue to improve.

</details>


### [4] [PVRF: All-in-one Adverse Weather Removal via Prior-modulated and Velocity-constrained Rectified Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14045)
*Wei Dong,Han Zhou,Terry Ji,Guanhua Zhao,Shahab Asoodeh,Yulun Zhang,Guangtao Zhai,Jun Chen,Xiaohong Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PVRF is a unified adverse weather removal framework that combines zero-shot weather perception from frozen VLMs with velocity-constrained rectified-flow refinement to improve fidelity and perceptual quality across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AWR in real-world images suffers from heterogeneous/unseen degradations and distortion-driven training often yields overly smooth results.

Method: Introduce AWR-QA to estimate soft weather-type probabilities and low-level attribute scores using frozen VLMs; condition restoration networks via attribute-modulated normalization (AMN) and weather-weighted adapters (WWA); form an anchor estimate; train a terminal-consistent residual rectified flow with perception-adaptive source perturbation and terminal-consistent velocity parameterization.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved fidelity and perceptual quality over state-of-the-art baselines; strong cross-dataset generalization for single and combined degradations; code release planned.

Conclusion: PVRF demonstrates that perception-guided conditioning coupled with flow-based refinement yields robust AWR performance and generalization across degradations.

Abstract: Adverse weather removal (AWR) in real-world images remains challenging due to heterogeneous and unseen degradations, while distortion-driven training often yields overly smooth results. We propose PVRF, a unified framework that integrates zero-shot soft weather perceptions with velocity-constrained rectified-flow refinement. PVRF introduces an AWR-specific question answering module (AWR-QA) that uses frozen vision--language models (VLMs) to estimate soft probabilities of weather types and low-level attribute scores. These perceptions condition restoration networks via attribute-modulated normalization (AMN) and weather-weighted adapters (WWA), producing an anchor estimate for refinement. We then learn a terminal-consistent residual rectified flow with perception-adaptive source perturbation and a terminal-consistent velocity parameterization to stabilize learning near the terminal regime. Extensive experiments show that PVRF improves both fidelity and perceptual quality over state-of-the-art baselines, with strong cross-dataset generalization on single and combined degradations. Code will be released at https://github.com/dongw22/PVRF.

</details>


### [5] [Evolving Layer-Specific Scalar Functions for Hardware-Aware Transformer Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14047)
*Kieran Carrigg,Sigur de Vries,Amirhossein Sadough,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GP-evolved, layer-specific scalar approximations for normalization in ViTs to replace global layer norm, enabling edge-friendly computation with minimal retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs suffer from high compute and global reduction bottlenecks due to layer normalization; homogeneous scalar replacements are suboptimal and require costly retraining. A hardware-aware, heterogeneous, layer-specific solution could dramatically improve edge deployment.

Method: Use genetic programming to evolve heterogeneous, layer-specific scalar functions derived from pre-trained ViT weights, coupled with a post-training re-alignment step to adapt the modified model without full retraining.

Result: Evolved expressions achieve 91.6% of variance captured (R^2) vs 70.2% for homogeneous baselines; the modified ViT recovers 84.25% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K in 20 epochs; reduces arithmetic complexity and off-chip memory traffic by removing the global reduction bottleneck.

Conclusion: A hardware-aware GP-based framework can yield efficient, layer-specific normalization approximations that maintain performance with minimal retraining, enabling practical edge deployment of ViTs.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve state-of-the-art performance on challenging vision tasks, but their deployment on edge devices is severely hindered by the computational complexity and global reduction bottleneck imposed by layer normalization. Recent methods attempt to bypass this by replacing normalization layers with hardware-friendly scalar approximations. However, these homogeneous replacements do not optimally fit to all layers' behaviour and rely on expensive model retraining. In this work, we propose a highly efficient, hardware-aware framework that utilizes genetic programming (GP) to evolve heterogeneous, layer-specific scalar functions directly from pre-trained weights. Coupled with a novel post-training re-alignment strategy, our approach eliminates the need to retrain models from scratch entirely. Our evolved expressions accurately approximate the target normalization behaviours, capturing $91.6\%$ of the variance ($R^2$) compared to only $70.2\%$ for homogeneous baselines, allowing our modified architecture to recover $84.25\%$ Top-1 ImageNet-1K accuracy in only 20 epochs. By preserving this performance while eliminating the global reduction bottleneck, our approach establishes a highly favourable trade-off between arithmetic complexity and off-chip memory traffic, removing a primary barrier to the efficient deployment of ViTs on edge accelerators.

</details>


### [6] [Venus-DeFakerOne: Unified Fake Image Detection & Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14091)
*GuangJian Team*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified, data-centric foundation model (DeFakerOne) for FIDL that bridges cross-domain forgery generation and detection, achieving state-of-the-art results across many benchmarks and offering insights on data scaling and supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented FIDL research lags behind the rise of unified forgery generation; there is a need to understand cross-domain artifact transfer/interference and to build a high-capacity, unified model for joint detection and localization.

Method: Develop DeFakerOne by integrating InternVL2 and SAM2 in a data-centric framework; enable simultaneous image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse scenarios; conduct systematic analysis of data scaling, cross-domain transfer-interference, supervision granularity, and artifact preservation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on 39 detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks; robust to real-world perturbations and to generators like GPT-Image-2; provides systematic analysis of data scaling laws, cross-domain artifacts transfer-interference, necessity of fine-grained supervision, and original-resolution artifacts preservation.

Conclusion: Propose design principles for scalable, robust, and unified FIDL; demonstrate the viability of a unified foundation model for joint detection and localization and offer guidance on data strategies and supervision to support broad forgery detection.

Abstract: In recent years, the rapid evolution of generative AI has fundamentally reshaped the paradigm of image forgery, breaking the traditional boundaries between document editing, natural image manipulation, DeepFake generation, and full-image AIGC synthesis. Despite this shift toward unified forgery generation, existing research in Fake Image Detection and Localization (FIDL) remains fragmented. This creates a mismatch between increasingly unified forgery generation mechanisms and the domain-specific detection paradigm. Bridging this mismatch poses two key challenges for FIDL: understanding cross-domain artifacts transfer and interference, and building a high-capacity unified foundation model for joint detection and localization. To address these challenges, we propose DeFakerOne, a data-centric, unified FIDL foundation model integrating InternVL2 and SAM2. DeFakerOne enables simultaneous image-level detection and pixel-level forgery localization across diverse scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeFakerOne achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming baselines on 39 forgery detection benchmarks and 9 localization benchmarks. Furthermore, the model exhibits superior robustness against real-world perturbations and state-of-the-art generators such as GPT-Image-2. Finally, we provide a systematic analysis of data scaling laws, cross-domain artifacts transfer-interference patterns, the necessity of fine-grained supervision, and the original resolution artifacts preservation, highlighting the design principles for scalable, robust, and unified FIDL.

</details>


### [7] [DUET: Dual-Paradigm Adaptive Expert Triage with Single-cell Inductive Prior for Spatial Transcriptomics Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14104)
*Junchao Zhu,Ruining Deng,Junlin Guo,Tianyuan Yao,Chongyu Qu,Juming Xiong,Zhengyi Lu,Yanfan Zhu,Marilyn Lionts,Yuechen Yang,Yu Wang,Shilin Zhao,Haichun Yang,Yuankai Huo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DUET: a dual-paradigm framework that combines parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval to infer spatially resolved gene expression from histology, guided by single-cell references and an adaptive adapter, achieving SOTA across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current morphology-to-expression mappings can misalign visual similarity with molecular state; underutilization of abundant single-cell data; need a method that balances expressive flexibility with biological fidelity.

Method: DUET uses parallel regression and retrieval branches that are adaptively reconciled. It leverages cellular inductive priors by incorporating large-scale single-cell references as biological constraints, followed by structural refinement. A lightweight adapter dynamically assigns branch preference across spatial contexts.

Result: Extensive experiments on three public datasets with varying gene scales show state-of-the-art performance, with consistent gains attributed to each proposed component.

Conclusion: DUET effectively fuses parametric and retrieval pathways under cellular priors to improve accuracy in spatial gene-expression inference from histology, with code released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Inferring spatially resolved gene expression from histology images offers a cost-effective complement to spatial transcriptomics (ST). However, existing methods reduce this task to a simple morphology-to-expression mapping, where visual similarity does not guarantee molecular consistency. Meanwhile, single-cell data has amassed rich resources far surpassing the scale of ST data, yet it remains underexplored in vision-omics modeling. Furthermore, current approaches commit to a monolithic paradigm with bottlenecks, unable to balance expressive flexibility with biological fidelity. To bridge these gaps, we propose DUET, a novel dual-paradigm framework that synergizes parametric prediction and memory-based retrieval under cellular inductive priors. DUET implements a parallel regression-retrieval paradigm, adaptively reconciling the outputs of its complementary pathways. To mitigate aleatoric vision ambiguity, we incorporate large-scale single-cell references to impose molecular states as biological constraints for faithful learning. Building upon structural refinement, we further design a lightweight adapter to dynamically assign branch preference across spatial contexts to achieve optimal performance. Extensive experiments on three public datasets across varied gene scales demonstrate that DUET achieves SOTA performance, with consistent gains contributed by each proposed component. Code is available at https://github.com/Junchao-Zhu/DUET

</details>


### [8] [Bridging the Rural Healthcare Gap: A Cascaded Edge-Cloud Architecture for Automated Retinal Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14108)
*Nishi Doshi,Shrey Shah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-tier edge-cloud cascade for diabetic retinopathy screening uses on-device MobileNetV3-small for binary triage and cloud RETFoundDINOv2 for ordinal grading on flagged cases, achieving similar accuracy to cloud-only while halving cloud usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accurate diabetic retinopathy screening in rural/low-bandwidth settings by reducing cloud dependencies and latency without sacrificing performance.

Method: Tier 1: on-device lightweight MobileNetV3-small performs binary triage (Referable DR vs Non-referable). Tier 2: cloud-based RETFoundDINOv2 performs ordinal severity grading on the subset flagged as referable. Evaluation on stratified APTOS test split (n=733).

Result: Tier 1: 98.99% sensitivity, 84.37% specificity at high-sensitivity threshold. Cascade forwards 49.52% of images to Tier 2; cloud calls reduced by 50.48% vs cloud-only. Full 4-class output: cascade 80.49% accuracy, 0.8167 quadratic weighted kappa; cloud-only baseline 80.76% accuracy, 0.8184 kappa.

Conclusion: The cascade achieves substantial cloud-call reduction with only a modest drop in grading performance, offering a practical edge-cloud solution for retinal screening in bandwidth-constrained environments.

Abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is one of the leading causes of preventable blindness, yet rural regions often lack the specialists and infrastructure needed for early detection. Although cloud-based deep learning systems offer high accuracy, they face significant challenges in these settings due to high latency, limited bandwidth, and high data transmission costs. To address these challenges, we propose a two-tier edge-cloud cascade on the public APTOS 2019 Blindness Detection dataset. Tier 1 runs a lightweight MobileNetV3-small model on a local clinic device to perform a binary triage between Referable DR (Classes 2-4) and Non-referable DR (Classes 0-1). Tier 2 runs a RETFoundDINOv2 model in the cloud for ordinal severity grading, but only on the subset of images flagged as referable by Tier 1. On a stratified APTOS test split of 733 images, Tier 1 reaches 98.99% sensitivity and 84.37% specificity at a validation-tuned high-sensitivity threshold. The default cascade forwards 49.52% of test images to Tier 2, reducing cloud calls by 50.48% relative to using a cloud-based model for all images. In the deployed 4-class output space (Class 0-1 / Class 2 / Class 3 / Class 4), the cascade obtains 80.49% accuracy and 0.8167 quadratic weighted kappa; the cloud-only baseline obtains 80.76% accuracy and 0.8184 quadratic weighted kappa. On APTOS, the cascade cuts cloud use by about half with a modest drop in grading performance. Index Terms: Diabetic Retinopathy, Edge-Cloud Cascade, MobileNetV3-small, RETFound-DINOv2, Retinal Screening, tele-ophthalmology

</details>


### [9] [SToRe3D: Sparse Token Relevance in ViTs for Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14110)
*Sandro Papais,Lezhou Feng,Charles Cossette,Lingting Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SToRe3D introduces relevance-aligned sparsity for ViTs in 3D detection, jointly selecting 2D image tokens and 3D object queries with mutual relevance heads, enabling up to 3x faster inference with minimal accuracy loss on nuScenes and a new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs enable multi-view 3D detection but suffer high latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Current sparsity methods largely prune 2D tokens and do not extend to full-model sparsity or 3D object queries, hindering real-time performance.

Method: SToRe3D proposes a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that (1) jointly selects a subset of 2D image tokens and 3D object queries, (2) stores filtered features for reactivation to preserve information for later layers, and (3) uses mutual 2D-3D relevance heads to allocate compute toward driving-critical content while keeping other embeddings intact.

Result: Empirical evaluation on nuScenes and a new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark shows up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, enabling real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while preserving planning-critical accuracy.

Conclusion: SToRe3D demonstrates that cross-modal relevance-guided sparsity can substantially reduce computation in ViT-based 3D detectors with limited accuracy penalties, offering a practical path toward real-time perception for planning-critical agents.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) enable strong multi-view 3D detection but are limited by high inference latency from dense token and query processing across multiple views and large 3D regions. Existing sparsity methods, designed mainly for 2D vision, prune or merge image tokens but do not extend to full-model sparsity or address 3D object queries. We introduce SToRe3D, a relevance-aligned sparsity framework that jointly selects 2D image tokens and 3D object queries while storing filtered features for reactivation. Mutual 2D-3D relevance heads allocate compute to driving-critical content and preserve other embeddings. Evaluated on nuScenes and our new nuScenes-Relevance benchmark, SToRe3D achieves up to 3x faster inference with marginal accuracy loss, establishing real-time large-scale ViT-based 3D detection while maintaining accuracy on planning-critical agents.

</details>


### [10] [ProtoMedAgent: Multimodal Clinical Interpretability via Privacy-Aware Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14113)
*Alvaro Lopez Pellicer,Plamen Angelov,Marwan Bukhari,Yi Li,Eduardo Soares,Jemma Kerns*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProtoMedAgent uses a discrete semantic memory within a frozen prototype backbone to generate faithful clinical narratives at test time, avoiding RAG hallucinations and enhancing privacy—a clear improvement over standard RAG in faithfulness and risk reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the semantic gap between raw continuous outputs of interpretable prototype networks and clinical documentation, and mitigate retrieval-based hallucinations while ensuring patient privacy and safety in medical reporting.

Method: Freeze the prototype backbone; distill latent visual and tabular features into a discrete semantic memory; perform iterative zero-gradient test-time optimization over a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck; online generation constrained by exact set-theoretic differentials and a reflective Scribe-Critic loop; introduce a semantic privacy gate based on k-anonymity and ell-diversity; employ a binding ell-diversity phase transition.

Result: On a 4,160-patient cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2% Comparison Set Faithfulness versus 46.2% for standard RAG; the ell-diversity phase transition reduces artifact-level membership inference risk by an absolute 9.8%.

Conclusion: ProtoMedAgent delivers faithful, privacy-aware clinical reporting by combining a frozen prototype backbone with discrete semantic memory and strict logical constraints, mitigating post-hoc rationalizations while maintaining data confidentiality.

Abstract: While interpretable prototype networks offer compelling case-based reasoning for clinical diagnostics, their raw continuous outputs lack the semantic structure required for medical documentation. Bridging this gap via standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) routinely triggers ``retrieval sycophancy,'' where Large Language Models (LLMs) hallucinate post-hoc rationalizations to align with visual predictions. We introduce ProtoMedAgent, a framework that formalizes multimodal clinical reporting as an iterative, zero-gradient test-time optimization problem over a strict neuro-symbolic bottleneck. Operating on a frozen prototype backbone, we distill latent visual and tabular features into a discrete semantic memory. Online generation is strictly constrained by exact set-theoretic differentials and a reflective Scribe-Critic loop, mathematically precluding unsupported narrative claims. To safely bound data disclosure, we introduce a semantic privacy gate governed by $k$-anonymity and $\ell$-diversity. Evaluated on a 4,160-patient clinical cohort, ProtoMedAgent achieves 91.2\% Comparison Set Faithfulness where it fundamentally outperforms standard RAG (46.2\%). ProtoMedAgent additionally leverages a binding $\ell$-diversity phase transition to systematically reduce artifact-level membership inference risks by an absolute 9.8\%.

</details>


### [11] [PanoPlane: Plane-Aware Panoramic Completion for Sparse-View Indoor 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14135)
*Adil Qureshi,Dongki Jung,Jaehoon Choi,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free panoramic scene completion guides diffusion-based indoor novel-view synthesis. PanoPlane uses 360° panorama completion and Layout Anchored Attention Steering to ground unobserved regions on observed planar surfaces, enabling high-fidelity 3D Gaussian Splatting from as few as 3 views and achieving state-of-the-art PSNR (up to +17.8%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor sparse-view NVS methods struggle with unconstrained hallucination and limited field-of-view. A full 360° spatial context and geometry-aware supervision can improve consistency and realism.

Method: Panoramic scene completion as supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting. Introduces Layout Anchored Attention Steering, a training-free mechanism that steers diffusion-model attention toward detected planar surfaces at inference, grounding completions to known geometry without fine-tuning. Applicable to 3, 6, 9 input views.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art novel-view synthesis quality on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D across 3/6/9 input views, with up to +17.8% PSNR improvement over the current SOTA baseline without diffusion training or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Grounded, panoramic completions produce reliable supervision for unobserved regions, enabling accurate sparse-view NVS via 3D Gaussian Splatting. The training-free attention steering reduces reliance on diffuse hallucination and broadens applicability to indoor scenes; potential limitations include reliance on accurate plane detection and panorama completion quality.

Abstract: We present PanoPlane, an approach for high-fidelity sparse-view indoor novel view synthesis that reconstructs closed room geometry via panoramic scene completion. Unlike perspective-based methods that generate training views from limited fields of view, PanoPlane leverages $360^{\circ}$ panoramic completion to condition the generative process on the full spatial layout. We propose Layout Anchored Attention Steering, a training-free mechanism that steers attention within the diffusion model's internal representation toward scene's detected planar surfaces at inference time. By directing each unobserved region's attention toward geometrically consistent observed content, our method replaces unconstrained hallucination with grounded surface extrapolation. The resulting panoramic completions provide supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling accurate novel-view synthesis across unobserved regions from as few as three input views. Experiments on Replica, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D demonstrate state-of-the-art novel view synthesis quality across 3, 6, and 9 input views, achieving up to $+17.8\%$ improvement in PSNR over the current state-of-the-art baseline without any training or fine-tuning of the diffusion model.

</details>


### [12] [TeDiO: Temporal Diagonal Optimization for Training-Free Coherent Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14136)
*Nurislam Tursynbek,Zhiqiang Lao,Heather Yu,Gedas Bertasius,Marc Niethammer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TeDiO is a training-free, inference-time method that improves temporal coherence in text-to-video diffusion by regularizing self-attention diagonals, yielding smoother motion without changing model weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion models often suffer from temporal incoherence (flicker, drift). Incoherent videos show irregular, fragmented temporal diagonals in intermediate self-attention maps; stable motion shows smooth band-diagonal patterns. A lightweight, model-agnostic approach is needed to enhance temporal realism without retraining.

Method: Estimate diagonal smoothness in intermediate self-attention maps, identify unstable regions, and perform lightweight latent updates during inference to promote coherent frame-to-frame dynamics. The method is plug-and-play, requiring no weight updates or external motion supervision.

Result: Across multiple video diffusion models (e.g., Wan2.1, CogVideoX), TeDiO yields markedly smoother motion while preserving per-frame visual quality, offering an efficient approach to improving dynamic realism in video generation systems.

Conclusion: TeDiO provides an efficient, training-free inference-time regularization that enhances temporal consistency in video diffusion by stabilizing self-attention diagonals, with broad compatibility and minimal overhead.

Abstract: Recent text-to-video diffusion transformers generate visually compelling frames, yet still struggle with temporal coherence, often producing flickering, drifting, or unstable motion. We show that these failures leave a clear imprint inside the model: incoherent videos consistently exhibit irregular, fragmented temporal diagonals in their intermediate self-attention maps, whereas stable motion corresponds to smooth, band-diagonal patterns. Building on this observation, we introduce TeDiO, a training-free, inference-time method that reinforces temporal consistency by regularizing these internal attention patterns. TeDiO estimates diagonal smoothness, identifies unstable regions, and performs lightweight latent updates that promote coherent frame-to-frame dynamics, without modifying model weights or using external motion supervision. Across multiple video diffusion models (e.g., Wan2.1, CogVideoX), TeDiO delivers markedly smoother motion while preserving per-frame visual quality, offering an efficient plug-and-play approach to improving dynamic realism in modern video generation systems.

</details>


### [13] [Rethinking the Good Enough Embedding for Easy Few-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14145)
*Michael Karnes,Alper Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A study arguing that universal, off-the-shelf embeddings (e.g., DINOv2-L features) can perform competitively in few-shot learning with a simple non-parametric pipeline (kNN on frozen features) and optional PCA/ICA refinement, challenging the need for heavy task-specific fine-tuning and even surpassing some meta-learning methods across four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the Platonic Representation Hypothesis that diverse architectures converge to a shared, ideally useful latent space, and to assess whether “good embeddings” are sufficient for complex tasks without backpropagation-based fine-tuning.

Method: Layer-wise evaluation of frozen DINOv2-L features using a k-Nearest Neighbor classifier. Non-parametric pipeline that bypasses backpropagation. Feature extraction is optimized through layer selection, and manifold refinement is applied via PCA and ICA to regularize the representation.

Result: Across four major benchmarks, the proposed pipeline consistently surpasses sophisticated meta-learning algorithms and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: Off-the-shelf embeddings, when properly prepared (including potential layer selection and manifold refinement), can be sufficient for strong few-shot learning performance, implying a convergence toward universal representations and reducing reliance on task-specific fine-tuning.

Abstract: The field of deep visual recognition is undergoing a paradigm shift toward universal representations. The Platonic Representation Hypothesis suggests that diverse architectures trained on massive datasets are converging toward a shared, "ideal" latent space. This again raises a critical question: is a "Good Embedding All You Need?" In this paper, we leverage this convergence to demonstrate that off-the-shelf embeddings are inherently "good enough" for complex tasks, rendering intensive task-specific fine-tuning unnecessary. We explore this hypothesis within the few-shot learning framework, proposing a straightforward, non-parametric pipeline that entirely bypasses backpropagation. By utilizing a k-Nearest Neighbor classifier on frozen DINOv2-L features, we conduct a layer-wise characterization to identify an optimal feature extraction. We further demonstrate that manifold refinement via PCA and ICA provides a beneficial regularizing effect. Our results across four major benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently surpasses sophisticated meta-learning algorithms, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [14] [You Only Landmark Once: Lightweight U-Net Face Super Resolution with YOLO-World Landmark Heatmaps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14166)
*Riccardo Carraro,Anna Briotto,Endi Hysa,Marco Fiorucci,Lamberto Ballan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight U-Net for 8x face super-resolution from 16x16 to 128x128 uses heatmap-guided supervision derived from YOLO-World detections to emphasize key facial regions, avoiding adversarial training or alignment networks; shows improved metrics and sharper reconstructions on aligned CelebA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme upscaling of faces leads to loss of fine details and existing solutions rely on heavy architectures or separate alignment networks. The work aims to enable high-quality reconstruction with a lightweight model by leveraging detection priors without extra supervision networks.

Method: A U-Net-based SR network trained with a novel auxiliary-training-free supervision: heatmaps from YOLO-World (open-vocabulary detector) are converted into spatial weights to form a heatmap-guided loss, emphasizing errors in semantically important regions (eyes, nose, mouth) without auxiliary landmark/alignment networks or adversarial training.

Result: The proposed loss improves quantitative metrics and yields sharper, more realistic reconstructions on aligned CelebA, demonstrating that detection-driven priors can be effectively exploited by lightweight networks for perceptually convincing extreme upscaling without increasing computational cost.

Conclusion: Detection-driven priors from open-vocabulary detectors enable effective, perceptually convincing extreme upscaling with lightweight networks, avoiding adversarial training and extra alignment components while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Face image super-resolution aims to recover high-resolution facial images from severely degraded inputs. Under extreme upscaling factors, fine facial details are often lost, making accurate reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically rely on heavy network architectures, adversarial training schemes, or separate alignment networks, increasing model complexity and computational cost. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight U-Net based-architecture designed to reconstructs $128{ \times }128$ facial images from severely degraded $16{ \times }16$ inputs, achieving an $8 \times $ magnification. A key contribution is a novel auxiliary-training-free supervision strategy that leverages heatmaps generated by YOLO-World, an open-vocabulary object detector, to localize key facial features such as eyes, nose, and mouth. These heatmaps are converted into spatial weights to form a heatmap-guided loss that emphasizes reconstruction errors in semantically important regions. Unlike prior methods that require dedicated landmark or alignment networks, our approach directly reuses detector outputs as supervision, maintaining an efficient training and inference pipeline. Experiments on the aligned CelebA dataset demonstrate that the proposed loss consistently improves quantitative metrics and produces sharper, more realistic reconstructions. Overall, our results show that lightweight networks can effectively exploit detection-driven priors for perceptually convincing extreme upscaling, without adversarial training or increased computational cost.

</details>


### [15] [CoReDiT: Spatial Coherence-Guided Token Pruning and Reconstruction for Efficient Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14191)
*Zhuojin Li,Hsin-Pai Cheng,Hong Cai,Shizhong Han,Fatih Porikli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoReDiT introduces structured token pruning for diffusion transformers, using a linear-time spatial coherence score to skip redundant latent tokens; skipped outputs are reconstructed via coherence-guided aggregation; a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule allocates more pruning where redundancy is higher. This yields up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and 1.33x–1.72x speedups on cloud GPUs and mobile NPUs, with preserved visual quality and increased on-device memory headroom across PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion transformers deliver high-quality image/video generation but incur high computational cost, hindering scalability and on-device deployment; a structured approach to prune tokens can reduce cost while maintaining quality.

Method: Compute a linear-time spatial coherence score to estimate local redundancy in the latent token lattice; skip high-coherence (redundant) tokens in self-attention; reconstruct skipped attention outputs via coherence-guided aggregation of neighboring retained tokens; employ a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule that increases pruning gradually and allocates larger budgets to blocks and denoising steps with higher redundancy.

Result: Achieves up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and inference speedups of 1.33x on cloud GPUs and 1.72x on mobile NPUs; maintains high visual quality; increases on-device memory head-room enabling higher-resolution generation; demonstrated across diffusion backbones PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2.

Conclusion: CoReDiT provides an effective structured token-pruning framework for diffusion transformers, delivering substantial compute savings while preserving generation quality and enabling on-device deployment.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) deliver remarkable image and video generation quality but incur high computational cost, limiting scalability and on-device deployment. We introduce CoReDiT, a structured token pruning framework for DiTs across vision tasks. CoReDiT uses a linear-time spatial coherence score to estimate local redundancy in the latent token lattice and skips high coherence (redundant) tokens in self-attention. To maintain a dense representation and avoid visual discontinuities, we reconstruct skipped attention outputs via coherence-guided aggregation of spatially neighboring retained tokens. We further introduce a progressive, block-adaptive pruning schedule that increases pruning gradually and allocates larger budgets to blocks and denoising steps with higher redundancy. Across state-of-the-art diffusion backbones including PixArt-α and MagicDrive-V2, CoReDiT achieves up to 55% self-attention FLOPs reduction and inference speedups of 1.33x on cloud GPUs and 1.72x on mobile NPUs, while maintaining high visual quality. Notably, CoReDiT also increases on-device memory head-room, enabling higher-resolution generation.

</details>


### [16] [Automatic Landmark-Based Segmentation of Human Subcortical Structures in MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14221)
*Ahmed Rekik,R. Jarrett Rushmore,Sylvain Bouix,Linda Marrakchi-Kacem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation improves boundary accuracy by mimicking Harvard-Oxford atlas protocol through a 16-landmark Global-to-Local detector, a coarse 12-label segmentation, and a landmark-driven post-processing to yield 26 structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Voxel-wise CNNs often produce anatomically inconsistent boundaries that deviate from expert-defined delineations; enforcing anatomical plausibility can improve reliability.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) Global-to-Local network detects 16 subcortical landmarks; (2) semantic segmentation outputs a coarse 12-label map; (3) landmark-driven post-processing splits the 12 labels into 26 structures by enforcing local anatomical constraints.

Result: Reported consistent improvements in boundary accuracy and better alignment with manual protocols.

Conclusion: Incorporating learned landmarks tightens agreement with manual segmentation protocols, yielding more anatomically plausible and reliable brain structure delineations.

Abstract: Precise segmentation of brain structures in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential for reliable neuroimaging analysis, yet voxel-wise deep models often yield anatomically inconsistent results that diverge from expert-defined boundaries. In this research, we propose a landmark-guided 3D brain segmentation approach that explicitly mimics the manual segmentation protocol of the Harvard--Oxford Atlas. A Global-to-Local network automatically detects 16 landmarks representing key subcortical reference points. Then, a semantic segmentation model produces a coarse segmentation of 12 anatomical labels, each grouping multiple subcortical regions. Finally, a landmark-driven post-processing step separates these 12 labels into 26 distinct structures by enforcing local anatomical constraints. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in boundary accuracy. Overall, integrating learned landmarks aligns segmentations more closely with manual protocols.

</details>


### [17] [Implicit spatial-frequency fusion of hyperspectral and lidar data via kolmogorov-arnold networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14239)
*Zekun Long,Judy X. Yang,Jing Wang,Ali Zia,Guanyiman Fu,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IFGNet fuses hyperspectral and LiDAR data using Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with spline-based learnable activations, plus a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module in spatial and frequency domains, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on Houston 2013 and MUUFL with efficient architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and nonlinear interactions between HSI and LiDAR; overcome limitations of CNN/MLP with fixed activations and linear weights, and explore joint spatial-frequency fusion guided by LiDAR.

Method: Propose IFGNet, leveraging KANs with learnable spline functions to model nonlinear cross-modal relationships; introduce LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation in both spatial and frequency domains; evaluate on Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks.

Result: Consistently improvements in overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa over existing fusion methods; efficient architecture.

Conclusion: IFGNet effectively models nonlinear HSI-LiDAR interactions and geometry-aware representations; demonstrates strong fusion performance and efficiency on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification is challenging in complex scenes due to spectral ambiguity, spatial heterogeneity, and the strong coupling between material properties and geometric structures. Although LiDAR provides complementary elevation information, most HSI-LiDAR fusion methods rely on CNNs or MLPs with fixed activation functions and linear weights. These methods struggle to model structural discontinuities in LiDAR data, intricate spectral features of HSI, and their interactions. In addition, fusion of the two modalities in both spatial and frequency domains with LiDAR guidance remains underexplored.
  To address these issues, we propose the Implicit Frequency-Geometry Fusion Network (IFGNet), which leverages Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based functions to adaptively capture highly nonlinear relationships between hyperspectral and LiDAR features. Furthermore, IFGNet introduces a LiDAR-guided implicit aggregation module in both spatial and frequency domains, enhancing geometry-aware spatial representations while capturing global structural patterns.
  Experiments on the Houston 2013 and MUUFL benchmarks demonstrate that IFGNet consistently outperforms existing fusion methods in overall accuracy, average accuracy, and Cohen's Kappa, while maintaining an efficient architecture.

</details>


### [18] [Generative Deep Learning for Computational Destaining and Restaining of Unregistered Digital Pathology Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14251)
*Aarushi Kulkarni,Alarice Lowe,Pratik Shah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: cGAN-based computational H&E staining/destaining can generalize to external WSIs using preprocessing-based adaptation alone, achieving strong destaining metrics and improved restaining performance, though domain shift and morphological artifacts remain a concern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate generalization of cGAN staining/destaining across institutions without retraining and characterize domain shift effects on external WSI datasets.

Method: Use a cGAN trained on 102 registered Brigham & Women’s Hospital prostate core biopsy WSIs, tested on 82 spatially unregistered Stanford WSIs; employ a preprocessing pipeline with histogram-based stain normalization for H&E and channel-wise intensity calibration for unstained inputs; omit image registration to reflect deployment conditions; report conservative lower-bound quantitative metrics (PCC, SSIM, PSNR).

Result: Destaining: PCC 0.854, SSIM 0.699, PSNR 18.41 dB. Restaining from destained outputs outperforms direct staining (PCC 0.798 vs 0.715; SSIM 0.756 vs 0.718; PSNR 20.08 vs 18.51 dB). Qualitative review: benign glands preserved; malignant glands often exhibit vessel-like morphologies. Preprocessing quality may be more limiting than model capacity.

Conclusion: External WSI datasets can be tackled with preprocessing-based adaptation for cGAN-based staining/destaining; future domain adaptation should define explicit morphological targets.

Abstract: Conditional generative adversarial networks (cGANs) have enabled high-fidelity computational staining and destaining of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) in digital pathology whole-slide images (WSI). However, their ability to generalize to out-of-distribution WSI across institutions without retraining remains insufficiently characterized. Previously developed cGAN models trained on 102 registered prostate core biopsy WSIs from Brigham and Women's Hospital were evaluated on 82 spatially unregistered WSIs acquired at Stanford University. To mitigate domain shift without retraining, a preprocessing pipeline consisting of histogram-based stain normalization for H&E-stained WSIs and channel-wise intensity calibration for unstained WSIs was developed. Because image registration was intentionally omitted for real-world deployment conditions, the reported quantitative results are conservative lower bounds reflecting both model performance and limited spatial alignment. Under these conditions, virtual destaining achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.854, structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.699, and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of 18.41 dB. H&E restaining from computationally destained outputs outperformed direct staining from ground-truth unstained inputs across all metrics (PCC: 0.798 vs. 0.715; SSIM: 0.756 vs. 0.718; PSNR: 20.08 vs. 18.51 dB), suggesting that preprocessing quality may be more limiting than model capacity. Qualitative pathological review indicated preservation of benign glandular structures while showing that malignant glands were often rendered with vessel-like morphologies. These findings support the feasibility of applying cGAN-based computational H&E staining and destaining generative models to external WSI datasets using preprocessing-based adaptation alone while defining specific morphological targets for future domain adaptation.

</details>


### [19] [Towards Real-Time Autonomous Navigation: Transformer-Based Catheter Tip Tracking in Fluoroscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14253)
*Harry Robertshaw,Yanghe Hao,Weiyuan Deng,Benjamin Jackson,S. M. Hadi Sadati,Nikola Fischer,Tom Vercauteren,Alejandro Granados,Thomas C. Booth*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A real-time, multi-threaded fluoroscopic catheter-tip tracking pipeline using deep learning (SegFormer with two-class segmentation) achieving MAE ~4.44 mm, outperforming U-Net variants and three-class formulations; surpasses CathAction in Dice; suitable as a foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses limited local access to mechanical thrombectomy by enabling autonomous navigation through RL; requires reliable live tip tracking under noisy, low-contrast fluoroscopy with occlusion challenges.

Method: Design of a multi-threaded pipeline: frame read → preprocessing → inference → post-processing. Evaluated segmentation models (U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, SegFormer) in two-class and three-class settings. Post-processing includes two-step component filtering, one-pixel medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back.

Result: Two-class SegFormer achieved MAE 4.44 mm (best among methods) vs U-Net 4.60 mm, U-Net+Transformer 6.20 mm, three-class 5.19–7.74 mm. SegFormer-based segmentation improved Dice by up to 5 percentage points over CathAction. The framework demonstrated stability under challenging imaging and surpassed prior benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed tracking framework provides accurate, efficient, real-time catheter-tip localization under fluoroscopy and offers a solid foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation, with good robustness to noise, low contrast, and occlusion.

Abstract: Purpose: Mechanical thrombectomy (MT) improves stroke outcomes, but is limited by a lack of local treatment access. Widespread distribution of reinforcement learning (RL)-based robotic systems can be used to alleviate this challenge through autonomous navigation, but current RL methods require live device tip coordinate tracking to function. This paper aims to develop and evaluate a real-time catheter tip tracking pipeline under fluoroscopy, addressing challenges such as low contrast, noise, and device occlusion. Methods: A multi-threaded pipeline was designed, incorporating frame reading, preprocessing, inference, and post-processing. Deep learning segmentation models, including U-Net, U-Net+Transformer, and SegFormer, were trained and benchmarked using two-class and three-class formulations. Post-processing involved two-step component filtering, one-pixel medial skeletonization, and greedy arc-length path following with contour fall-back. Results: On manually-labeled moderate complexity fluoroscopic video data, the two-class SegFormer achieved a mean absolute error of 4.44 mm, outperforming U-Net (4.60 mm), U-Net+Transformer (6.20 mm) and all three-class models (5.19-7.74 mm). On segmentation benchmarks, the system exceeded state-of-the-art CathAction results with improvements of up to +5% in Dice scores for three-segmentation. Conclusion: The results demonstrate that the proposed multi-threaded tracking framework maintains stable performance under challenging imaging conditions, outperforming prior benchmarks, while providing a reliable and efficient foundation for RL-based autonomous MT navigation.

</details>


### [20] [Image Restoration via Diffusion Models with Dynamic Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14267)
*Yang Zheng,Wen Li,Zhaoqiang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SubDPS/SubDAPS via dynamic-resolution diffusion to compute in low-dimensional subspaces, achieving faster inference with strong restoration quality; SubDAPS++ further improves; outperforms recent DM-based methods; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-space diffusion models incur high computational cost; latent DMs reduce space but require encoder-decoder and can be slower; aim to accelerate inference without heavy overhead.

Method: Fine-tune pre-trained DMs for dynamic resolution priors; adapt DPS and DAPS to a dynamic-subspace framework; implement SubDPS and SubDAPS; introduce SubDAPS++.

Result: Empirical results across diverse datasets and tasks show the proposed methods outperform most recent DM-based approaches; SubDAPS achieves favorable speed and fidelity; SubDAPS++ further boosts efficiency and quality.

Conclusion: Dynamic-resolution subspace diffusion is effective for fast, high-fidelity image restoration; SubDPS/SubDAPS provide practical frameworks; SubDAPS++ is a superior variant; code available.

Abstract: Diffusion models (DMs) have exhibited remarkable efficacy in various image restoration tasks. However, existing approaches typically operate within the high-dimensional pixel space, resulting in high computational overhead. While methods based on latent DMs seek to alleviate this issue by utilizing the compressed latent space of a variational autoencoder, they require repeated encoder-decoder inference. This introduces significant additional computational burdens, often resulting in runtime performance that is even inferior to that of their pixel-space counterparts. To mitigate the computational inefficiency, this work proposes projecting data into lower-dimensional subspaces using dynamic resolution DMs to accelerate the inference process. We first fine-tune pre-trained DMs for dynamic resolution priors and adapt DPS and DAPS, which are two widely used pixel-space methods for general image restoration tasks, into the proposed framework, yielding methods we refer to as SubDPS and SubDAPS, respectively. Given the favorable inference speed and reconstruction fidelity of SubDAPS, we introduce an enhanced variant termed SubDAPS++ to further boost both reconstruction efficiency and quality. Empirical evaluations across diverse image datasets and various restoration tasks demonstrate that the proposed methods outperform recent DM-based approaches in the majority of experimental scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/StarNextDay/SubDAPS.git.

</details>


### [21] [Diagnosing and Correcting Concept Omission in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14270)
*Kanghyun Baek,Jaihyun Lew,Chaehun Shin,Jungbeom Lee,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OSI is a novel intervention that amplifies an omission signal in MM-DiTs to reduce concept omission in text-to-image generation, achieving notable improvements on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept omission undermines controlled generation in multimodal diffusion models; linear probing reveals an omission signal in text embeddings; a method to actively trigger missing concepts is needed.

Method: Propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI) that amplifies the omission signal during diffusion to promote inclusion of target concepts.

Result: OSI significantly mitigates concept omission in experiments, including extreme cases, on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium.

Conclusion: OSI is an effective approach to address concept omission in MM-DiTs, enhancing fidelity to user-specified concepts.

Abstract: Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) have achieved remarkable progress in text-to-image generation, yet they frequently suffer from concept omission, where specified objects or attributes fail to emerge in the generated image. By performing linear probing on text tokens, we demonstrate that text embeddings can distinguish a characteristic `omission signal' representing the absence of target concepts. Leveraging this insight, we propose Omission Signal Intervention (OSI), which amplifies the omission signal to actively catalyze the generation of missing concepts. Comprehensive experiments on FLUX.1-Dev and SD3.5-Medium demonstrate that OSI significantly alleviates concept omission even in extreme scenarios.

</details>


### [22] [CreFlow: Corrective Reflow for Sparse-Reward Embodied Video Diffusion RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14274)
*Zhenyang Ni,Yijiang Li,Ruochen Jiao,Simon Sinong Zhan,Sipeng Chen,Zhenfei Yin,Minshuo Chen,Philip Torr,Zhaoran Wang,Qi Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces CreFlow, an online RL framework for embodied video generation that uses compositional Linear Temporal Logic rewards and two specialized losses to localize and stabilize updates, achieving substantial improvements in bimanual manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video generation models trained on heterogeneous data can produce plausible rollouts that violate physical constraints; post-training RL needs logic-based, compositional task verification rather than low-level visual metrics.

Method: Formulates task requirements as a composition of Linear Temporal Logic constraints; proposes credit-aware NFT loss to confine RL updates to reward-relevant regions; introduces corrective reflow loss that uses within-group positive samples to estimate the correction direction.

Result: Rewards align better with human and simulator success labels; downstream execution success improves by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Composable, constraint-based rewards combined with targeted loss designs enable more faithful and efficient post-training improvements for embodied video generation models.

Abstract: Video generation models trained on heterogeneous data with likelihood-surrogate objectives can produce visually plausible rollouts that violate physical constraints in embodied manipulation. Although reinforcement-learning post-training offers a natural route to adapting VGMs, existing video-RL rewards often reduce each rollout to a low-level visual metric, whereas manipulation video evaluation requires logic-based verification of whether the rollout satisfies a compositional task specification. To fill this gap, we introduce a compositional constraint-based reward model for post-training embodied video generation models, which automatically formulates task requirements as a composition of Linear Temporal Logic constraints, providing faithful rewards and localized error information in generated videos. To achieve effective improvement in high-dimensional video generation using these reward signals, we further propose CreFlow, a novel online RL framework with two key designs: i) a credit-aware NFT loss that confines the RL update to reward-relevant regions, preventing perturbations to unrelated regions during post-training; and ii) a corrective reflow loss that leverages within-group positive samples as an explicit estimate of the correction direction, stabilizing and accelerating training. Experiments show that CreFlow yields reward judgments better aligned with human and simulator success labels than existing methods and improves downstream execution success by 23.8 percentage points across eight bimanual manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [23] [KVPO: ODE-Native GRPO for Autoregressive Video Alignment via KV Semantic Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14278)
*Ruicheng Zhang,Kaixi Cong,Jun Zhou,Zhizhou Zhong,Zunnan Xu,Shuiyang Mao,Wei Liu,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: KVPO is an ODE-native online GRPO framework that aligns streaming autoregressive video generators with human preferences by shifting exploration from stochastic noise to a causal-semantic exploration over historical KV caches and using a velocity-field TVE surrogate policy for ODE-consistent optimization, yielding improved visual, motion, and text-video alignment in both short- and long-video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for AR video generation rely on noise-based exploration and SDE surrogates that misalign with deterministic ODE dynamics and tend to perturb low-level appearance rather than long-horizon semantic coherence. There is a need for an ODE-native, semantically meaningful, data-manifold-consistent optimization framework.

Method: KVPO introduces (1) a causal-semantic exploration paradigm that routes historical KV entries to generate semantically diverse branches while remaining on the data manifold, (2) a velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE) that evaluates branch likelihood in velocity space, and (3) a reward-weighted contrastive objective compatible with the native ODE formulation, within an online Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework.

Result: Across multiple distilled AR video generators, KVPO yields consistent gains in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment for both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video tasks.

Conclusion: KVPO provides an ODE-native, semantically aware, online policy optimization framework for aligning streaming AR video generators with human preferences, addressing the mismatch between traditional noise-based exploration and deterministic ODE dynamics while improving long-horizon coherence and alignment.

Abstract: Aligning streaming autoregressive (AR) video generators with human preferences is challenging. Existing reinforcement learning methods predominantly rely on noise-based exploration and SDE-based surrogate policies that are mismatched to the deterministic ODE dynamics of distilled AR models, and tend to perturb low-level appearance rather than the high-level semantic storyline progression critical for long-horizon coherence. To address these limitations, we present KVPO, an ODE-native online Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework for aligning streaming video generators. For diversity exploration, KVPO introduces a causal-semantic exploration paradigm that relocates the source of variation from stochastic noise to the historical KV cache. By stochastically routing historical KV entries, it constructs semantically diverse generation branches that remain strictly on the data manifold. For policy modeling, KVPO introduces a velocity-field surrogate policy based on Trajectory Velocity Energy (TVE), which quantifies branch likelihood in flow-matching velocity space and yields a reward-weighted contrastive objective fully consistent with the native ODE formulation. Experiments on multiple distilled AR video generators demonstrate consistent gains in visual quality, motion quality, and text-video alignment across both single-prompt short-video and multi-prompt long-video settings.

</details>


### [24] [ICED: Concept-level Machine Unlearning via Interpretable Concept Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14309)
*Shen Lin,Jing Lin,Junhao Dong,Piotr Koniusz,Li Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A concept-level unlearning framework for Vision-Language Models that builds a task-specific concept vocabulary via a multimodal LLM and decomposes visual representations into sparse semantic mixtures to selectively forget targeted concepts while preserving non-target semantics and cross-modal knowledge, achieving improved forgetting with competitive utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-level unlearning risks entangling target concepts with contextual information; there is a need for fine-grained, interpretable control to forget specific concepts without damaging related semantics in VLMs.

Method: 1) Construct a compact, task-specific concept vocabulary from the forgetting set using a multimodal large language model. 2) Decompose visual representations into sparse, nonnegative combinations of semantic concepts to create an explicit interface for manipulation. 3) Formulate unlearning as concept-level optimization that suppresses target concepts while preserving intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge (modality alignment).

Result: Empirical studies in both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting settings show more comprehensive target forgetting, better preservation of non-target semantics within the same image, and competitive model utility compared with existing VLM unlearning methods.

Conclusion: Concept-level unlearning with an interpretable vocabulary and sparse semantic decompositions enables fine-grained, robust forgetting in VLMs and provides a practical interface for targeted knowledge removal.

Abstract: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is typically performed at the image or instance level, making it difficult to precisely remove target knowledge without affecting unrelated semantics. This issue is especially pronounced since a single image often contains multiple entangled concepts, including both target concepts to be forgotten and contextual information that should be preserved. In this paper, we propose an interpretable concept-level unlearning framework for VLMs, which constructs a compact task-specific concept vocabulary from the forgetting set using a multimodal large language model. In addition to modality alignment, visual representations are decomposed into sparse, nonnegative combinations of semantic concepts, providing an explicit interface for fine-grained knowledge manipulation. Based on this decomposition, our method formulates unlearning as concept-level optimization, where target concepts are selectively suppressed while intra-instance non-target semantics and global cross-modal knowledge are preserved. Extensive experiments across both in-domain and out-of-domain forgetting settings demonstrate that our method enables more comprehensive target forgetting, better preserves non-target knowledge within the same image, and maintains competitive model utility compared with existing VLM unlearning methods.

</details>


### [25] [CoRDS: Coreset-based Representative and Diverse Selection for Streaming Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14310)
*Ailar Mahdizadeh,Puria Azadi,Muchen Li,Xiangteng He,Leonid Sigal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a coreset-based KV-cache compression for streaming vision-language models, formulating cache selection as a joint KV coreset problem with bicriteria coverage and an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion linked to log-determinant subset selection, yielding improved performance under fixed cache budgets across multiple VLMs and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming VLMs face memory constraints for growing visual history. Traditional token-wise heuristics (recency, redundancy, saliency) do not explicitly optimize the representativeness of the retained history. A coreset-based approach aims to preserve geometry and information content of the accumulated cache.

Method: Model the KV-cache as a coreset selection problem in a joint key-value representation. Optimize a bicriteria objective that balances coverage in key and value spaces to preserve retrieval structure and output-relevant information. Introduce an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion to encourage new directions beyond current selections, connecting this criterion to log-determinant subset selection.

Result: Experiments on four open-source VLMs and five long/video streaming benchmarks show the proposed method consistently improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines at a fixed cache budget.

Conclusion: Representative coreset selection provides a more effective principle than token-wise pruning for memory-constrained streaming video understanding, by preserving the geometry and diversity of the visual history.

Abstract: Streaming video understanding with large vision-language models (VLMs) requires a compact memory that can support future reasoning over an ever-growing visual history. A common solution is to compress the key-value (KV) cache, but existing streaming methods typically rely on local token-wise heuristics, such as recency, temporal redundancy, or saliency, which do not explicitly optimize whether the retained cache is representative of the accumulated history. We propose to view KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem: rather than scoring tokens independently for retention, we select a small subset that covers the geometry of the accumulated visual cache. Our method operates in a joint KV representation and introduces a bicriteria objective that balances coverage in key and value spaces, preserving both retrieval structure and output-relevant information. To encourage a more diverse retained subset, we further introduce an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion that favors candidates contributing new directions beyond the current selection, and connect this criterion to log-determinant subset selection. Across four open-source VLMs and five long-video and streaming-video benchmarks, our method improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget. These results highlight that representative coreset selection offers a more effective principle, than token-wise pruning, for memory-constrained streaming video understanding.

</details>


### [26] [Systematic Discovery of Semantic Attacks in Online Map Construction through Conditional Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14396)
*Chenyi Wang,Ruoyu Song,Raymond Muller,Jean-Philippe Monteuuis,Jonathan Petit,Z. Berkay Celik,Ryan Gerdes,Ming F. Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MIRAGE shows semantic perturbations using diffusion models can degrade HD map-based perception and planning in autonomous vehicles, outperforming pixel-based attacks and bypassing defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses often neutralize pixel attacks; real-world mapping relies on environmental cues that can vary semantically (shadows, wet roads). A semantic attack framework is needed to test robustness of HD-map perception. 

Method: MIRAGE uses diffusion-model latent manifolds to generate semantically mutated scenes with the same road topology as ground-truth. It discovers neighboring scenes that mislead mapping predictions. Two attacks are evaluated on nuScenes: (1) boundary removal (suppressing detections and corrupting planned trajectories) and (2) boundary injection (inserting fictitious boundaries). Compared to pixel PGD and AdvPatch, MIRAGE remains effective and defenses are bypassed. Realism is quantified with two independent Vision-Language Model judges.

Result: Boundary removal: 57.7% of detections suppressed; 96% of planned trajectories corrupted. Boundary injection: successfully injects fictitious boundaries; pixel PGD and AdvPatch fail. Attacks remain potent under various defenses. Realism: MIRAGE judged 80–84% realistic vs 97–99% for clean nuScenes; AdvPatch 0–9%.

Conclusion: Semantic perturbations—environmental variations that align with real-world semantics—create a categorical gap in current adversarial defenses, which are stronger against pixel-level attacks. Robust HD-map perception requires defenses that address semantic-level perturbations and the latent data manifold exploited by diffusion models.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles depend on online HD map construction to perceive lane boundaries, dividers, and pedestrian crossings -- safety-critical road elements that directly govern motion planning. While existing pixel perturbation attacks can disrupt the mapping, they can be neutralized by standard adversarial defenses. We present MIRAGE, a framework for systematic discovery of semantic attacks that bypass adversarial defenses and degrade mapping predictions by finding plausible environmental variation (e.g. shadows, wet roads). MIRAGE exploits the latent manifold of real-world data learned by diffusion models, and searches for semantically mutated scenes neighboring the ground truth with the same road topology yet mislead the mapping predictions. We evaluate MIRAGE on nuScenes and demonstrate two attacks: (1) boundary removal, suppressing 57.7% of detections and corrupting 96% of planned trajectories; and (2) boundary injection, the only method that successfully injects fictitious boundaries, while pixel PGD and AdvPatch fail entirely. Both attacks remain potent under various adversarial defenses. We use two independent VLM judges to quantify realism, where MIRAGE passes as realistic 80--84% of the time (vs. 97--99% for clean nuScenes), while AdvPatch only 0--9%. Our findings expose a categorical gap in current adversarial defenses: semantic-level perturbations that manifest as legitimate environmental variation are substantially harder to mitigate than pixel-level perturbations.

</details>


### [27] [TurboVGGT: Fast Visual Geometry Reconstruction with Adaptive Alternating Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14315)
*David Huang,Guile Wu,Chengjie Huang,Bingbing Liu,Dongfeng Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TurboVGGT introduces adaptive sparse global attention and adaptive frame-wise attention in a visual geometry transformer to enable fast, scalable multi-view 3D reconstruction with competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency in feed-forward 3D reconstruction; existing efficient transformers often use fixed sparsity and lack adaptive token selection, leading to suboptimal global modeling.

Method: An end-to-end trainable framework with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection and a frame attention mechanism. It learns representative tokens with varying sparsity across frames, operates tokens at different abstraction levels, and focuses on structurally informative regions to model global dependencies and local details.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks show that TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Adaptive alternating attention with learnable sparse tokens enables fast, scalable multi-view 3D reconstruction without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods, such as visual geometry transformers, have substantially advanced the traditional per-scene optimization paradigm by enabling effective multi-view reconstruction in a single forward pass. However, most existing methods struggle to achieve a balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, which limits their scalability and efficiency.
