Although some efficient visual geometry transformers have recently emerged, they typically use the same sparsity ratio across layers and frames and lack mechanisms to adaptively learn representative tokens to capture global relationships, leading to suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose TurboVGGT, a novel approach that employs an efficient visual geometry transformer with adaptive alternating attention for fast multi-view 3D reconstruction. Specifically, TurboVGGT employs an end-to-end trainable framework with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection to capture global relationships across frames and frame attention to aggregate local details within each frame. In the adaptive sparse global attention, TurboVGGT adaptively learns representative tokens with varying sparsity levels for global geometry modeling, considering that token importance varies across frames, attention layers operate tokens at different levels of abstraction, and global dependencies rely on structurally informative regions. Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate that TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods. Project page: https://turbovggt.github.io/.

</details>


### [28] [SceneFunRI: Reasoning the Invisible for Task-Driven Functional Object Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14704)
*Posheng Chen,Powen Cheng,Gueter Josmy Faure,Hung-Ting Su,Winston H. Hsu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SceneFunRI benchmark for reasoning about occluded/invisible objects in 2D spatial tasks; current VLMs perform poorly, highlighting instability of invisible-region reasoning and prompting future work on integrating task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where vision-language models struggle to infer hidden object locations in real-world scenes, despite human capabilities, by providing a focused benchmark and analysis.

Method: Construct SceneFunRI from SceneFun3D, cast as a semi-automatic 2D spatial reasoning task with 855 instances; evaluate state-of-the-art VLM baseline (Gemini 3 Flash) and perform prompting analyses in three categories: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE).

Result: Baseline Gemini 3 Flash achieves CAcc@75 15.20, mIoU 0.74, Dist 28.65; results show invisible-region reasoning remains unstable; the prompting analyses reveal limited gains and indicate brittleness of current methods.

Conclusion: Invisible-region reasoning is a challenging and unstable capability for current VLMs; future work should pursue tighter task intent alignment, integration of commonsense priors, enhanced spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search strategies; the dataset provides a benchmark to drive progress.

Abstract: In real-world scenes, target objects may reside in regions that are not visible. While humans can often infer the locations of occluded objects from context and commonsense knowledge, this capability remains a major challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). To address this gap, we introduce SceneFunRI, a benchmark for Reasoning the Invisible. Based on the SceneFun3D dataset, SceneFunRI formulates the task as a 2D spatial reasoning problem via a semi-automatic pipeline and comprises 855 instances. It requires models to infer the locations of invisible functional objects from task instructions and commonsense reasoning. The strongest baseline model (Gemini 3 Flash) only achieves an CAcc@75 of 15.20, an mIoU of 0.74, and a Dist of 28.65. We group our prompting analysis into three categories: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE). These findings indicate that invisible-region reasoning remains an unstable capability in current VLMs, motivating future work on models that more tightly integrate task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

</details>


### [29] [D2-CDIG: Controlled Diffusion Remote Sensing Image Generation with Dual Priors of DEM and Cloud-Fog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14326)
*Zuopeng Zhao,Ying Liu,Kanyaphakphachsorn Pharksuwan,Su Luo,Xiaoyu Li,Maocai Ning*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based remote sensing image generation framework (D2-CDIG) leveraging dual priors (DEM and cloud–fog) to independently control ground and atmospheric features, improving realism over edge/segmentation-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current controllable RS image generation relies on segmentation/edge detection, which struggles with complex terrains and atmospheric phenomena, leading to inaccurate and unnatural images. Integrating geospatial priors aims to enhance realism and utility for RS tasks.

Method: Employ a diffusion model with dual-prior control: separate ground and atmospheric branches powered by DEM and cloud–fog priors; a refined cloud–fog slider to adjust thickness/distribution; layer-wise injection of control signals during training to ensure seamless transitions between terrain and atmosphere.

Result: Claims significant improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism compared with traditional segmentation/edge-based methods.

Conclusion: D2-CDIG provides a flexible, precise framework for remote sensing image generation, offering high-quality data for training large RS models and downstream tasks.

Abstract: Remote sensing image generation provides a reliable data foundation for remote sensing large models and downstream tasks. However, existing controllable remote sensing image generation methods typically rely on traditional techniques such as segmentation and edge detection, which do not fully leverage terrain or atmospheric conditions. As a result, the generated images often lack accuracy and naturalness when dealing with complex terrains and atmospheric phenomena. In this paper, we propose a novel remote sensing image generation framework, D2-CDIG, which integrates diffusion models with a dual-prior control mechanism. By incorporating both Digital Elevation Model (DEM) and cloud-fog information as dual prior knowledge, D2-CDIG precisely controls ground features and atmospheric phenomena within the generated images. Specifically, D2-CDIG decouples the terrain and atmospheric generation processes through independent control of ground and atmospheric branches. Additionally, a refined cloud-fog slider is introduced to flexibly adjust cloud thickness and distribution. During training, ground and atmospheric control signals are injected in layers to ensure a seamless transition within the images. Compared to traditional methods based on segmentation or edge detection, D2-CDIG shows significant improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism. D2-CDIG offers a flexible and precise solution for remote sensing image generation, providing high-quality data for training large remote sensing models and downstream tasks.

</details>


### [30] [EARL: Towards a Unified Analysis-Guided Reinforcement Learning Framework for Egocentric Interaction Reasoning and Pixel Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14742)
*Yuejiao Su,Xinshen Zhang,Zhen Ye,Lei Yao,Lap-Pui Chau,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage EARL framework for egocentric interaction understanding and pixel grounding, using coarse textual interpretation and a query-focused answer/mask, aided by an Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) and GRPO-trained RL; achieves strong pixel grounding on Ego-IRGBench and solid OOD transfer on EgoHOS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in multimodal LLMs for egocentric vision where accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding are challenging.

Method: A two-stage parsing framework: Stage 1 performs coarse-grained interpretation to generate a structured textual description of egocentric interactions. Stage 2 outputs a textual answer and pixel-level grounding mask in response to a user query. A global interaction descriptor is extracted as semantic prior and integrated via an Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) to support query-oriented reasoning. The model optimizes heterogeneous outputs (text, bounding boxes, masks) using a multi-faceted reward and trains the response stage with GRPO.

Result: On Ego-IRGBench, EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming prior RL-based methods by 8.37 percentage points. It also demonstrates strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios in out-of-distribution (OOD) results on EgoHOS.

Conclusion: EARL effectively enables coarse-to-fine egocentric interaction understanding and grounding with an RL-based, analysis-guided architecture. The integration of a semantic prior via AFS and the GRPO-trained response stage yields robust pixel grounding and cross-domain transfer.

Abstract: Understanding human--environment interactions from egocentric vision is essential for assistive robotics and embodied intelligent agents, yet existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle with accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding. To this end, this paper introduces EARL, an Egocentric Analysis-guided Reinforcement Learning framework that explicitly transfers coarse interaction semantics to query-oriented answering and grounding. Specifically, EARL adopts a two-stage parsing framework including coarse-grained interpretation and fine-grained response. The first stage holistically interprets egocentric interactions and generates a structured textual description. The second stage produces the textual answer and pixel-level mask in response to the user query. To bridge the two stages, we extract a global interaction descriptor as a semantic prior, which is integrated via a novel Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) for query-oriented reasoning. To optimize heterogeneous outputs, including textual answers, bounding boxes, and grounding masks, we design a multi-faceted reward function and train the response stage with GRPO. Experiments on Ego-IRGBench show that EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming previous RL-based methods by 8.37%, while OOD grounding results on EgoHOS indicate strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios.

</details>


### [31] [InsightTok: Improving Text and Face Fidelity in Discrete Tokenization for Autoregressive Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14333)
*Yang Yue,Fangyun Wei,Tianyu He,Jinjing Zhao,Zanlin Ni,Zeyu Liu,Jiayi Guo,Lei Shi,Yue Dong,Li Chen,Ji Li,Gao Huang,Dong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InsightTok introduces a content-aware discrete tokenizer with a compact 16k codebook and 16x downsampling, using localized perceptual losses to improve text readability and facial fidelity, improving reconstruction and autoregressive generation (InsightAR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete tokenizers often drive readable text and distinctive facial features through aggressive downsampling and generic reconstruction losses, which poorly align with text legibility and facial fidelity.

Method: Propose InsightTok, a content-aware tokenizer trained with localized perceptual losses; uses a 16k codebook and 16x downsampling; paired with InsightAR for autoregressive generation to improve text/face fidelity.

Result: Outperforms prior tokenizers in text and face reconstruction while maintaining general reconstruction quality; gains transfer to autoregressive generation, yielding clearer text and more faithful facial details.

Conclusion: Specialized supervision in tokenizer training can substantially advance discrete image generation, particularly for content-critical regions like text and faces; encourages targeted loss design for tokenizers.

Abstract: Text and faces are among the most perceptually salient and practically important patterns in visual generation, yet they remain challenging for autoregressive generators built on discrete tokenization. A central bottleneck is the tokenizer: aggressive downsampling and quantization often discard the fine-grained structures needed to preserve readable glyphs and distinctive facial features. We attribute this gap to standard discrete-tokenizer objectives being weakly aligned with text legibility and facial fidelity, as these objectives typically optimize generic reconstruction while compressing diverse content uniformly. To address this, we propose InsightTok, a simple yet effective discrete visual tokenization framework that enhances text and face fidelity through localized, content-aware perceptual losses. With a compact 16k codebook and a 16x downsampling rate, InsightTok significantly outperforms prior tokenizers in text and face reconstruction without compromising general reconstruction quality. These gains consistently transfer to autoregressive image generation in InsightAR, producing images with clearer text and more faithful facial details. Overall, our results highlight the potential of specialized supervision in tokenizer training for advancing discrete image generation.

</details>


### [32] [IG-Diff: Complex Night Scene Restoration with Illumination-Guided Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14337)
*Yifan Chen,Fei Yin,Chunle Guo,Chongyi Li,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a diffusion-model-based nighttime restoration method guided by illumination, aided by new complex nighttime degradation datasets to handle multiple degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In low-light/nighttime scenes, multiple coexisting degradations (low illumination, weather, noise, blur) hinder restoration; lack of paired data for such combined degradations limits end-to-end solutions.

Method: Construct complex nighttime scene datasets simulating simultaneous illumination degradation and other deteriorations; integrate an illumination-guided module into a diffusion model to steer illumination restoration; aim to preserve texture while mitigating multiple degradations.

Result: The approach yields improved texture fidelity and robustness to combined degradations in low-light settings (as claimed by abstract), enabling more faithful restoration under varied nocturnal conditions.

Conclusion: Provides both data and a model to address end-to-end nighttime restoration with multiple degradations; suggests potential generalization to real-world nocturnal scenes, though real-data validation is implied but not detailed.

Abstract: In nighttime circumstances, it is challenging for individuals and machines to perceive their surroundings. While prevailing image restoration methods adeptly handle singular forms of degradation, they falter when confronted with intricate nocturnal scenes, such as the concurrent presence of weather and low-light conditions. Compounding this challenge, the lack of paired data that encapsulates the coexistence of low-light situations and other forms of degradation hinders the development of a comprehensive end-to-end solution. In this work, we contribute complex nighttime scene datasets that simulate both illumination degradation and other forms of deterioration. To address the complexity of night degradation, we propose an integration of an illumination-guided module embedded in the diffusion model to guide the illumination restoration process. Our model can preserve texture fidelity while contending with the adversities posed by various degradation in low-light scenarios.

</details>


### [33] [Evo-Depth: A Lightweight Depth-Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14950)
*Tao Lin,Yuxin Du,Jiting Liu,Nuobei Zhu,Yunhe Li,Yuqian Fu,Yinxinyu Chen,Hongyi Cai,Zewei Ye,Bing Cheng,Kai Ye,Yiran Mao,Yilei Zhong,MingKang Dong,Junchi Yan,Gen Li,Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Evo-Depth is a lightweight depth-enhanced Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that uses an implicit depth encoding module and a depth-aware spatial modulation to improve spatial understanding for robotic manipulation, paired with Progressive Alignment Training. It achieves strong performance with only 0.9B parameters, offering competitive accuracy while minimizing model size, memory, and computation in both simulation and real-world settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA systems rely on 2D visual representations that lack depth and precise spatial relationships. Explicit 3D inputs improve spatial grounding but add sensor complexity and vulnerability to noise; implicit 3D models reduce cost but can be heavy. There is a need for sensor-free, efficient depth-aware VLA that preserves deployment efficiency.

Method: Introduce Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. Incorporate depth features into vision-language representations via a Spatial Enhancement Module that performs depth-aware modulation for spatial-semantic enhancement. Employ Progressive Alignment Training to align depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning. The approach uses a compact 0.9B-parameter model and does not require additional sensing hardware.

Result: Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, it attains the highest average success rate and exhibits the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

Conclusion: Evo-Depth demonstrates that depth-aware, sensor-free VLA can achieve strong spatial grounding and real-world performance with high deployment efficiency, validating a scalable pathway for efficient depth-enhanced robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by unifying perception, language grounding, and action generation. However, they often struggle in scenarios requiring precise spatial understanding, as current VLA models primarily rely on 2D visual representations that lack depth information and detailed spatial relationships. While recent approaches incorporate explicit 3D inputs such as depth maps or point clouds to address this issue, they often increase system complexity, require additional sensors, and remain vulnerable to sensing noise and reconstruction errors. Another line of work explores implicit 3D-aware spatial modeling directly from RGB observations without extra sensors, but it often relies on large geometry foundation models, resulting in higher training and deployment costs. To address these challenges, we propose Evo-Depth, a lightweight depth-enhanced VLA framework that enhances spatially grounded manipulation without relying on additional sensing hardware or compromising deployment efficiency. Evo-Depth employs a lightweight Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. These features are incorporated into vision-language representations through a Spatial Enhancement Module via depth-aware modulation, enabling efficient spatial-semantic enhancement. A Progressive Alignment Training strategy is further introduced to align the resulting depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning. With only 0.9B parameters, Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, Evo-Depth attains the highest average success rate while also exhibiting the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

</details>


### [34] [AnyBand-Diff: A Unified Remote Sensing Image Generation and Band Repair Framework with Spectral Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14341)
*Zuopeng Zhao,Ying Liu,Xiaoyu Li,Su Luo,Lu Li,Wenwen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A spectral-prior-guided diffusion framework (AnyBand-Diff) for robust spectral reconstruction in remote sensing, featuring a Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone with dual stochastic masking, Physics-Guided Sampling using gradients from a differentiable physical model, and a Multi-Scale Physical Loss to enforce radiometric fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard diffusion models distort spectral radiometry in remote sensing. There is a need for physics-aware generative methods that preserve radiometric and spectral consistency for Earth observation data.

Method: Introduce Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone with dual stochastic masking to recover full spectra from arbitrary band subsets; implement Physics-Guided Sampling that uses gradients from a differentiable physical model to steer denoising toward physically plausible solutions; formulate Multi-Scale Physical Loss enforcing constraints at pixel, region, and global scales.

Result: Extensive experiments show AnyBand-Diff achieves reliable imagery and accurate spectral reconstruction, demonstrating improved spectral fidelity and potential for physics-aware EO diffusion methods.

Conclusion: AnyBand-Diff advances spectroscopy-aware generative modeling for Earth observation by integrating spectral priors, physics-guided inference, and multi-scale constraints to ensure radiometric and spectral plausibility in generated data.

Abstract: Existing diffusion models have made significant progress in generating realistic images. However, their direct adaptation to remote sensing imagery often disregards intrinsic physical laws. This oversight frequently leads to spectral distortion and radiometric inconsistency, severely limiting the scientific utility of generated data. To address this issue, this paper introduces AnyBand-Diff, a novel spectral-prior-guided diffusion framework tailored for robust spectral reconstruction. Specifically, we design a Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone integrated with a dual stochastic masking strategy, empowering the model to recover complete spectral information from arbitrary band subsets. Subsequently, to ensure radiometric fidelity, a Physics-Guided Sampling mechanism is proposed, leveraging gradients from a differentiable physical model to explicitly steer the denoising trajectory toward the manifold of physically plausible solutions. Furthermore, a Multi-Scale Physical Loss is formulated to enforce rigorous constraints across pixel, region, and global levels in a joint manner. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of AnyBand-Diff in generating reliable imagery and achieving accurate spectral reconstruction, contributing to the advancement of physics-aware generative methods for Earth observation.

</details>


### [35] [Articraft: An Agentic System for Scalable Articulated 3D Asset Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15187)
*Matt Zhou,Ruining Li,Xiaoyang Lyu,Zhaomou Song,Zhening Huang,Chuanxia Zheng,Christian Rupprecht,Andrea Vedaldi,Shangzhe Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An LLM-driven program synthesis pipeline (Articraft) generates articulated 3D assets via a domain-specific SDK, with a harness that validates assets. It yields Articraft-10K (10K assets, 245 categories) and improves over existing generators and generic agents, aiding robotics and VR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of large, diverse articulated-3D asset datasets. The approach leverages LLMs to automate asset creation by coding against a structured SDK, thereby scaling generation while constraining the process to produce valid, testable assets.

Method: Develop a domain-specific SDK for parts, geometry, joints, and tests. An LLM writes programmatic pipelines against this SDK to build articulated assets. A restricted harness enforces workspace constraints and provides structured feedback from asset validation, removing the need to manually handle URDF and software environments. The system, Articraft, generates assets and a dataset (Articraft-10K) for downstream tasks.

Result: Articraft-produced assets surpass baselines in quality, outperforming state-of-the-art articulated-asset generators and generic coding agents. Articraft-10K contains over 10,000 assets across 245 categories and demonstrates usefulness for model training and downstream robotics/VR applications.

Conclusion: Programmatic, LLM-guided asset generation with a constrained SDK and harness enables scalable creation of high-quality articulated 3D assets, with immediate applicability to robotics simulation and VR, and provides a valuable dataset (Articraft-10K) for research and development.

Abstract: A bottleneck in learning to understand articulated 3D objects is the lack of large and diverse datasets. In this paper, we propose to leverage large language models (LLMs) to close this gap and generate articulated assets at scale. We reduce the problem of generating an articulated 3D asset to that of writing a program that builds it. We then introduce a new agentic system, Articraft, that writes such programs automatically. We design a programmatic interface and harness to help the LLM do so effectively. The LLM writes code against a domain-specific SDK for defining parts, composing geometry, specifying joints, and writing tests to validate the resulting assets. The harness exposes a restricted workspace and interface to the LLM, validates the resulting assets, and returns structured feedback. In this way, the LLM is not distracted by details such as authoring a URDF file or managing a complex software environment. We show that this produces higher-quality assets than both state-of-the-art articulated-asset generators and general-purpose coding agents. Using Articraft, we build Articraft-10K, a curated dataset of over 10K articulated assets spanning 245 categories, and show its utility both for training models of articulated assets and in downstream applications such as robotics simulation and virtual reality.

</details>


### [36] [Learning with Semantic Priors: Stabilizing Point-Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection via Hierarchical Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14346)
*Yuanhang Yao,Ping Qian,Zhu Liu,Long Ma,Weimin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hierarchical VFM-driven knowledge distillation for point-supervised infrared small target detection, using a frozen Vision Foundation Model with bilevel optimization, SCAM for semantic injection, and cluster-level reweighting to improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotating pixel-level masks for ISTD is costly; lightweight CNNs lack sufficient semantics, leading to noisy pseudo-labels and unstable training. A frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM) is leveraged to inject rich semantics during training.

Method: Bilevel optimization: inner loop adapts a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training samples; outer loop transfers validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student to suppress pseudo-label noise. Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) injects VFM semantics into CNN features at multiple layers. Dynamic collaborative learning with cluster-level sample reweighting enhances robustness to imperfect pseudo-masks.

Result: Experiments on diverse challenging ISTD cases across multiple backbones show consistent improvements in detection accuracy and training stability; code is released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: The approach effectively leverages a frozen VFM for point-supervised ISTD, delivering improved accuracy and stability, with potential to generalize to other weakly-supervised detection tasks.

Abstract: Single-frame Infrared Small Target Detection (ISTD) aims to localize weak targets under heavy background clutter, yet dense pixel-wise annotations are expensive. Point supervision with online label evolution reduces annotation cost; however, lightweight CNN detectors often lack sufficient semantics, leading to noisy pseudo-masks and unstable optimization. To address this, we propose a hierarchical VFM-driven knowledge distillation framework that uses a frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM) during training. We formulate point-supervised learning as a bilevel optimization process: the inner loop adapts a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training samples, while the outer loop transfers validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student to mitigate pseudo-label noise and training-set bias. We further introduce Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) to inject VFM semantics into CNN features at multiple layers. In addition, a dynamic collaborative learning strategy with cluster-level sample reweighting enhances robustness to imperfect pseudo-masks. Experiments on diverse challenging cases across multiple ISTD backbones demonstrate consistent improvements in detection accuracy and training stability. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanhang-yao/semantic-prior.

</details>


### [37] [Delta Forcing: Trust Region Steering for Interactive Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14382)
*Yuheng Wu,Xiangbo Gao,Tianhao Chen,Xinghao Chen,Qing Yin,Zhengzhong Tu,Dongman Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Delta Forcing constrains teacher supervision in autoregressive video generation within an adaptive trust region to reduce conditional bias, balancing stability and reactivity and improving long-horizon consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive real-time video generation requires prompt adaptation to new events while maintaining temporal coherence over long horizons. Distilling bidirectional teachers can introduce conditional bias that aligns locally but drifts globally after condition changes.

Method: Introduce Delta Forcing, a TRPO-inspired framework that estimates transition consistency from the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories and enforces supervision within an adaptive trust region, coupled with a monotonic continuity objective to suppress unreliable shifts while preserving responsiveness.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved temporal consistency and reduced drift without sacrificing event reactivity, demonstrating the approach's effectiveness in balancing stability and responsiveness.

Conclusion: Delta Forcing offers a simple, effective mechanism to constrain unreliable teacher guidance while maintaining responsiveness in interactive autoregressive video generation, yielding better long-horizon coherence.

Abstract: Interactive real-time autoregressive video generation is essential for applications such as content creation and world modeling, where visual content must adapt to dynamically evolving event conditions. A fundamental challenge lies in balancing reactivity and stability: models must respond promptly to new events while maintaining temporal coherence over long horizons. Existing approaches distill bidirectional models into autoregressive generators and further adapt them via streaming long tuning, yet often exhibit persistent drift after condition changes. We identify the cause as conditional bias, where the teacher may provide condition-aligned but trajectory-agnostic guidance, biasing generation toward locally valid yet globally inconsistent modes. Inspired by Trust Region Policy Optimization, we propose Delta Forcing, a simple yet effective framework that constrains unreliable teacher supervision within an adaptive trust region. Specifically, Delta Forcing estimates transition consistency from the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories, and uses it to balance teacher supervision with a monotonic continuity objective. This suppress unreliable teacher-induced shifts while preserving responsiveness to new events. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Delta Forcing significantly improves consistency while maintaining event reactivity.

</details>


### [38] [Dual-Latent Collaborative Decoding for Fidelity-Perception Balanced Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14391)
*Qi Mao,Zijian Wang,Zhengxue Cheng,Lingyu Zhu,Siwei Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoDE introduces a dual-latent image compression framework that splits reconstruction into an SQ fidelity-focused branch and a VQ perception-focused branch, with decoder-side modules ESE and CEM enabling branch-specific enhancement and cross-expert modulation for improved fidelity-perception balance across bitrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve conflicting demands of latent representations in learned image compression: scalar-quantized latent fidelity at low bitrate vs. vector-quantized tokens that preserve semantics but limit fidelity and scalability.

Method: A dual-latent decoder framework (MoDE) with an SQ branch and a VQ branch, coupled by two decoder-side modules: Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE) to maintain branch references, and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM) to enable selective information transfer between branches, operating over a shared dual-stream bitstream.

Result: Empirical evaluations show MoDE achieves a preferable balance between distortion fidelity and perceptual realism across a wide bitrate range, outperforming distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent baselines.

Conclusion: Decoder-side collaboration between complementary latent paradigms is an effective design for achieving wide-range fidelity-perception balanced learned image compression.

Abstract: Learned image compression (LIC) increasingly requires reconstructions that balance distortion fidelity and perceptual realism across a wide range of bitrates. However, most existing methods still rely on a single compressed latent representation to simultaneously carry structural details, semantic cues, and perceptual priors, requiring the same latent representation to serve multiple, potentially conflicting roles. This tension becomes evident across different latent paradigms: scalar-quantized (SQ) continuous latents provide rate-scalable fidelity but tend to lose perceptual details at low rates, while vector-quantized (VQ) discrete tokens preserve compact semantic cues but suffer from limited structural fidelity and bitrate scalability. To address this issue, we propose Mixture of Decoder Experts (MoDE), a dual-latent collaborative decoding framework that decomposes reconstruction responsibilities across complementary latent paradigms. Specifically, MoDE treats the SQ branch as a fidelity-oriented expert and the VQ branch as a perception-oriented expert, and coordinates them through two decoder-side modules: Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE), which preserves branch-specific expert references, and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM), which enables selective complementary transfer during reconstruction. The resulting framework supports selective cross-latent collaboration under a shared dual-stream bitstream and enables both fidelity-anchored and perception-anchored decoding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoDE achieves a more favorable fidelity-perception balance than representative distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent baselines across a wide bitrate range, highlighting decoder-side expert collaboration as an effective design for wide-range fidelity-perception balanced LIC.

</details>


### [39] [Analogical Trajectory Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14393)
*Junho Kim,Eun Sun Lee,Gwangtak Bae,Seunggu Kang,Young Min Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A non-parametric, multi-cluster trajectory transfer method that decomposes scenes into object-centric clusters, predicts cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth maps using 3D foundation model features, assembles and refines to produce semantically consistent, collision-free trajectories in ~0.6s without training, outperforming LLM/VLM/scene-graph baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transferring motion trajectories across semantically similar but geometrically different 3D environments is challenging due to object placement, scale, and layout differences. Naive semantic matching causes collisions or distortions, and mapping each trajectory point entails a large search space to preserve semantics and functionality.

Method: Partition scenes into object-centric clusters and estimate cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth map prediction. Use 3D foundation model features to encode contextual information from object and open-space arrangements. Combinatorially assemble per-cluster maps into an initial transfer, then refine to remove collisions and distortions for a spatially coherent trajectory. No training required; ~0.6s runtime.

Result: The approach outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene graph matching on analogical trajectory transfer tasks. Demonstrated applications include virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer.

Conclusion: Illustrates the viability of analogical trajectory transfer for AR/VR and robotics. The method yields semantically consistent and spatially coherent transfers efficiently without training, highlighting broad applicability to AR/VR content creation and robotics.

Abstract: We study analogical trajectory transfer, where the goal is to translate motion trajectories in one 3D environment to a semantically analogous location in another. Such a capacity would enable machines to perform analogical spatial reasoning, with applications in AR/VR co-presence, content creation, and robotics. However, even semantically similar scenes can still differ substantially in object placement, scale, and layout, so naively matching semantics leads to collisions or geometric distortions. Furthermore, finding where each trajectory point should transfer to has a large search space, as the mapping must preserve semantics and functionality without tearing the trajectory apart or causing collisions. Our key insight is to decompose the problem into spatially segregated subproblems and merge their solutions to produce semantically consistent and spatially coherent transfers. Specifically, we partition scenes into object-centric clusters and estimate cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth map prediction, using 3D foundation model features that encode contextual information from object and open-space arrangements. We then combinatorially assemble the per-cluster maps into an initial transfer and refine the result to remove collisions and distortions, yielding a spatially coherent trajectory. Our method does not require training, attains a fast runtime around 0.6 seconds, and outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene graph matching. We further showcase applications in virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer, which indicates the broad applicability of our work to AR/VR and robotics.

</details>


### [40] [SceneForge: Structured World Supervision from 3D Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14399)
*Jizhizi Li,Jiayang Ao,Danny Wicks,Petru-Daniel Tudosiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SceneForge treats scenes as editable, persistent 3D worlds with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies to generate intervention-consistent supervision for multimodal learning, producing counterfactuals, multi-view data, and effect-aware signals from a shared world state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observation-level datasets lack explicit scene state and failure-to-change propagation, hindering supervision that remains consistent under edits, viewpoints, and scene interventions.

Method: Model scenes as structured world states with dependencies; apply explicit interventions (e.g., removal, camera variations) and propagate effects through dependencies to generate aligned supervision (counterfactuals, multi-view, shadows/reflections); instantiate with Infinigen and Blender to create a licensing-clean indoor dataset from 2K+ scenes with diverse single-view and registered multi-view settings.

Result: With matched training budgets, SceneForge supervision improves object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks, with qualitative and quantitative gains, demonstrating scalable, intervention-consistent supervision.

Conclusion: Modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds is practical and scalable for intervention-consistent multimodal learning; SceneForge provides a reusable framework and dataset resources for this paradigm.

Abstract: Many multimodal learning tasks require supervision that remains consistent across edits, viewpoints, and scene-level interventions. However, such supervision is difficult to obtain from observation-level datasets, which do not expose the underlying scene state or how changes propagate through it. We present SceneForge, an intervention-driven framework that generates structured supervision from editable 3D world states. SceneForge represents each scene as a persistent world with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies. By applying explicit interventions (e.g., object removal or camera variation) and propagating their effects through scene dependencies, SceneForge renders supervision that remains consistent with object structure and scene-level effects. This produces aligned outputs including counterfactual observations, multi-view observations, and effect-aware signals such as shadows and reflections, all derived from a shared world state rather than post hoc image-space processing. We instantiate SceneForge using Infinigen and Blender to construct a licensing-clean indoor supervision resource with a large number of counterfactual pairs and aligned annotations from over 2K scenes, covering both diverse single-view and registered multi-view settings. Under matched training budgets, incorporating SceneForge supervision improves both object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks in both quantitative and qualitative evaluation. These results indicate that modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds provides a practical and scalable foundation for intervention-consistent multimodal learning.

</details>


### [41] [DermAgent: A Self-Reflective Agentic System for Dermatological Image Analysis with Multi-Tool Reasoning and Traceable Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14403)
*Yize Liu,Siyuan Yan,Ming Hu,Lie Ju,Xieji Li,Feilong Tang,Wei Feng,Zongyuan Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DermAgent is a multi-tool, Plan-Execute-Reflect framework that coordinates seven vision-language modules for dermatology, using external evidence retrieval and a deterministic critic to reduce hallucinations, achieving state-of-the-art results on five benchmarks and outperforming GPT-4o in diagnostic accuracy and captioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address insufficient domain grounding and hallucinations in multimodal LLMs for dermatology by integrating specialized perception modules, external evidence retrieval, and post-hoc auditing.

Method: Plan-Execute-Reflect with seven specialized perception tools for morphology description, dermoscopic concept annotation, and disease diagnosis; dual-modality retrieval against large external datasets; deterministic critic with confidence, coverage, and conflict gates; automated self-correction; evaluation on five dermatology benchmarks across zero-shot diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning.

Result: DermAgent consistently outperforms SOTA MLLMs and medical baselines; +17.6% diagnostic accuracy over GPT-4o; +3.15% ROUGE-L in captioning; robust across tasks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a modular, evidence-grounded, auditable MLLM for dermatology with improved reliability and interpretability; release of code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Dermatological diagnosis requires integrating fine-grained visual perception with expert clinical knowledge. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) facilitate interactive medical image analysis, their application in dermatology is hindered by insufficient domain-specific grounding and hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose DermAgent, a collaborative multi-tool agent that orchestrates seven specialized vision and language modules within a Plan-Execute-Reflect framework. DermAgent delivers stepwise, traceable diagnostic reasoning through three core components. First, it employs complementary visual perception tools for comprehensive morphological description, dermoscopic concept annotation, and disease diagnosis. Second, to overcome the lack of domain prior, a dual-modality retrieval module anchors every prediction in external evidence by cross-referencing 413,210 diagnosed image cases and 3,199 clinical guideline chunks. To further mitigate hallucinations, a deterministic critic module conducts strict post-hoc auditing via confidence, coverage, and conflict gates, automatically detecting inter-source disagreements to trigger targeted self-correction. Extensive experiments on five dermatology benchmarks demonstrate that DermAgent consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical agent baselines across zero-shot fine-grained disease diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning tasks, exceeding GPT-4o by 17.6% in skin disease diagnostic accuracy and 3.15% in captioning ROUGE-L. Our code is available at https://github.com/YizeezLiu/DermAgent.

</details>


### [42] [Think When Needed: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings with a Dual-LoRA Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14448)
*Longxiang Zhang,Weilong Dai,Guanghao Zhang,Hao Jiang,Pipei Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified adaptive reasoning framework (Think When Needed, TWN) for multimodal embeddings using dual-LoRA adapters on a frozen backbone and a self-supervised routing gate to decide per-input whether to generate chain-of-thought, achieving SOTA embedding quality with low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational and parameter overhead of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in multimodal embedding pipelines, while avoiding gradient conflicts from jointly optimized reasoner and embedder, and leveraging simple inputs with discriminative embeddings.

Method: Attach reasoning and embedding adapters (dual-LoRA) to a shared frozen backbone, with gradients detached at the interface to mitigate conflicts; introduce a self-supervised routing gate that decides per input whether to generate CoT; optionally apply embedding-guided RL to improve CoT quality beyond supervised training.

Result: On MMEB-V2 across 78 tasks, TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality, requires only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone, and uses up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens than full generative mode, showing substantial efficiency gains over existing generative methods.

Conclusion: Adaptive, selective reasoning via TWN improves retrieval quality while reducing inference cost, validating the viability of per-input CoT routing in multimodal embeddings and highlighting directions for learning-based routing and RL optimization of CoT.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have emerged as a powerful backbone for multimodal embeddings. Recent methods introduce chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline to improve retrieval quality, but remain costly in both model size and inference cost. They typically employ separate reasoner and embedder with substantial parameter overhead, and generate CoT indiscriminately for every input. However, we observe that for simple inputs, discriminative embeddings already perform well, and redundant reasoning can even mislead the model, degrading performance. To address these limitations, we propose Think When Needed (TWN), a unified multimodal embedding framework with adaptive reasoning. TWN introduces a dual-LoRA architecture that attaches reasoning and embedding adapters to a shared frozen backbone, detaching gradients at their interface to mitigate gradient conflicts introduced by joint optimization while keeping parameters close to a single model. Building on this, an adaptive think mechanism uses a self-supervised routing gate to decide per input whether to generate CoT, skipping unnecessary reasoning to reduce inference overhead and even improve retrieval quality. We further explore embedding-guided RL to optimize CoT quality beyond supervised training. On the 78 tasks of MMEB-V2, TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality while being substantially more efficient than existing generative methods, requiring only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens compared to the full generative mode.

</details>


### [43] [ClickRemoval: An Interactive Open-Source Tool for Object Removal in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14461)
*Ledun Zhang,Yatu Ji,Xufei Zhuang,Xinying Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ClickRemoval is an open-source interactive object removal tool that uses user clicks to guide self-attention modulation in Stable Diffusion, removing objects without masks or prompts and achieving competitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing object removal tools require manual masks or text prompts; non-expert users struggle with precise removal in complex scenes, leading to incomplete removal or unnatural backgrounds.

Method: The approach is built on pretrained Stable Diffusion models and is driven solely by user clicks. It requires no additional training or hand-drawn masks or text descriptions. Localization of target objects and background restoration are achieved via self-attention modulation during the denoising process.

Result: Experiments show competitive performance on quantitative metrics and favorable user study results; the authors release the software package under Apache-2.0 at https://github.com/zld-make/ClickRemoval.

Conclusion: ClickRemoval demonstrates that interactive, click-guided diffusion can effectively remove objects without masks or prompts, offering accessible, model-based background restoration; the work emphasizes practicality and openness, with potential considerations around click quality and limitations of diffusion-based edits.

Abstract: Existing object removal tools often rely on manual masks or text prompts, making precise removal difficult for non-expert users in complex scenes and often leading to incomplete removal or unnatural background completion. To address this issue, we present ClickRemoval, an open-source interactive object removal tool built on pretrained Stable Diffusion models and driven solely by user clicks. Without additional training, hand-drawn masks, or text descriptions, ClickRemoval localizes target objects and restores the background through self-attention modulation during denoising. Experiments show that ClickRemoval achieves competitive results across quantitative metrics and user studies. We release a complete software package at https://github.com/zld-make/ClickRemoval under the Apache-2.0 license.

</details>


### [44] [Real2Sim in HOI: Toward Physically Plausible HOI Reconstruction from Monocular Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14462)
*Yubo Zhao,Yujin Chai,Yunao Dong,Chengfeng Zhao,Zijiao Zeng,Yuan Liu,Chi-Keung Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HA-HOI proposes a human-first, object-follow framework to recover physically plausible 4D HOI from monocular video, using the human as an interaction anchor and refining the object motion relative to it, then guiding a physics-based humanoid-object simulation with a teacher trajectory to improve stability and simulation readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current monocular HOI reconstructions yield visually plausible trajectories but often lack stable contact, functional manipulation semantics, and physical plausibility; there is a need to explicitly model the interaction relationship to enable stable simulation and learning.

Method: Adopt a human-first, object-follow formulation: recover human motion as the interaction anchor, reconstruct and refine the object relative to the human action, and project the resulting 4D trajectory into a physics-based humanoid-object simulator, where it serves as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout.

Result: HA-HOI improves human-object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness on benchmark and in-the-wild videos compared with prior monocular HOI methods.

Conclusion: Shifts HOI reconstruction from visually plausible trajectories to physically grounded interaction animation, enabling scalable monocular HOI-to-simulation demonstrations and broader use in humanoid-object behavior modeling.

Abstract: Recovering 4D human-object interaction (HOI) from monocular video is a key step toward scalable 3D content creation, embodied AI, and simulation-based learning. Recent methods can reconstruct temporally coherent human and object trajectories, but these trajectories often remain visual artifacts while failing to preserve stable contact, functional manipulation, or physical plausibility when used as reference motions for humanoid-object simulation. This reveals a fundamental interaction gap: HOI reconstruction should not stop at tracking a human and an object, but should recover the relation that makes their motion a coherent interaction. We introduce $\textbf{HA-HOI}$, a framework for reconstructing physically plausible 4D HOI animation from in-the-wild monocular videos. Instead of treating the human and object as independent entities in an ambiguous monocular 3D space, we propose a $\textit{human-first, object-follow}$ formulation. The human motion is recovered as the interaction anchor, and the object is reconstructed, aligned, and refined relative to the human action. The resulting kinematic trajectory is then projected into a physics-based humanoid-object simulation, where it acts as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout. Across benchmark and in-the-wild videos, $\textbf{HA-HOI}$ improves human-object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness over prior monocular HOI reconstruction methods. By moving beyond visually plausible trajectory recovery toward physically grounded interaction animation, our work takes a step toward turning general monocular HOI videos into scalable demonstrations for humanoid-object behavior. Project page: https://knoxzhao.github.io/real2sim_in_HOI/

</details>


### [45] [GeoVista: Visually Grounded Active Perception for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14475)
*Jiashun Zhu,Ronghao Fu,Jiasen Hu,Nachuan Xing,Xu Na,Xiao Yang,Zhiwen Lin,Weipeng Zhang,Lang Sun,Zhiheng Xue,Haoran Liu,Weijie Zhang,Bo Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoVista presents a planning-driven active perception framework for ultra-high-resolution remote sensing interpretation, enabling multi-path global planning with branch-wise local inspections and cross-region evidence tracking; introduces APEX-GRO trajectory corpus and an Observe-Plan-Track mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art on RSHR-Bench, XLRS-Bench, and LRS-VQA; code is available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RS-VL models mostly explore with a single zooming path, risking loss of global context, missed sparse evidence, and duplicate counting. A global, multi-path planning approach with cross-region evidence management is needed for robust UHR interpretation.

Method: GeoVista constructs a global exploration plan, then verifies multiple candidate regions via branch-wise local inspections while maintaining an explicit cross-region evidence state for de-duplication. It introduces APEX-GRO, a cold-start supervised trajectory corpus reframing UHR tasks as Global-Region-Object interactive reasoning with a scale-invariant spatial representation. The Observe-Plan-Track loop enables global observation, adaptive region inspection, and evidence tracking, and it adopts a GRPO-based strategy with step-wise rewards for planning, localization, and final answer correctness.

Result: Empirical evaluation on RSHR-Bench, XLRS-Bench, and LRS-VQA demonstrates state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: GeoVista confirms the effectiveness of planning-driven active perception for UHR RS interpretation and provides a new trajectory corpus and framework; code and dataset are publicly available.

Abstract: Interpreting ultra-high-resolution (UHR) remote sensing images requires models to search for sparse and tiny visual evidence across large-scale scenes. Existing remote sensing vision-language models can inspect local regions with zooming and cropping tools, but most exploration strategies follow either a one-shot focus or a single sequential trajectory. Such single-path exploration can lose global context, leave scattered regions unvisited, and revisit or count the same evidence multiple times. To this end, we propose GeoVista, a planning-driven active perception framework for UHR remote sensing interpretation. Instead of committing to one zooming path, GeoVista first builds a global exploration plan, then verifies multiple candidate regions through branch-wise local inspection, while maintaining an explicit evidence state for cross-region aggregation and de-duplication. To enable this behavior, we introduce APEX-GRO, a cold-start supervised trajectory corpus that reformulates diverse UHR tasks as Global-Region-Object interactive reasoning processes with a unified, scale-invariant spatial representation. We further design an Observe-Plan-Track mechanism for global observation, adaptive region inspection, and evidence tracking, and align the model with a GRPO-based strategy using step-wise rewards for planning, localization, and final answer correctness. Experiments on RSHR-Bench, XLRS-Bench, and LRS-VQA show that GeoVista achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/ryan6073/GeoVista

</details>


### [46] [Reduce the Artifacts Bias for More Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14486)
*Yiheng Li,Yang Yang,Zichang Tan,Gao Li,Zhen Lei,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a SEF framework that uses domain-specific experts via LoRA on a frozen base model to fuse reconstruction-based and GAN-based artifacts for robust AIGC fake image detection, improving generalization across diverse generative methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited and homogeneous artifacts of reconstruction-based detectors and the domain shift between reconstruction- and GAN-based fake images to enhance generalization.

Method: Train domain-specific experts with LoRA on a frozen foundation model; decoupled fusion with a gating network to adaptively combine expert features while preserving domain specialization; handle two aligned but distinct fake image domains (reconstruction-based and upsampling/GAN-based).

Result: Demonstrates strong detection performance across 13 benchmarks; shows improved generalization and robustness by leveraging complementary artifact patterns; code released.

Conclusion: SEF provides a robust, generalizable detection framework that captures diverse artifact cues and mitigates inter-domain interference, expanding applicability to a wider range of generative methods.

Abstract: As the misuse of AI-generated images grows, generalizable image detection techniques are urgently needed. Recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods adopt aligned training datasets to reduce content, size, and format biases, empowering models to capture robust forgery cues. A common strategy is to employ reconstruction techniques, e.g., VAE and DDIM, which show remarkable results in diffusion-based methods. However, such reconstruction-based approaches typically introduce limited and homogeneous artifacts, which cannot fully capture diverse generative patterns, such as GAN-based methods. To complement reconstruction-based fake images with aligned yet diverse artifact patterns, we propose a GAN-based upsampling approach that mimics GAN-generated fake patterns while preserving content, size, and format alignment. This naturally results in two aligned but distinct types of fake images. However, due to the domain shift between reconstruction-based and upsampling-based fake images, direct mixed training causes suboptimal results, where one domain disrupts feature learning of the other. Accordingly, we propose a Separate Expert Fusion (SEF) framework to extract complementary artifact information and reduce inter-domain interference. We first train domain-specific experts via LoRA adaptation on a frozen foundational model, then conduct decoupled fusion with a gating network to adaptively combine expert features while retaining their specialized knowledge. Rather than merely benefiting GAN-generated image detection, this design introduces diverse and complementary artifact patterns that enable SEF to learn a more robust decision boundary and improve generalization across broader generative methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method yields strong results across 13 diverse benchmarks. Codes are released at: https://github.com/liyih/SEF_AIGC_detection.

</details>


### [47] [Head Forcing: Long Autoregressive Video Generation via Head Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14487)
*Jiahao Tian,Yiwei Wang,Gang Yu,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, head-type aware KV caching for autoregressive video diffusion; categorizes attention heads into local, anchor, and memory roles with tailored caching, enabling minute-scale generation and interactive multi-prompt synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AR video diffusion models suffer from error accumulation and context loss over long horizons; treating all attention heads uniformly is suboptimal for long-range coherence and detail preservation.

Method: Identify three head roles (local for detail refinement, anchor for structure, memory for long-range context). Apply tailored KV-cache strategies per head type; implement hierarchical memory with dynamic episodic updates for memory heads; use head-wise RoPE re-encoding to keep positional encodings within the pretrained range; training-free (no fine-tuning) framework.

Result: Without additional training, extends generation from ~5 seconds to minute-level durations, supports multi-prompt interactive synthesis, and outperforms baselines.

Conclusion: Head Forcing demonstrates that head-wise specialization and caching, coupled with RoPE re-encoding, can dramatically improve long-horizon AR video diffusion performance without extra training.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time synthesis but suffer from error accumulation and context loss over long horizons. We discover that attention heads in AR video diffusion transformers serve functionally distinct roles as local heads for detail refinement, anchor heads for structural stabilization, and memory heads for long-range context aggregation, yet existing methods treat them uniformly, leading to suboptimal KV cache allocation. We propose Head Forcing, a training-free framework that assigns each head type a tailored KV cache strategy: local and anchor heads retain only essential tokens, while memory heads employ a hierarchical memory system with dynamic episodic updates for long-range consistency. A head-wise RoPE re-encoding scheme further ensures positional encodings remain within the pretrained range. Without additional training, Head Forcing extends generation from 5 seconds to minute-level duration, supports multi-prompt interactive synthesis, and consistently outperforms existing baselines. Project Page: https://jiahaotian-sjtu.github.io/headforcing.github.io/.

</details>


### [48] [HASTE: Training-Free Video Diffusion Acceleration via Head-Wise Adaptive Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14513)
*Xuzhe Zheng,Yuexiao Ma,Jing Xu,Xiawu Zheng,Rongrong Ji,Fei Chao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Head-wise adaptive, training-free sparse attention for diffusion-based video generation that uses Temporal Mask Reuse and Error-guided Budgeted Calibration to reduce computation, achieving up to 1.93x speedup at 720P on Wan2.1 models while preserving quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the quadratic cost of full attention in diffusion-based video generation and address shortcomings of existing online top-p sparse attention, specifically mask-prediction overhead and uniform head thresholds amid heterogeneity.

Method: Introduce a head-wise adaptive framework with two plug-ins: (1) Temporal Mask Reuse to skip mask prediction based on query-key drift, and (2) Error-guided Budgeted Calibration to assign per-head top-p thresholds by minimizing model-output error within a global sparsity budget.

Result: Empirical improvements on Wan2.1-1.3B and Wan2.1-14B for XAttention and SVG2, achieving up to 1.93x speedup at 720P while maintaining competitive video quality and similarity metrics.

Conclusion: Head-wise adaptive, plug-in sparse attention components substantially improve speed-accuracy trade-offs for training-free sparse attention in diffusion-based video generation, enhancing practicality for large-scale video models.

Abstract: Diffusion-based video generation has advanced substantially in visual fidelity and temporal coherence, but practical deployment remains limited by the quadratic complexity of full attention. Training-free sparse attention is attractive because it accelerates pretrained models without retraining, yet existing online top-$p$ sparse attention still spends non-negligible cost on mask prediction and applies shared thresholds despite strong head-level heterogeneity. We show that these two overlooked factors limit the practical speed-quality trade-off of training-free sparse attention in Video DiTs. To address them, we introduce a head-wise adaptive framework with two plug-in components: Temporal Mask Reuse, which skips unnecessary mask prediction based on query-key drift, and Error-guided Budgeted Calibration, which assigns per-head top-$p$ thresholds by minimizing measured model-output error under a global sparsity budget. On Wan2.1-1.3B and Wan2.1-14B, our method consistently improves XAttention and SVG2, achieving up to 1.93 times speedup at 720P while maintaining competitive video quality and similarity metrics.

</details>


### [49] [ArcGate: Adaptive Arctangent Gated Activation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14518)
*Avik Bhattacharya,Siddhant Dnyanesh Gole,Subhasis Chaudhuri,Alejandro C. Frery,Biplab Banerjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adaptive ArcGate: a 7-parameter per-layer activation enabling flexible, learnable nonlinearities; shows state-of-the-art performance and robustness in high-resolution remote sensing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard fixed-shape activations (ReLU, GELU, SiLU) may not match the evolving feature distributions across deep layers and data modalities, particularly in high-resolution remote sensing. An adaptive activation could optimize non-linearity to the feature hierarchy and data distribution.

Method: Introduce ArcGate: a three-stage nonlinear transformation with seven learnable parameters per layer. Evaluate on ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16 across PatternNet, UC Merced, and EuroSAT MSI datasets. Compare against standard baselines.

Result: ArcGate achieves a peak overall accuracy of 99.67% on PatternNet and offers superior robustness, maintaining a 26.65% performance lead over ReLU under moderate Gaussian noise (std 0.1). Learned parameters show depth-dependent gating strength, increasing in deeper layers to enhance signal propagation.

Conclusion: ArcGate is a robust, adaptive general activation function suitable for high-resolution earth observation tasks, enabling data-driven shaping of non-linearity and improved performance and resilience.

Abstract: Activation functions are central to deep networks, influencing non-linearity, feature learning, convergence, and robustness. This paper proposes the Adaptive Arctangent Gated Activation (ArcGate) function, a flexible formulation that generates a broad spectrum of activation shapes via a three-stage non-linear transformation. Unlike conventional fixed-shape activations such as ReLU, GELU, or SiLU, ArcGate uses seven learnable parameters per layer, allowing the neural network to autonomously optimize its non-linearity to the specific requirements of the feature hierarchy and data distribution. We evaluate ArcGate using ResNet-50 and Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) architectures on three widely used remote sensing benchmarks: PatternNet, UC Merced Land Use, and the 13-band EuroSAT MSI multispectral dataset. Experimental results show that ArcGate consistently outperforms standard baselines, achieving a peak overall accuracy of 99.67% on PatternNet. Most notably, ArcGate exhibits superior structural resilience in noisy environments, maintaining a 26.65% performance lead over ReLU under moderate Gaussian noise (standard deviation 0.1). Analysis of the learned parameters reveals a depth-dependent functional evolution, where the model increases gating strength in deeper layers to enhance signal propagation. These findings suggest that ArcGate is a robust and adaptive general node activation function for high-resolution earth observation tasks.

</details>


### [50] [From Sparse to Dense: Spatio-Temporal Fusion for Multi-View 3D Human Pose Estimation with DenseWarper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14525)
*Ling Li,Changjie Chen,Yuyan Wang,Jiaqing Lyu,Kenglun Chang,Yiyun Chen,Zhidong Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes DenseWarper with sparse interleaved multi-view inputs for 3D pose estimation to boost temporal resolution and performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP using epipolar-based heatmap exchange; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional multi-view 3D pose estimation uses simultaneous frames, which neglects temporal dependencies and limits temporal resolution; leveraging inter-view temporal cues can improve accuracy and increase effective frame rate.

Method: Introduce sparse interleaved input: views captured at different times (e.g., view 1 at t, view 2 at t+δ). DenseWarper uses epipolar geometry to exchange spatio-temporal heatmaps and fuse cues across time and views, enabling higher temporal resolution. Evaluated on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP; source code available at the provided GitHub link.

Result: Outperforms traditional dense multi-view input methods and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the two datasets. The approach theoretically increases the output pose frame rate by a factor up to N with N cameras and reduces data redundancy by using sparse frames.

Conclusion: Sparse interleaved multi-view input combined with epipolar-based fusion yields higher temporal resolution and better 3D pose estimates, with data efficiency and practical open-source implementation.

Abstract: In multi-view 3D human pose estimation, models typically rely on images captured simultaneously from different camera views to predict a pose at a specific moment. While providing accurate spatial information, this traditional approach often overlooks the rich temporal dependencies between adjacent frames. We propose a novel 3D human pose estimation input method: the sparse interleaved input to address this. This method leverages images captured from different camera views at various time points (e.g., View 1 at time $t$ and View 2 at time $t+δ$), allowing our model to capture rich spatio-temporal information and effectively boost performance. More importantly, this approach offers two key advantages: First, it can theoretically increase the output pose frame rate by N times with N cameras, thereby breaking through single-view frame rate limitations and enhancing the temporal resolution of the production. Second, using a sparse subset of available frames, our method can reduce data redundancy and simultaneously achieve better performance. We introduce the DenseWarper model, which leverages epipolar geometry for efficient spatio-temporal heatmap exchange. We conducted extensive experiments on the Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP datasets. Results demonstrate that our method, utilizing only sparse interleaved images as input, outperforms traditional dense multi-view input approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance. The source code for this work is available at: https://github.com/lingli1724/DenseWarper-ICLR2026

</details>


### [51] [Mitigating Mask Prior Drift and Positional Attention Collapse in Large Diffusion Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14530)
*Sujung Hong,Chanyong Yoon,Seongjae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two key failure modes in large diffusion vision-language models during long-form generation (mask prior drift and misaligned positional attention), addressed by training-free Mask Prior Suppression and Monotonic RoPE Scaling; yields improvements across benchmarks and architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDVLMs enable parallel decoding and bidirectional context but exhibit repetition and degraded visual grounding in long-form generation; a lightweight, training-free remedy is needed that generalizes across architectures.

Method: Introduce Mask Prior Suppression to curb drift of generated token representations away from mask priors; apply Monotonic RoPE Scaling to align positional attention bias with the iterative unmasking process; both are training-free and plug-and-play.

Result: Empirical improvements over baselines on general multimodal benchmarks and visual grounding tasks; robust gains on long-form description tasks; method generalizes across LDVLM architectures with no additional training.

Conclusion: A training-free, plug-and-play mitigation can effectively address long-form generation failures in LDVLMs, improving repetition control and visual grounding and generalizing across architectures.

Abstract: Large diffusion vision-language models (LDVLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models, enabling parallel decoding for efficient inference and leveraging bidirectional attention for global context. Despite these advances, their behavior under long-form generation remains underexplored. In this work, we show that existing LDVLMs suffer from repetitive generation and degraded visual grounding, and identify two underlying causes. First, repetitive generation originates from a mask token prior: since generation tokens are initialized as mask tokens, their hidden representations progressively drift toward a shared prior direction over generation steps. Second, a fundamental misalignment between the positional attention bias and the iterative unmasking process suppresses attention toward informative visual tokens, degrading visual grounding. Based on these insights, we propose a training-free approach, introducing Mask Prior Suppression and Monotonic RoPE Scaling to mitigate mask prior drift and positional attention collapse during decoding. Experiments on general multimodal benchmarks and visual grounding tasks demonstrate improvements over baseline LDVLMs, with robust gains on long-form description benchmarks. Our results show that these failures can be effectively addressed with a lightweight, plug-and-play strategy that requires no additional training and generalizes across diverse LDVLM architectures.

</details>


### [52] [PROVE: A Perceptual RemOVal cohErence Benchmark for Visual Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14534)
*Fuhao Li,Shaofeng You,Jiagao Hu,Yu Liu,Yuxuan Chen,Zepeng Wang,Fei Wang,Daiguo Zhou,Jian Luan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A perceptual evaluation framework for object removal in images/videos, introducing RC-S (spatial coherence) and RC-T (temporal coherence) metrics, along with PROVE-Bench (PROVE-M and PROVE-H) benchmark, achieving stronger alignment with human judgments than existing metrics, with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing full-reference metrics incentivize copy-paste erasure, no-reference metrics bias toward blur, and global temporal metrics miss localized artifacts. There is a need for perception-aligned, localized, and temporally coherent evaluation for object removal tasks.

Method: RC-S measures spatial coherence via sliding-window feature comparisons between masked regions and their backgrounds; RC-T assesses temporal consistency by tracking distributions within shared restored regions across adjacent frames. PROVE-Bench comprises PROVE-M (80-video paired dataset with motion augmentation) and PROVE-H (100-video challenging subset without ground truth). Code and benchmark are released publicly.

Result: RC metrics show substantially stronger alignment with human judgments across diverse image and video benchmarks compared to existing evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: Propose PROVE (Perceptual RemOVal cohErence) framework for perceptual evaluation of removal tasks; RC-S and RC-T provide localized, perception-aligned metrics; PROVE-Bench enables community benchmarking with real-world data; code is available online.

Abstract: Evaluating object removal in images and videos remains challenging because the task is inherently one-to-many, yet existing metrics frequently disagree with human perception. Full-reference metrics reward copy-paste behaviors over genuine erasure; no-reference metrics suffer from systematic biases such as favoring blurry results; and global temporal metrics are insensitive to localized artifacts within edited regions. To address these limitations, we propose RC (Removal Coherence), a pair of perception-aligned metrics: RC-S, which measures spatial coherence via sliding-window feature comparison between masked and background regions, and RC-T, which measures temporal consistency via distribution tracking within shared restored regions across adjacent frames. To validate RC and support community benchmarking, we further introduce PROVE-Bench, a two-tier real-world benchmark comprising PROVE-M, an 80-video paired dataset with motion augmentation, and PROVE-H, a 100-video challenging subset without ground truth. Together, RC metrics and PROVE-Bench form the PROVE (Perceptual RemOVal cohErence) evaluation framework for visual media. Experiments across diverse image and video benchmarks demonstrate that RC achieves substantially stronger alignment with human judgments than existing evaluation protocols. The code for RC metrics and PROVE-Bench are publicly available at: https://github.com/xiaomi-research/prove/.

</details>


### [53] [Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network for Robust Gait Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14548)
*Xiaoyun Wang,Cunrong Li,Wu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network (LSTCN) for gait recognition that enhances 2D CNNs with temporal processing via global bidirectional spatial pooling and a local spatiotemporal convolution layer, plus asymmetric kernels to attend to temporal, spatial and spatiotemporal domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to overcome reliance on static silhouette features and computationally heavy sequential models (LSTM, 3D CNNs) and to robustly capture intrinsic gait motion under covariates while maintaining efficiency.
