Method: A dual-branch architecture: Global Bidirectional Spatial Pooling (GBSP) that decomposes spatial features into horizontal and vertical strips to reduce gait tensor dimensionality, enabling standard 2D convolutions to operate on temporal dimension; Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional (LSTC) layer that jointly processes temporal and spatial dims; asymmetric convolution kernels that separately attend to temporal, spatial, and joint spatiotemporal patterns.

Result: The abstract asserts effectiveness of LSTCN but provides no empirical results or benchmarks.

Conclusion: LSTCN offers a simple yet effective approach to infusing temporal reasoning into 2D CNNs for gait recognition, with potential gains in efficiency and robustness; further experiments needed to quantify gains and compare against LSTM/3D CNN baselines.

Abstract: Gait recognition, as a promising biometric technology, identifies individuals through their unique walking patterns and offers distinctive advantages including non-invasiveness, long-range applicability, and resistance to deliberate disguise. Despite these merits, capturing the intrinsic motion patterns concealed within consecutive video frames remains challenging due to the complexity of video data and the interference of external covariates such as viewpoint changes, clothing variations, and carrying conditions. Existing approaches predominantly rely on either static appearance features extracted from individual silhouette frames or employ complex sequential models (\eg, LSTM, 3D convolutions) that demand substantial computational resources and sophisticated training strategies. To address these limitations, we propose a Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network (LSTCN), a structurally simple yet highly effective dual-branch architecture that endows standard two-dimensional convolutional networks with the capacity to extract temporal information. Specifically, we introduce a Global Bidirectional Spatial Pooling (GBSP) mechanism that reduces the dimensionality of gait tensors by decomposing spatial features into horizontal and vertical strip-based local representations, enabling the temporal dimension to participate in standard 2D convolution operations. Building upon this, we design a Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional (LSTC) layer that jointly processes temporal and spatial dimensions, allowing the network to adaptively learn strip-based gait motion patterns. We further extend this formulation with asymmetric convolution kernels that independently attend to the temporal, spatial, and joint spatiotemporal domains, thereby enriching the extracted feature representations.

</details>


### [54] [LiWi: Layering in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14552)
*Yu He,Fang Li,Haoyang Tong,Lichen Ma,Xinyuan Shan,Jingling Fu,Dong Chen,Luohang Liu,Junshi Huang,Yan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: New ADD data synthesis pipeline for layered in-the-wild images and a joint shadow-guided + degradation-restoration model achieve SoTA natural image decomposition (RGB L1, Alpha IoU) on LiWi-100k; dataset and code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome lack of scalable layered data and limited modeling of illumination and boundary effects in natural images; enable fine-grained editing in the wild.

Method: Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) to synthesize layered data; LiWi-100k dataset; shadow-guided learning to model illumination; degradation-restoration objective to improve alpha boundary accuracy by recovering clean foreground from degraded input.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on natural image decomposition; better RGB L1 and Alpha IoU compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of combining synthetic, agent-guided data with illumination-aware and boundary-correcting objectives; promises improved editing workflows for real-world imagery; code and dataset release planned.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative models have empowered impressive layered image generation, yet their success is largely confined to graphic design domains. The layering of in-the-wild images remains an underexplored problem, limiting fine-grained editing and applications of images in real-world scenarios. Specifically, challenges remain in scalable layered data and the modeling of object interaction in natural images, such as illumination effects and structural boundary. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a novel framework for high-fidelity natural image decomposition. First, we introduce an Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) pipeline that orchestrates agents and tools to synthesize layered data without manual intervention. Utilizing this pipeline, we construct a large-scale dataset, named LiWi-100k, with over 100,000 high-quality layered in-the-wild images. Second, we present a novel framework that jointly improves photometric fidelity and alpha boundary accuracy. Specifically, shadow-guided learning explicitly models the illumination effects, and degradation-restoration objective provides boundary-correction supervision by recovering clean foreground image from degraded one. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance in natural image decomposition, outperforming existing models in RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics. We will soon release our code and dataset.

</details>


### [55] [SpectraFlow: Unifying Structural Pretraining and Frequency Adaptation for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14566)
*Zhiquan Chen,Haitao Wang,Guowei Zou,Hejun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage, structure-aware pretraining and boundary-focused decoding framework improves medical image segmentation in low-data regimes by aligning image and mask representations and refining high-frequency boundaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation in scarce-annotation settings suffers from poor generalization and blurred boundaries due to texture bias in self-supervised pretraining; segmentation quality relies on geometry, topology, and precise boundaries.

Method: Stage 1: Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space via latent transport regression; masks serve as conditional structural guidance rather than targets, with a lightweight Dispersive Loss to mitigate representation collapse. Stage 2: a lightweight decoder is trained with Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution to refine high-frequency boundaries under appearance variation.

Result: Experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS show consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data settings and sharper boundary delineation.

Conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework effectively couples structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding, yielding robust, accurate segmentation in low-data medical imaging scenarios.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation remains challenging in low-data regimes, where scarce annotations often yield poor generalization and ambiguous boundaries with missing fine structures. Recent self-supervised pretraining has improved transferability, but it often exhibits a texture bias. In contrast, accurate segmentation is inherently geometry-aware and depends on both topological consistency and precise boundary preservation. To address this problem, we propose a two-stage framework that couples structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding. In Stage-1, we aim to learn structure-aware representations for downstream segmentation in low-data regimes. To this end, we propose Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining, which aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space through latent transport regression, where masks act as conditional structural guidance rather than prediction targets, making the pretraining task-agnostic. To further improve training stability under scarce supervision, we incorporate a lightweight Dispersive Loss to prevent representation collapse. In Stage-2, we fine-tune the pretrained encoder with a lightweight decoder that combines Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution for high-frequency boundary refinement under appearance variation. Experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS demonstrate consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data settings and sharper boundary delineation.

</details>


### [56] [Bridging Brain and Semantics: A Hierarchical Framework for Semantically Enhanced fMRI-to-Video Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14569)
*Yujie Wei,Chenglong Ma,Jianxiong Gao,Chenhui Wang,Shiwei Zhang,Biao Gong,Shuai Tan,Hangjie Yuan,Hongming Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CineNeuron integrates a two-stage, memory-augmented approach to reconstruct videos from fMRI: bottom-up semantic enrichment to a rich multimodal embedding, followed by top-down memory integration (Mixture-of-Memories) to refine reconstructions, achieving state-of-the-art on two benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and rich video content, including missing video-specific cues (actions) and lack of prior knowledge in traditional embeddings.

Method: A hierarchical framework with: (1) bottom-up semantic enrichment mapping fMRI signals to a rich embedding space capturing textual semantics, image content, actions, and objects; (2) top-down memory integration using Mixture-of-Memories to select and fuse relevant memories from prior data with the fMRI embedding to refine video reconstruction.

Result: Experimental results on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks show CineNeuron outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics.

Conclusion: A two-stage, memory-augmented architecture effectively narrows the semantic gap between fMRI and video content, leveraging prior experiences to improve reconstruction quality.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic visual experiences as videos from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is pivotal for advancing the understanding of neural processes. However, current fMRI-to-video reconstruction methods are hindered by a semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and the rich content of videos, stemming from a reliance on incomplete semantic embeddings that neither capture video-specific cues (e.g., actions) nor integrate prior knowledge. To this end, we draw inspiration from the dual-pathway processing mechanism in human brain and introduce CineNeuron, a novel hierarchical framework for semantically enhanced video reconstruction from fMRI signals with two synergistic stages. First, a bottom-up semantic enrichment stage maps fMRI signals to a rich embedding space that comprehensively captures textual semantics, image contents, action concepts, and object categories. Second, a top-down memory integration stage utilizes the proposed Mixture-of-Memories method to dynamically select relevant "memories" from previously seen data and fuse them with the fMRI embedding to refine the video reconstruction. Extensive experimental results on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks demonstrate that CineNeuron surpasses state-of-the-art methods across various metrics.

</details>


### [57] [Med-DisSeg: Dispersion-Driven Representation Learning for Fine-Grained Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14579)
*Zhiquan Chen,Haitao Wang,Guowei Zou,Hejun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Med DisSeg introduces a dispersion-driven framework for medical image segmentation that uses a lightweight Dispersive Loss to enlarge inter-sample representation margins and adaptive attention to preserve fine-grained structure, aiming to mitigate representation collapse and improve multi-scale decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Segmentation in medical imaging suffers from heterogeneous appearances, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability, leading to blurred activations and unreliable separation. Representation collapse and insufficient multi-scale decoding are identified as core causes.

Method: A Dispersive Loss treats in-batch hidden representations as negative pairs to expand inter-sample margins, producing dispersed, boundary-aware embeddings with low overhead. An adaptive attention mechanism strengthens structure-sensitive encoder responses, while an adaptive multi-scale decoder calibrates features to preserve local texture and global shape.

Result: Extensive experiments on five datasets across three imaging modalities show state-of-the-art performance and robust cross-task applicability, including competitive results on multi-organ CT segmentation.

Conclusion: Med DisSeg effectively couples improved representation learning with fine-grained, scale-aware decoding, achieving robust, boundary-sensitive segmentation across diverse medical imaging tasks.

Abstract: Accurate medical image segmentation is fundamental to precision medicine, yet robust delineation remains challenging under heterogeneous appearances, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability. Similar intensity and texture patterns between targets and surrounding tissues often lead to blurred activations and unreliable separation. We attribute these failures to representation collapse during encoding and insufficient fine grained multi scale decoding. To address these issues, we propose Med DisSeg, a dispersion driven medical image segmentation framework that jointly improves representation learning and anatomical delineation. Med DisSeg combines a lightweight Dispersive Loss with adaptive attention for fine grained structure segmentation. The Dispersive Loss enlarges inter sample margins by treating in batch hidden representations as negative pairs, producing well dispersed and boundary aware embeddings with negligible overhead. Based on these enhanced representations, the encoder strengthens structure sensitive responses, while the decoder performs adaptive multi scale calibration to preserve complementary local texture and global shape information. Extensive experiments on five datasets spanning three imaging modalities demonstrate consistent state of the art performance. Moreover, Med DisSeg achieves competitive results on multi organ CT segmentation, supporting its robustness and cross task applicability.

</details>


### [58] [A Picture is Worth a Thousand Words? An Empirical Study of Aggregation Strategies for Visual Financial Document Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14581)
*Ho Hung Lim,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-vector aggregation in Visual RAG for financial documents can erase critical digit-level semantic changes; patch-level signals reveal these changes and expose risks of single-vector approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual RAG trades off retrieval efficiency for richer document representation by converting documents into patch tokens; practicality demands aggregation to a single vector, but this risks information loss in high-stakes domains like finance.

Method: Construct a diagnostic benchmark with financial documents designed so small digit changes cause semantic shifts; compare patch-level vs single-vector representations; test across model scales and retrieval-optimized embeddings; evaluate multiple mitigation strategies; analyze root cause via texture dominance.

Result: Single-vector aggregation collapses distinct documents into nearly identical vectors; patch-level representations detect semantic changes; aggregation obscures these details; global texture dominance identified as root cause; results hold across scales and mitigation approaches.

Conclusion: Caution is warranted for single-vector visual document retrieval in financial applications; may require hybrid or per-region representations, alternative aggregation schemes, or more robust retrieval architectures to preserve digit-level semantic information.

Abstract: Visual RAG has offered an alternative to traditional RAG. It treats documents as images and uses vision encoders to obtain vision patch tokens. However, hundreds of patch tokens per document create retrieval and storage challenges in a vector database. Practical deployment requires aggregating them into a single vector. This raises a critical question: does single-vector aggregation lose key information in financial documents? We develop a diagnostic benchmark using financial documents where changes in single digits can lead to significant semantic shifts. Our experiments show that single-vector aggregation collapses different documents with almost identical vectors. Metrics show that the patch level detects semantic changes, and confirm that aggregation obscures these details. We identify global texture dominance as the root cause. Our findings are consistent across model scales, retrieval-optimized embeddings, and multiple mitigation strategies, highlighting significant risks for single-vector visual document retrieval in financial applications.

</details>


### [59] [FedStain: Modeling Higher-Order Stain Statistics for Federated Domain Generalization in Computational Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14590)
*Fengyi Zhang,Junya Zhang,Wenzhuo Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FedStain introduces a stain-aware Federated DG framework that exchanges higher-order stain moments (skewness and kurtosis) instead of pixel data, enabling robust cross-site WSI analysis under privacy constraints. It adds a contrastive cross-site aggregation and achieves up to 3.9 percentage points absolute accuracy gains on Camelyon17 and the MvMidog-Fed benchmark, outperforming FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines; it is the first FedDG approach to model higher-order stain statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-institutional stain heterogeneity in whole-slide imaging (WSI) under strict data governance. Domain generalization (DG) requires centralized data; FedL preserves privacy but relies on low-order statistics assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions. Real-world stains are asymmetric and heavy-tailed due to diffusion and scanner nonlinearity, making higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics critical for robust generalization.

Method: FedStain exchanges higher-order stain moments (skewness and kurtosis) as compact descriptors during federated optimization, with no pixel-level data transmission. It combines these descriptors with a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations while maintaining strict data constraints.

Result: Experiments on Camelyon17 and the new MvMidog-Fed benchmark show FedStain yields consistent improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to 3.9 percentage points in absolute accuracy.

Conclusion: FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology while preserving data privacy and achieving improved generalization to non-Gaussian stain distributions.

Abstract: Robust whole-slide image (WSI) analysis under strict data-governance remains challenging due to substantial cross-institutional stain heterogeneity. Domain generalization (DG) mitigates these shifts but typically requires centralized data, conflicting with privacy regulations. Federated learning (FedL) provides a decentralized alternative; however, existing FedL and federated DG (FedDG) approaches rely almost exclusively on low-order statistics, assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions. In contrast, real-world staining processes often produce asymmetric, heavy-tailed color distributions due to biochemical diffusion and scanner nonlinearity. Consequently, current methods fail to model the higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics dominating real-world stain variability. To address this, we propose FedStain, a stain-aware FedDG framework explicitly incorporating higher-order stain moments--skewness and kurtosis--as compact statistical descriptors exchanged during federated optimization. These descriptors require no pixel-level data transmission, preserving strict privacy and communication efficiency, while enabling the global model to capture stain variability missed by low-order statistics. FedStain also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations without relaxing data constraints. Extensive experiments on Camelyon17 and our new MvMidog-Fed benchmark show FedStain yields consistent improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to +3.9% absolute accuracy. To our knowledge, FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology.

</details>


### [60] [TOPOS: High-Fidelity and Efficient Industry-Grade 3D Head Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14594)
*Bojun Xiong,Zoubin Bi,Xinghui Peng,Yunmu Wang,Junchen Deng,Jun Liang,Jing Li,Bowen Cai,Huan Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TOPOS enables production-grade, single-image conditioned 3D head generation with a fixed, studio-style topology, via a topology-mapping VAE (TOPOS-VAE) using a Perceiver Resampler, a rectified flow transformer (TOPOS-DiT) for mesh generation, and an end-to-end texture module (TOPOS-Texture), achieving state-of-the-art results with consistent vertex-level correspondence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial pipelines require a fixed reference topology for rigging and asset reuse; existing 3D generative models produce meshes with varying topology and excessive vertices, hindering correspondence and workflow efficiency.

Method: TOPOS combines three components: TOPOS-VAE (a topology-constrained VAE that uses Perceiver Resampler to map heterogeneous input point clouds to a common reference topology); TOPOS-DiT (a rectified flow transformer to generate high-fidelity meshes from a single image within the fixed topology); TOPOS-Texture (an end-to-end module fine-tuning a multimodal image generator to output relightable UV textures aligned to the mesh).

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art 3D head generation performance, outperforming classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generators; demonstrated consistent vertex-level correspondence and superior digital human creation.

Conclusion: TOPOS provides a production-ready framework for single-image conditioned 3D head generation with a fixed topology, enabling reliable asset-level reuse, rigging, and animation, and can serve as a blueprint for topology-constrained 3D generation in industry pipelines.

Abstract: High-fidelity 3D head generation plays a crucial role in the film, animation and video game industries. In industrial pipelines, studios typically enforce a fixed reference topology across all head assets, as such a clean and uniform topology is a prerequisite for production-level rigging, skinning and animation. In this paper, we present TOPOS, a framework tailored for single image conditioned 3D head generation that jointly recovers geometry and appearance under such an industry-standard topology. In contrast to general 3D generative models which produce triangle meshes with inconsistent topology and numerous vertices, hindering semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse, TOPOS generates head meshes with a fixed, studio-style topology, enabling consistent vertex-level correspondence across all generated heads. To model heads under this unified topology, we proposed a novel variational autoencoder structure, termed TOPOS-VAE. Inspired by multi-model large language models (MLLMs), our TOPOS-VAE leverages the Perceiver Resampler to convert input pointclouds sampled from head meshes of diverse topologies into the target reference topology. Building upon TOPOS-VAE's structured latent space, we train a rectified flow transformer, TOPOS-DiT, to efficiently generate high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. We further present TOPOS-Texture, an end-to-end module that produces relightable UV texture maps from the same portrait image via fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model. The generated textures are spatially aligned with the underlying mesh geometry and faithfully preserve high-frequency appearance details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TOPOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D head generation, surpassing both classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, highlighting its effectiveness for digital human creation.

</details>


### [61] [VMU-Diff: A Coarse-to-fine Multi-source Data Fusion Framework for Precipitation Nowcasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14597)
*Chunlei Shi,Hao Li,Yufeng Zhu,Boyu Liu,Yongchao Feng,Zengliang Zang,Hongbin Wang,Yanlan Yang,Dan Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage coarse-to-fine precipitation nowcasting framework (VMU-Diff) that fuses multi-source data via Vision Mamba blocks: (1) a deterministic coarse stage predicting global motion from radar + satellite data; (2) a residual diffusion-based fine stage refining details, reducing blurring/artifacts and improving short-term accuracy. Evaluated on Jiangsu SWAN with improvements over state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address chaotic precipitation dynamics and limitations of single-source radar-based models: deterministic MSE leads to blur, probabilistic diffusion causes artifacts and inefficiency. Leverage multi-source inputs and a coarse-to-fine strategy to separate global motion from fine details.

Method: Two-stage architecture: deterministic coarse stage uses spatial-temporal attention and Vision Mamba state-space blocks to fuse radar and multi-band satellite data and predict global echo dynamics. Fine stage employs a spatio-temporal residual generator based on residual conditional diffusion, reconstructing residuals from the coarse prediction and ground truth via a conditional Mamba state-space module.

Result: Reported improvements over state-of-the-art methods on Jiangsu SWAN datasets, especially for short-term forecasts.

Conclusion: The proposed VMU-Diff framework effectively integrates multi-source data and a coarse-to-fine strategy to enhance precipitation nowcasting, achieving better short-term accuracy while mitigating common deterministic blur and diffusion artifacts.

Abstract: Precipitation nowcasting is a vital spatio-temporal prediction task for meteorological applications but faces challenges due to the chaotic property of precipitation systems. Existing methods predominantly rely on single-source radar data to build either deterministic or probabilistic models for extrapolation. However, the single deterministic model suffers from blurring due to MSE convergence. The single probabilistic model, typically represented by diffusion models, can generate fine details but suffers from spurious artifacts that compromise accuracy and computational inefficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel coarse-to-fine Vision Mamba Unet and residual Diffusion (VMU-Diff) based precipitation nowcasting framework. It realizes precipitation nowcasting through a two-stage process, i.e., a deterministic model-based coarse stage to predict global motion trends and a probabilistic model-based fine stage to generate fine prediction details. In the coarse prediction stage, rather than single-source radar data, both radar and multi-band satellite data are taken as input. A spatial-temporal attention block and several Vision mamba state-space blocks realize multi-source data fusion, and predict the future echo global dynamics. The fine-grained stage is realized by a spatio-temporal refine generator based on residual conditional diffusion models. It first obtains spatio-temporal residual features based on coarse prediction and ground truth, and further reconstructs the residual via conditional Mamba state-space module. Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets demonstrate the improvements of our method over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in short-term forecasts.

</details>


### [62] [Towards Accurate Single Panoramic 3D Detection: A Semantic Gaussian Centric Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14601)
*Kanglin Ning,Yiran Zhao,Wenrui Li,Shaoru Sun,Xingtao Wang,Xiaopeng Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PanoGSDet introduces a monocular panoramic 3D detector using continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations to replace discrete 3D grids, combining panoramic depth estimation with a semantic Gaussian pipeline to produce 3D bounding boxes, achieving state-of-the-art results on Structured3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mapping 2D panoramic features to 3D is challenging; grid-based 3D representations disrupt geometric continuity and are inefficient. A continuous 3D Gaussian representation preserves geometry and enables smoother 3D reasoning.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) panoramic depth estimation to extract equirectangular semantic and depth features; (2) semantic Gaussian component with (a) a lifting module projecting spherical features into 3D Gaussians, (b) an optimization module refining Gaussians, and (c) a Gaussian-guided head predicting 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussians.

Result: Extensive experiments on Structured3D show significant performance gains over existing methods, validating the effectiveness of the continuous Gaussian representation.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that continuous semantic 3D Gaussians are a viable representation for monocular panoramic 3D detection, addressing discretized-grid limitations and improving accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Three-dimensional object detection in panoramic imagery is crucial for comprehensive scene understanding, yet accurately mapping 2D features to 3D remains a significant challenge. Prevailing methods often project 2D features onto discrete 3D grids, which break geometric continuity and limit representation efficiency. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes PanoGSDet, a monocular panoramic 3D detection framework built upon continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations. The proposed framework comprises a panoramic depth estimation component and a semantic Gaussian component. The panoramic depth estimation component extracts the equirectangular semantic and depth features from the monocular panorama input. The semantic Gaussian component includes a semantic Gaussian lifting module that projects spherical features into 3D semantic Gaussians, a semantic Gaussian optimization module that refines these semantic Gaussians, and a Gaussian guided prediction head that generates 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussian representations. Extensive experiments on the Structured3D dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing methods.

</details>


### [63] [MambaRain: Multi-Scale Mamba-Attention Framework for 0-3 Hour Precipitation Nowcasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14606)
*Chunlei Shi,Cui Wu,Xiang Xu,Hao Li,Ni Fan,Xue Han,Yongchao Feng,Yufeng Zhu,Boyu Liu,Zengliang Zang,Hongbin Wang,Yanlan Yang,Dan Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid MambaRain: a multi-scale encoder-decoder that combines linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling (Mamba blocks) with spatial self-attention to extend deterministic nowcasting to 2-3 hours, aided by a spectral loss to reduce blurring; shows strong 0-3h gains, especially in the 2-3h window.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic nowcasting methods struggle beyond ~90 minutes due to difficulty in capturing long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar data.

Method: Hybrid architecture integrating Mamba blocks (state-space, long-range temporal modeling with linear complexity) for temporal dynamics and self-attention modules for explicit spatial correlations; spectral loss to mitigate blur artifacts in chaotic precipitation.

Result: Substantial accuracy improvements over existing deterministic methods for 0-3 hour nowcasting, with particularly large gains in the 2-3 hour window.

Conclusion: The complementary synergy of temporal and spatial modeling extends viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours while preserving fine-scale motion details through spectral loss.

Abstract: Accurate precipitation nowcasting over extended horizons (0-3 hours) is essential for disaster mitigation and operational decision-making, yet remains a critical challenge in the field. Existing deterministic approaches are predominantly constrained to shorter prediction windows (0-2 hours), exhibiting severe performance degradation beyond 90 minutes owing to their inherent difficulty in capturing long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar-derived observations. To address these fundamental limitations, we propose MambaRain, a novel multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture that synergistically integrates Mamba's linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with self-attention mechanisms for explicit spatial correlation capture. The core innovation lies in a hybrid design paradigm wherein Mamba blocks leverage selective state space mechanisms to model global temporal dynamics across extended sequences with computational efficiency, while self-attention modules explicitly characterize spatial correlations within precipitation fields - a capability inherently absent in Mamba's sequential processing paradigm. This complementary synergy enables comprehensive spatiotemporal representation learning, effectively extending the viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours with substantial accuracy improvements. Furthermore, we introduce a spectral loss formulation to mitigate blurring artifacts characteristic of chaotic precipitation systems, thereby preserving fine-scale motion details critical for nowcasting accuracy. Experimental validation demonstrates that MambaRain substantially outperforms existing deterministic methodologies in 0-3 hour nowcasting tasks, with particularly pronounced performance gains in the challenging 2-3 hour prediction range.

</details>


### [64] [ViMU: Benchmarking Video Metaphorical Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14607)
*Qi Li,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViMU benchmarks subtext understanding in videos; evaluates whether models infer implicit meanings with multimodal grounding; includes hint-free open-ended and multiple-choice questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video understanding emphasizes literal perception; need systematic evaluation of metaphor, irony, and social meanings in videos across cultures.

Method: Construct ViMU dataset with video clips and subtext questions; questions require inference of implicit meaning; questions are hint-free; supports open-ended and multiple-choice formats; evaluation with multimodal evidence grounding.

Result: ViMU differentiates subtext understanding capabilities; baseline models lag on subtext tasks; demonstrates need for improved multimodal reasoning; provides a benchmark for future research.

Conclusion: ViMU provides a standardized testbed for subtext in video understanding; fosters models capable of handling metaphor, irony, and social meaning; hint-free design ensures robust assessment; enables cross-cultural evaluation.

Abstract: Any new medium, once it emerges, is used for more than the transmission of overt content alone. The information it carries typically operates on two levels: one is the content directly presented, while the other is the subtext beneath it-the implicit ideas and intentions the creator seeks to convey through the medium. Likewise, since video technologies became widely adopted, video has served not only as a powerful tool for recording and communicating visual information, but also as a vehicle for emotions, attitudes, and social meanings that are often difficult to articulate explicitly. Thus, the true meaning of many videos does not reside solely in what is shown on screen; it is often embedded in context, style of expression, and the viewer's social experience. Some forms of such video subtext are humorous, while others carry irony, mockery, or criticism. These implicit meanings can also be interpreted very differently across cultural backgrounds and social groups. However, most existing video understanding models still focus primarily on literal visual comprehension, such as recognizing objects, actions, or temporal relations, and lack a systematic ability to understand the metaphorical, ironic, and social meanings embedded in videos. To bridge this gap, we introduce ViMU, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the subtext understanding capabilities of frontier models in videos. ViMU assesses whether video understanding models can go beyond literal perception to infer implicit meaning while grounding their interpretations in multimodal evidence and answering both open-ended and multiple-choice questions. Importantly, all questions are designed to be hint-free, ensuring that no key evidence is disclosed to models before answering.

</details>


### [65] [Deep Image Segmentation via Discriminant Feature Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14609)
*Adam Dawid Sztamborski,Raül Pérez-Gonzalo,Antonio Agudo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a differentiable Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA) loss that embeds Fisher-like discriminant principles into segmentation training to improve boundary sharpness and confidence without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard segmentation losses (Cross-Entropy, Dice) underutilize discriminative feature structure, often yielding blurred boundaries. A simple, architecture-agnostic discriminant-based objective could enhance feature separability and boundary precision.

Method: A differentiable DDA loss that explicitly maximizes between-class variance while minimizing within-class variance in the learned feature space, integrated into training and applicable across architectures without affecting inference time.

Result: On the DIS5K benchmark, DDA yields consistent improvements in segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across multiple backbone architectures.

Conclusion: Incorporating discriminant analysis into the loss fosters more robust, confident segmentations in a lightweight, architecture-agnostic manner.

Abstract: Accurate image segmentation remains challenging, particularly in generating sharp, confident boundaries. While modern architectures have advanced the field, many of them still rely on standard loss functions like Cross-Entropy and Dice, which often neglect the discriminative structure of learned features, leading to inaccurate boundaries. This work introduces Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA), a differentiable, architecture-agnostic loss function that embeds classical discriminant principles for network training. DDA explicitly maximizes between-class variance while minimizing within-class one, promoting compact and separable feature distributions without increasing inference cost. Evaluations on the DIS5K benchmark demonstrate that DDA consistently improves segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across various architectures. Our results show that integrating discriminant analysis offers a simple, effective path for building more robust segmentation models.

</details>


### [66] [CalibAnyView: Beyond Single-View Camera Calibration in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14615)
*Boying Li,Cheng Zhang,Weirong Chen,Daniel Cremers,Ian Reid,Hamid Rezatofighi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CalibAnyView introduces a unified multi-view calibration framework that leverages cross-view geometric consistency for arbitrary N views, combining a multi-view transformer with a geometric optimization to estimate intrinsics and gravity, backed by a large-scale multi-view dataset; it achieves state-of-the-art results and robustness in the wild.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical calibration relies on controlled setups; single-view learning lacks cross-view consistency; practical in-the-wild calibration requires utilizing multiple views and diverse distortions while ensuring geometric coherence.

Method: Propose a unified N-view formulation; construct a large-scale multi-view video dataset with diverse camera models, dynamic scenes, realistic motion trajectories, and heterogeneous distortions; develop a multi-view transformer that predicts dense perspective fields; integrate these into a geometric optimization to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction.

Result: Experimental results show CalibAnyView outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with robustness in single-view settings and further gains from multi-view inference, providing a reliable foundation for downstream tasks like 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in the wild.

Conclusion: CalibAnyView offers a scalable, robust in-the-wild multi-view calibration solution by explicitly enforcing cross-view geometric consistency, enabling accurate intrinsic calibration and gravity estimation across diverse scenes and camera models.

Abstract: Camera calibration is a fundamental prerequisite for reliable geometric perception, yet classical approaches rely on controlled acquisition setups that are impractical for in-the-wild imagery. Recent learning-based methods have shown promising results for single-view calibration, but inherently neglect geometric consistency across multiple views. We introduce CalibAnyView, a unified formulation that supports an arbitrary number of input views ($N \geq 1$) by explicitly modeling cross-view geometric consistency. To facilitate this, we construct a large-scale multi-view video dataset covering diverse real-world scenarios, including multiple camera models, dynamic scenes, realistic motion trajectories, and heterogeneous lens distortions. Building on this dataset, we develop a multi-view transformer that predicts dense perspective fields, which are further integrated into a geometric optimization framework to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CalibAnyView consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieves strong robustness under single-view settings, and further improves with multi-view inference, providing a reliable foundation for downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in the wild.

</details>


### [67] [Do We Really Need External Tools to Mitigate Hallucinations? SIRA: Shared-Prefix Internal Reconstruction of Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14621)
*Tian Qin,Junzhe Chen,Yuqing Shi,Tianshu Zhang,Qiang Ju,Lijie Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIRA: a training-free internal contrastive decoding method for LVLMs that constructs an internal counterfactual reference to curb language-prior-driven hallucinations without external perturbations, with lower overhead than two-pass methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations arise when language priors dominate weak or ambiguous visual cues; external contrastive decoding requires perturbed inputs and extra forward passes; need a training-free, efficient, open-weight LVLM compatible approach.

Method: Within the multimodal transformer, compute a shared prefix where image and text tokens interact to form an aligned multimodal state; then create a counterfactual branch in later layers with image-token attention masked, preserving context but removing fine-grained visual access; during decoding, suppress tokens that don't rely on late visual access and favor those whose advantage depends on full visual pathway.

Result: Empirical evaluation on POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5 shows reduced hallucinations, maintained descriptive coverage, and lower computational overhead relative to two-pass contrastive decoding; zero-shot training required; applicable to open-weight LVLMs with white-box access.

Conclusion: SIRA offers a training-free, internal contrastive decoding mechanism that mitigates LVLM hallucinations while preserving coverage and efficiency, broadening practical deployment for open-weight models.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) often hallucinate when language priors dominate weak or ambiguous visual evidence. Existing contrastive decoding methods mitigate this problem by comparing predictions from the original image with those from externally perturbed visual inputs, but such references can introduce off-manifold artifacts and require costly extra forward passes. We propose SIRA, a training-free internal contrastive decoding framework that constructs a counterfactual reference inside the same LVLM by exploiting the staged information flow of multimodal transformers. Instead of removing visual information from the input, SIRA first lets image and text tokens interact through a shared prefix, forming an aligned multimodal state that preserves prompt interpretation, decoding history, positional structure, and early visual grounding. It then forks a counterfactual branch in later transformer layers, where attention to image-token positions is masked. This branch retains the shared multimodal context but lacks continued access to fine-grained visual evidence, yielding a language-prior-dominated internal reference for token-level contrast. During decoding, SIRA suppresses tokens that remain strong without late visual access and favors predictions whose advantage depends on the full visual pathway. Experiments on POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5 show that SIRA consistently reduces hallucinations while preserving descriptive coverage and incurring lower overhead than two-pass contrastive decoding. SIRA requires no training, external verifier, or perturbed input, and applies to open-weight LVLMs with white-box inference access.

</details>


### [68] [UniTriGen: Unified Triplet Generation of Aligned Visible-Infrared-Label for Few-Shot RGB-T Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14626)
*Ping Zhou,Haoyu Wang,Mengmeng Zheng,Lei Zhang,Wei Wei,Chen Ding,Fei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniTriGen introduces a unified triplet generation framework to produce spatially aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets under text prompts, via a shared latent diffusion model with modality-specific adapters and a few-shot sampling strategy to handle limited paired data, improving RGB-T semantic segmentation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-T semantic segmentation suffers from scarce strictly aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets and cascade-based generation often breaks spatial, semantic, and cross-modal consistency; a unified approach aims to enforce global cross-modal alignment and leverage text guidance.

Method:  VIS, IR, and Label are jointly encoded into a shared latent space and modeled with a diffusion process to enforce global cross-modal consistency. Modality-specific residual adapters handle imaging differences and output formats. A scene-balanced and class-aware few-shot sampling strategy mitigates biases due to limited data and distribution imbalances.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that UniTriGen can generate high-quality, spatially aligned triplets from limited real paired data, yielding consistent performance improvements across multiple RGB-T semantic segmentation models.

Conclusion: A unified cross-modal triplet generation framework with diffusion in a shared latent space, supplemented by modality adapters and balanced few-shot sampling, effectively addresses data scarcity and alignment issues, enhancing RGB-T segmentation performance.

Abstract: RGB-T semantic segmentation requires strictly aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets; however, such aligned triplet data are often scarce in real-world scenarios. Existing generative augmentation methods usually adopt cascaded generation paradigms, decomposing joint triplet generation into local conditional processes. As a result, consistency among VIS, IR, and Label in spatial structure, semantic content, and cross-modal details cannot be reliably maintained. To address this issue, we propose UniTriGen, a unified triplet generation framework that directly generates spatially aligned, semantically consistent, and modality complementary VIS-IR-Label triplets under the guidance of text prompts. UniTriGen first introduces a unified triplet generation mechanism, where VIS, IR, and Label are jointly encoded into a shared latent space and modeled with a diffusion process to enforce global cross-modal consistency. Lightweight modality-specific residual adapters are further integrated into this mechanism to accommodate modality-specific imaging characteristics and output formats. To mitigate generation bias caused by imbalanced scene and class distributions in limited paired triplets, UniTriGen also employs a scene-balanced and class-aware few-shot sampling strategy, which induces a more balanced sampling distribution and enhances the scene and class diversity of generated triplets. Experiments show that UniTriGen generates high-quality aligned triplets from limited real paired data, thereby achieving consistent performance improvements across various RGB-T semantic segmentation models.

</details>


### [69] [MultiEmo-Bench: Multi-label Visual Emotion Analysis for Multi-modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14635)
*Tianwei Chen,Takuya Furusawa,Yuki Hirakawa,Ryotaro Shimizu,Mo Fan,Takashi Wada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a multi-label visual emotion benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs, crowdsourcing 20 annotators per image to derive emotion distributions across eight emotions, enabling dominant emotion and distribution prediction tasks; evaluates several models and finds progress with room for improvement, and limited benefit from LLM-as-judge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-label annotation schemes inadequately capture the multi-emotional responses images can evoke, leading to biased or incomplete evaluation of multimodal models; a multi-label benchmark with distributional emotion annotations is needed to properly assess model capabilities.

Method: Crowdsourcing: 20 annotators per image select all emotions they feel from each image; aggregate votes to form a distribution over eight emotions; dataset: 10,344 images with 236,998 votes; evaluate multiple MLLMs (Qwen3-VL, GPT-family, Gemini, Claude) on dominant emotion prediction and emotion distribution prediction; explore LLM-as-a-judge as an evaluation mechanism.

Result: Dataset comprises 10,344 images and 236,998 votes across eight emotions; current MLLMs show progress on emotion prediction tasks but considerable improvement is still needed; LLM-as-judge did not consistently enhance performance, highlighting limitations in using LLMs to judge subjective emotion analysis.

Conclusion: A multi-label, distribution-aware benchmark better reflects the complexity of visual emotion and provides a more reliable evaluation framework for MLLMs; while progress is evident, substantial room for improvement remains, and relying on LLMs as judges for this subjective task may be limited.

Abstract: This paper introduces a multi-label visual emotion analysis benchmark dataset for comprehensively evaluating the ability of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to predict the emotions evoked by images. Recent user studies report an unintuitive finding: humans may prefer the predictions of MLLMs over the labels in existing datasets. We argue that this phenomenon stems from the suboptimal annotation scheme used in existing datasets, where each annotator is shown a single candidate emotion for each image and judges whether it is evoked or not. This approach is clearly limited because a single image can evoke multiple emotions with varying intensities. As a result, evaluations based on these datasets may underestimate the capabilities of MLLMs, yet an appropriate benchmark for evaluating such models remains lacking. To address this issue, we introduce a new multi-label benchmark dataset for visual emotion analysis toward MLLMs evaluation. We hire $20$ annotators per image and ask them to select all emotions they feel from an image. Then, we aggregate the votes across all annotators, providing a more reliable and representative dataset labeled with a distribution of emotions. The resulting dataset contains $10,344$ images with $236,998$ valid votes across eight emotions. Based on this benchmark dataset, we evaluate several recent models, including Qwen3-VL, OpenAI's GPT, Gemini, and Claude. We assess model performance on both dominant emotion prediction and emotion distribution prediction. Our results demonstrate the progress achieved by recent MLLMs while also indicating that substantial room for improvement remains. Furthermore, our experiments with LLM-as-a-judge show that the method does not consistently improve MLLMs' performance, indicating its limitations for the subjective task of visual emotion analysis.

</details>


### [70] [How to Evaluate and Refine your CAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14641)
*Luca Domeniconi,Alessandra Stramiglio,Michele Lombardi,Samuele Salti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARCC improves CAM evaluation with a synthetic ground-truth attribution dataset; RefineCAM yields high-resolution attribution maps by multi-layer aggregation, outperforming existing CAM methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of ground-truth explanations for CAM evaluation and the low spatial resolution of typical CAMs hinder reliable interpretability and metric validation.

Method: (1) Construct a synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions for CAM evaluation. (2) Analyze existing CAM evaluation metrics. (3) Propose ARCC, a composite metric for faithful explanations. (4) Develop RefineCAM by aggregating CAMs across multiple CNN layers to produce high-resolution attribution maps.

Result: ARCC more reliably identifies faithful explanations; RefineCAM consistently outperforms existing CAM methods under the proposed evaluation framework.

Conclusion: The work provides a robust evaluation framework for CAMs and a practical method for high-resolution local explanations, improving interpretability of CNN decisions.

Abstract: Class attribution maps (CAMs) provide local explanations for the decisions of convolutional neural networks. While widely used in practice, the evaluation of CAMs remains challenging due to the lack of ground-truth explanations, making it difficult to evaluate the soundness of existing metrics. Independently, most commonly used CAM methods produce low-resolution attribution maps, which limits their usefulness for detailed interpretability.
  To address the evaluation challenge, we introduce a synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions that enables a rigorous comparison of CAM evaluation metrics. Using this dataset, we analyze existing metrics and propose ARCC, a new composite metric that more reliably identifies faithful explanations. To address the low resolution issue, we introduce RefineCAM, a method that produces high-resolution attribution maps by aggregating CAMs across multiple network layers. Our results show that RefineCAM consistently outperforms existing methods according to the proposed evaluation.

</details>


### [71] [Vision-Based Water Level and Flow Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14645)
*ZhiXin Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An integrated framework combining SOTA vision models with statistical methods and physical priors to improve water level and river flow estimation, with code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and calibration challenges in vision-based water monitoring; aim to enhance accuracy, interpretability, and robustness.

Method: Hybrid pipeline that uses SOTA vision models for feature extraction, followed by statistical modeling and robust filtering (e.g., Kalman/particle filters) incorporating physical priors (gravity, continuity) to estimate water level and flow; includes calibration refinements and data fusion aspects.

Result: Conceptual framework with expected improvements in accuracy and robustness; code will be released at the provided GitHub link; empirical validation not stated in abstract.

Conclusion: Promising approach that integrates computer vision with statistical and physical priors; requires empirical validation across diverse sites and conditions.

Abstract: With the rapid evolution of computer vision, vision-based methodologies for water level and river surface velocity estimation have reached significant maturity. Compared to traditional sensing, these techniques offer superior interpretability, automated data archiving, and enhanced system robustness. However, challenges such as environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and complex site calibration persist. This work proposes an integrated framework that synergizes state-of-the-art (SOTA) vision models with statistical modeling. By leveraging physical priors and robust filtering strategies, we improve the accuracy of water level detection and flow estimation. Code will be available at https://github.com/sunzx97/Vision_Based_Water_Level_and_Flow_Estimation.git

</details>


### [72] [TERRA-CD: Multi-Temporal Framework for Multi-class and Semantic Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14651)
*Omkar Oak,Rukmini Nazre,Rujuta Budke,Suraj Sawant*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TERRA-CD is a large, multi-annotation benchmark of 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs across 232 cities (2019 vs 2024) for urban vegetation change detection, featuring 4-class land cover masks, 3-class vegetation change masks, and 13-class semantic change masks; evaluated with diverse DL models; data and code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the shortage of comprehensive, multi-scheme change-detection datasets for urban vegetation monitoring, enabling robust evaluation of multi-class and semantic change detection tasks.

Method: Assembled 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024 across 232 cities in the USA and Europe. Generated three annotation schemes: (i) 4-class land cover masks, (ii) 3-class vegetation change masks, and (iii) 13-class semantic change masks capturing all land-cover transitions. Evaluated using multiple deep learning approaches: Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies; focused on vegetation multi-class change detection and semantic change detection.

Result: The abstract reports feasibility and applicability of the dataset and methods, with multiple architectures tested, but does not provide quantitative results. It establishes a benchmark and baseline suite, and the dataset/methods are publicly available.

Conclusion: TERRA-CD offers a comprehensive, multi-scheme benchmark for urban vegetation change detection, enabling rigorous evaluation of MCD and semantic change detection methods and supporting reproducible research; available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Abstract: Urban vegetation monitoring plays a vital role in understanding environmental changes, yet comprehensive datasets for this purpose remain limited. To address this gap, we present the Temporal Remote-sensing Repository for Analyzing Change Detection (TERRA-CD), a benchmark dataset comprising 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024, covering 232 cities across the USA and Europe. The dataset features three distinct annotation schemes: 4-class land cover mapping masks, 3-class vegetation change masks, and 13-class semantic change masks capturing all possible land cover transitions. Using various deep learning approaches including Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies, we evaluated the dataset's effectiveness for both vegetation Multi-class Change Detection as well as Semantic Change Detection. The proposed dataset and methods are available at https://github.com/omkarsoak/TERRA-CD.

</details>


### [73] [Beyond Instance-Level Self-Supervision in 3D Multi-Modal Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14654)
*Tan Pan,Shuhao Mei,Yixuan Sun,Kaiyu Guo,Chen Jiang,Zhaorui Tan,Mengzhu Li,Limei Han,Xiang Zou,Yuan Cheng,Mahsa Baktashmotlagh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cross-instance topological consistency as supervision for self-supervised learning in multi-modal medical imaging, using intra-instance cross-modal triplet objective with pixel-level correspondences and inter-instance pseudo-correspondences to align neighborhoods; yields modest segmentation gains and larger gains in classification, with improved robustness to missing modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals despite size, shape, or pathology variations. Many SSL methods in medical imaging ignore this global topology, potentially limiting representation quality. Leveraging cross-instance topology could provide a richer supervisory signal that respects anatomy.

Method: Two alignment regimes: (i) intra-instance: with pixel-level correspondences across modalities, apply a cross-modal triplet loss to preserve local neighborhood topology; (ii) inter-instance: without pixel-level correspondences, derive pseudo-correspondences to constrain partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities.

Result: Validated on 7 downstream multi-modal tasks; average improvements: segmentation ~1.1%, classification ~5.94%; also demonstrates significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

Conclusion: The proposed framework shows that cross-instance topological consistency is a viable supervisory signal for multi-modal medical imaging SSL, improving performance and robustness by explicitly encoding anatomical neighborhood relations across and within modalities.

Abstract: Self-supervised pre-training methods in medical imaging typically treat each individual as an isolated instance, learning representations through augmentation-based objectives or masked reconstruction. They often do not adequately capitalize on a key characteristic of physiological features: anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals (instances), such as the thalamus being medial to the basal ganglia, regardless of variations in brain size, shape, or pathology. We propose leveraging this cross-instance topological consistency as a supervisory signal. The challenge arises from the inherent variability in medical imaging, which can differ significantly across instances and modalities. To tackle this, we focus on two alignment regimes. (i) Intra-instance: with pixel-level correspondences available, a cross-modal triplet objective explicitly preserves local neighborhood topology. (ii) Inter-instance: without such supervision, we derive pseudo-correspondences to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities. We validate our approach across 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% and 5.94% in segmentation and classification tasks, respectively, and demonstrating significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

</details>


### [74] [MiVE: Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14664)
*Tong Wang,Meng Zou,Chengjing Wu,Xiaochao Qu,Luoqi Liu,Xiaolin Hu,Ting Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MiVE uses multiscale vision-language features from VLMs to guide diffusion-based video editing, addressing modality gaps and preserving local details; it achieves state-of-the-art performance in human evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reference-guided video editing must apply edits faithfully while preserving motion and content. Decoupled encoders suffer from modality gaps; unified vision-language encoders lose spatial detail by relying on final representations. Hierarchical VLM layers offer complementary spatial (early) and semantic (late) information.

Method: Repurpose VLMs (Qwen3-VL) as multiscale feature extractors to obtain hierarchical features across layers. Fuse these features into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer, eliminating cross-attention and modality mismatch while preserving spatial details for editing.

Result: Empirical results show MiVE ranks highest in human preference, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

Conclusion: Leverage hierarchical, multiscale VLM features to bridge instruction understanding and spatial detail, enabling state-of-the-art reference-guided video editing with a unified diffusion framework.

Abstract: Reference-guided video editing takes a source video, a text instruction, and a reference image as inputs, requiring the model to faithfully apply the instructed edits while preserving original motion and unedited content. Existing methods fall into two paradigms, each with inherent limitations: decoupled encoders suffer from modality gaps when processing instructions and visual content independently, while unified vision-language encoders lose fine-grained spatial details by relying solely on final-layer representations. We observe that VLM layers encode complementary information hierarchically -- early layers capture localized spatial details essential for precise editing, while deeper layers encode global semantics for instruction comprehension. Building on this insight, we present MiVE (Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing), a framework that repurposes VLMs as multiscale feature extractors. MiVE extracts hierarchical features from Qwen3-VL and integrates them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer, eliminating the modality mismatch inherent in cross-attention designs. Experiments demonstrate that MiVE achieves state-of-the-art performance by ranking highest in human preference, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

</details>


### [75] [Are Candidate Models Really Needed for Active Learning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14689)
*Harshini Mridula Mohan,Maanya Manjunath,Vipul Arya,S. H. Shabbeer Basha,Nitin Cheekatla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A study proposes active learning with randomly initialized CNNs/transformers, removing the need for candidate models. It tests HC, LC, and HCLC sampling; LC generally performs best, suggesting a simpler, robust active-learning approach across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce labeling cost and computational overhead inherent to traditional AL frameworks that require iterative candidate models and retraining, by showing that randomly initialized networks can still yield competitive sampling decisions.

Method: Evaluate three confidence-based sampling strategies (high confidence, low confidence, and a hybrid HC-LCLC scheme) using CNNs and transformers trained from random initialization, and compare against conventional AL that relies on candidate models. Extensive experiments assess robustness across datasets and domains.

Result: Low-confidence (LC) sampling often yields the best performance, making AL feasible without initial candidate models. HC and HCLC are less effective in the reported experiments. The approach demonstrates robustness across datasets and broad applicability.

Conclusion: Active learning can be streamlined by removing the need for pre-existing candidate models, achieving competitive results with LC-based sampling. This enhances efficiency and flexibility, though further study on limitations, initialization variability, and domain-specific effects is warranted.

Abstract: Deep learning has profoundly impacted domains such as computer vision and natural language processing by uncovering complex patterns in vast datasets. However, the reliance on extensive labeled data poses significant challenges, including resource constraints and annotation errors, particularly in training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transformers due to a larger number of parameters. Active learning offers a promising solution to reduce labeling burdens by strategically selecting the most informative samples for annotation. However, the current active learning frameworks are time-intensive which select the samples iteratively with the help of initial candidate models. This study investigates the feasibility of using CNNs and transformers with randomly initialized weights, eliminating the need for initial candidate models while achieving results comparable to active learning frameworks that depend on such candidate models. We evaluate three confidence-based sampling strategies: high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a combination of high confidence in the early stages of training and low confidence at later stages of training (HCLC). Among these, mostly LC demonstrated the best performance in our experiments, showcasing its effectiveness as an active learning strategy without the need for candidate models. Further, extensive experiments verify the robustness of the proposed active learning methods. By challenging traditional frameworks, the proposed work introduces a streamlined approach to active learning, advancing efficiency and flexibility across diverse datasets and domains.

</details>


### [76] [Generating HDR Video from SDR Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14703)
*SaiKiran Tedla,Francesco Banterle,Trevor Canham,Karanpreet Raja,David B. Lindell,Kiriakos N. Kutulakos,Jiacheng Li,Feiran Li,Daisuke Iso*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework that upconverts SDR to HDR using a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) to generate exposure-bracketed SDR sequences from a single SDR input, and a Video Merging Model (VMM) to fuse them into an HDR sequence; demonstrated robust HDR synthesis on diverse in-the-wild videos and iconic films, with integration possible on top of existing SDR generative video models; includes a public demo page.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDR upconversion of legacy SDR content remains challenging and often result in artifacts or loss of detail. The authors leverage large-scale generative video models to synthesize plausible HDR content by predicting multiple exposure-bracketed SDR streams and learning to merge them into high-quality HDR, enabling practical SDR-to-HDR conversion for consumer footage and film archives.

Method: The approach introduces MEVM, which predicts exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR video input. A learnable VMM then merges the predicted bracketed sequences into a single HDR video while preserving detail in shadows and highlights. The framework is designed to be compatible with existing SDR generative video models, and the authors evaluate on in-the-wild casual footage and iconic films using extensive quantitative, qualitative, and user-study evaluations. A supplementary webpage demonstrates the produced HDR outputs.

Result: Extensive experiments, including quantitative and qualitative evaluations as well as a user study, demonstrate robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild casual videos and iconic films. The method can support HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models, with outputs showcased on a public webpage.

Conclusion: The MEVM+VMM pipeline provides a practical, model-based HDR synthesis solution that can be integrated with current SDR generative video frameworks. It enables robust SDR-to-HDR conversion for broad real-world content and archival material. Future work could address temporal coherence, artifact mitigation, and computational efficiency to further improve real-time applicability.

Abstract: The high dynamic range (HDR) video ecosystem is approaching maturity, but the problem of upconverting legacy standard dynamic range (SDR) videos persists without a convincing solution. We propose a framework for HDR video synthesis from casual SDR footage by leveraging large-scale generative video models. We introduce a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that can predict exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR video input. We further propose a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that merges the predicted exposure-bracketed video into a high-quality HDR sequence while preserving detail in both shadows and highlights. Extensive experiments, quantitative and qualitative evaluation, and a user study demonstrate that our approach enables robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild examples from casual consumer videos and even iconic films. Finally, our model can support HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models. Output HDR videos can be viewed on our supplementary webpage: sdr2hdrvideo.github.io

</details>


### [77] [Towards Continuous Sign Language Conversation from Isolated Signs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14705)
*Youngmin Kim,Kyobin Choo,Jiwoo Park,Minseo Kim,Chanyoung Kim,Junhyeok Kim,Seong Jae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SignaVox-W/U to bootstrap sign-to-sign conversation from isolated signs; combines retrieval-guided translation and BRAID diffusion Transformer to align duration and co-articulation; trains a direct sign-to-sign model producing 3D motion without text/glosses; shows improved motion quality and semantic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data scarcity and vocabulary limits for continuous sign language by reusing labeled isolated signs to create large-scale, lexically grounded datasets and enable direct sign-to-sign dialogue without spoken language intermediaries.

Method: Construct SignaVox-W as the largest labeled isolated-sign vocabulary and SignaVox-U as a continuous 3D sign conversation dataset derived from SignaVox-W. Use a retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator to bridge speech and gloss; introduce BRAID, a diffusion Transformer for duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting to fuse isolated clips into coherent utterances. Train SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign model that maps prior signing context to 3D motion (body, hand, face) without relying on spoken text or glosses at inference.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality and stronger response-level semantic alignment, enabling scalable signer-centered interaction with enhanced visual-spatial articulation.

Conclusion: A scalable pipeline for sign-to-sign conversation from isolated data; demonstrates that lexically grounded primitives combined with diffusion-based alignment can produce coherent continuous signing without external text or gloss input, advancing accessibility for DHH users.

Abstract: Sign language is the primary language for many Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing (DHH) signers, yet most conversational AI systems still mediate interaction through spoken or written language. This spoken-language-centered interface can limit access for signers for whom spoken or written language is not the most accessible medium, motivating direct sign-to-sign conversational modeling. However, sentence-level sign video data are expensive to collect and annotate, leaving existing sign translation and production models with limited vocabulary coverage and weak open-domain generalization. We address this bottleneck by constructing continuous sign conversations from isolated signs: large-scale labeled isolated clips are collected as lexically grounded motion primitives and recomposed into sign-language-ordered utterances derived from existing dialogue corpora. We introduce SignaVox-W, which provides, to our knowledge, the largest labeled isolated-sign vocabulary to date, and SignaVox-U, a continuous 3D sign conversation dataset built from SignaVox-W. To bridge structural mismatch between spoken and signed languages, we use a retrieval-guided spoken-to-gloss translator; to bridge independently collected isolated clips, we propose BRAID, a diffusion Transformer that performs duration alignment and co-articulatory boundary inpainting. With the resulting data, we train SignaVox, a direct sign-to-sign conversational model that generates 3D body, hand, and facial motion responses from prior signing context without spoken-language text or externally provided glosses at inference time. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations show improved isolated-to-continuous motion quality, stronger response-level semantic alignment, and scalable signer-centered interaction that better supports visual-spatial articulation.

</details>


### [78] [StyleTextGen: Style-Conditioned Multilingual Scene Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14708)
*Zeyu Chen,Fangmin Zhao,Yan Shu,Yichao Liu,Liu Yu,Yu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multilingual, style-conditioned scene text generation framework (StyleTextGen) with a dual-branch style encoder, style-consistency loss, and mask-guided inference, plus a bilingual StyleText-CE benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art cross-lingual style generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting precise text styles from complex scenes across languages is challenging; existing methods struggle with cross-language style coherence and robust style representation.

Method: Three core components: (1) dual-branch style encoder for robust multilingual style representations; (2) text style consistency loss to promote coherence; (3) mask-guided inference to align styles between generated and reference text; also a new bilingual StyleText-CE benchmark for evaluation.

Result: StyleTextGen significantly outperforms prior methods in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on multilingual style-conditioned text generation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective multilingual style modeling and alignment in real-world scenes and provides a standardized benchmark for future evaluation.

Abstract: Style-conditioned scene text generation faces unique challenges in extracting precise text styles from complex backgrounds and maintaining fine-grained style consistency across characters, especially for multilingual scripts. We propose StyleTextGen, a novel framework that learns to perceive and replicate visual text styles across different languages and writing systems. Our approach features three key contributions: First, we introduce a dual-branch style encoder dedicated to style modeling, yielding robust multilingual text style representations in complex real-world scenes. Second, we design a text style consistency loss that enhances style coherence and improves overall visual quality. Third, we develop a mask-guided inference strategy that ensures precise style alignment between generated and reference text. To facilitate systematic evaluation, we construct StyleText-CE, a bilingual scene text style benchmark covering both monolingual and cross-lingual settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StyleTextGen significantly outperforms existing methods in style consistency and cross-lingual generalization, establishing new state-of-the-art performance in multilingual style-conditioned text generation.

</details>


### [79] [Breaking Dual Bottlenecks: Evolving Unified Multimodal Models into Self-Adaptive Interleaved Visual Reasoners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14709)
