*Qingyang Liu,Bingjie Gao,Canmiao Fu,Zhipeng Huang,Chen Li,Feng Wang,Shuochen Chang,Shaobo Wang,Yali Wang,Keming Ye,Jiangtong Li,Li Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical, adaptive framework for anything-to-image generation that switches among direct generation, self-reflection, and planning modes; introduces a 50k-sample dataset; two-stage SFT+RL training with step-wise rewards; claims improved pixel-level fidelity over baselines for simple-to-complex instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the understanding-generation gap in unified multimodal models, specifically the two bottlenecks of attention entanglement during prompt planning and the visual refinement bottleneck during feedback correction. Proposes autonomous mode switching to handle varying instruction complexity and model capability.

Method: A hierarchical data pipeline that generates execution paths across three adaptive modes: direct generation for simple prompts, self-reflection for iterative quality refinement, and multi-step planning for decomposing complex scenarios. Builds a 50k+ sample dataset. Trains with two-stage SFT and RL, incorporating step-wise reasoning rewards to enforce logical consistency and an intra-group complexity penalty to avoid redundant computation.

Result: Experimental results claim superior generation fidelity on X2I compared to baselines, particularly for simple-to-complex instructions, indicating effective adaptation of generation strategy to instruction complexity.

Conclusion: The framework enables unified models to autonomously switch generation strategies to improve pixel-level manipulation, supported by a new dataset and a staged training regime; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Recent unified models integrate multimodal understanding and generation within a single framework. However, an "understanding-generation gap" persists, where models can capture user intent but often fail to translate this semantic knowledge into precise pixel-level manipulation. This gap results in two bottlenecks in anything-to-image task (X2I): the attention entanglement bottleneck, where blind planning struggles with complex prompts, and the visual refinement bottleneck, where unstructured feedback fails to correct imperfections efficiently. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that empowers unified models to autonomously switch between generation strategies based on instruction complexity and model capability. To achieve this, we construct a hierarchical data pipeline that constructs execution paths across three adaptive modes: direct generation for simple cases, self-reflection for quality refinement, and multi-step planning for decomposing complex scenarios. Building on this pipeline, we contribute a high-quality dataset with over 50,000 samples and implement a two-stage training strategy comprising SFT and RL. Specifically, we design step-wise reasoning rewards to ensure logical consistency and intra-group complexity penalty to prevent redundant computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing baselines on X2I, achieving superior generation fidelity among simple-to-complex instructions. The code is released at https://github.com/WeChatCV/Interleaved_Visual_Reasoner.

</details>


### [80] [Vision-Core Guided Contrastive Learning for Balanced Multi-modal Prognosis Prediction of Stroke](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14710)
*Liren Chen,Lidong Sun,Mingyan Huang,Junzhe Tang,Yinghui Zhu,Guanjie Wang,Yiqing Xia,Ting Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes tri-modal ischemic stroke prognosis using MRI, structured data, and LLM-generated text; introduces Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) with dual alignment losses; achieves state-of-the-art on real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current prognosis methods mainly use dual-modal fusion and lack deep bidirectional interactions among images, structured data, and unstructured text. There is a need to leverage all three modalities and address the scarcity of expert annotations.

Method: Generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs using a large language model to augment data and regularize fusion. Introduce Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM) that uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interactions with the generated text. Employ dual semantic alignment losses to mitigate modal heterogeneity and enable robust tri-modal fusion.

Result: Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset show state-of-the-art prognostic performance.

Conclusion: Tri-modal fusion with LLM-assisted text augmentation and VDAFM improves prognostic accuracy and robustness, addressing annotation scarcity and cross-modal alignment challenges.

Abstract: Deep learning and multi-modal fusion have demonstrated transformative potential in medical diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources. However, accurate prognosis for ischemic stroke remains challenging due to limitations in existing multi-modal approaches. First, current methods are predominantly confined to dual-modal fusion, lacking a framework that effectively integrates the trifecta of medical images, structured clinical data, and unstructured text. Second, they often fail to establish deep bidirectional interactions between modalities; To address these critical gaps, this paper proposes a novel tri-modal fusion model for ischemic stroke prognosis. Our approach first enriches the data representation by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) to automatically generate semi-structured diagnostic text from brain MRIs. This process not only addresses the scarcity of expert annotations but also serves as a regularized semantic enhancement, improving multimodal fusion robustness. Furthermore, we design a core component termed the Vision-Conditioned Dual Alignment Fusion Module (VDAFM), which strategically uses visual features as a conditional prior to guide fine-grained interaction with the generated text. This module achieves a dynamic and profound fusion through a dual semantic alignment loss, effectively mitigating modal heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on a real-world clinical dataset demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [81] [Towards Label-Free Single-Cell Phenotyping Using Multi-Task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14717)
*Saqib Nazir,Ardhendu Behera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified DL framework for label-free WBC classification and continuous protein-expression regression from DPC images, using a hybrid CNN-Transformer with cross-branch gating and LLM-based summaries; validated on BSCCM and Blood Cells Image benchmarks with strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable scalable, non-invasive hematological profiling by inferring molecular phenotypes directly from label-free cell imagery, avoiding fluorescent stains while providing quantitative biomarker estimates.

Method: Hybrid architecture that fuses convolutional fine-grained texture features with transformer-based global representations via a learnable cross-branch gating module; regression of protein expression (e.g., CD16) from DPC images; integration of a Large Language Model to generate concise summaries of predicted cell states.

Result: Achieves 91.3% WBC classification accuracy and 0.72 Pearson correlation for CD16 expression regression on BSCCM; demonstrates strong performance on BSCCM and Blood Cells Image benchmarks.

Conclusion: Shows promise of label-free single-cell imaging for cost-effective hematological profiling, enabling simultaneous phenotype identification and quantitative biomarker estimation without fluorescent staining; code available.

Abstract: Label-free single-cell imaging offers a scalable, non-invasive alternative to fluorescence-based cytometry, yet inferring molecular phenotypes directly from bright-field morphology remains challenging. We present a unified Deep Learning (DL) framework that jointly performs White Blood Cell (WBC) classification and continuous protein-expression regression from label-free Differential Phase Contrast (DPC) images. Our model employs a Hybrid architecture that fuses convolutional fine-grained texture features with transformer-based global representations through a learnable cross-branch gating module, enabling robust morpho-molecular inference from DPC images. To support downstream interpretability, we further incorporate a Large Language Model (LLM) that generates concise, biologically grounded summaries of the predicted cell states. Experiments on the Berkeley Single Cell Computational Microscopy (BSCCM) and Blood Cells Image benchmarks demonstrate strong performance, achieving a 91.3% WBC classification accuracy and a 0.72 Pearson correlation for CD16 expression regression on BSCCM. These results underscore the promise of label-free single-cell imaging for cost-effective hematological profiling, enabling simultaneous phenotype identification and quantitative biomarker estimation without fluorescent staining. The source code is available at https://github.com/saqibnaziir/Single-Cell-Phenotyping.

</details>


### [82] [Video-Zero: Self-Evolution Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14733)
*Ruixu Zhang,Deyi Ji,Lanyun Zhu,Xuanyi Liu,Yuxin Meng,Ruihang Chu,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Video-Zero proposes an annotation-free self-evolution framework for video understanding that grounds reasoning in temporally localized evidence via a Questioner-Solver loop, improving video-language models across 13 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grounding bottleneck in video self-evolution: naive difficulty from full videos yields weak supervision; temporally localized evidence is needed to ground QA and learning without manual annotations.

Method: Co-evolution framework Video-Zero: a Questioner identifies informative evidence segments and generates evidence-grounded questions; a Solver answers and aligns its predictions with the supporting evidence; iterative loop of evidence discovery, grounded supervision, and evidence-aligned learning; annotation-free.

Result: Consistent improvements across multiple video VLM backbones on 13 benchmarks spanning temporal grounding, long-video understanding, and video reasoning; demonstrates effectiveness and transferability of evidence-centered self-evolution.

Conclusion: Evidence-centered self-evolution effectively improves video understanding without annotations by solving the grounding bottleneck; the approach is transferable across backbones and benchmarks, suggesting broader applicability to video reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Self-evolution offers a promising path for improving reasoning models without relying on intensive human annotation. However, extending this paradigm to video understanding remains underexplored and challenging: videos are long, dynamic, and redundant, while the evidence needed for reasoning is often sparse and temporally localized. Naively generating difficult question-answer pairs from full videos can therefore produce supervision that appears challenging but is weakly grounded, relying on static cues or language priors rather than temporal evidence. In this work, we argue that the key bottleneck of video self-evolution is not difficulty alone, but grounding. We propose Video-Zero, an annotation-free Questioner--Solver co-evolution framework that centers self-evolution on temporally localized evidence. The Questioner discovers informative evidence segments and generates evidence-grounded questions, while the Solver learns to answer and align its predictions with the supporting evidence. This closes an iterative loop of evidence discovery, grounded supervision, and evidence-aligned learning. Across 13 benchmarks spanning temporal grounding, long-video understanding, and video reasoning, Video-Zero consistently improves multiple video VLM backbones, demonstrating the effectiveness and transferability of evidence-centered self-evolution.

</details>


### [83] [BioHuman: Learning Biomechanical Human Representations from Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14772)
*Yujun Huo,He Zhang,Chentao Song,Honglin Song,Zongyu Zuo,Tao Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces BioHuman10M, a large-scale dataset of synchronized video, motion, and muscle activations, and BioHuman, an end-to-end model that predicts motion and muscle activations from monocular video; together enabling data-driven, physically grounded biomechanical understanding from vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of large-scale biomechanical annotations and the gap between visual observations and internal biomechanical states, enabling estimation of muscle activations from video.

Method: Develops a simulation-based pipeline to estimate activations for BioHuman10M from motion capture; trains an end-to-end monocular-video model that jointly predicts kinematics and muscle activations.

Result: BioHuman10M provides rich multimodal data; BioHuman achieves accurate reconstruction of both motion and muscle activity and generalizes across subjects/motions; sets a new benchmark.

Conclusion: This work advances video-based biomechanical understanding, enabling physically grounded human modeling and broad applications in analysis, rehab, and injury risk assessment.

Abstract: Understanding human motion beyond surface kinematics is crucial for motion analysis, rehabilitation, and injury risk assessment. However, progress in this domain is limited by the lack of large-scale datasets with biomechanical annotations, and by existing approaches that cannot directly infer internal biomechanical states from visual observations. In this paper, we introduce a simulation-based framework for estimating muscle activations from existing motion capture datasets, resulting in BioHuman10M, a large-scale dataset with synchronized video, motion, and activations. Building on BioHuman10M, we propose BioHuman, an end-to-end model that takes monocular video as input and jointly predicts human motion and muscle activations, effectively bridging visual observations and internal biomechanical states. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BioHuman enables accurate reconstruction of both kinematic motion and muscle activity, and generalizes across diverse subjects and motions. We believe our approach establishes a new benchmark for video-based biomechanical understanding and opens up new possibilities for physically grounded human modeling.

</details>


### [84] [MonoPRIO: Adaptive Prior Conditioning for Unified Monocular 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14781)
*Leon Davies,Qinggang Meng,Mohamad Saada,Baihua Li,Simon Sølvsten*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MonoPRIO introduces adaptive prior conditioning in the size pathway for monocular 3D object detection, using class-aware size prototypes, soft mixture priors, log-space uncertainty conditioning, and CAP regularisation; achieves state-of-the-art unified multi-class KITTI results and strong car-only 3D AP with less compute than MonoCLUE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Metric size estimation in monocular 3D detection is underdetermined, especially in unified multi-class settings where object sizes vary across Car/Pedestrian/Cyclist and are affected by occlusion, truncation, and projection-induced scale-depth ambiguity.

Method: Offline construction of class-aware size prototypes; routing each decoder query to a soft mixture prior; uncertainty-aware log-space conditioning; Cluster-Aligned Prior (CAP) regularisation on matched positives during training; evaluation on KITTI with ablations showing routed injection and CAP gains; results indicate strongest unified multi-class performance and competitive car-only 3D AP with reduced compute.

Result: On KITTI test, MonoPRIO delivers the strongest fully reported unified multi-class results among methods reporting Car/Pedestrian/Cyclist metrics; in car-only setting, it achieves the strongest 3D bounding-box AP across Easy/Moderate/Hard without extra data; uses substantially less compute than MonoCLUE; ablations show significant gains from routed priors and CAP, especially in ambiguity-prone and occluded regimes.

Conclusion: Adaptive priors effectively mitigate scale-depth ambiguity when image evidence underdetermines metric size in monocular 3D detection, while atypical geometry or severe visibility loss can still lead to prior–geometry mismatch; code and models are publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Monocular 3D object detection remains challenging because metric size and depth are underdetermined by single-view evidence, particularly under occlusion, truncation, and projection-induced scale-depth ambiguity. Although recent methods improve depth and geometric reasoning, metric size remains unstable in unified multi-class settings, where class variability and partial visibility broaden plausible size modes. We propose MonoPRIO, a unified monocular 3D detector that targets this bottleneck through adaptive prior conditioning in the size pathway. MonoPRIO constructs class-aware size prototypes offline, routes each decoder query to a soft mixture prior, applies uncertainty-aware log-space conditioning, and uses Cluster-Aligned Prior (CAP) regularisation on matched positives during training. On the official KITTI test server, MonoPRIO achieves the strongest fully reported unified multi-class result among methods reporting complete Car, Pedestrian, and Cyclist metrics. In the car-only setting, it also achieves the strongest 3D bounding-box AP across Easy/Moderate/Hard categories among compared methods without extra data, while using substantially less compute than MonoCLUE. Ablations and diagnostics show complementary gains from routed injection and CAP, with the largest benefits in ambiguity-prone, partially occluded, and low-data regimes. These findings indicate that adaptive priors are most effective when image evidence underdetermines metric size, while atypical geometry or extreme visibility loss can still cause mismatch between routed priors and true instance geometry. Code, trained models, result logs, and reproducibility material are available at https://github.com/bigggs/MonoPRIO.

</details>


### [85] [Do Composed Image Retrieval Benchmarks Require Multimodal Composition?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14787)
*Matteo Attimonelli,Alessandro De Bellis,Aryo Pradipta Gema,Rohit Saxena,Monica Sekoyan,Wai-Chung Kwan,Claudio Pomo,Alessandro Suglia,Dietmar Jannach,Tommaso Di Noia,Pasquale Minervini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Many CIR queries are solvable with a single modality, undermining claims of true multimodal composition; a two-stage audit shows benchmarks conflate shortcuts with genuine multimodality, as validation reduces unimodal solvability and increases reliance on combining inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether Composed Image Retrieval benchmarks truly require multimodal composition or are exploitable by unimodal shortcuts, potentially inflating reported capabilities.

Method: Two-stage audit: (1) cross-model analysis to identify shortcut-solvable queries across 11 generalist multimodal models on four CIR benchmarks; (2) human validation on 4,741 shortcut-free queries, yielding 1,689 well-formed queries; re-evaluate models on this subset to assess reliance on modalities.

Result: A large fraction of queries (32.2%–83.6%) are solvable using a single modality across benchmarks and models, indicating pervasive unimodal shortcuts. On the shortcut-free subset, unimodal solvability diminishes and multimodal combination becomes necessary; accuracy declines but true multimodal reliance increases. Overall, benchmarks conflate shortcut-solvable, noisy, and genuinely compositional queries.

Conclusion: Current CIR benchmarks overestimate true multimodal compositional ability by counting shortcut-solvable and noisy queries as valid; future work should curate or reweight benchmarks to isolate genuinely compositional, shortcut-free queries.

Abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is a multimodal retrieval task where a query consists of a reference image and a textual modification, and the goal is to retrieve a target image satisfying both. In principle, strong performance on CIR benchmarks is assumed to require multimodal composition, i.e., combining complementary information from reference image and textual modification. In this work, we show that this assumption does not always hold. Across four widely used CIR benchmarks and eleven Generalist Multimodal Embedding models, a large fraction of queries can be solved using a single modality (from 32.2% to 83.6%), revealing pervasive unimodal shortcuts. Thus, high CIR performance can arise from unimodal signals rather than true multimodal composition. To better understand this issue, we perform a two-stage audit. First, we identify shortcut-solvable queries through cross-model analysis. Second, we conduct human validation on 4,741 shortcut-free queries, of which only 1,689 are well-formed, with common issues including ambiguous edits and mismatched targets. Re-evaluating models on this validated subset reveals qualitatively different behaviour: queries can no longer be solved with a single modality, and successful retrieval requires combining both inputs. While accuracy decreases, reliance on multimodal information increases. Overall, current CIR benchmarks conflate shortcut-solvable, noisy, and genuinely compositional queries, leading to an overestimation of model capability in multimodal composition.

</details>


### [86] [COAL: Counterfactual and Observation-Enhanced Alignment Learning for Discriminative Referring Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14795)
*Shukun Jia,Shiyu Hu,Yipei Wang,Ximeng Cheng,Yichao Cao,Xiaobo Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: COAL introduces knowledge-regularized RMOT with ESI (via VLM), CFL (via LLM reasoning), and HMSI to resolve sparsity-discriminability paradox, achieving SOTA gains on Refer-KITTI benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RMOT faces a structural conflict: high discriminability requirements versus sparse semantic supervision. This leads to overfitting to salient cues and semantic collapse, especially in homogeneous scenes.

Method: Explicit Semantic Injection (ESI) using a Vision-Language Model to densify observation space; Counterfactual Learning (CFL) using Large Language Model reasoning to enforce strict attribute verification for robust compositional recognition; integration through Hierarchical Multi-Stream Integration (HMSI) that distills external knowledge into domain-specific discriminative representations.

Result: Validated on Refer-KITTI and Refer-KITTI-V2; COAL surpasses state-of-the-art by 7.28% HOTA on Refer-KITTI-V2, demonstrating effectiveness of knowledge regularization for RMOT under sparse supervision.

Conclusion: Knowledge regularization via ESI and CFL within HMSI effectively mitigates sparsity-induced discriminability gaps in RMOT, enabling more robust and granular object-reference tracking.

Abstract: Referring Multi-Object Tracking (RMOT) faces a fundamental structural contradiction between the high-discriminability demand and the sparse semantic supervision. This mismatch is particularly acute in highly homogeneous scenarios that require fine-grained discrimination over complex compositional semantics. However, under sparse supervision, models overfit to salient yet insufficient cues, thereby encouraging shortcut learning and semantic collapse. To resolve this, we propose COAL (Counterfactual and Observation-enhanced Alignment Learning), a framework that advances RMOT beyond isolated structural optimization through knowledge regularization. First, we introduce Explicit Semantic Injection (ESI) via a VLM to densify the observation space and enhance instance discriminability. Second, leveraging LLM reasoning, we propose Counterfactual Learning (CFL) to augment supervision, enforcing strict attribute verification for robust compositional recognition. These strategies are unified within a Hierarchical Multi-Stream Integration (HMSI) architecture, which distills external knowledge into domain-specific discriminative representations. Experiments on Refer-KITTI and Refer-KITTI-V2 benchmarks validate COAL's efficacy. Notably, it surpasses the state-of-the-art by 7.28% HOTA on the highly challenging Refer-KITTI-V2. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of knowledge regularization for resolving the sparsity-discriminability paradox in RMOT.

</details>


### [87] [Can Visual Mamba Improve AI-Generated Image Detection? An In-Depth Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14799)
*Mamadou Keita,Wassim Hamidouche,Hessen Bougueffa Eutamene,Abdelmalik Taleb-Ahmed,Xianxun Zhu,Abdenour Hadid*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision Mamba models show potential for detecting AI-generated images but currently trail or are inconsistent with CNN/ViT/VLM detectors across diverse datasets and generators; further work is needed to improve generalization and efficiency for robust detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the rising prevalence of AI-generated imagery and the need to evaluate whether Vision Mamba architectures, widely used for image analysis, can effectively detect synthetic content and how they compare to established detector paradigms.

Method: Benchmark a range of Vision Mamba variants against representative CNN, ViT, and VLM-based detectors. Evaluate on diverse datasets and synthetic image sources from multiple generative models, focusing on accuracy, efficiency, and generalizability.

Result: Vision Mamba approaches offer competitive accuracy in some settings but generally exhibit weaker generalization to unseen generators and varied image types compared with CNN/ViT/VLM detectors. Efficiency profiles vary, with some Vision Mamba models offering favorable inference costs, but overall detection reliability remains contingent on dataset/model specifics.

Conclusion: Vision Mamba can contribute to AI-generated image detection as part of a broader detector ensemble, but current limitations in generalization and consistency require further research and model optimization to achieve robust, real-world performance.

Abstract: In recent years, computer vision has witnessed remarkable progress, fueled by the development of innovative architectures such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), diffusion-based architectures, Vision Transformers (ViTs), and, more recently, Vision-Language Models (VLMs). This progress has undeniably contributed to creating increasingly realistic and diverse visual content. However, such advancements in image generation also raise concerns about potential misuse in areas such as misinformation, identity theft, and threats to privacy and security. In parallel, Mamba-based architectures have emerged as versatile tools for a range of image analysis tasks, including classification, segmentation, medical imaging, object detection, and image restoration, in this rapidly evolving field. However, their potential for identifying AI-generated images remains relatively unexplored compared to established techniques. This study provides a systematic evaluation and comparative analysis of Vision Mamba models for AI-generated image detection. We benchmark multiple Vision Mamba variants against representative CNNs, ViTs, and VLM-based detectors across diverse datasets and synthetic image sources, focusing on key metrics such as accuracy, efficiency, and generalizability across diverse image types and generative models. Through this comprehensive analysis, we aim to elucidate Vision Mamba's strengths and limitations relative to established methodologies in terms of applicability, accuracy, and efficiency in detecting AI-generated images. Overall, our findings highlight both the promise and current limitations of Vision Mamba as a component in systems designed to distinguish authentic from AI-generated visual content. This research is crucial for enhancing detection in an age where distinguishing between real and AI-generated content is a major challenge.

</details>


### [88] [SuperADD: Training-free Class-agnostic Anomaly Segmentation -- CVPR 2026 VAND 4.0 Workshop Challenge Industrial Track](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14808)
*Lukas Roming,Felix Lehnerer,Jonas V. Funk,Andreas Michel,Georg Maier,Thomas Längle,Jürgen Beyerer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, class-agnostic visual anomaly detection pipeline for industrial inspection under distribution shifts; improves on SuperAD with robust adaptations (DINOv3 backbone, overlapping patches, intensity-based augmentations, improved memory-bank subsampling, iterative morphological closing) and uses a single architecture with shared hyperparameters across all classes; achieves state-of-the-art segmentation F1 on MVTec AD 2 test sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial anomaly detection must cope with distribution shifts caused by changing acquisition conditions; class-specific models and per-class tuning hinder deployment. A training-free, generalizable solution is highly desirable.

Method: Based on SuperAD with several robustness-focused adaptations: DINOv3 backbone, overlapping patch-wise processing, intensity-based augmentations, improved memory-bank subsampling for broader data coverage, and iterative morphological closing to produce cleaner, spatially consistent anomaly maps; single architecture with a shared hyperparameter configuration across all object classes.

Result: Segmentation F1 scores: 62.61% (test public), 57.42% (test private), 54.35% (private mixed) on MVTec AD 2; outperforms SuperAD and other state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Demonstrates robust generalization under distribution shifts using a simple, training-free, class-agnostic pipeline suitable for industrial deployment; achieves competitive performance with a single architecture and hyperparameter settings; code available.

Abstract: Visual anomaly detection (AD) for industrial inspection is a highly relevant task in modern production environments. The problem becomes particularly challenging when training and deployment data differ due to changes in acquisition conditions during production. In the VAND 4.0 Industrial Track, models must remain robust under distribution shifts such as varying illumination and their performance is assessed on the MVTec AD 2 dataset. To address this setting, we propose a training-free and class-agnostic anomaly detection pipeline based on the work of SuperAD. Our approach improves generalization through several modifications designed to enhance robustness under distribution shifts. These adaptations include using a DINOv3 backbone, overlapping patch-wise processing, intensity-based augmentations, improved memory-bank subsampling for better coverage of the data distribution, and iterative morphological closing for cleaner and more spatially consistent anomaly maps. Unlike methods that rely on class-specific architectures or per-class hyperparameter tuning, our method uses a single architecture and one shared hyperparameter configuration across all object classes. This makes the approach well suited for industrial deployment, where product variants and appearance changes must be handled with minimal adaptation effort. We achieve segmentation F1 scores of $62.61\%$, $57.42\%$, and $54.35\%$ on test public, private, and private mixed of MVTec AD 2 respectively, thereby outperforming SuperAD and other state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/LukasRoom/SuperADD.

</details>


### [89] [Probing into Camera Control of Video Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14815)
*Chen Hou,Christian Rupprecht*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Camera control is reformulated as latent displacement fields applied via differentiable resampling, enabling training-free, geometry-guided video generation across diffusion models and serving as a probe for model biases and multi-view capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bypass data-scale and bias limitations of learned camera-mapping modules by using geometric guidance instead of implicit mappings, enabling broader priors and model analysis.

Method: Introduce displacement fields that steer camera motion, applied as differentiable latent-space resampling during diffusion denoising; compatible with pre-trained video diffusion models and without additional training.

Result: Achieves effective camera control with minimal degradation compared to fine-tuned baselines; reveals universal biases and model-specific disparities; facilitates multi-view benchmarking for 3D/4D tasks.

Conclusion: Geometric guidance via latent displacement is a simple, training-free camera-control framework that also serves as a diagnostic probe and supports 3D/4D video generation.

Abstract: Video is a rich and scalable source of 3D/4D visual observations, and camera control is a key capability for video generation models to produce geometrically meaningful content. Existing approaches typically learn a mapping from camera motion to video using additional camera modules and paired data. However, such datasets are often limited in scale, diversity, and scene dynamics, which can bias the model toward a narrow output distribution and compromise the strong prior learned by the base model. These limitations motivate a different perspective on camera control. In this paper, we show that camera control need not be modeled as an implicit mapping problem, but can instead be treated as a form of geometric guidance that induces displacements across frames. Specifically, we reformulate camera control into a set of displacement fields and apply them via differentiable resampling of latent features during denoising. Our simple approach achieves effective camera control with minimal degradation across diverse quality metrics compared to fine-tuned baselines. Since our method is applicable to most video diffusion models without training, it can also serve as a probe to study the camera control capabilities of base models. Using this probe, we identify universal biases shared by representative video models, as well as disparities in their responses to camera control. Finally, we benchmark their performance in multi-view generation, offering insights into their potential for 3D/4D tasks.

</details>


### [90] [The Velocity Deficit: Initial Energy Injection for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14819)
*Linze Li,Zong-Wei Hong,Shen Zhang,Bo Lin,Jinglun Li,Yao Tang,Jiajun Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow Matching shows velocity deficit in high-dim; propose magnitude-aware objective (MAFM) and a training-free Scale Schedule Corrector (SSC) to inject energy; results show major FID improvements and speedups on ImageNet-1k 256x256 and MS-COCO; generalizes to Text-to-Image.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although Flow Matching guarantees constant-velocity trajectories theoretically, high-dimensional practice suffers from velocity underestimation (Velocity Deficit) causing Integration Lag; need remedies.

Method: Two complementary approaches: training-based Magnitude-Aware Flow Matching (MAFM) and training-free Scale Schedule Corrector (SSC); also leverage asymmetry that velocity contraction harms at start but helps denoise at end; SSC uses a one-line code change and zero retraining.

Result: On ImageNet-1k (256x256): FID improves from 13.68 to 7.58 (≈44.6% improvement); 5x speedup; 50-step generator with FID 7.58 beats 250-step baseline at 8.65; On MS-COCO FID improves ~22%; generalizes to Text-to-Image and high-res tasks.

Conclusion: Initial Energy Injection via MAFM and SSC yields efficient, training-efficient improvements; SSC provides immediate gains; methods broadly applicable across tasks.

Abstract: While Flow Matching theoretically guarantees constant-velocity trajectories, we identify a critical breakdown in high-dimensional practice: the Velocity Deficit. We show that the MSE objective systematically underestimates velocity magnitude, causing generated samples to fail to reach the data manifold-a phenomenon we term Integration Lag. To rectify this, we propose Initial Energy Injection, instantiated via two complementary methods: the training-based Magnitude-Aware Flow Matching (MAFM) and the training-free Scale Schedule Corrector (SSC). Both are grounded in our discovery of a crucial asymmetry: velocity contraction causes harmful kinetic stagnation at the trajectory's start, yet acts as a beneficial denoising mechanism at its end. Empirically, SSC yields significant efficiency gains with zero retraining and just one line of code. On ImageNet-1k (256x256), it improves FID by 44.6% (from 13.68 to 7.58) and achieves a 5x speedup, enabling a 50-step generator (FID 7.58) to beat a 250-step baseline (FID 8.65). Furthermore, our methods generalize to Text-to-Image tasks and high-resolution generation, improving FID on MS-COCO by ~22%.

</details>


### [91] [HDRFace: Rethinking Face Restoration with High-Dimensional Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14821)
*Zirui Wang,Xianhui Lin,Yi Dong,Bo Wei,Gangjian Zhang,Siteng Ma,Zebiao Zheng,Xing Liu,Hong Gu,Minjing Dong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HDRFace is a diffusion-conditioned face restoration framework that injects high-dimensional semantic priors to better recover identity-critical details under severe degradations, without modifying the generative backbone; it uses an intermediate restoration, a high-dimensional feature encoder, and a Structure-Detail aware Fusion Mechanism (SDFM), and demonstrates gains across SD V2.1-base and Qwen-Image backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ill-posed nature of face restoration under heavy degradations and limitations of prior methods that only condition on low-quality inputs, which struggle to preserve identity-critical details.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: 1) obtain a structurally reliable intermediate restoration using an off-the-shelf restorer; 2) extract fine-grained facial representations from both the low-quality input and intermediate result via a pretrained high-dimensional feature encoder; inject as additional conditioning for generation; introduce SDFM to balance structure and detail; validate by integrating with two backbones (SD V2.1-base and Qwen-Image).

Result: Consistent performance gains in both structural fidelity and detail synthesis, across architectures; stable and coherent improvements observed for both backbones.

Conclusion: HDRFace with SDFM provides a general, plug-and-play conditioning framework that leverages rich semantic priors to improve face restoration under severe degradations; demonstrates cross-model generalization and potential for broader adoption.

Abstract: Face restoration under complex degradations still remains an ill-posed inverse problem due to severe information loss. Although diffusion models benefit from strong generative priors, most methods still condition only on low-quality inputs, making it difficult to recover identity-critical details under heavy degradations. In this work, we propose HDRFace, a High-Dimensional Representation conditioned Face restoration framework that injects semantically rich priors into the conditional flow without modifying the generative backbone. Our pipeline first obtains a structurally reliable intermediate restoration with an off-the-shelf restorer, then uses a pretrained high-dimensional feature encoder to extract fine-grained facial representations from both the low-quality input and the intermediate result, and injects them as additional conditions for generation. We further introduce SDFM, a Structure-Detail aware adaptive Fusion Mechanism that emphasizes global constraints during structure modeling and strengthens representation guidance during detail synthesis, balancing structural consistency and detail fidelity. To validate the generalization ability of our method, we implement the proposed framework on two generative models, SD V2.1-base and Qwen-Image, and consistently observe stable and coherent performance gains across different architectures.

</details>


### [92] [Multi-proposal Collaboration and Multi-task Training for Weakly-supervised Video Moment Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14838)
*Bolin Zhang,Chao Yang,Bin Jiang,Takahiro Komamizu,Ichiro Ide*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MCMT: a weakly-supervised VMR method that uses multiple proposals and learnable Gaussian masks to form a high-quality positive mask, with forward/inverse masked query reconstruction and robust negative sampling, achieving improved results on two benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome low-quality temporal proposals, misalignment of moments within the same video, and instability due to reliance on a single auxiliary task in weakly-supervised Video Moment Retrieval (VMR).

Method: Generate multiple proposals and corresponding learnable Gaussian masks; fuse them into a high-quality positive sample mask. Treat other clips as easy negatives and the entire video as hard negative. Apply forward and inverse masked query reconstruction tasks during training to enforce stronger constraints and improve robustness.

Result: Demonstrated effectiveness on two standard VMR benchmarks, outperforming baselines/previous methods in weakly-supervised VMR.

Conclusion: MCMT improves proposal quality, disambiguates misaligned moments, and stabilizes training through multi-proposal collaboration and multi-task reconstruction, validating its effectiveness for weakly-supervised VMR.

Abstract: This study focuses on weakly-supervised Video Moment Retrieval (VMR), aiming to identify a moment semantically similar to the given query within an untrimmed video using only video-level correspondences, without relying on temporal annotations during training. Previous methods either aggregate predictions for all instances in the video, or indirectly address the task by proposing reconstructions for the query. However, these methods often produce low-quality temporal proposals, struggle with distinguishing misaligned moments in the same video, or lack stability due to a reliance on a single auxiliary task. To address these limitations, we present a novel weakly-supervised method called Multi-proposal Collaboration and Multi-task Training (MCMT). Initially, we generate multiple proposals and derive corresponding learnable Gaussian masks from them. These masks are then combined to create a high-quality positive sample mask, highlighting video clips most relevant to the query. Concurrently, we classify other clips in the same video as the easy negative sample and the entire video as the hard negative sample. During training, we introduce forward and inverse masked query reconstruction tasks to impose more substantial constraints on the network, promoting more robust and stable retrieval performance. Extensive experiments on two standard benchmarks affirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in VMR.

</details>


### [93] [Editor's Choice: Evaluating Abstract Intent in Image Editing through Atomic Entity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14842)
*Mor Ventura,Roy Hirsch,Yonatan Bitton,Regev Cohen,Roi Reichart*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Formalizes abstract image editing, introduces Entity-Rubrics and AbstractEdit benchmark; reveals current models struggle to balance intent and preservation, tending to under-/over-edit; improvements hinge on LLM encoders and iterative reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between human abstract communication and machine image editing by formalizing abstract edits and providing a robust evaluation framework.

Method: Define taxonomy for abstract edits; propose Entity-Rubrics for entity-level assessment; introduce AbstractEdit benchmark; evaluate 11 leading models across diverse scenes.

Result: Entity-Rubrics correlates strongly with human judgment; standard architectures inadequately balance intent and preservation, leading to under-editing or over-editing; improvements rely on advanced LLM text encoders and iterative thinking.

Conclusion: Entity-based evaluation can generalize to reward models and test-time critique loops, advancing multimodal interaction and interpretation of abstract communication.

Abstract: Humans naturally communicate through abstract concepts like "mood". However, current image editing benchmarks focus primarily on explicit, literal commands, leaving abstract instructions largely underexplored. In this work, we first formalize the definition and taxonomy of abstract image editing. To measure instruction-following in this challenging domain, we introduce Entity-Rubrics, a framework that breaks down abstract edits into individual, entity-level assessments and achieves strong correlation with human judgment. Alongside this framework, we contribute AbstractEdit, the first benchmark dedicated to abstract image editing across diverse real-world scenes. Evaluating 11 leading models on this dataset reveals a fundamental challenge: standard architectures struggle to balance intent and preservation, commonly defaulting to under-editing or over-editing. Our analysis demonstrates that driving meaningful improvements relies heavily on integrating advanced LLM text encoders and iterative thinking. Looking forward, our entity-based paradigm can generalize beyond assessment to serve as a reward model, enable models to correctly interpret abstract communication, or highlight specific failures in test-time critique loops. Ultimately, we hope this work serves as a stepping stone toward seamless multimodal interaction, closing the gap between rigid machine execution and the natural, open-ended way humans communicate.

</details>


### [94] [MechVerse: Evaluating Physical Motion Consistency in Video Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14843)
*Rahul Jain,Mayank Patel,Asim Unmesh,Karthik Ramani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A benchmark (MechVerse) for mechanically consistent image-to-video generation, revealing gaps in current models' ability to enforce kinematic constraints across varying coupling complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between photorealistic motion and underlying mechanical constraints in articulated assemblies; existing text- or image-conditioned video generation often yields plausible visuals but violates rigid-link, contact, and coupling requirements.

Method: Create 21,156 synthetic clips from 1,357 mechanical assemblies across 141 categories, organized into three tiers of increasing kinematic complexity: independent articulation, pairwise coupling, and densely coupled multi-part mechanisms. Each clip is paired with a structured prompt describing part identities, stationary supports, moving components, motion primitives, direction, speed/extent, and inter-part dependencies. Evaluation uses standard video metrics, instruction-following scores, and human judgments of motion correctness and kinematic coupling.

Result: Current models preserve appearance and smoothness but fail to produce mechanically admissible motion; errors increase with coupling complexity. MechVerse provides a benchmark to measure and improve mechanism-aware video generation.

Conclusion: MechVerse offers a benchmark to measure and improve mechanism-aware video generation from image and language inputs.

Abstract: Text- and image-conditioned video generation models have achieved strong visual fidelity and temporal coherence, but they often fail to generate motion governed by kinematic and geometric constraints. In these settings, object parts must remain rigid, maintain contact or coupling with neighboring components, and transfer motion consistently across connected parts. These requirements are especially explicit in articulated mechanical assemblies, where motion is constrained by rigid-link geometry, contact/coupling relations, and transmission through kinematic chains. A generated video may therefore appear plausible while violating the intended mechanism, such as rotating a part that should translate, deforming a rigid component, breaking coupling between parts, or failing to move downstream components. To evaluate this gap, We introduce MechVerse, a benchmark for mechanically consistent image-to-video generation. MechVerse contains 21,156 synthetic clips from 1,357 mechanical assemblies across 141 categories, organized into three tiers of increasing kinematic complexity: independent articulation, pairwise coupling, and densely coupled multi-part mechanisms. Each clip is paired with a structured prompt describing part identities, stationary supports, moving components, motion primitives, direction, speed/extent, and inter-part dependencies. We evaluate proprietary, open-source, and fine-tuned image-to-video models using standard video metrics, instruction-following scores, and human judgments of motion correctness and kinematic coupling. Results show that current models can preserve appearance and smoothness while failing to generate mechanically admissible motion, with errors increasing as coupling complexity grows. MechVerse provides a benchmark for measuring and improving mechanism-aware video generation from image and language inputs.

</details>


### [95] [Exploring Vision-Language Models for Online Signature Verification: A Zero-Shot Capability Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14845)
*Marta Robledo-Moreno,Ruben Vera-Rodriguez,Ruben Tolosana,Javier Ortega-Garcia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Zero-shot VLMs (GPT-5.2 and Gemini 2.5 Pro) show strong discrimination for random signature forgeries in SVC when time-series are converted to images with stroke opacity, but perform poorly on skilled forgeries due to a Rationalization Trap where chain-of-thought reasoning induces kinematic hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether general vision-language models can be applied effectively to biometric verification tasks, specifically signature verification, in a zero-shot setting, and identify potential pitfalls.

Method: Convert raw kinematic time-series into static images encoding pressure information via stroke opacity; evaluate zero-shot VLMs (GPT-5.2 and Gemini 2.5 Pro) on the Signature Verification Challenge (SVC) benchmark; introduce a scoring protocol that uses latent token probabilities to compute biometric similarity scores; compare performance across random forgery and skilled forgery scenarios.

Result: In random forgery scenarios, the zero-shot VLM achieves exceptional discrimination; GPT-5.2 attains an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.32% on mobile tasks, outperforming supervised state-of-the-art systems. In skilled forgery scenarios, performance degrades significantly, and a Rationalization Trap emerges: attempting chain-of-thought reasoning leads to kinematic hallucinations that misclassify forged signatures as genuine.

Conclusion: Zero-shot VLMs show promise for biometric verification in easier, random-forgery contexts but exhibit critical limitations in more challenging skilled-forgery cases due to CoT-induced hallucinations. This highlights the need for robust scoring strategies and prompts to mitigate rationalization-based errors in biometric tasks.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in general visual reasoning, yet their applicability to rigorous biometric tasks remains unexplored. This work presents an exploratory study evaluating the zero-shot performance of state-of-the-art VLMs (GPT-5.2 and Gemini 2.5 Pro) on the Signature Verification Challenge (SVC) benchmark. To enable visual processing, raw kinematic time-series are converted into static images, encoding pressure information into stroke opacity whenever available in the source data. Furthermore, we introduce a scoring protocol that extracts latent token probabilities to compute robust biometric scores. Experimental results reveal a significant performance dichotomy dependent on signal quality and forgery type. In random forgery scenarios, the zero-shot VLM achieves exceptional discrimination, with GPT-5.2 reaching an Equal Error Rate of 0.32% in mobile tasks, outperforming supervised state-of-the-art systems. Conversely, in skilled forgery scenarios, where the task is more challenging because both signatures are almost identical, the results are significantly worse, and a critical "Rationalization Trap" emerges: chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning degrades performance as the model produces kinematic hallucinations to justify forgery artifacts as natural variability.

</details>


### [96] [SR-Prominence: A Crowdsourced Protocol and Dataset Suite for Perceptually-Weighted Super-Resolution Artifact Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14847)
*Ivan Molodetskikh,Kirill Malyshev,Mark Mirgaleev,Nikita Zagainov,Evgeney Bogatyrev,Dmitriy Vatolin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces artifact prominence as a perceptual metric for SR artifacts; builds SR-Prominence with 3,935 masks across datasets; reveals many artifacts are not noticed; shows full-reference metrics correlate with local prominence; provides benchmarking protocol and dataset release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SR artifact evaluation is binary and often ignores perceptual impact; need a dataset and protocol to quantify how noticeable artifacts are to viewers; address generalization gaps in no-reference metrics.

Method: Crowdsourced annotation protocol; curate SR-Prominence dataset from DeSRA, Open Images, Urban100, Urban100-HR-no-ground-truth; re-annotate DeSRA; annotate prominence (fraction of viewers noticing artifact); evaluate SR artifact detectors, IQA metrics, SR methods; provide objective scoring protocol.

Result: 48.2% of in-lab binary artifacts in DeSRA are not noticed by a majority; classical full-reference metrics like SSIM and DISTS align with local prominence; no-reference IQA and artifact detectors generalize poorly; dataset and protocol enable benchmarking without further crowdsourcing; SR-Prominence released.

Conclusion: Prominence-based evaluation shifts SR artifact assessment toward perceptual impact; dataset supports robust benchmarking and cross-method comparison; enables better understanding of which artifacts matter to viewers.

Abstract: Modern image super-resolution methods generate detailed, visually appealing results, but they often introduce visual artifacts: unnatural patterns and texture distortions that degrade perceived quality. These defects vary widely in perceptual impact--some are barely noticeable, while others are highly disturbing--yet existing detection methods treat them equally. We propose artifact prominence as an evaluative target, defined as the fraction of viewers who judge a highlighted region to contain a noticeable artifact. We design a crowdsourced annotation protocol and construct SR-Prominence, a dataset suite containing 3,935 artifact masks from DeSRA, Open Images, Urban100, and a realistic no-ground-truth Urban100-HR setting, annotated with prominence. Re-annotating DeSRA reveals that 48.2% of its in-lab binary artifacts are not noticed by a majority of viewers. Across the suite, we audit SR artifact detectors, image-quality metrics, and SR methods. We find that classical full-reference metrics, especially SSIM and DISTS, provide surprisingly strong localized prominence signals, whereas no-reference IQA methods and specialized artifact detectors often fail to generalize across datasets and reference settings. SR-Prominence is released with an objective scoring protocol that allows new metrics to be benchmarked on our suite without further crowdsourcing. Together, the data and protocols enable SR artifact evaluation to move from binary defect presence toward perceptual impact. SR-Prominence is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/imolodetskikh/sr-artifact-prominence.

</details>


### [97] [FactorizedHMR: A Hybrid Framework for Video Human Mesh Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14854)
*Patrick Kwon,Chen Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FactorizedHMR uses a two-stage, factorized approach to human mesh recovery: a deterministic torso-root regression followed by a probabilistic flow-based completion of distal joints, aided by a composite target representation, geometry-aware supervision, and classifier-free guidance, trained with a synthetic data pipeline; it achieves competitive results with notable gains under occlusion and in world-space metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HMR is inherently ambiguous under occlusion and weak depth cues; torso/root pose is relatively well-constrained while distal articulations are uncertain. A regime-aware, two-stage framework can stabilize the torso and (probabilistically) complete the rest to improve reliability.

Method: Two-stage architecture: (1) deterministic regression to recover a stable torso-root anchor; (2) probabilistic flow-matching to complete distal articulation. Uses a composite target representation, geometry-aware supervision, and feature-aware classifier-free guidance to preserve the torso-root while improving single-reference recovery. A synthetic data pipeline provides paired image-camera-motion supervision across diverse viewpoints.

Result: The approach is competitive with strong baselines across camera-space and world-space benchmarks, with the clearest gains in occlusion-heavy recovery and in drift-sensitive world-space metrics.

Conclusion: FactorizedHMR demonstrates that separating a stable, deterministic torso-root estimation from a probabilistic distal completion yields improved robustness to occlusion and better world-space consistency, aided by synthetic supervision and geometry-aware training.

Abstract: Human Mesh Recovery (HMR) is fundamentally ambiguous: under occlusion or weak depth cues, multiple 3D bodies can explain the same image evidence. This ambiguity is not uniform across the body, as torso pose and root structure are often relatively well constrained, whereas distal articulations such as the arms and legs are more uncertain. Building on this observation, we propose FactorizedHMR, a two-stage framework that treats these two regimes differently. A deterministic regression module first recovers a stable torso-root anchor, and a probabilistic flow-matching module then completes the remaining non-torso articulation. To make this completion reliable, we combine a composite target representation with geometry-aware supervision and feature-aware classifier-free guidance, preserving the torso-root anchor while improving single-reference recovery of ambiguity-prone articulation. We also introduce a synthetic data pipeline that provides the paired image-camera-motion supervision under diverse viewpoints. Across camera-space and world-space benchmarks, FactorizedHMR remains competitive with strong baselines, with the clearest gains in occlusion-heavy recovery and drift-sensitive world-space metrics.

</details>


### [98] [LPH-VTON: Resolving the Structure-Texture Dilemma of Virtual Try-On via Latent Process Handover](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14874)
*Yixin Liu,Baihong Qian,Jinglin Jiang,Jeffery Wu,Yan Chen,Wei Wang,Yida Wang,Lanqing Yang,Guangtao Xue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LPH-VTON introduces a single, continuous denoising diffusion framework that decouples structure and texture generation for virtual try-on. It uses a structure-biased phase to enforce geometric alignment and a texture-biased phase for high-fidelity details, achieving a favorable Pareto trade-off on VITON-HD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based VTON methods struggle to simultaneously preserve geometric integrity and texture fidelity due to conflicting inductive biases. A unified approach that leverages complementary priors within a single generative process could overcome this tension.

Method: Within a single diffusion process, the model first builds a geometry-consistent latent scaffold using a structure-biased component, then transitions to a texture-biased component to render rich details, effectively performing temporal architectural decoupling between structure and texture.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate a superior Pareto-optimal balance between perceptual faithfulness and structural alignment. On VITON-HD, LPH-VTON achieves strong perceptual fidelity with competitive geometry, setting new benchmarks for VTON tasks.

Conclusion: Temporal architectural decoupling in a unified diffusion framework can achieve better trade-offs between structure and texture in VTON, suggesting a general strategy for leveraging complementary priors in a single generative pipeline.

Abstract: Virtual Try-On (VTON) aims to synthesize photorealistic images of garments precisely aligned with a person's body and pose. Current diffusion-based methods, however, face a fundamental trade-off between structural integrity and textural fidelity. In this paper, we formalize this challenge as a consequence of complementary inductive biases inherent in prevailing architectures: models heavily reliant on spatial constraints naturally favor geometric alignment but often suppress textures, whereas models dominated by unconstrained generative priors excel at vibrant detail rendering but are prone to structural drift. Based on this diagnosis, we propose LPH-VTON, a new synergistic framework that resolves this tension within a single, continuous denoising process. LPH-VTON strategically decomposes the generation, leveraging a structure-biased model to establish a geometrically consistent latent scaffold in the early stages, before handing over control to a texture-biased model for high-fidelity detail rendering. Extensive experiments validate our approach. Our model achieves a superior Pareto-optimal balance, establishing new benchmarks in perceptual faithfulness while maintaining highly competitive structural alignment across the standard dataset VITON-HD, proving the efficacy of temporal architectural decoupling.

</details>


### [99] [Unlocking Complex Visual Generation via Closed-Loop Verified Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14876)
*Hanbo Cheng,Limin Lin,Ruo Zhang,Yicheng Pan,Jun Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A Closed-Loop Visual Reasoning (CLVR) framework for T2I that couples structured visual-language planning with pixel-level diffusion, featuring automated step-wise verification, Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning, and a fast inference technique (Δ-Space Weight Merge) to beat single-step baselines and approach proprietary performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address key bottlenecks in current text-to-image models: reliance on single-step generation, ungrounded multi-step reasoning with hallucinations, long-context optimization instabilities, and high inference latency.

Method: Proposes CLVR framework with (1) an automated data engine that generates step-level visual verification for reliable reasoning trajectories, (2) Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning (PPRL) that distills interleaved multimodal histories into explicit reward signals for causal attribution, and (3) Δ-Space Weight Merge (DSWM) that fuses alignment weights with distillation priors to reduce per-step inference to 4 NFEs without re-distillation.

Result: Extensive experiments show CLVR outperforms open-source baselines and approaches proprietary model performance, enabling scalable test-time generation for complex visual tasks and reducing latency without sacrificing quality.

Conclusion: CLVR offers a unified, verification-driven approach to iterative T2I generation that addresses hallucinations and efficiency, enabling scalable, high-quality image synthesis with better control over multi-step reasoning.

Abstract: Despite rapid advancements, current text-to-image (T2I) models predominantly rely on a single-step generation paradigm, which struggles with complex semantics and faces diminishing returns from parameter scaling.
  While recent multi-step reasoning approaches show promise, they are hindered by ungrounded planning hallucinations lacking verification, monolithic post-hoc reflection, long-context optimization instabilities, and prohibitive inference latency. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose the Closed-Loop Visual Reasoning (CLVR) framework, a comprehensive system that deeply couples visual-language logical planning with pixel-level diffusion generation. CLVR introduces an automated data engine with step-level visual verification to synthesize reliable reasoning trajectories, and proposes Proxy Prompt Reinforcement Learning (PPRL) to resolve long-context optimization instabilities by distilling interleaved multimodal histories into explicit reward signals for accurate causal attribution. Furthermore, to mitigate the severe latency bottleneck caused by iterative denoising, we propose $Δ$-Space Weight Merge (DSWM), a theoretically grounded method that fuses alignment weights with off-the-shelf distillation priors, reducing the per-step inference cost to just 4 NFEs without requiring expensive re-distillation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CLVR outperforms existing open-source baselines across multiple benchmarks and approaches the performance of proprietary commercial models, unlocking general test-time scaling capabilities for complex visual generation.

</details>


### [100] [HeatKV: Head-tuned KV-cache Compression for Visual Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14877)
*Jonathan Cederlund,Axel Berg,Durmus Alp Emre Acar,Chuteng Zhou,Pontus Giselsson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeatKV introduces head-specific KV-cache pruning for Visual Autoregressive models, achieving 2× memory compression with comparable image quality on Infinity-2B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual Autoregressive models suffer from large KV-cache memory usage during generation. Existing cache-pruning methods are coarse-grained; there is a need to exploit per-head variation in importance to prior scales for memory efficiency.

Method: Offline calibration ranks attention heads by their attention scores over prior scales using a small dataset. Based on this ranking, a static pruning schedule is constructed to fit a given memory budget, and KV-cache allocation is pruned accordingly. Applied to the Infinity-2B VAR model.

Result: Achieves 2× higher compression ratio in KV-cache memory allocation than existing methods while maintaining similar or better image fidelity, prompt alignment, and human perception scores; sets a new state-of-the-art in VAR KV-cache compression.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, head-specific cache allocation is effective for memory-efficient VAR generation without sacrificing quality; demonstrates the potential of adaptive, per-head cache management.

Abstract: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models have recently demonstrated impressive image generation quality while maintaining low latency. However, they suffer from severe KV-cache memory constraints, often requiring gigabytes of memory per generated image. We introduce HeatKV, a novel compression method that adapts cache allocation in each head based on its attention to previously generated scales. Using a small offline calibration set, the attention heads are ranked according to their attention scores over prior scales. Based on this ranking, we construct a static pruning schedule tailored to a given memory budget. Applied to the Infinity-2B model, HeatKV achieves $2 \times$ higher compression ratio in memory allocation for KV cache compared to existing methods, while maintaining similar or better image fidelity, prompt alignment and human perception score. Our method achieves a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) for VAR model KV-cache compression, showcasing the effectiveness of fine-grained, head-specific cache allocation.

</details>


### [101] [Denoising-GS: Gaussian Splatting with Spatial-aware Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14880)
*Qingyuan Zhou,Xinyi Liu,Weidong Yang,Ning Wang,Shuquan Ye,Ben Fei,Ying He,Wanli Ouyang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Denoising-GS introduces a spatially aware denoising framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting to reduce noisy primitives from SfM initialization, improving NVS fidelity while maintaining compact representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting optimization often yields noisy primitives due to sparse/incomplete initialization from SfM point clouds and typically updates only positions, neglecting spatial structure. A method that preserves spatial optimization flow and denoises with respect to spatial cues can improve fidelity and efficiency.

Method: Propose Denoising-GS: (1) an optimizer that preserves the spatial optimization flow of primitives; (2) Spatial Gradient-based Denoising leveraging spatial supports for gradient-consistent updates; (3) Uncertainty-based Denoising to prune noisy/redundant primitives; (4) Spatial Coherence Refinement to selectively split primitives in sparse regions to preserve structure.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmark datasets shows consistent improvements in NVS fidelity and representation compactness, achieving state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks; source code/models will be released publicly.

Conclusion: Spatially aware denoising of 3D Gaussian primitives yields higher fidelity and compact representations by jointly modeling position and spatial structure during optimization, with practical implications for more robust NVS using 3DGS.

Abstract: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity Novel View Synthesis (NVS), yet the optimization process inevitably introduces noisy Gaussian primitives due to the sparse and incomplete initialization from Structure-from-Motion (SfM) point clouds. Most existing methods focus solely on adjusting the positions of primitives during optimization, while neglecting the underlying spatial structure. To this end, we introduce a new perspective by formulating the optimization of 3DGS as a primitive denoising process and propose Denoising-GS, a spatial-aware denoising framework for Gaussian primitives by taking both the positions and spatial structure into consideration. Specifically, we design an optimizer that preserves the spatial optimization flow of primitives, facilitating coherent and directed denoising rather than random perturbations. Building upon this, the Spatial Gradient-based Denoising strategy jointly considers the spatial supports of primitives to ensure gradient-consistent updates. Furthermore, the Uncertainty-based Denoising module estimates primitive-wise uncertainty to prune redundant or noisy primitives, while the Spatial Coherence Refinement strategy selectively splits primitives in sparse regions to maintain structural completeness. Experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that Denoising-GS consistently enhances NVS fidelity while maintaining representation compactness, achieving state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks. Source code and models will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [102] [Masked Next-Scale Prediction for Self-supervised Scene Text Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14885)
*Zhuohao Chen,Zeng Li,Yifei Zhang,Chang Liu,Yu Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MNSP is a self-supervised framework for scene text recognition that models cross-scale hierarchical features via Next-Scale Prediction and masked image reconstruction, with an alignment module, achieving state-of-the-art results and robustness to scale/layout variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scene text recognition relies on hierarchical visual structures from coarse layouts to fine strokes; large labeled data is scarce; single-scale MIM misses cross-scale structure, necessitating a model that explicitly captures hierarchical evolution.

Method: Masked Next-Scale Prediction (MNSP) framework combining Next-Scale Prediction (NSP) to predict higher-resolution features from lower-resolution contexts, masked image reconstruction to constrain local details, and a Multi-scale Linguistic Alignment module to maintain semantic consistency across resolutions.

Result: State-of-the-art performance reported: 86.2% average accuracy on Union14M and 96.7% across six standard datasets; method shows robustness to extreme scale and layout variations; code released at provided URL.

Conclusion: Cross-scale hierarchical modeling with NSP plus masked reconstruction, reinforced by linguistic alignment, yields strong recognition accuracy and robustness in scene text recognition; MNSP advances self-supervised text representations.

Abstract: Scene Text Recognition requires modeling visual structures that evolve from coarse layouts to fine-grained character strokes. Training such models relies on large amounts of annotated data. Recent self-supervised approaches, such as Masked Image Modeling (MIM), alleviate this dependency by leveraging large-scale unlabeled data. Yet most existing MIM methods operate at a single spatial scale and fail to capture the hierarchical nature of scene text. In this work, we introduce Masked Next-Scale Prediction (MNSP), a unified self-supervised framework designed to explicitly model cross-scale structural evolution. The framework incorporates Next-Scale Prediction (NSP), which learns hierarchical representations by predicting higher-resolution features from lower-resolution contexts. Naive scale prediction, however, tends to produce spatially diffuse attention, directing the model toward background regions rather than textual structures. MNSP resolves this limitation by jointly learning cross-scale prediction and masked image reconstruction. NSP captures global layout priors across resolutions, while masked reconstruction imposes strong local constraints that guide attention toward informative text regions. A Multi-scale Linguistic Alignment module further maintains semantic consistency across different resolutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MNSP achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 86.2\% average accuracy on the challenging Union14M benchmark and 96.7\% across six standard datasets. Additional analyses show that our method improves robustness under extreme scale and layout variations. Code is available at https://github.com/CzhczhcHczh/MNSP

</details>


### [103] [Hierarchical Image Tokenization for Multi-Scale Image Super Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14891)
*Isma Hadji,Enrique Sanchez,Adrian Bulat,Brais Martinez,Georgios Tzimiropoulos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Hierarchical Image Tokenization (HIT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to enable flexible, multi-scale image super-resolution (ISR) with a compact Visual Auto-Regressive (VAR) model, achieving state-of-the-art results without external data and enabling multi-scale outputs in a single forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome two main drawbacks of prior VAR-based ISR: fixed predefined scale outputs and the need for large backbones or large annotated datasets.

Method: Propose HIT to progressively represent images at multiple scales with overlapping tokens; add a DPO regularization term that uses only LR–HR pairs to bias the model toward producing HR outputs. Train a VAR-based ISR with these components to obtain a small (≈300M params) model that delivers multi-scale outputs in one pass without external data.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art ISR performance without external training data and provides multi-scale outputs in a single forward pass, using a compact model (≈300M params) versus VARSR’s ~1B params.

Conclusion: HIT provides a strong inductive bias that enhances VAR training for ISR, enabling flexible, data-efficient, multi-scale inference with a compact model and single-pass generation.

Abstract: We introduce a multi-scale Image Super Resolution (ISR) method building on recent advances in Visual Auto-Regressive (VAR) modeling. VAR models break image tokenization into additive, gradually increasing scales, using Residual Quantization (RQ), an approach that aligns perfectly with our target ISR task. Previous works taking advantage of this synergy suffer from two main shortcomings. First, due to the limitations in RQ, they only generate images at a predefined fixed scale, failing to map intermediate outputs to the corresponding image scales. They also rely on large backbones or a large corpus of annotated data to achieve better performance. To address both shortcomings, we introduce two novel components to the VAR training for ISR, aiming at increasing its flexibility and reducing its complexity. In particular, we introduce a) a \textbf{Hierarchical Image Tokenization (HIT)} approach that progressively represents images at different scales while enforcing token overlap across scales, and b) a \textbf{Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) regularization term} that, relying solely on the (LR,HR) pair, encourages the transformer to produce the latter over the former. Our proposed HIT acts as a strong inductive bias for the VAR training, resulting in a small model (300M params vs 1B params of VARSR), that achieves state-of-the-art results without external training data, and that delivers multi-scale outputs with a single forward pass.

</details>


### [104] [Your CLIP has 164 dimensions of noise: Exploring the embeddings covariance eigenspectrum of contrastively pretrained vision-language transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14893)
*Jakub Grzywaczewski,Dawid Płudowski,Przemysław Biecek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Spectral decomposition reveals a shared, non-semantic noise subspace in Vision-Language Model (VLM) latent spaces. Pruning this common noise largely preserves or improves downstream performance, suggesting architecture-driven noise, not semantics, shapes much of the latent geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and mitigate non-semantic, multi-modal noise in VLM latent spaces and determine whether shared noise is architecture-induced and task-agnostic.

Method: Compute covariance matrices of VLM latent representations, perform spectral decomposition to separate a multi-modal semantic signal subspace from a shared noise subspace, identify invariances across data subsets, prune the shared noise dimensions, and evaluate downstream task performance.

Result: The noise geometry exhibits strong subgroup invariance across different data subsets; pruning the shared noise dimensions is largely harmless and can preserve or even improve downstream performance; isolating semantic signals clarifies the contribution of true semantics versus artifactual noise.

Conclusion: A substantial portion of VLM latent geometry is governed by shared, architecture-level noise rather than task-relevant semantics; removing this noise yields clearer semantic signals and can enhance downstream performance, offering mechanistic insight into VLM representations.

Abstract: Contrastively pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) serve as powerful feature extractors. Yet, their shared latent spaces are prone to structural anomalies and act as repositories for non-semantic, multi-modal noise.
