To address this phenomenon, we employ spectral decomposition of covariance matrices to decompose the VLM latent space into a multi-modal semantic signal component and a shared noise subspace. We observe that this noise geometry exhibits strong subgroup invariance across distinct data subsets. Crucially, pruning these shared noise dimensions is mainly harmless, preserving or actively improving downstream task performance. By isolating true semantic signals from artifactual noise, this work provides new mechanistic insights into the representational structure of modern VLMs, suggesting that a substantial fraction of their latent geometry is governed by shared, architecture-level noise rather than task-relevant semantics alone.

</details>


### [105] [CineMesh4D: Personalized 4D Whole Heart Reconstruction from Sparse Cine MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13994)
*Xiaoyue Liu,Xiaohan Yuan,Mark Y Chan,Ching-Hui Sia,Lei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end 4D (3D+t) whole-heart mesh reconstruction from cine MRI using differentiable rendering and temporal fusion to overcome sparse 2D sampling and shape–motion coupling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate 3D+t heart meshes are hampered by sparse sampling of 3D anatomy by 2D cine slices and the strong coupling between cardiac shape and motion; existing methods typically reconstruct only partial chambers or a single phase.

Method: CineMesh4D proposes an end-to-end 4D pipeline that maps multi-view 2D cine contours to a 3D+t heart mesh. It introduces a differentiable rendering loss to supervise meshes from sparse contours and a dual-context temporal block to fuse global and local temporal information for robust motion capture.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative experiments show CineMesh4D outperforms existing methods in both reconstruction quality and motion consistency, enabling practical personalized real-time cardiac assessment.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a practical pathway to accurate, patient-specific 4D whole-heart meshes from cine MRI and suggests readiness for real-time clinical deployment, with code to be released after acceptance.

Abstract: Accurate 3D+t whole-heart mesh reconstruction from cine MRI is a clinically crucial yet technically challenging task. The difficulty of this task arises from two coupled factors: inherently sparse sampling of 3D cardiac anatomy by 2D image slices and the tight coupling between cardiac shape and motion. Current cardiac image-to-mesh approaches typically reconstruct only a subset of cardiac chambers or a single phase of the cardiac cycle. In this work, we propose CineMesh4D, a novel end-to-end 4D (3D+t) pipeline that directly reconstructs patient-specific whole-heart mesh from multi-view 2D cine MRI via cross-domain mapping. Specifically, we introduce a differentiable rendering loss that enables supervision of 3D+t whole-heart mesh from multi-view sparse contours of cine MRI. Furthermore, we develop a dual-context temporal block that fuses global and local cardiac temporal information to capture high-dimensional sequential patterns. In quantitative and qualitative evaluations, CineMesh4D outperforms existing approaches in terms of reconstruction quality and motion consistency, providing a practical pathway for personalized real-time cardiac assessment. The code will be publicly released once the manuscript is accepted.

</details>


### [106] [SEDiT: Mask-Free Video Subtitle Erasure via One-step Diffusion Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14894)
*Zheng Hui,Yunlong Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A one-stage, mask-free video subtitle erasure method (SEDiT) using a diffusion transformer achieves reliable one-step editing with streaming inference, preserving temporal consistency for long, high-resolution videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome dependence on precomputed masks and two-stage video editing pipelines; reduce segmentation quality impact; enable efficient, high-resolution, long-video subtitle removal with temporal coherence.

Method: Propose SEDiT: a one-stage diffusion transformer with mask-free inference for localized subtitle removal. Utilizes rectified flow and Lipschitz-continuous conditional OT maps to justify one-step sampling; hybrid training with occasional first-frame latent conditioning for temporal consistency; feed-forward of original video to avoid seams; chunk-wise streaming inference forlong videos.

Result: The approach demonstrates reliable one-step denoising and provides theoretical justification for one-step sampling. It achieves temporal continuity and can handle native 1440p video with streaming inference, maintaining quality across long sequences.

Conclusion: A feasible, efficient, one-step solution for localized video editing (subtitle erasure) that preserves quality and temporal coherence in long/high-resolution videos, with solid theoretical underpinnings and practical streaming capability.

Abstract: Recent breakthroughs in video diffusion models have significantly accelerated the development of video editing techniques. However, existing methods often rely on inpainting video frames based on masked input, which requires extracting the target video mask in advance, and the precision of the segmentation directly affects the quality of the completion. In this paper, we present SEDiT, a novel one-stage video Subtitle Erasure approach via One-step Diffusion Transformer. We introduce a mask-free inference approach that enables direct erasure of the targeted subtitle. The proposed one-stage framework mitigates the sub-optimality inherent in the two-stage processing of prior models. Since subtitle removal is a localized editing task in which most pixels remain unchanged, the underlying distribution shift is minimal, making it well-suited to one-step generation under rectified flow. We empirically validate the reliability of one-step denoising and further provide a formal theoretical justification. Under the localized-editing structure of subtitle removal, the conditional optimal transport (OT) map and its induced rectified flow velocity field are Lipschitz continuous with respect to the latent variable, which underpins the theoretical feasibility of one-step sampling. To address the challenge of long-term temporal consistency, we adopt a hybrid training strategy by occasionally conditioning the model with a clean first-frame latent. This facilitates temporal continuity, allowing each segment during inference to leverage the output of its predecessor. To avoid visible seams caused by cropping and reinserting processed targets, particularly in scenarios involving substantial motion, we feed the original video directly into SEDiT. Thanks to one-step and chunk-wise streaming inference, our method can efficiently handle native 1440p video with infinite length.

</details>


### [107] [MemLens: Benchmarking Multimodal Long-Term Memory in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14906)
*Xiyu Ren,Zhaowei Wang,Yiming Du,Zhongwei Xie,Chi Liu,Xinlin Yang,Haoyue Feng,Wenjun Pan,Tianshi Zheng,Baixuan Xu,Zhengnan Li,Yangqiu Song,Ginny Wong,Simon See*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MEMLENS is a new benchmark that contrasts long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents on multimodal, multi-session questions, revealing complementary strengths and weaknesses and motivating hybrid architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no unified benchmark comparing long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents on questions requiring genuine multimodal evidence across multi-session conversations.

Method: The benchmark includes 789 questions over five memory abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge update, answer refusal) at four context lengths (32K–256K tokens) with cross-modal token counting. An image-ablation study tests the necessity of visual evidence. Evaluations cover 27 LVLMs and 7 memory-augmented agents.

Result: Visual evidence is essential; removing images drops two frontier LVLMs below 2% accuracy on 80.4% of questions containing image evidence. Long-context LVLMs excel in short contexts but degrade with longer conversations; memory-augmented agents are stable in length but suffer from visual fidelity after storage-time compression. Multi-session reasoning remains below 30% for most systems; neither approach alone solves MEMLENS, indicating a need for hybrid architectures combining long-context attention with structured multimodal retrieval.

Conclusion: MEMLENS provides a rigorous, scalable platform to study memory in multimodal conversations and supports a hybrid-direction research agenda that fuses long-context processing with explicit multimodal retrieval; the work is complemented by code availability.

Abstract: Memory is essential for large vision-language models (LVLMs) to handle long, multimodal interactions, with two method directions providing this capability: long-context LVLMs and memory-augmented agents. However, no existing benchmark conducts a systematic comparison of the two on questions that genuinely require multimodal evidence. To close this gap, we introduce MEMLENS, a comprehensive benchmark for memory in multimodal multi-session conversations, comprising 789 questions across five memory abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge update, and answer refusal) at four standard context lengths (32K-256K tokens) under a cross-modal token-counting scheme. An image-ablation study confirms that solving MEMLENS requires visual evidence: removing evidence images drops two frontier LVLMs below 2% accuracy on the 80.4% of questions whose evidence includes images. Evaluating 27 LVLMs and 7 memory-augmented agents, we find that long-context LVLMs achieve high short-context accuracy through direct visual grounding but degrade as conversations grow, whereas memory agents are length-stable but lose visual fidelity under storage-time compression. Multi-session reasoning caps most systems below 30%, and neither approach alone solves the task. These results motivate hybrid architectures that combine long-context attention with structured multimodal retrieval. Our code is available at https://github.com/xrenaf/MEMLENS.

</details>


### [108] [SteerSeg: Attention Steering for Reasoning Video Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14908)
*Ali Cheraghian,Hamidreza Dastmalchi,Abdelwahed Khamis,Morteza Saberi,Aijun An,Lars Petersson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SteerSeg steers LVLM-based video grounding via input-level soft prompts and reasoning-guided prompts to produce focused spatial attention, guiding a segmentation model with point prompts while ranking tracklets by correlation; relies on frozen LVLM and segmentation models and training only soft prompts, achieving strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between LVLM attention maps (optimized for text generation) and the need for precise spatial localization in video grounding, by correcting attention at the source.

Method: Freeze the LVLM and segmentation model; learn lightweight input-level soft prompts and apply reasoning-guided Chain-of-Thought prompting to derive discriminative attributes. Generate spatially concentrated attention maps, convert them into point prompts per keyframe, guide a segmentation model, and rank candidate tracklets using correlation-based scoring.

Result: SteerSeg yields significantly improved spatial grounding without fine-tuning large models, trained only on Ref-YouTube-VOS, with strong generalization across diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: By correcting attention alignment through lightweight prompts and CoT-derived attributes, SteerSeg preserves pretrained reasoning while substantially improving grounding, offering a data-efficient approach to LVLM-based video segmentation.

Abstract: Video reasoning segmentation requires localizing objects across video frames from natural language expressions, often involving spatial reasoning and implicit references. Recent approaches leverage frozen large vision-language models (LVLMs) by extracting attention maps and using them as spatial priors for segmentation, enabling training-free grounding. However, these attention maps are optimized for text generation rather than spatial localization, often resulting in diffuse and ambiguous grounding signals. In this work, we introduce SteerSeg, a lightweight framework that identifies attention misalignment as the key bottleneck in attention-based grounding and proposes to steer attention at its source through input-level conditioning. SteerSeg combines learnable soft prompts with reasoning-guided Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. The soft prompts reshape the attention distribution to produce more spatially concentrated maps, while CoT-derived attributes resolve ambiguity among similar objects by guiding attention toward the correct instance. The resulting attention maps are converted into point prompts across keyframes to guide a segmentation model, while candidate tracklets are ranked and selected using correlation-based scoring. Our approach freezes the LVLM and segmentation model parameters and learns only a small set of soft prompts, preserving the model's pretrained reasoning capabilities while significantly improving grounding. Despite being trained only on Ref-YouTube-VOS, SteerSeg generalizes well across diverse benchmarks, significantly improving the spatial grounding capability of LVLMs. Project page: https://steerseg.github.io

</details>


### [109] [Representative Attention For Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14913)
*Yuntong Li,Hainuo Wang,Hengxing Liu,Mingjia Li,Xiaojie Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RPAttention is a representation-space linear global attention for Vision Transformers that compresses tokens into learned representatives via a Gather-Interact-Distribute workflow, enabling semantically related regions to communicate with linear complexity while preserving global context; validated across image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing linear attention methods compress tokens using predefined spatial layouts, anchoring compression to image coordinates and potentially misaligning communication with semantic content. A semantically driven, representation-space compression is needed to ensure global information exchange follows content structure rather than fixed geometry.

Method: Introduce Gather-Interact-Distribute (GID): soft gathering of spatial tokens into a compact set of learned representative tokens via competitive similarity-based routing; representatives perform global interaction in a small latent space; refined information is broadcast back to spatial tokens through query-driven cross-attention. This replaces coordinate-driven aggregation with representation-driven compression, preserving global receptive fields while achieving linear (vs. quadratic) complexity with token count.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple vision transformer backbones show RPAttention is effective across image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation, demonstrating that representation-space token compression can maintain strong global context with linear scalability.

Conclusion: RPAttention delivers a practical, content-aware linear global attention mechanism for Vision Transformers, achieving linear scalability in token interactions while preserving global receptive fields and aligning communication with semantic content across diverse vision tasks.

Abstract: Linear attention has emerged as a promising direction for scaling Vision Transformers beyond the quadratic cost of dense self-attention. A prevalent strategy is to compress spatial tokens into a compact set of intermediate proxies that mediate global information exchange. However, existing methods typically derive these proxy tokens from predefined spatial layouts, causing token compression to remain anchored to image coordinates rather than the semantic organization of visual content. To overcome this limitation, we propose Representative Attention (RPAttention), a linear global attention mechanism that performs token compression directly in representation space. Instead of constructing intermediate tokens from fixed spatial partitions, it dynamically forms a compact set of learned representative tokens to enable semantically related regions to communicate regardless of their spatial distance, by following a lightweight Gather-Interact-Distribute paradigm. Spatial tokens are first softly gathered into representative tokens through competitive similarity-based routing. The representatives then perform global interaction within a compact latent space, before broadcasting the refined information back to all spatial tokens via query-driven cross-attention. Via replacing coordinate-driven aggregation with representation-driven compression, RPAttention preserves global receptive fields while adaptively aligning token communication with the content structure of each input.RPAttention reduces the dominant token interaction complexity from quadratic to linear scaling with respect to the number of spatial tokens, while maintaining expressive global context modeling. Extensive experiments across diverse vision transformer backbones on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation demonstrate the effectiveness of our design.

</details>


### [110] [SceneParser: Hierarchical Scene Parsing for Visual Semantics Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14923)
*Pengxin Xu,Xincheng Lin,Luping Xiao,Qing Jiang,Meishan Zhang,Hao Fei,Shanghang Zhang,Xingyu Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Hierarchical Scene Parsing with a SceneParser model and SceneParser-Bench benchmark to capture scene, object, part, and affordance hierarchies, demonstrating improved structure-aware parsing over existing multi-label methods using new hierarchical metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current open-vocabulary grounding and part/affordance predictions are often isolated and lack cross-level bindings and hierarchical completeness, limiting interaction-oriented scene understanding; a scalable, unified framework and benchmark are needed.

Method: Introduce a hierarchical scene representation (scene -> object -> part -> affordance) and train a VLM-based SceneParser for unified hierarchical generation using structural-completion pseudo labels and curriculum learning. Build SceneParser-Bench with 110K training images, 5K validation, 777K objects, 1.14M parts, 1.74M affordances, and 1.74M valid chains. Define Level-1 to Level-3 conditional metrics and ParseRate to assess localization, cross-level binding, and completeness.

Result: SceneParser outperforms existing MLLMs and perception-stitching pipelines on hierarchical parsing; ablations validate components; competitive or superior on COCO and AGD20K; planning-probe shows the representation is actionable and compatible with conventional tasks.

Conclusion: Hierarchical parsing enables robust, interaction-oriented scene understanding; the proposed SceneParser and SceneParser-Bench provide scalable data, new metrics, and practical representations for planning and downstream tasks.

Abstract: General scene perception has progressed from object recognition toward open-vocabulary grounding, part localization, and affordance prediction. Yet these capabilities are often realized as isolated predictions that localize objects, parts, or interaction points without capturing the structured dependencies needed for interaction-oriented scene understanding. To address this gap, we introduce Hierarchical Scene Parsing, an interaction-oriented parsing task that represents physical scenes as explicit scene -> object -> part -> affordance hierarchies with cross-level bindings. We instantiate this task with SceneParser, a VLM-based parser trained for unified hierarchical generation with structural-completion pseudo labels and curriculum learning. To support training and evaluation, we construct SceneParser-Bench, a large-scale benchmark built with a scalable hierarchical data engine, containing 110K training images, a 5K validation split, 777K objects, 1.14M parts, 1.74M affordance annotations, and 1.74M valid object-part-affordance chain instances. We further introduce Level-1 to Level-3 conditional metrics and ParseRate to evaluate localization, cross-level binding, and hierarchical completeness. Experiments show that existing MLLMs and perception-stitching pipelines struggle with hierarchical parsing on our SceneParser-Bench, while SceneParser achieves stronger structure-aware performance. Besides, ablations, evaluations on COCO and AGD20K, and a downstream planning probe demonstrate that our SceneParser is compatible with conventional tasks and provides an actionable representation for visual understanding.

</details>


### [111] [Road Maps as Free Geometric Priors: Weather-Invariant Drone Geo-Localization with GeoFuse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14925)
*Yunsong Fang,Tingyu Wang,Zhedong Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoFuse fuses geo-aligned road maps with satellite imagery to improve drone-to-satellite geo-localization under adverse weather, using a cross-modal fusion module with token- and channel-level interactions, a dynamic gating mechanism, and class-level cross-view contrastive learning; achieves notable recall@1 gains on University-1652 and DenseUAV.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weather-induced degradation widens the cross-view domain gap in drone-view geo-localization. Road map data provide weather-invariant geometric priors (e.g., road networks, footprints) that are inexpensive and readily available.

Method: Augment datasets with geo-aligned road maps; introduce a flexible fusion module that fuses satellite and road-map features via token-level and channel-level interactions, equipped with a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism to adapt modality contributions per instance; apply class-level cross-view contrastive learning to align weather-degraded drone features with fused satellite-roadmap representations.

Result: Significant improvements in Recall@1 on two benchmarks: University-1652 (+3.46%) and DenseUAV (+23.18%).

Conclusion: GeoFuse yields more discriminative, weather-resilient representations by leveraging road-map priors and cross-modal fusion, outperforming state-of-the-art methods under diverse weather conditions.

Abstract: Drone-view geo-localization aims to match a query drone image, often captured under adverse weather conditions (e.g., rain, snow, fog), against a gallery of geo-tagged satellite images. Weather-induced degradations in the drone view, such as noise, reduced visibility, and partial occlusions, severely exacerbate the intrinsic cross-view domain gap. While prior methods predominantly rely on weather-specific architectures or data augmentations, they have largely overlooked road map data, a readily available modality that provides strong, inherently weather-invariant geometric layout cues (e.g., road networks and building footprints) at negligible additional cost. We introduce GeoFuse, a cross-modal fusion framework that integrates precisely aligned road map tiles with satellite imagery to yield more discriminative and weather-resilient representations. We first augment the existing University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks with geo-aligned road maps, supplying structural priors robust to meteorological variations. Building on this, we propose a flexible fusion module that combines satellite and road map features via token-level and channel-level interactions, with a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism that adaptively weights modality contributions per instance. Finally, we employ class-level cross-view contrastive learning to promote robust alignment between weather-degraded drone features and the fused satellite-roadmap representations. Extensive experiments under diverse weather conditions show that GeoFuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving +3.46% and +23.18% Recall@1 accuracy on the University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks, respectively.

</details>


### [112] [SCRWKV: Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision-RWKV for Topological Crack Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14926)
*Hanxu Zhang,Chen Jia,Hui Liu,Xu Cheng,Fan Shi,Shengyong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A compact segmentation model SCRWKV for pixel-level crack segmentation, featuring a Structure-Field Encoder backbone with AMCM and SCIU (GBST, DSCD), plus a cross-scale decoder, achieving SOTA-like accuracy with 1.22M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level crack segmentation requires precise topology modeling across diverse textures while remaining computationally efficient; existing methods struggle to balance accuracy and resource demands.

Method: Proposes SCRWKV with a Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone. Key components: Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) for texture representation; Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) using Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) to capture topology; Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) integrated into Dy-WKV to suppress noise; lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder for feature fusion. Claims linear complexity and high precision with a compact 1.22M params.

Result: On TUT crack dataset, achieves F1 = 0.8428 and mIoU = 0.8512, outperforming SOTA methods across benchmarks with complex textures and interference; code released.

Conclusion: SCRWKV demonstrates that a compact, structure-calibrated architecture can achieve high-precision crack segmentation with low resource usage, indicating strong potential for real-world deployment and further efficiency-accuracy trade-off exploration.

Abstract: Achieving pixel-level accurate segmentation of structural cracks across diverse scenarios remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods face significant bottlenecks in balancing crack topology modeling with computational efficiency, often failing to reconcile high segmentation quality with low resource demands. To address these limitations, we propose the Ultra-Compact Structure-Calibrated Vision RWKV (SCRWKV), a network that achieves high-precision modeling via a novel Structure-Field Encoder (SFE) backbone while maintaining linear complexity. The SFE integrates the Adaptive Multi-scale Cascaded Modulator (AMCM) to enhance texture representation and utilizes the Structure-Calibrated Insight Unit (SCIU) as its core engine. Specifically, the SCIU employs the Geometry-guided Bidirectional Structure Transformation (GBST) to capture topological correlations and integrates the Dynamic Self-Calibrating Decay (DSCD) into Dy-WKV to suppress noise propagation. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight Cross-Scale Harmonic Fusion (CSHF) decoder to achieve precise feature aggregation. Systematic evaluations on multiple benchmarks characterized by complex textures and severe interference demonstrate that SCRWKV, with only 1.22M parameters, significantly outperforms SOTA methods. Achieving an F1 score of 0.8428 and mIoU of 0.8512 on the TUT dataset, the model confirms its robust potential for efficient real-world deployment. The code is available at https://github.com/zhxhzy/SCRWKV.

</details>


### [113] [Multi-scale Coarse-to-fine Modeling for Test-time Human Motion Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14935)
*Nhat Le,Daochang Liu,Anh Nguyen,Ajmal Mian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSCoT introduces a multi-scale coarse-to-fine motion synthesis framework that uses discrete hierarchical tokens, a multi-scale token guidance strategy, and a lightweight token refiner to enable differentiable test-time refinement, achieving state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion with faster sampling on HumanML3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies and rigidity in existing test-time motion generation methods, such as diffusion-based approaches and module-specific controllers, which suffer from slow sampling, iterative denoising, and limited flexibility for control signals.

Method: Discretize motion into a multi-scale hierarchical token representation and predict full token sequences at each temporal scale in a coarse-to-fine manner. Employ a multi-scale token guidance strategy to steer the discrete token distribution toward control goals. Introduce a lightweight token refiner that adds continuous residuals to discrete embeddings, enabling differentiable test-time refinement for precise control alignment.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion performance on HumanML3D with substantial gains: 48% improvement in motion quality (FID), 61% reduction in average control error, and about 10x faster inference than diffusion-based methods.

Conclusion: MSCoT delivers fast, high-quality, controllable motion synthesis by combining multi-scale discrete token representations with a refinement mechanism, offering improved control accuracy and sampling efficiency over existing baselines.

Abstract: We present MSCoT, a multi-scale, coarse-to-fine model for test-time human motion synthesis and control. Unlike recent approaches that rely on multiple iterative denoising/token-prediction steps, or modules tailored for specific control signals, MSCoT discretizes motion into a multi-scale hierarchical representation and predicts the entire token sequence at each temporal scale in a coarse-to-fine fashion. Building on this coarse-to-fine paradigm, we propose an efficient multi-scale token guidance strategy that overcomes the challenge of discrete sampling and steers the token distribution towards the control goals, allowing for fast and flexible control. To address the limitations of a discrete codebook, a lightweight token refiner further adds continuous residuals to the discrete token embeddings and allows differentiable test-time refinement optimization to ensure precise alignment with the control objectives. MSCoT is able to produce quality motions, consistent with the control constraints, while offering substantially faster sampling than diffusion-based approaches. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art controllable text-to-motion generation performance of MSCoT over existing baselines, with better motion quality (48% FID improvement), higher control accuracy (-61% avg error), and $10 \times$ faster inference speed on HumanML3D.

</details>


### [114] [ACE-LoRA: Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling for Continual Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14948)
*Yuehao Liu,Weijia Zhang,Xuanming Shang,Zhizhou Chen,Yanhao Ge,Shanyan Guan,Chao Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ACE-LoRA introduces a dynamic, regularized fine-tuning framework for continual image editing in diffusion models, using Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to separate task interference and Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression for scalable updates, plus CIE-Bench as a standardized evaluation benchmark; it achieves improved fidelity and reduced forgetting compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address continual learning in image editing with diffusion models, enabling continual task adaptation without catastrophic forgetting and ensuring scalable updates; fill the lack of standardized evaluation through a comprehensive benchmark (CIE-Bench).

Method: ACE-LoRA employs Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to detect and orthogonalize task interference during fine-tuning; introduces Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression to maintain historical information with fixed rank, enabling scalable continual updates; provides a dynamic regularization framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Result: Experiments show consistent superiority over baselines in instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting across diverse editing tasks, validating the approach for continual learning in image editing.

Conclusion: The work establishes a foundation for continual learning in image editing and delivers a standardized benchmark (CIE-Bench), facilitating robust evaluation and progress in this domain.

Abstract: State-of-the-art diffusion models often rely on parameter-efficient fine-tuning to perform specialized image editing tasks. However, real-world applications require continual adaptation to new tasks while preserving previously learned knowledge. Despite the practical necessity, continual learning for image editing remains largely underexplored. We propose ACE-LoRA, a dynamic regularization framework for continual image editing that effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting. ACE-LoRA leverages Adaptive Orthogonal Decoupling to identify and orthogonalize task interference, and introduces a Rank-Invariant Historical Information Compression strategy to address scalability issues in continual updates. To facilitate continual learning in image editing and provide a standardized evaluation protocol, we introduce CIE-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark in this domain. CIE-Bench encompasses diverse and practically relevant image editing scenarios with a balanced level of difficulty to effectively expose limitations of existing models while remaining compatible with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing baselines in terms of instruction fidelity, visual realism, and robustness to forgetting, establishing a strong foundation for continual learning in image editing.

</details>


### [115] [A CUBS-Compatible Ultrasound Morphology and Uncertainty-Aware Baseline for Carotid Intima-Media Segmentation and Preliminary Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14949)
*Aueaphum Aueawatthanaphisut*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AtheroFlow-XNet offers a morphology-driven carotid intima-media segmentation with uncertainty-aware risk prediction; achieves Dice 0.793 for LI-MA masks and AUC 0.691 for risk, but remains a morphology baseline lacking Doppler/CFD biomarkers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional ultrasound metrics (morphology and velocity) may miss patient-specific vascular risk. The study aims to provide an automated, uncertainty-aware segmentation framework and a preliminary risk predictor compatible with the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study (CUBS).

Method: Supervised segmentation of LI-MA boundaries from Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study images to dense intima-media masks. An auxiliary clinical-variable-based risk-prediction branch is added. Uncertainty estimated via Monte Carlo dropout. Evaluation uses a patient-level train/validation/test split (1,522 training, 326 validation, 328 testing images). Metrics include Dice for LI-MA mask segmentation (0.7930), segmentation loss (0.2359), and ROC-AUC for preliminary risk prediction (0.6910). Qualitative results include alignment with manual annotations and uncertainty maps highlighting ambiguous wall-boundary regions.

Result: Dice coefficient for LI-MA segmentation: 0.7930; segmentation loss: 0.2359; AUC for risk prediction: 0.6910. Uncertainty maps correspond to ambiguous wall-boundary regions; masks generally align with manual annotations.

Conclusion: Ultrasound-derived carotid morphology shows promise for automated wall analysis with uncertainty-aware interpretation. The approach provides a reproducible morphology-driven baseline within CUBS. Limitations include absence of Doppler waveforms and CFD-derived hemodynamic biomarkers; future work should integrate Doppler flow profiles, patient-specific vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based wall shear biomarkers.

Abstract: Carotid atherosclerosis is a major contributor to ischemic stroke and transient ischemic attack. Conventional ultrasound assessment is commonly based on intima-media thickness, plaque appearance, stenosis degree, and peak systolic velocity, but these morphology- and velocity-based indicators may not fully capture patient-specific vascular risk. This study presents AtheroFlow-XNet, a CUBS-compatible ultrasound morphology and uncertainty-aware learning baseline for carotid intima-media segmentation and preliminary risk prediction. Using the Carotid Ultrasound Boundary Study dataset, manual lumen-intima and media-adventitia boundary annotations were converted into dense intima-media masks for supervised segmentation. Clinical variables were incorporated into an auxiliary risk-prediction branch, and Monte Carlo dropout was used for uncertainty-aware inference. The model was evaluated using a patient-level train-validation-test split with 1,522 training images, 326 validation images, and 328 testing images. The proposed model achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.7930 for LI-MA mask segmentation, a segmentation loss of 0.2359, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.6910 for preliminary risk prediction. Qualitative results showed that predicted masks were generally aligned with manual annotations, while uncertainty maps highlighted ambiguous wall-boundary regions. These results suggest that ultrasound-derived carotid morphology can support automated wall analysis and uncertainty-aware interpretation. Since CUBS does not provide Doppler waveforms or CFD-derived hemodynamic biomarkers, this work should be interpreted as a reproducible morphology-driven baseline. Future work will incorporate Doppler-derived flow profiles, patient-specific vascular reconstruction, and CFD-based wall shear biomarkers.

</details>


### [116] [H-OmniStereo: Zero-Shot Omnidirectional Stereo Matching with Heading-Aligned Normal Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14963)
*Chenxing Jiang,Zhe Tong,Pusen Gao,Peize Liu,Yang Xu,Chuan Fang,Ping Tan,Shaojie Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes H-OmniStereo: zero-shot omnidirectional stereo matching from 2.8M synthetic top-bottom equirectangular pairs; an equirectangular monocular normal estimator in heading-aligned coords provides robust priors; achieves better generalization to out-of-domain data and real-world cameras; dataset and model to be open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and distortion of monocular priors under spherical maps; leverage synthetic data and geometry-aware priors to enable effective stereo on top-bottom equirectangular images; improve cross-view consistency and FoV mismatch handling.

Method: Construct large synthetic dataset (~2.8M pairs) of top-bottom equirectangular stereo images; develop an equirectangular monocular normal estimator in a heading-aligned coordinate system; use distortion-robust priors to establish correspondences; design for training efficiency and FoV mismatch accommodation; zero-shot generalization.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved accuracy on out-of-domain datasets and successful generalization to real-world consumer camera setups with a single model.

Conclusion: Dataset and model will be open-sourced; demonstrates practical viability of omnidirectional stereo matching via synthetic data and geometry-aware priors.

Abstract: Stereo matching on top-bottom equirectangular images provides an effective framework for full-surround perception, as vertically aligned epipolar lines enable the use of advanced perspective stereo architectures that are largely driven by large-scale datasets and monocular priors. However, the performance of such adaptations is severely limited by the scarcity of omnidirectional stereo datasets and the degradation of perspective monocular priors under spherical distortions.To address these challenges, we propose H-OmniStereo, a zero-shot omnidirectional stereo matching framework. First, we construct high-quality synthetic dataset comprising over 2.8 million top-bottom equirectangular stereo pairs to scale up training. Second, we introduce an equirectangular monocular normal estimator, specifically operating in a heading-aligned coordinate system. Beyond providing distortion-robust and cross-view-consistent geometric priors for establishing reliable correspondences in stereo matching, this design boosts training efficiency and accommodates train-test FoV mismatches.Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves higher accuracy than existing methods on out-of-domain datasets and successfully generalizes to real-world consumer camera setups using a single model. Both the model and the dataset will be open-sourced.

</details>


### [117] [MHSA: A Lightweight Framework for Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention in LVLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14966)
*Wei Ding,Yilin Li,Yudong Zhang,Ruobing Xie,Xingwu Sun,Jiansheng Chen,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MHSA is a lightweight framework that mitigates hallucinations in LVLMs by steering cross-modal attention with a small three-layer MLP, replacing the original attention during inference while keeping LVLM weights fixed, and using DHCP-derived supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations persist in LVLMs despite existing cross-modal attention cues. DHCP detects hallucinations via cross-modal attention but does not mitigate them. A lightweight, model-agnostic mitigation method is needed to improve reliability across datasets and LVLMs.

Method: Train a three-layer MLP generator to output corrected cross-modal attention. The generator is supervised by signals from the DHCP discriminator and the LVLM itself. During inference, replace the LVLM’s original cross-modal attention with the corrected attention, without changing any LVLM parameters.

Result: The approach mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across multiple datasets and LVLMs by simply swapping in the corrected cross-modal attention, demonstrating effective mitigation without LVLM fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Extends the idea of cross-modal attention from hallucination detection to mitigation, offering a lightweight, practical pathway to improve the reliability of LVLMs without retraining base models.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across diverse multimodal tasks, yet they continue to suffer from hallucinations, generating content that is inconsistent with the visual input. Prior work DHCP (Detecting Hallucinations by Cross-modal Attention Pattern) has explored hallucination detection from the perspective of cross-modal attention, but does not address hallucination mitigation. In this paper, we propose MHSA (Mitigating Hallucinations via Steered Attention), a lightweight framework that mitigates hallucinations by learning to correct cross-modal attention patterns in LVLMs. MHSA trains a simple three-layer MLP generator to produce corrected attention, guided by supervisory signals from the DHCP discriminator and the LVLM itself. During inference, MHSA mitigates both discriminative and generative hallucinations across various datasets and LVLMs by simply replacing the original cross-modal attention with the corrected one, without modifying any LVLM parameters. By extending cross-modal attention mechanisms from hallucination detection to hallucination mitigation, MHSA offers a novel perspective on hallucination research in LVLMs and helps enhance their reliability.

</details>


### [118] [MicroscopyMatching: Towards a Ready-to-use Framework for Microscopy Image Analysis in Diverse Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14980)
*Xiaofei Hui,Haoxuan Qu,Hossein Rahmani,Shuohong Wang,Jeff W. Lichtman,Jun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A ready-to-use microscopy image analysis framework, MicroscopyMatching, unifies segmentation, tracking, and counting by formulating them as a single matching problem and exploiting pre-trained latent diffusion models for robust performance across diverse settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biological microscopy analysis is highly variable due to differences in object types, sample processing, instruments, and tasks, making bespoke deep learning solutions brittle and hard to adapt, causing manual analysis bottlenecks.

Method: Recast diverse microscopy tasks as a unified matching problem and leverage the robust matching capabilities of pre-trained latent diffusion models to obtain consistent segmentation, tracking, and counting across varied settings.

Result: Demonstrates reliable performance across diverse microscopy analysis settings for segmentation, tracking, and counting, with reduced need for task-specific adaptation.

Conclusion: MicroscopyMatching provides a first ready-to-use framework that uses a unified matching paradigm and latent diffusion priors to address variability in microscopy analysis tasks, potentially accelerating biomedical research by reducing manual effort.

Abstract: Analyzing microscopy images to extract biological object properties (e.g., their morphological organization, temporal dynamics, and population density) is fundamental to various biomedical research. Yet conducting this manually is costly and time-consuming. Though deep learning-based approaches have been explored to automate this process, the substantial diversity of microscopy analysis settings in practice (including variations of biological object types, sample processing protocols, imaging equipment, and analysis tasks, etc.) often renders them ineffective. As a result, these approaches typically require extensive adaptation for different settings, which, however, can impose burdens that are often practically unsustainable for laboratories, forcing biomedical researchers to still commonly rely on manual analysis, thereby severely bottlenecking the pace of biomedical research progress. This situation has created a pressing and long-standing need for a reliable and broadly applicable microscopy image analysis tool, yet such a tool is still missing. To address this gap, we present the first ready-to-use microscopy image analysis framework, MicroscopyMatching, that can reliably perform key analysis tasks (including segmentation, tracking, and counting) across diverse microscopy analysis settings. From a fundamentally different perspective, MicroscopyMatching reformulates diverse microscopy image analysis tasks as a unified matching problem, effectively handling this problem by exploiting the robust matching capability from pre-trained latent diffusion models.

</details>


### [119] [Compositional Video Generation via Inference-Time Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14988)
*Ariel Shaulov,Eitan Shaar,Amit Edenzon,Gal Chechik,Lior Wolf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Inference-time compositional guidance for frozen text-to-video models using cross-attention signals; trains a lightweight compositional classifier on attention features and uses its gradients during early denoising to steer the latent trajectory, improving prompt faithfulness without retraining generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address failures of text-to-video models on fine-grained compositional prompts (relations among entities, attributes, actions, motion directions) without expensive fine-tuning or architectural changes, by leveraging internal grounding signals in the model.

Method: Train a lightweight compositional classifier on cross-attention features derived from the frozen model's backbone. During early denoising steps, use the classifier's gradients to steer the latent trajectory toward the target composition, effectively guiding the diffusion process at inference time. The approach transfers across semantically related composition labels, not relying on narrow category-specific features, and requires no extra user-provided layouts, boxes, or controls.

Result: Empirical evaluation on compositional text-to-video benchmarks shows improved prompt faithfulness (compositional accuracy) while preserving the underlying generator's visual quality; works without modifying architecture or fine-tuning.

Conclusion: CVG provides a practical, plug-in inference-time strategy to enhance compositional faithfulness in frozen text-to-video models by exploiting cross-attention grounding signals, enabling broader compositional control without retraining.

Abstract: Text-to-video diffusion models generate realistic videos, but often fail on prompts requiring fine-grained compositional understanding, such as relations between entities, attributes, actions, and motion directions. We hypothesize that these failures need not be addressed by retraining the generator, but can instead be mitigated by steering the denoising process using the model's own internal grounding signals. We propose \textbf{CVG}, an inference-time guidance method for improving compositional faithfulness in frozen text-to-video models. Our key observation is that cross-attention maps already encode how prompt concepts are grounded across space and time. We train a lightweight compositional classifier on these attention features and use its gradients during early denoising steps to steer the latent trajectory toward the desired composition. Built on a frozen VLM backbone, the classifier transfers across semantically related composition labels rather than relying only on narrow category-specific features. CVG improves compositional generation without modifying the model architecture, fine-tuning the generator, or requiring layouts, boxes, or other user-supplied controls. Experiments on compositional text-to-video benchmarks show improved prompt faithfulness while preserving the visual quality of the underlying generator.

</details>


### [120] [Characterizing the visual representation of objects from the child's view](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14990)
*Jane Yang,Tarun Sepuri,Alvin Wei Ming Tan,Khai Loong Aw,Michael C. Frank,Bria Long*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Children's early object category exposure is highly skewed and variable, yet category groupings align with strong superordinate structure; learning likely arises from sparse, non-canonical exemplars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize real-world inputs to early visual category learning and test if exposure is biased, variable, and whether category organization reflects superordinate structure rather than canonical depictions.

Method: Analyze first-person home videos from the BabyView dataset (N=31 participants, 868 hours, ages 5–36 months) using a supervised object detector to extract common object categories from >3 million frames.

Result: Exposure is highly skewed toward a few categories (e.g., cups, chairs); many categories are rare; exemplars are highly variable (unusual angles, clutter, partial occlusion); animals commonly appear as depictions; detected categories show stronger grouping within superordinate categories than groupings derived from canonical photographs; this pattern is also seen in deep self-supervised and multimodal embeddings and in densely sampled individual data.

Conclusion: Visual category learning robustness likely depends on exploiting strong superordinate structure and learning from non-canonical, sparse, and variable exemplars; models should reflect this hierarchical organization to account for real-world learning.

Abstract: Children acquire object category representations from their everyday experiences in the first few years of life. What do the inputs to this learning process look like? We analyzed first-person videos of young children's visual experience at home from the BabyView dataset ($N$ = 31 participants, 868 hours, ages 5--36 months), using a supervised object detection model to extract common object categories from more than 3 million frames. We found that children's object category exposure was highly skewed: a few categories (e.g., cups, chairs) dominated children's visual experiences while most categories appeared rarely, replicating previous findings from a more restricted set of contexts. Category exemplars were highly variable: children encountered objects from unusual angles, in highly cluttered scenes, and partially occluded views; many categories (especially animals) were most frequently viewed as depictions. Surprisingly, despite this variability, detected categories (e.g., giraffes, apples) showed stronger groupings within superordinate categories (e.g., animals, food) relative to groupings derived from canonical photographs of these categories. We found this same pattern when using high-dimensional embeddings from both self-supervised visual and multimodal models; this effect was also recapitulated in densely sampled data from individual children. Understanding the robustness and efficiency of visual category learning will require the development of models that can exploit strong superordinate structure and learn from non-canonical, sparse, and variable exemplars.

</details>


### [121] [Predicting Response to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Ovarian Cancer from CT Baseline Using Multi-Loss Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14991)
*Francesco Pastori,Francesca Fati,Marina Rosanu,Luigi De Vitis,Lucia Ribero,Gabriella Schivardi,Giovanni Damiano Aletti,Nicoletta Colombo,Jvan Casarin,Francesco Multinu,Elena De Momi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deep learning framework using pre-treatment CT and automatically derived 3D lesion masks to predict ovarian cancer NACT response; achieved ROC-AUC 0.73 and F1-score 0.70 on a retrospective single-center test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy to prevent ineffective therapy and surgical delays; non-invasive imaging-based predictive tool is needed.

Method: Encode axial CT slices with a partially fine-tuned pretrained encoder; derive 3D lesion masks; aggregate slice representations into a volumetric embedding via attention; train with classification loss plus supervised contrastive regularization and hard-negative mining; single-center retrospective cohort; 280 patients; test results reported.

Result: ROC-AUC 0.73 (95% CI 0.58-0.86); F1-score 0.70 (95% CI 0.56-0.82).

Conclusion: Indicates the architecture captures clinically relevant predictive patterns and could underpin imaging-based patient stratification for NACT response assessment.

Abstract: Ovarian cancer is the most lethal gynecologic malignancy: around 60% of patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, with an associated 5-year survival rate of about 30%. Early identification of non-responders to neoadjuvant chemotherapy remains a key unmet need, as it could prevent ineffective therapy and avoid delays in optimal surgical management. This work proposes a non-invasive deep learning framework to predict neoadjuvant chemotherapy response from pre-treatment contrast-enhanced CT by leveraging automatically derived 3D lesion masks. The approach encodes axial slices with a partially fine-tuned pretrained image encoder and aggregates slice-level representations into a volumetric embedding through an attention-based module. Training combines classification loss with supervised contrastive regularization and hard-negative mining to improve separation between ambiguous responders and non-responders. The method was developed on a retrospective single-center cohort from the European Institute of Oncology (Milan, IT), including 280 eligible patients (147 responder, 133 non-responder). On the test cohort, the model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.73 (95% CI: 0.58-0.86) and an F1-score of 0.70 (95% CI: 0.56-0.82). Overall, these results suggest that the proposed architecture learns clinically relevant predictive patterns and provides a robust foundation for an imaging-based stratification tool.

</details>


### [122] [3D Skew-Normal Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15010)
*Xiangru Wu,Ke Fan,Yanwei Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Skew-Normal primitives for 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling continuous skewness control to better model asymmetric surfaces, with decoupled optimization and improved NVS performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite primitive budget and symmetric Gaussian shapes limit representation, especially at object boundaries and one-sided surfaces. Prior non-Gaussian kernels were either elliptic or hard-truncated, causing discontinuities and limited shape control.

Method: Adopt Azzalini Skew-Normal as primitive, with a learnable bounded skewness parameter enabling interpolation between Gaussian and Half-Gaussian shapes. Preserve analytical tractability under affine transforms and marginalization. Propose decoupled parameterization and block-wise optimization to reduce coupling between scale, rotation, and skewness.

Result: Empirical evaluation on standard NVS benchmarks shows SNS outperforms Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels, with notable gains at sharp boundaries and thin/one-sided regions.

Conclusion: SNS provides continuous shape control within a tractable framework, enabling better representation of asymmetric geometry and seamless integration into existing 3D Gaussian Splatting pipelines; benefits include improved reconstruction quality and training stability.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a leading representation for real-time novel view synthesis and been widely adopted in various downstream applications. The core strength of 3DGS lies in its efficient kernel-based scene representation, where Gaussian primitives provide favorable mathematical and computational properties. However, under a finite primitive budget, the symmetric shape of each primitive directly affects representation compactness, especially near asymmetric structures such as object boundaries and one-sided surfaces. Recent works have explored more complex kernel distributions, yet they either remain within the elliptical family or rely on hard truncation, which limits continuous shape control and introduces distributional discontinuities. In this paper, we propose Skew-Normal Splatting (SNS), which adopts the Azzalini Skew-Normal distribution as the fundamental primitive. By introducing a learnable and bounded skewness parameter, SNS can continuously interpolate between symmetric Gaussians and Half-Gaussian-like shapes, enabling flexible modeling of both sharp boundaries and interior regions. Moremover, SNS preserves analytical tractability under affine transformations and marginalization. This property allows seamless integration into existing Gaussian Splatting rasterization pipelines.Furthermore, to address the strong coupling between scale, rotation, and skewness parameters, we introduce a decoupled parameterization and a block-wise optimization strategy to enhance training stability and accuracy. Extensive experiments on standard novel-view synthesis benchmarks show that SNS consistently improves reconstruction quality over Gaussian and recent non-Gaussian kernels, with clearer benefits on sharp boundaries and thin or one-sided structures.

</details>


### [123] [HiSem: Hierarchical Semantic Disentangling for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15024)
*Man Wang,Chenyang Liu,Wenjun Li,Feng Ni,Bing Jia,Baoqi Huang,Riting Xia,Zhenwei Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiSem disentangles semantic representations in RSICC through a hierarchical framework combining a Bidirectional Differential Attention Modulation (BDAM) and Hierarchical Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement (HASD), achieving a notable BLEU-4 gain (+7.52) on WHU-CDC and clarifying the semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RSICC models share a single strategy for changed and unchanged pairs, which have different semantic granularities, causing semantic entanglement between change existence and fine-grained change semantics. This misalignment motivates a dedicated hierarchical disentangling approach.

Method: Introduce BDAM to enhance cross-temporal interaction via discrepancy-aware attention, amplifying true change signals; develop HASD with coarse image-level routing to distinguish changed vs unchanged, and a token-level MoE block for diverse change semantics in changed samples.

Result: Extensive experiments on two benchmarks show that HiSem outperforms prior methods, notably achieving +7.52 BLEU-4 on the WHU-CDC dataset.

Conclusion: HiSem provides a structured perspective for RSICC by aligning model design with intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes; code will be released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Remote sensing image change captioning (RSICC) aims to achieve high-level semantic understanding of genuine changes occurring between bi-temporal images. Despite notable progress, existing methods are fundamentally limited by a shared modeling assumption: changed and unchanged image pairs, which have intrinsically different semantic granularities, are processed under a unified modeling strategy. This modeling inconsistency leads to semantic entanglement between coarse-grained change existence judgment and fine-grained semantic understanding.To address the above limitation, we propose a novel hierarchical semantic disentangling network (HiSem) that explicitly disentangles semantic representations of different granularities. Specifically, we first introduce the Bidirectional Differential Attention Modulation (BDAM) module that leverages discrepancy-aware attention to enhance cross-temporal interactions, thereby amplifying true change signals while suppressing irrelevant variations. Building upon this, we design a Hierarchical Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement (HASD) module that performs adaptive routing at two hierarchical levels: a coarse-grained image-level routing mechanism distinguishes changed and unchanged image pairs, while a fine-grained token-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) block models diverse and heterogeneous change semantics for changed samples. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that HiSem outperfoms previous methods, achieving a significant improvement of +7.52\% BLEU-4 on the WHU-CDC dataset. More importantly, our approach provides a structured perspective for RSICC by explicitly aligning model design with the intrinsic semantic heterogeneity of bi-temporal scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Man-Wang-star/HiSem

</details>


### [124] [EverAnimate: Minute-Scale Human Animation via Latent Flow Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15042)
*Wuyang Li,Yang Gao,Mariam Hassan,Lan Feng,Wentao Pan,Po-Chien Luan,Alexandre Alahi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EverAnimate is a post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that preserves visual quality and character identity by anchoring generation to a persistent latent context memory and by introducing restorative flow matching during sampling, with lightweight LoRA tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-form animation suffers from drift when chunk-based generation is used: low-level background degradation and high-level semantic drift in character identity and view-dependent attributes. A robust method is needed to maintain visual fidelity and identity across extended sequences.

Method: Two complementary mechanisms: (i) Persistent Latent Propagation to maintain a context memory across chunks for identity and motion in latent space, mitigating temporal forgetting; (ii) Restorative Flow Matching with an implicit restoration objective during sampling via velocity adjustment to improve intra-chunk fidelity. The method requires only lightweight LoRA tuning.

Result: Quantitative gains reported: at 10 seconds, PSNR/SSIM improvements of 8%/7% and LPIPS/FID reductions of 22%/11%; at 90 seconds, PSNR/SSIM improvements of 15%/15% and LPIPS/FID reductions of 32%/27%. These results suggest better long-horizon quality and stability than state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: EverAnimate provides an efficient post-training approach to long-horizon animated video generation that better preserves character identity and visual quality across long sequences, outperforming existing long-animation methods with modest fine-tuning

Abstract: We propose EverAnimate, an efficient post-training method for long-horizon animated video generation that preserves visual quality and character identity. Long-form animation remains challenging because highly dynamic human motion must be synthesized against relatively static environments, making chunk-based generation prone to accumulated drift: (i) low-level quality drift, such as progressive degradation of static backgrounds, and (ii) high-level semantic drift, such as inconsistent character identity and view-dependent attributes. To address this issue, EverAnimate restores drifted flow trajectories by anchoring generation to a persistent latent context memory, consisting of two complementary mechanisms. (i) Persistent Latent Propagation maintains a context memory across chunks to propagate identity and motion in latent space while mitigating temporal forgetting. (ii) Restorative Flow Matching introduces an implicit restoration objective during sampling through velocity adjustment, improving within-chunk fidelity. With only lightweight LoRA tuning, EverAnimate outperforms state-of-the-art long-animation methods in both short- and long-horizon settings: at 10 seconds, it improves PSNR/SSIM by 8%/7% and reduces LPIPS/FID by 22%/11%; at 90 seconds, the gains increase to 15%/15% and 32%/27%, respectively.

</details>


### [125] [LATERN: Test-Time Context-Aware Explainable Video Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15054)
*Mitchell Piehl,Muchao Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LATERN reframes VAD as temporal evidence aggregation using two modules (CEA and REA) to provide coherent event-level anomaly detection and explanations, improving accuracy and explanation consistency on UCF-Crime and XD-Violence for frozen VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM-based VAD pipelines perform segment-level, independent inferences due to token constraints and lack structured temporal context, yielding fragmented predictions; there is a need to leverage evolving video dynamics through temporal evidence and to provide explainable anomaly reasoning.

Method: Two-stage framework: Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) with an image-grounded memory that selectively incorporates diverse, visually and textually aligned historical frames as expanded context for robust anomaly scoring; Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA) that recursively aggregates anomaly scores over time to identify coherent anomaly intervals and produce event-level decisions and explanations grounded in visual-textual evidence.

Result: On challenging benchmarks UCF-Crime and XD-Violence, LATERN improves detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs during test time, yielding temporally coherent and semantically grounded event-level explanations.

Conclusion: Reformulating VAD as temporal evidence aggregation with memory-augmented scoring and recursive aggregation yields better performance and interpretable, semantically grounded event-level explanations even when using frozen VLMs.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for video anomaly detection (VAD) due to their strong visual reasoning ability and natural language-based explainability. In this paper, we aim to address a key limitation of such pipelines, which perform segment-level inference independently owing to token constraints and reason without structured temporal context, allowing VLMs to interpret anomalies as deviations from evolving video dynamics rather than producing fragmented predictions and explanations. To specify, we propose a context-aware framework named LATERN, which reformulates VAD as a temporal evidence aggregation process. LATERN consists of two complementary modules: Context-Aware Anomaly Scoring (CEA) and Recursive Evidence Aggregation (REA). CEA introduces a novel image-grounded memory mechanism, which selectively chooses historical content via frame diversity and visual-textual alignment as expanded context to help generate reliable anomaly scores. Building upon these scores, REA performs recursive temporal aggregation to identify coherent anomaly intervals and produce event-level decisions and explanations grounded in visual-textual evidence. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks, including UCF-Crime and XD-Violence, show that LATERN enhances detection accuracy and explanation consistency for frozen VLMs during test time, while generating temporally coherent and semantically grounded event-level explanations.

</details>


### [126] [Computational Imaging Priors for Wireless Capsule Endoscopy: Monte Carlo-Guided Hemoglobin Mapping for Rare-Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15062)
*Chengshuai Yang,Lei Xing,Gregory Entin,Roopa Vemulapalli,Lisa Casey,Raiyan Tripti Zaman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior to RGB-based capsule-endoscopy classifier yields small but consistent macro-AUC gains and notable per-class lift (Lymphangiectasia); distillation head provides 3-channel RGB backbone with interpretability heatmaps; results show seed-dependent variability for rare vascular classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address misattribution of hemoglobin contrast in RGB-trained capsule-endoscopy classifiers, which confounds hemoglobin signal with bile and illumination falloff.

Method: Compare RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 with two software-only configurations on Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames, 11 classes) across 6 seeds. (i) analytic prior P_blood fused as two auxiliary channels; (ii) a distillation head training RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Statistical tests: DeLong, McNemar, bootstrap CIs with Bonferroni correction.

Result: Macro-AUC: RGB 0.760±0.027; input-fusion 0.783±0.024 (Δ=+0.023); distillation 0.773±0.028. Largest per-class lift: Lymphangiectasia 0.238±0.057 to 0.337±0.019. Rare classes show bimodal per-seed effects (e.g., Angiectasia seed 42: 0.528→0.916). Across seeds (n=6) the mean cross-seed performance is 0.646→0.608 with high seed variance (σ_PI=0.23).

Conclusion: A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior yields a small, direction-consistent macro-AUC gain on Kvasir-Capsule; the distillation variant enables interpretability heatmaps on 3-channel RGB. Largest robust improvement is in Lymphangiectasia.

Abstract: Background. RGB-trained capsule-endoscopy classifiers underperform on small-vessel vascular findings by
  conflating hemoglobin contrast with bile and illumination falloff. Thus, here we test whether a Monte
  Carlo-inspired analytic model can compute hemoglobin from RGB signal built upon extracted classifier.
  Methods. On Kvasir-Capsule (47,238 frames, video-level 70/15/15 split, 11 evaluable classes) we evaluate two
  software-only configurations against RGB-only EfficientNet-B0 across 6 seeds: (i) a prior P_blood =
  sigma(alpha * (H_norm - 0.5)) * Phi(r) fused as 2 zero-init auxiliary channels; (ii) a distillation head
  training a 3-channel RGB backbone to predict P_blood. Significance: paired DeLong, McNemar, bootstrap CIs
  with Bonferroni correction.
  Results. Across 6 seeds (n=6,423), the analytic prior provides a small but direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement: RGB-only 0.760 +/- 0.027, input-fusion 0.783 +/- 0.024 (paired Delta = +0.023, sign-positive on
  5/6 seeds), distillation 0.773 +/- 0.028. The largest robust per-class lift is on Lymphangiectasia, where AUC
  rises from RGB 0.238 +/- 0.057 to input-fusion 0.337 +/- 0.019, sign-consistent across all 6 seeds. On rare
  focal-vascular classes (Angiectasia, Blood - fresh) the prior's per-seed effects are bimodal: seed=42 reaches
  Angiectasia AUC 0.528 -> 0.916, but the cross-seed mean is 0.646 -> 0.608 with sigma_PI = 0.23 - reported as
  a high-variance per-seed exemplar.
  Conclusion. A Monte Carlo-inspired analytic prior provides a small, direction-consistent macro-AUC
  improvement on Kvasir-Capsule across 6 seeds with the largest robust per-class lift on Lymphangiectasia; the
  distillation variant runs on plain 3-channel RGB and yields a free interpretability heatmap.

</details>


### [127] [On the Cultural Anachronism and Temporal Reasoning in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15071)
*Mukul Ranjan,Prince Jha,Khushboo Kumari,Zhiqiang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new benchmark TAB-VLM evaluates temporal reasoning in vision-language models on 1,600 Indian artifacts across six categories, revealing cultural anachronism as a fundamental limitation; the best model attains 58.7% accuracy, highlighting persistent gaps regardless of model size; dataset and code are publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs often misinterpret historical objects due to temporally inappropriate concepts and underrepresentation of non-Western cultures. There is a need to quantify and reduce this cultural bias by assessing temporal cognition in multimodal systems.

Method: Construct TAB-VLM, a dataset of 600 questions across six categories, spanning 1,600 Indian artifacts from prehistoric to modern periods. Evaluate ten state-of-the-art models (including GPT-5.2) on temporal reasoning tasks and report overall accuracy and category-level performance.

Result: Models show significant deficiencies in temporal reasoning about historical artifacts; the best-performing model (GPT-5.2) achieves 58.7% overall accuracy. Performance gaps persist across architectures and scales, indicating a systemic limitation in current VLMs, with non-Western visual cultures being particularly underrepresented in training data.

Conclusion: Cultural anachronism is a core limitation of modern visual AI. TAB-VLM provides a benchmark to push research toward improved temporal cognition in multimodal systems dealing with cultural heritage, enabling more accurate interpretation of artifacts. The dataset and code are publicly available for adoption and extension.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly applied to cultural heritage materials, from digital archives to educational platforms. This work identifies a fundamental issue in how these models interpret historical artifacts. We define this phenomenon as cultural anachronism, the tendency to misinterpret historical objects using temporally inappropriate concepts, materials, or cultural frameworks. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Temporal Anachronism Benchmark for Vision-Language Models (TAB-VLM), a dataset of 600 questions across six categories, designed to evaluate temporal reasoning on 1,600 Indian cultural artifacts spanning prehistoric to modern periods. Systematic evaluations of ten state-of-the-art models reveal significant deficiencies on our benchmark, and even the best model (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% overall accuracy. The performance gap persists across varying architectures and scales, suggesting that cultural anachronism represents a significant limitation in visual AI systems, regardless of model size. These findings highlight the disparity between current VLM capabilities and the requirements for accurately interpreting cultural heritage materials, particularly for non-Western visual cultures underrepresented in training data. Our benchmark provides a foundation for enhancing temporal cognition in multimodal AI systems that interact with historical artifacts. The dataset and code are available in our project page.

</details>


### [128] [SAGE3D: Soft-guided attention and graph excitation for 3D point cloud corner detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15088)
*Batuhan Arda Bekar,Can Sarı,Hüseyin Can Gülkan,Barış Özcan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical hybrid Transformer model (SAGE3D) for corner detection in airborne LiDAR, integrating Soft-Guided Attention and an Excitatory Graph Neural Network within a multi-stage encoder-decoder to enhance recall and precision across scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate corner detection in sparse airborne LiDAR is challenging due to varying density, noise, and scale; existing methods often dilute corner signals during multi-scale feature aggregation.

Method: A hybrid Transformer-based encoder-decoder with hierarchical Set Abstraction downsampling and Feature Propagation upsampling. Introduces Soft-Guided Attention that injects ground-truth corner log-priors into attention logits during training. Adds an Excitatory Graph Neural Network with positive-only message passing at strategic resolutions, letting high-confidence corners reinforce predictions to boost recall.

Result: Claims improved precision and recall through guided attention and excitatory boosting, leveraging multi-scale feature extraction so corner signals are amplified rather than lost across scales.

Conclusion: SAGE3D demonstrates that integrating guided attention and excitatory graph signaling within a multi-scale Transformer framework yields more reliable corner detection in airborne LiDAR, with potential benefits for downstream tasks requiring precise corner localization.

Abstract: We present SAGE3D, a hybrid Transformer-based model for corner detection in airborne LiDAR point clouds. We propose a multi-stage solution built on a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture that progressively downsamples point clouds through Set Abstraction layers and recovers per-point predictions via Feature Propagation. We introduce two innovations: Soft-Guided Attention, which injects ground-truth corner labels as a log-prior into attention logits during training to improve precision; then an Excitatory Graph Neural Network positioned at strategic resolutions in the hierarchy, employing positive-only message passing where high-confidence corners reinforce predictions through learned boosting, optimizing for recall. The hierarchical design enables multi-scale feature extraction while our guided attention and excitatory modules ensure corner signals are amplified rather than diluted across scales.

</details>


### [129] [CoralLite: μCT Reconstruction of Coral Colonies from Individual Corallites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15093)
*Jess Jones,Leonardo Bertini,Kenneth Johnson,Erica Hendy,Tilo Burghardt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces CoralLite, a μCT-based dataset of Porites skeletons and a baseline V-Trans-UNet for corallite segmentation and 3D reconstruction; shows high topology accuracy and Dice on the same colony, with lower performance on a different specimen; dataset and models released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify polyp division timing and skeletal growth by tracking corallite deposits in the skeleton, enabling 3D reconstruction from μCT data across colony scales.

Method: Hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture for segmenting tiled μCT slabs of Porites sp.; pre-trained on weakly annotated data, then topology-aware fine-tuned using 8k+ manual corallite annotations on fully labeled slices; cross-slice linking and 3D visualization; dataset comprises 697 μCT slices, 37 annotated slices, with network weights and source code released.

Result: Topological accuracy of 0.94 on the same colony projection axis; mean Dice scores of 0.77 on the same colony and axis, and 0.63 on a biologically unrelated specimen; demonstrates feasibility of 3D corallite modeling from μCT skeletons; full dataset and code provided as baseline for future work.
