Conclusion: First demonstration that machine learning can enable full 3D corallite modeling from coral skeleton μCT scans; provides a reproducible baseline, though current work is limited in scale and context.

Abstract: The life history of an individual coral is archived within the accreting skeleton of the colony. While reef-forming coral colonies (e.g. massive \emph{Porites} sp.) may live for hundreds of years and deposit calcareous structures many metres in height and width, their living tissue is a thin outer surface layer comprised of asexually-dividing polyps that only survive a few years. To understand the rate and timing of polyp division and the consequences for colony skeletal growth, scientists need to track the skeletal corallite deposited around each polyp. Here we propose CoralLite, an annotated μCT scan dataset of entire calcareous skeletons and an associated, first corallite deep learning reconstruction baseline. CoralLite combines fully quantified volumetric segmentations with cross-slice linking for visualisations of 3D models for each corallite up to colony scale. For segmentation, we propose and evaluate in detail a hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture applicable to segmenting tiled μCT virtual slabs of \emph{Porites} sp. colonies. The model is pre-trained on weakly annotated data and topology-aware fine-tuned using fully annotated slice sections with 8k+ manual corallite region annotations. On unseen slices of the same colony, the resulting model reaches 0.94 topological accuracy at mean Dice scores of 0.77 on the same colony and projection axis, and 0.63 mean Dice scores on a different, biologically unrelated specimen. Whilst our experiments are limited in scale and context, our results show for the first time that visual machine learning can effectively support full 3D individual corallite modelling from μCT scans of coral skeletons alone. For reproducibility and as a baseline for future research we publish our full dataset of 697 μCT slices, 37 partial or full slice annotations, and all network weights and source code with this paper.

</details>


### [130] [DriveCtrl: Conditioned Sim-to-Real Driving Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15116)
*Haonan Zhao,Yiting Wang,Jingkun Chen,Valentina Donzella,Thomas Bashford-Rogers,Kurt Debattista*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveCtrl offers depth-conditioned, structure-aware sim-to-real driving video generation built on a pretrained video model, preserving scene layout and motion with a scalable pipeline and a Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a critical gap between synthetic driving videos and real-world footage for downstream perception tasks; existing methods struggle to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal coherence, and realism while keeping frame-level annotations.

Method: A depth-conditioned structure-aware adapter built on a pretrained video foundation model enables depth-guided generation that preserves the source simulation's layout and motion. A scalable data generation pipeline transforms simulator videos to realistic driving footage matching a target dataset's style, using conditioning signals of structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, while preserving frame-level annotations. A driving-domain DVRS metric evaluates realism.

Result: DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing methods in realism, temporal quality, and downstream perception performance, effectively narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

Conclusion: DriveCtrl provides an effective, annotation-preserving path for realistic driving video synthesis and introduces DVRS as a domain-specific realism metric.

Abstract: Large-scale labelled driving video data is essential for training autonomous driving systems. Although simulation offers scalable and fully annotated data, the domain gap between synthetic and real-world driving videos significantly limits its utility for downstream deployment. Existing video generation methods are not well-suited for this task, as they fail to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and visual realism, all of which are critical for maintaining annotation validity in generated data. In this paper, we present DriveCtrl, a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework for realistic driving video synthesis. Built upon a pretrained video foundation model, DriveCtrl introduces a structure-aware adapter that enables depth-guided generation while preserving the scene layout and motion patterns of the source simulation, producing temporally coherent driving videos that remain aligned with the original simulated sequences. We further introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms simulator videos into realistic driving footage matching the visual style of a target real-world dataset. The pipeline supports three conditioning signals: structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, while preserving frame-level annotations for downstream perception tasks. To better assess this task, we propose a driving-domain-specific knowledge-informed evaluation metric called Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS) that assesses the realism of generated videos. Experiments demonstrate that DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing alternatives in realism, temporal quality, and perception task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

</details>


### [131] [MemEye: A Visual-Centric Evaluation Framework for Multimodal Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15128)
*Minghao Guo,Qingyue Jiao,Zeru Shi,Yihao Quan,Boxuan Zhang,Danrui Li,Liwei Che,Wujiang Xu,Shilong Liu,Zirui Liu,Mubbasir Kapadia,Vladimir Pavlovic,Jiang Liu,Mengdi Wang,Yiyu Shi,Dimitris N. Metaxas,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MemEye introduces a two-dimensional evaluation framework for long-term multimodal memory, examining (1) granularity of decisive visual evidence from scene-level to pixel-level and (2) how retrieved evidence is used from single evidence to evolutionary synthesis. It evaluates 8 life-scenario tasks with ablation gates and tests 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, revealing current models struggle with preserving fine-grained visual details and reasoning about state changes over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of visually grounded memory often rely on captions or textual traces, failing to test preservation of fine-grained visual evidence necessary for later reasoning, especially regarding changing visual states. There is a need to push beyond surface-level evidence to require durable, veridical visual traces for long-term reasoning.

Method: Propose MemEye framework with two axes: evidence granularity (scene-level to pixel-level) and evidence usage (single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). Build a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks with ablation-driven gates for answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. Evaluate 13 memory methods across 4 vision-language model backbones.

Result: Across 8 tasks and 13 memory methods on 4 VLM backbones, current architectures struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and to reason about state changes over time; accuracy degrades when evidence must be retrieved with temporal dynamics and when fine visual evidence is required.

Conclusion: Long-term multimodal memory performance hinges on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction. MemEye provides a diagnostic benchmark to stress-test these capabilities and guide future model design toward robust, veridical visual memory and temporal reasoning.

Abstract: Long-term agent memory is increasingly multimodal, yet existing evaluations rarely test whether agents preserve the visual evidence needed for later reasoning. In prior work, many visually grounded questions can be answered using only captions or textual traces, allowing answers to be inferred without preserving the fine-grained visual evidence. Meanwhile, harder cases that require reasoning over changing visual states are largely absent. Therefore, we introduce MemEye, a framework that evaluates memory capabilities from two dimensions: one measures the granularity of decisive visual evidence (from scene-level to pixel-level evidence), and the other measures how retrieved evidence must be used (from single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). Under this framework, we construct a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks, with ablation-driven validation gates for assessing answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. By evaluating 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, we show that current architectures still struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time. Our findings show that long-term multimodal memory depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction.

</details>


### [132] [Causal Forcing++: Scalable Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation for Real-Time Interactive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15141)
*Min Zhao,Hongzhou Zhu,Kaiwen Zheng,Zihan Zhou,Bokai Yan,Xinyuan Li,Xiao Yang,Chongxuan Li,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Causal Forcing++ enables frame-wise autoregressive diffusion video generation with only 1–2 steps by using causal consistency distillation for efficient few-step initialization, achieving SOTA performance in the frame-wise 2-step regime, with half the first-frame latency and substantially lower training cost; extends to action-conditioned world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time interactive video generation requires ultralow latency, streaming capability, and controllable rollout. Traditional 4-step chunk-wise distillation suffers from coarse granularity and residual latency; a more aggressive frame-wise 1–2 step AR setting could meet real-time constraints but requires a better initialization strategy.

Method: Propose Causal Forcing++ using causal consistency distillation (causal CD) to initialize few-step AR. Causal CD trains the same AR-conditional flow as causal ODE distillation but learns from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding precomputation of full PF-ODE trajectories. This yields an efficient and scalable initialization pipeline. The approach is extended to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3.

Result: In frame-wise 2-step setting, Causal Forcing++ surpasses the SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing by: +0.1 in VBench Total, +0.3 in VBench Quality, and +0.335 in VisionReward. It also halves first-frame latency and reduces Stage 2 training cost by ~4×.

Conclusion: Causal Forcing++ provides an effective, scalable initialization for few-step frame-wise autoregressive diffusion, enabling real-time video generation with improved efficiency and quality, and is extensible to action-conditioned world modeling.

Abstract: Real-time interactive video generation requires low-latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Existing autoregressive (AR) diffusion distillation methods have achieved strong results in the chunk-wise 4-step regime by distilling bidirectional base models into few-step AR students, but they remain limited by coarse response granularity and non-negligible sampling latency. In this paper, we study a more aggressive setting: frame-wise autoregression with only 1--2 sampling steps. In this regime, we identify the initialization of a few-step AR student as the key bottleneck: existing strategies are either target-misaligned, incapable of few-step generation, or too costly to scale. We propose \textbf{Causal Forcing++}, a principled and scalable pipeline that uses \emph{causal consistency distillation} (causal CD) for few-step AR initialization. The core idea is that causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation, but obtains supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding the need to precompute and store full PF-ODE trajectories. This makes the initialization both more efficient and easier to optimize. The resulting pipeline, \ours, surpasses the SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing under the \textit{\textbf{frame-wise 2-step setting}} by 0.1 in VBench Total, 0.3 in VBench Quality, and 0.335 in VisionReward, while reducing first-frame latency by 50\% and Stage 2 training cost by $\sim$$4\times$. We further extend the pipeline to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3. Project Page: https://github.com/thu-ml/Causal-Forcing and https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM .

</details>


### [133] [Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15167)
*Kam Man Wu,Haolin Yang,Qingyu Chen,Yihu Tang,Jingye Chen,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A data-centric study showing purely synthetic layered data (SynLayers) can train a layer-decomposition model to outperform non-scalable datasets, with scale effects up to ~50k samples, and synthetic data enables balanced layer-count distributions, advocating synthetic data as a scalable foundation for layered design editing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the last-mile gap in post-generation editing for image generation by enabling scalable, well-behaved layer decomposition without relying on scarce proprietary assets or limited synthetic data.

Method: Construct a synthetic layered dataset (SynLayers) based on a CLD baseline for layer decomposition. Use vision-language models to generate textual supervision and VLM-predicted bounding boxes to automate inputs. Train/evaluate the CLD model on synthetic data, compare against PrismLayersPro, and analyze how performance scales with training data size and layer-count distribution.

Result: Training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable baselines (e.g., PrismLayersPro). Performance improves with dataset size but saturates around 50K samples. Synthetic data provides balanced layer-count distributions, mitigating the imbalance seen in real-world datasets.

Conclusion: A data-centric study demonstrates that synthetic layered data is a viable and scalable foundation for layered design editing systems, encouraging broader adoption of synthetic data for this task.

Abstract: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within a fixed canvas. As a result, flexible post-generation editing remains challenging, revealing a clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct a data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is a state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as a scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

</details>


### [134] [Evidential Reasoning Advances Interpretable Real-World Disease Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15171)
*Chenyu Lian,Hong-Yu Zhou,Jing Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EviScreen is an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that uses region-level evidence from historical cases via dual knowledge banks and contrastive abnormality maps to improve accuracy and provide retrospection and localization interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current medical image screening models often suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance. There's a need for mechanisms to reference historical cases and provide transparent, evidence-based reasoning beyond post-hoc saliency maps.

Method: An evidential reasoning pipeline that retrieves region-level evidence from dual knowledge banks and integrates it in an evidence-aware reasoning module using both the current case and historical evidence; localization interpretability is enhanced through abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval.

Result: The approach achieves superior performance on real-world disease-screening benchmarks, notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: EviScreen offers retrospection interpretability and improved localization interpretability while delivering performance gains, illustrating the potential of evidence-grounded disease screening with retrieve-and-reason dynamics.

Abstract: Disease screening is critical for early detection and timely intervention in clinical practice. However, most current screening models for medical images suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance. They often lack effective mechanisms to reference historical cases or provide transparent reasoning pathways. To address these challenges, we introduce EviScreen, an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that leverages region-level evidence from historical cases. The proposed EviScreen offers retrospection interpretability through regional evidence retrieved from dual knowledge banks. Using this evidential mechanism, the subsequent evidence-aware reasoning module makes predictions using both the current case and evidence from historical cases, thereby enhancing disease screening performance. Furthermore, rather than relying on post-hoc saliency maps, EviScreen enhances localization interpretability by leveraging abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval. Our method achieves superior performance on our carefully established benchmarks for real-world disease screening, yielding notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DopamineLcy/EviScreen.

</details>


### [135] [SANA-WM: Efficient Minute-Scale World Modeling with Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15178)
*Haoyi Zhu,Haozhe Liu,Yuyang Zhao,Tian Ye,Junsong Chen,Jincheng Yu,Tong He,Song Han,Enze Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SANA-WM is a 2.6B open-source world model that efficiently generates 60-second 720p videos with 6-DoF camera control, achieving competitive quality and 36x throughput versus baselines, trained on 213k pose-supervised clips, using four core designs: Hybrid Linear Attention, Dual-Branch Camera Control, Two-Stage Generation, and Robust Annotation. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable, data-efficient, controllable world-models that can produce minute-scale videos with precise camera control, while maintaining high visual quality and practical compute. Existing large industrial baselines offer quality but are limited by efficiency and accessibility; a compact, well-architected model could enable broader research and deployment.

Method: Four core designs: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention blending frame-wise GDN with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling; (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control for precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence; (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline with a long-video refiner to improve quality and consistency; (4) Robust Annotation Pipeline to extract metric-scale 6-DoF camera poses from public videos for high-quality spatiotemporal labels. Training uses ~213K public video clips with metric-scale pose supervision; training on 64 H100 GPUs for 15 days; inference generates 60s clips on a single GPU; a distilled variant runs on an RTX 5090 with NVFP4 quantization to denoise a 60s 720p clip in 34s.

Result: On a one-minute world-model benchmark, SANA-WM achieves stronger action-following accuracy than prior open-source baselines and maintains comparable visual quality, while delivering ~36× higher throughput for scalable world modeling.

Conclusion: SANA-WM demonstrates that a compact 2.6B world model, with targeted architectural innovations and robust pose supervision, can deliver efficient, high-quality minute-scale video generation with precise camera control. The approach highlights data efficiency, practical training/inference compute, and potential for deployment, though it relies on public-video pose annotations and may face challenges in long-range coherence and generalization.

Abstract: We introduce SANA-WM, an efficient 2.6B-parameter open-source world model natively trained for one-minute generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, 720p, minute-scale videos with precise camera control. SANA-WM achieves visual quality comparable to large-scale industrial baselines such as LingBot-World and HY-WorldPlay, while significantly improving efficiency. Four core designs drive our architecture: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention combines frame-wise Gated DeltaNet (GDN) with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling. (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control ensures precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence. (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline applies a long-video refiner to stage-1 outputs, improving quality and consistency across sequences. (4) Robust Annotation Pipeline extracts accurate metric-scale 6-DoF camera poses from public videos to yield high-quality, spatiotemporally consistent action labels. Driven by these designs, SANA-WMdemonstrates remarkable efficiency across data, training compute, and inference hardware: it uses only $\sim$213K public video clips with metric-scale pose supervision, completes training in 15 days on 64 H100s, and generates each 60s clip on a single GPU; its distilled variant can be deployed on a single RTX 5090 with NVFP4 quantization to denoise a 60s 720p clip in 34s. On our one-minute world-model benchmark, SANA-WM demonstrates stronger action-following accuracy than prior open-source baselines and achieves comparable visual quality at $36\times$ higher throughput for scalable world modeling.

</details>


### [136] [From Plans to Pixels: Learning to Plan and Orchestrate for Open-Ended Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15181)
*Anirudh Sundara Rajan,Krishna Kumar Singh,Yong Jae Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An experiential planner–orchestrator framework for long-horizon image editing that uses a vision-language judge and outcome-based rewards to guide planning and execution, outperforming single-step or rule-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address abstraction and multi-step editing challenges in modern image editors, overcoming the limitations of handcrafted pipelines and imitation-based learning that disconnect planning from actual editing outcomes.

Method: A three-part system: (1) a planner that generates structured atomic decompositions of the editing task, (2) an orchestrator that selects tools and image regions to execute each step, and (3) a vision-language judge that assigns outcome-based rewards for instruction adherence and visual quality. The orchestrator is trained to maximize these rewards, and successful trajectories are used to refine the planner, forming a closed, reward-driven planning-execution loop.

Result: The tightly coupled planning and reward-driven execution yields more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multistep baselines.

Conclusion: Integrating planning with reward-guided execution improves long-horizon image editing and offers a flexible framework that learns from outcomes rather than relying on handcrafted pipelines or imitation.

Abstract: Modern image editing models produce realistic results but struggle with abstract, multi step instructions (e.g., ``make this advertisement more vegetarian-friendly''). Prior agent based methods decompose such tasks but rely on handcrafted pipelines or teacher imitation, limiting flexibility and decoupling learning from actual editing outcomes. We propose an experiential framework for long-horizon image editing, where a planner generates structured atomic decompositions and an orchestrator selects tools and regions to execute each step. A vision language judge provides outcome-based rewards for instruction adherence and visual quality. The orchestrator is trained to maximize these rewards, and successful trajectories are used to refine the planner. By tightly coupling planning with reward driven execution, our approach yields more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multistep baselines.

</details>


### [137] [Warp-as-History: Generalizable Camera-Controlled Video Generation from One Training Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15182)
*Yifan Wang,Tong He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free interface, Warp-as-History, enables zero-shot camera trajectory following in frozen video generation models by converting camera-induced warps into a camera-warped pseudo-history; optional one-video LoRA fine-tuning further enhances performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the need for camera-specific conditioning and post-training; exploit existing model history pathways to follow trajectories without costly training or test-time optimization.

Method: Create camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations aligned to the target-frame positions; feed it through the model's visual-history pathway; align positional encodings with target frames; prune tokens without valid sources; optionally apply lightweight LoRA fine-tuning on a single annotated video.

Result: Demonstrates non-trivial zero-shot camera adherence in a frozen video-generation model; LoRA fine-tuning further improves camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics, generalizing to unseen videos.

Conclusion: Warp-as-History provides a simple, training-free interface that leverages latent model capabilities and can be enhanced with minimal offline adaptation, reducing reliance on post-training or test-time optimization.

Abstract: Camera-controlled video generation has made substantial progress, enabling generated videos to follow prescribed viewpoint trajectories. However, existing methods usually learn camera-specific conditioning through camera encoders, control branches, or attention and positional-encoding modifications, which often require post-training on large-scale camera-annotated videos. Training-free alternatives avoid such post-training, but often shift the cost to test-time optimization or extra denoising-time guidance. We propose Warp-as-History, a simple interface that turns camera-induced warps into camera-warped pseudo-history with target-frame positional alignment and visible-token selection. Given a target camera trajectory, we construct camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations and feed it through the model's visual-history pathway. Crucially, we align its positional encoding with the target frames being denoised and remove warped-history tokens without valid source observations. Without any training, architectural modification, or test-time optimization, this interface reveals a non-trivial zero-shot capability of a frozen video generation model to follow camera trajectories. Moreover, lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on only one camera-annotated video further improves this capability and generalizes to unseen videos, improving camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics without test-time optimization or target-video adaptation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets confirm the effectiveness of our method.

</details>


### [138] [Quantitative Video World Model Evaluation for Geometric-Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15185)
*Jiaxin Wu,Yihao Pi,Yinling Zhang,Yuheng Li,Xueyan Zou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PDI-Bench is a quantitative framework for auditing geometric coherence in generative videos. It uses object-centric segmentation and monocular 3D reconstruction to compute projective-geometry residuals along scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D rigidity. It includes PDI-Dataset for stressed evaluation and reveals geometry-specific failures not captured by perceptual metrics, offering a diagnostic tool to advance physically grounded video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation evaluation relies on subjective judgments or learned graders, which often miss geometric/physical inconsistencies. There is a need for objective, geometry-focused diagnostics to quantify 3D structure and motion plausibility in generated videos.

Method: Extract object-centric observations via segmentation (SAM 2, MegaSaM, CoTracker3); lift observations to 3D world-space with monocular reconstruction; compute projective-geometry residuals for three dimensions: scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D structural rigidity; assemble PDI-Dataset with diverse stress scenarios; evaluate state-of-the-art video generators using these residuals.

Result: PDI exposes geometry-specific failure modes in leading video generators that perceptual metrics miss; provides a diagnostic signal guiding progress toward physically grounded video synthesis and physical world models; provides code and dataset for the community.

Conclusion: PDI-Bench offers a rigorous, geometry-centered evaluation framework to complement perceptual metrics, enabling systematic assessment and improvement of physically plausible video generation.

Abstract: Generative video models are increasingly studied as implicit world models, yet evaluating whether they produce physically plausible 3D structure and motion remains challenging. Most existing video evaluation pipelines rely heavily on human judgment or learned graders, which can be subjective and weakly diagnostic for geometric failures. We introduce PDI-Bench (Perspective Distortion Index), a quantitative framework for auditing geometric coherence in generated videos. Given a generated clip, we obtain object-centric observations via segmentation and point tracking (e.g., SAM 2, MegaSaM, and CoTracker3), lift them to 3D world-space coordinates via monocular reconstruction, and compute a set of projective-geometry residuals capturing three failure dimensions: scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D structural rigidity. To support systematic evaluation, we build PDI-Dataset, covering diverse scenarios designed to stress these geometric constraints. Across state-of-the-art video generators, PDI reveals consistent geometry-specific failure modes that are not captured by common perceptual metrics, and provides a diagnostic signal for progress toward physically grounded video generation and physical world model. Our code and dataset can be found at https://pdi-bench.github.io/.

</details>


### [139] [VGGT-Edit: Feed-forward Native 3D Scene Editing with Residual Field Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15186)
*Kaixin Zhu,Yiwen Tang,Yifan Yang,Renrui Zhang,Bohan Zeng,Ziyu Guo,Ruichuan An,Zhou Liu,Qizhi Chen,Delin Qu,Jaehong Yoon,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feed-forward, text-conditioned 3D scene editor (VGGT-Edit) with depth-synchronized guidance and a residual head for 3D displacement, plus a DeltaScene dataset; it outperforms 2D-lifting baselines in quality, consistency, and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of static generalizable 3D reconstructions and 2D-lifting editing, which struggle with dynamic instructions, blur textures, and inconsistent geometry across views.

Method: Depth-synchronized text injection aligns semantic guidance with 3D pose; a residual transformation head directly predicts 3D geometric displacements to edit scenes; multi-term objectives enforce geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency; training uses DeltaScene Dataset generated with 3D agreement filtering.

Result: VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, producing sharper details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective, fast, text-conditioned native 3D scene editing with stable grounding and geometry preservation; the DeltaScene dataset supports high-quality ground-truth data for supervision.

Abstract: High-quality 3D scene reconstruction has recently advanced toward generalizable feed-forward architectures, enabling the generation of complex environments in a single forward pass. However, despite their strong performance in static scene perception, these models remain limited in responding to dynamic human instructions, which restricts their use in interactive applications. Existing editing methods typically rely on a 2D-lifting strategy, where individual views are edited independently and then lifted back into 3D space. This indirect pipeline often leads to blurry textures and inconsistent geometry, as 2D editors lack the spatial awareness required to preserve structure across viewpoints. To address these limitations, we propose VGGT-Edit, a feed-forward framework for text-conditioned native 3D scene editing. VGGT-Edit introduces depth-synchronized text injection to align semantic guidance with the backbone's spatial poses, ensuring stable instruction grounding. This semantic signal is then processed by a residual transformation head, which directly predicts 3D geometric displacements to deform the scene while preserving background stability. To ensure high-fidelity results, we supervise the framework with a multi-term objective function that enforces geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. We also construct the DeltaScene Dataset, a large-scale dataset generated through an automated pipeline with 3D agreement filtering to ensure ground-truth quality. Experiments show that VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, producing sharper object details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.

</details>


### [140] [RAVEN: Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation with Consistency-model GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15190)
*Yanzuo Lu,Ronglai Zuo,Jiankang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAVEN addresses training-inference history mismatch in causal autoregressive video diffusion by repacking rollouts into interleaved historical endpoints and denoising states to align training with inference; CM-GRPO reframes consistency sampling as a conditional Gaussian transition and uses online RL to optimize it, avoiding Euler-Maruyama. Together they improve real-time video extrapolation quality over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A key challenge in causal autoregressive video diffusion is the mismatch between history distributions seen during training and those encountered during long-horizon inference, which harms long-range generation quality.

Method: RAVEN rewrites self rollouts into interleaved sequences of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states, enabling chunk-level supervision of history representations. CM-GRPO replaces the standard consistency sampling (Euler–Maruyama) with a conditional Gaussian transition and performs online RL directly on this kernel to optimize consistency.

Result: Empirical results show RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree metrics; CM-GRPO yields additional gains when used with RAVEN.

Conclusion: The proposed training-time test framework (RAVEN) and RL-based optimization (CM-GRPO) jointly improve real-time extrapolation in causal video diffusion, mitigating history-distribution gaps and enhancing consistency and quality.

Abstract: Causal autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time streaming generation by extrapolating future chunks from previously generated content. Distilling such generators from high-fidelity bidirectional teachers yields competitive few-step models, yet a persistent gap between the history distributions encountered during training and those arising at inference constrains generation quality over long horizons. We introduce the Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation Network (RAVEN), a training-time test framework that repacks each self rollout into an interleaved sequence of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states. This formulation aligns training attention with inference-time extrapolation and allows downstream chunk losses to supervise the history representations on which future predictions depend. We further propose Consistency-model Group Relative Policy Optimization (CM-GRPO), which reformulates a consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online Reinforcement Learning (RL) directly to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process adopted in prior flow-model RL formulations. Experiments demonstrate that RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations, and that CM-GRPO provides further gains when combined with RAVEN.

</details>


### [141] [Aligning Latent Geometry for Spherical Flow Matching in Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15193)
*Tuna Han Salih Meral,Kaan Oktay,Hidir Yesiltepe,Adil Kaan Akan,Pinar Yanardag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Latent flow is reframed on a sphere by decomposing latents into radial and angular parts; fixed-radius latents, spherical priors, and spherical interpolation yield geodesic, angular-only dynamics, improving ImageNet-256 FID without extra encoders or objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear latent transports between Gaussian noise and VAE latents cause trajectories that leave the latent sphere; perceptual/semantic content is largely encoded in direction rather than radius. A sphere-constrained, angular-focused approach should preserve semantic content and improve generation quality.

Method: Decompose latent tokens into radial and angular components; project onto a fixed token radius; use radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior; freeze encoder and fine-tune decoder; replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation (slerp); enforce geodesic paths on the sphere with purely angular velocity targets.

Result: Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, with the diffusion architecture unchanged and no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective.

Conclusion: Geodesic, sphere-constrained latent flows preserve angular information and yield better generation quality, with radius playing a minor role; the approach is robust across tokenizers and requires minimal training modifications.

Abstract: Latent flow matching for image generation usually transports Gaussian noise to variational autoencoder latents along linear paths. Both endpoints, however, concentrate in thin spherical shells, and a Euclidean chord leaves those shells even when preprocessing aligns their radii. By decomposing each latent token into radial and angular components, we show through component-swap probes that decoded perceptual and semantic content is carried predominantly by direction, with radius contributing much less. We therefore project data latents onto a fixed token radius, use the radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior, finetune the decoder with the encoder frozen, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation. The resulting geodesic paths stay on the sphere at every timestep, and their velocity targets are purely angular by construction. Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, leaves the diffusion architecture unchanged, and requires no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective.

</details>


### [142] [VGGT-$Ω$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15195)
*Jianyuan Wang,Minghao Chen,Shangzhan Zhang,Nikita Karaev,Johannes Schönberger,Patrick Labatut,Piotr Bojanowski,David Novotny,Andrea Vedaldi,Christian Rupprecht*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-Ω scales with model and data size, achieving substantial gains in reconstruction accuracy and efficiency for static/dynamic scenes, while enabling training at unprecedented scale via architectural simplifications, compact scene representations (registers), and a self-supervised protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To push feed-forward reconstruction models toward scalable performance and practical training efficiency, enabling large datasets and dynamic scenes while maintaining or improving accuracy and enabling cross-modal benefits.

Method: Proposes architectural simplifications: a single dense prediction head with multi-task supervision; removal of expensive high-resolution conv layers. Introduces registers to aggregate scene information and register attention to limit inter-frame exchange. Uses high-quality dynamic-scene annotations and a self-supervised training protocol to leverage unlabeled video. Achieves 30% GPU memory of VGGT, enabling 15x more supervised data. Training on scale with data augmentation and self-supervision.

Result: VGGT-Ω achieves strong results across static and dynamic scene reconstruction benchmarks, notably a 77% improvement in camera estimation accuracy on Sintel over the previous best. Learned registers also improve vision-language-action models and support alignment with language.

Conclusion: VGGT-Ω demonstrates that reconstruction quality scales predictably with model and data size, and that architectural and data-efficiency innovations enable large-scale training and broader applicability (including language alignment), validating reconstruction as a scalable proxy task for spatial understanding.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward reconstruction models, such as VGGT, have proven competitive with traditional optimization-based reconstructors while also providing geometry-aware features useful for other tasks. Here, we show that the quality of these models scales predictably with model and data size. We do so by introducing VGGT-$Ω$, which substantially improves reconstruction accuracy, efficiency, and capabilities for both static and dynamic scenes. To enable training this model at an unprecedented scale, we introduce architectural changes that improve training efficiency, a high-quality data annotation pipeline that supports dynamic scenes, and a self-supervised learning protocol. We simplify VGGT's architecture by using a single dense prediction head with multi-task supervision and removing the expensive high-resolution convolutional layers. We also use registers to aggregate scene information into a compact representation and introduce register attention, which restricts inter-frame information exchange to these registers, in part replacing global attention. In this way, during training, VGGT-$Ω$ uses only about 30% of the GPU memory of its predecessor, allowing us to train with 15x more supervised data than prior work and to leverage vast amounts of unlabeled video data. VGGT-$Ω$ achieves strong results for reconstruction of static and dynamic scenes across multiple benchmarks, for example, improving over the previous best camera estimation accuracy on Sintel by 77%. We also show that the learned registers can improve vision-language-action models and support alignment with language, suggesting that reconstruction can be a powerful and scalable proxy task for spatial understanding. Project Page: http://vggt-omega.github.io/

</details>


### [143] [RefDecoder: Enhancing Visual Generation with Conditional Video Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15196)
*Xiang Fan,Yuheng Wang,Bohan Fang,Zhongzheng Ren,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RefDecoder introduces a reference-conditioned decoder for video VAEs that injects high-fidelity reference frame tokens into the decoding process via reference attention, improving detail, consistency, and PSNR across multiple backbones and benchmarks, with zero-shot swap-in and broad applicability to style transfer and video editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard diffusion-based video decoders are often unconditional, causing loss of structural fidelity and inconsistency with input images. Conditioning the decoder to match the reference image should preserve structural integrity and enhance quality.

Method: Use a lightweight image encoder to map a reference frame into high-dimensional tokens, which are fused with the denoised video latent tokens at each up-sampling stage via reference attention; enables a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder that can be swapped into existing systems without fine-tuning.

Result: Consistent improvements across backbones (Wan 2.1, VideoVAE+), up to +2.1dB PSNR on Inter4K, WebVid, Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks; improved subject and background consistency and overall quality on VBench I2V; zero-shot swap-in; generalizes to style transfer and video editing refinement.

Conclusion: Reference-conditioned decoding via RefDecoder mitigates decoder unconditionality, yielding higher fidelity and consistency with input references and broad applicability across video generation tasks.

Abstract: Video generation powers a vast array of downstream applications. However, while the de facto standard, i.e., latent diffusion models, typically employ heavily conditioned denoising networks, their decoders often remain unconditional. We observe that this architectural asymmetry leads to significant loss of detail and inconsistency relative to the input image. To address this, we argue that the decoder requires equal conditioning to preserve structural integrity. We introduce RefDecoder, a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder by injecting high-fidelity reference image signal directly into the decoding process via reference attention. Specifically, a lightweight image encoder maps the reference frame into the detail-rich high-dimensional tokens, which are co-processed with the denoised video latent tokens at each decoder up-sampling stage. We demonstrate consistent improvements across several distinct decoder backbones (e.g., Wan 2.1 and VideoVAE+), achieving up to +2.1dB PSNR over the unconditional baselines on the Inter4K, WebVid, and Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks. Notably, RefDecoder can be directly swapped into existing video generation systems without additional fine-tuning, and we report across-the-board improvements in subject consistency, background consistency, and overall quality scores on the VBench I2V benchmark. Beyond I2V, RefDecoder generalizes well to a wide range of visual generation tasks such as style transfer and video editing refinement.

</details>


### [144] [ATLAS: Agentic or Latent Visual Reasoning? One Word is Enough for Both](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15198)
*Ziyu Guo,Rain Liu,Xinyan Chen,Pheng-Ann Heng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATLAS unifies agentic and latent visual reasoning by using functional tokens that act as both executable operations and latent reasoning units, enabling next-token generation without visual supervision; stabilized RL with LA-GRPO via an auxiliary objective; shows strong performance and interpretability without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field needs a unified, scalable approach that leverages the benefits of agentic (code/tool usage) and latent (hidden embeddings) reasoning while avoiding latency, generalization gaps, and training inefficiencies. A single token that can drive both reasoning modes could streamline training and inference.

Method: Introduce functional tokens as discrete tokens mapped to internalized visual operations. These tokens are trainable through standard next-token prediction, require no direct visual supervision, and are compatible with standard SFT and RL pipelines without architectural changes. To mitigate RL sparsity, propose LA-GRPO, an auxiliary objective with static weighting to stabilize gradient updates for functional tokens.

Result: Empirical results across benchmarks indicate superior performance and improved interpretability compared to baselines relying solely on agentic or latent strategies. The approach avoids verbose intermediate visual content generation and maintains compatibility with existing training regimes.

Conclusion: ATLAS provides a new paradigm for visual reasoning by unifying agentic and latent reasoning through a single discrete functional token, with LA-GRPO enhancing training stability; it suggests a path toward more interpretable and efficient visual reasoning models.

Abstract: Visual reasoning, often interleaved with intermediate visual states, has emerged as a promising direction in the field. A straightforward approach is to directly generate images via unified models during reasoning, but this is computationally expensive and architecturally non-trivial. Recent alternatives include agentic reasoning through code or tool calls, and latent reasoning with learnable hidden embeddings. However, agentic methods incur context-switching latency from external execution, while latent methods lack task generalization and are difficult to train with autoregressive parallelization. To combine their strengths while mitigating their limitations, we propose ATLAS, a framework in which a single discrete 'word', termed as a functional token, serves both as an agentic operation and a latent visual reasoning unit. Each functional token is associated with an internalized visual operation, yet requires no visual supervision and remains a standard token in the tokenizer vocabulary, which can be generated via next-token prediction. This design avoids verbose intermediate visual content generation, while preserving compatibility with the vanilla scalable SFT and RL training, without architectural or methodological modifications. To further address the sparsity of functional tokens during RL, we introduce Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO), which stabilizes the training by anchoring functional tokens with a statically weighted auxiliary objective, providing stronger gradient updates. Extensive experiments and analyses demonstrate that ATLAS achieves superior performance on challenging benchmarks while maintaining clear interpretability. We hope ATLAS offers a new paradigm inspiring future visual reasoning research.

</details>


### [145] [EntityBench: Towards Entity-Consistent Long-Range Multi-Shot Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15199)
*Ruozhen He,Meng Wei,Ziyan Yang,Vicente Ordonez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Benchmark and memory-augmented baseline for long-range cross-shot video generation. EntityBench provides 140 episodes (2,491 shots) with per-shot entity schedules across up to 50-shot sequences, plus a three-pillar evaluation with a fidelity gate. EntityMem stores per-entity visual references to improve cross-shot consistency; results show memory-based methods yield higher character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standardize and stress-test cross-shot consistency in multi-shot video generation by providing a dataset with explicit per-shot entity schedules and recurrence gaps, addressing limitations of prompt-based, independently evaluated baselines.

Method: Construct 140 episodes (2,491 shots) from real narrative media; annotate per-shot schedules for 13 cross-shot characters, 8 cross-shot locations, 22 cross-shot objects; three difficulty tiers (easy/medium/hard) up to 50 shots; evaluate intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, and cross-shot consistency with a fidelity gate; propose EntityMem, a memory-augmented generator with a persistent per-entity memory bank.

Result: Cross-shot entity consistency degrades with recurrence distance in existing methods; explicit per-entity memory yields the strongest character fidelity and presence (Cohen's d = +2.33) among methods evaluated.

Conclusion: EntityBench enables standardized, scalable evaluation of long-range cross-shot consistency; memory-augmented generation (EntityMem) improves character fidelity across shots; dataset and code are publicly available for replication.

Abstract: Multi-shot video generation extends single-shot generation to coherent visual narratives, yet maintaining consistent characters, objects, and locations across shots remains a challenge over long sequences. Existing evaluations typically use independently generated prompt sets with limited entity coverage and simple consistency metrics, making standardized comparison difficult. We introduce EntityBench, a benchmark of 140 episodes (2,491 shots) derived from real narrative media, with explicit per-shot entity schedules tracking characters, objects, and locations simultaneously across easy / medium / hard tiers of up to 50 shots, 13 cross-shot characters, 8 cross-shot locations, 22 cross-shot objects, and recurrence gaps spanning up to 48 shots. It is paired with a three-pillar evaluation suite that disentangles intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, and cross-shot consistency, with a fidelity gate that admits only accurate entity appearances into cross-shot scoring. As a baseline, we propose EntityMem, a memory-augmented generation system that stores verified per-entity visual references in a persistent memory bank before generation begins. Experiments show that cross-shot entity consistency degrades sharply with recurrence distance in existing methods, and that explicit per-entity memory yields the highest character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33) and presence among methods evaluated. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Catherine-R-He/EntityBench/.

</details>


### [146] [CurveBench: A Benchmark for Exact Topological Reasoning over Nested Jordan Curves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14068)
*Amirreza Mohseni,Mona Mohammadi,Morteza Saghafian,Naser Talebizadeh Saradari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CurveBench provides 756 images of non-intersecting Jordan curves annotated with rooted containment trees; task is to recover the full containment topology; strongest model scores 71.1% on Easy and 19.1% on Hard; RLVR-fine-tuning improves open-weight VL models to 33.3%, still gaps on Hard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To probe hierarchical topological reasoning from visual input and establish a benchmark for topology-aware reasoning beyond pixel-level accuracy.

Method: Dataset with multiple configurations (easy, polygonal, topographic-inspired, maze-like, dense counting); structured prediction task requiring recovery of the containment tree; evaluation via tree-generation accuracy; baselines include Gemini 3.1 Pro; RLVR-style fine-tuning of open-weight vision-language models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B); evaluation protocol comparing with larger LMs.

Result: Best model achieves 71.1% on CurveBench-Easy and 19.1% on CurveBench-Hard. RLVR-style fine-tuning of Qwen3-VL-8B yields 33.3% on Easy, surpassing GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5 under the authors’ protocol; remaining gap is substantial on Hard, indicating topology-aware reasoning is not yet solved.

Conclusion: Topology-aware visual reasoning remains far from solved. CurveBench exposes limitations of current models and provides a benchmark to drive progress in exact hierarchical/topological understanding from visual data.

Abstract: We introduce CurveBench, a benchmark for hierarchical topological reasoning from visual input. CurveBench consists of \textbf{756 images} of pairwise non-intersecting Jordan curves across easy, polygonal, topographic-inspired, maze-like, and dense counting configurations. Each image is annotated with a rooted tree encoding the containment relations between planar regions. We formulate the task as structured prediction: given an image, a model must recover the full rooted containment tree induced by the curves. Despite the visual simplicity of the task, the strongest evaluated model, Gemini 3.1 Pro, achieves only \textbf{71.1\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy and \textbf{19.1\%} on CurveBench-Hard. We further demonstrate benchmark utility through RLVR-style fine-tuning of open-weight vision-language models. Our trained Qwen3-VL-8B model improves over \texttt{Qwen-3-VL-8B-Thinking} from \textbf{2.8\%} to \textbf{33.3\%} tree-generation accuracy on CurveBench-Easy, exceeding GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.5 under our evaluation protocol. The remaining gap, especially on CurveBench-Hard, shows that exact topology-aware visual reasoning remains far from solved.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [147] [GraphBit: A Graph-based Agentic Framework for Non-Linear Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13848)
*Yeahia Sarker,Md Rahmat Ullah,Musa Molla,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GraphBit introduces an engine-driven DAG-based framework for LLM workflow orchestration, replacing prompted orchestration with deterministic, auditable routing. Agents are typed functions; a Rust engine handles routing, state, and tool calls. It uses a three-tier memory system (scratch, structured state, external connectors) to prevent context bloat. On GAIA benchmarks (zero-tool, document-augmented, web-enabled), GraphBit outperforms six baselines with 67.6% accuracy, zero hallucinations, 11.9 ms latency, and high throughput. Ablations show each memory tier helps, with deterministic execution providing the largest gains on tool-heavy tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LLMs that rely on prompted orchestration often hallucinate, loop, or produce non-reproducible workﬂows. There is a need for explicit, deterministic workflow definitions and auditability to ensure reliability in long-running pipelines.

Method: GraphBit defines workflows as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where agents are typed functions. A Rust-based engine administers routing, state transitions, and tool invocation, enabling reproducible and auditable execution. Features include parallel branch execution, conditional control over structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. A three-tier memory architecture (ephemeral scratch, structured state, external connectors) isolates context across stages to prevent cascading context bloat.

Result: Across GAIA benchmark tasks spanning zero-tool, document-augmented, and web-enabled workflows, GraphBit achieves the highest accuracy (67.6%), zero framework-induced hallucinations, the lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and the highest throughput compared to six existing frameworks. Ablation studies indicate each memory tier contributes to performance, with deterministic execution yielding the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks.

Conclusion: GraphBit provides reproducible, auditable, and efficient LLM workflow orchestration by combining explicit DAG-based design, typed function agents, a Rust engine for deterministic routing, and a three-tier memory model. This design mitigates hallucinations and latency while improving throughput, especially for tool-heavy deployments.

Abstract: Agentic LLM frameworks that rely on prompted orchestration, where the model itself determines workflow transitions, often suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution. We introduce GraphBit, an engine-orchestrated framework that defines workflows explicitly and deterministically as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Unlike prompted orchestration, agents in GraphBit operate as typed functions, while a Rust-based engine governs routing, state transitions, and tool invocation, ensuring reproducibility and auditability. The engine supports parallel branch execution, conditional control flow over structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. A three-tier memory architecture consisting of ephemeral scratch space, structured state, and external connectors isolates context across stages, preventing cascading context bloat that degrades reasoning in long-running pipelines. Across GAIA benchmark tasks spanning zero-tool, document-augmented, and web-enabled workflows, GraphBit outperforms six existing frameworks, achieving the highest accuracy (67.6 percent), zero framework-induced hallucinations, the lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and the highest throughput. Ablation studies demonstrate that each memory tier contributes measurably to performance, with deterministic execution providing the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks representative of real-world deployments.

</details>


### [148] [Mixed Integer Goal Programming for Personalized Meal Optimization with User-Defined Serving Granularity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13849)
*Francisco Aguilera Moreno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization, combining integer serving counts with soft nutrient targets via goal-programming deviations, yielding feasible solutions with small integrality gaps and fast solve times, outperforming GP with post-hoc rounding and hard-constraint IP in benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two limitations in diet optimization literature: impractical fractional servings from continuous models and infeasibility due to hard nutrient constraints. Prior work rarely combines integer programming with goal programming to handle both issues.

Method: Formulate MIGP with integer decision variables for per-food servings and goal-programming deviation variables for soft nutrient targets, using inverse-target normalization to balance multiple nutrients. Analyze integrality gap via deviation absorption property, showing GP deviations buffer integer-serving costs. Benchmark on 810 instances (30 USDA foods, 9 configurations, 3 methods) using HiGHS, comparing MIGP to GP with rounding and to hard-constraint IP; provide per-meal granularity and open-source Python implementation.

Result: MIGP achieves strictly better solutions than GP with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP reaches only 48% feasibility. For meals with 15+ foods, integer solutions match the continuous optimum. Solve times are under 100 ms for typical meals.

Conclusion: MIGP offers a practical, efficient framework for personalized meal optimization that reconciles integer serving counts with soft nutrient targets, mitigating integrality gaps via deviation absorption and enabling real-time planning in an open-source tool. Future work could extend to broader nutrient sets, dynamic menus, and user-preference constraints.

Abstract: Determining what to eat to satisfy nutritional requirements is one of the oldest optimization problems in operations research, yet existing formulations have two persistent limitations: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (1.7 eggs, 0.37 bananas), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A systematic review of 56 diet optimization papers found that none combine integer programming with goal programming to address both issues. We propose Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization. The formulation uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets, with inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization. Per-food serving granularity allows natural units (one egg, one tablespoon of oil) without post-hoc rounding. We characterize the integrality gap in the goal programming context and identify a deviation absorption property: GP deviation variables buffer the cost of requiring integer servings, making the gap structurally smaller than in hard-constraint MIP. For meals with 15+ foods, the integer solution matches the continuous optimum in every benchmark instance. A computational evaluation across 810 instances (30 USDA foods, 9 configurations, 3 methods) shows MIGP finds strictly better solutions than GP with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP achieves only 48%. Solve times stay under 100 ms for typical meal sizes using the open-source HiGHS solver. The implementation is available as an open-source Python module integrated into an interactive meal planning application.

</details>


### [149] [A Two-Dimensional Framework for AI Agent Design Patterns: Cognitive Function and Execution Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13850)
*Jia Huang,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-dimensional matrix classifies LLM-based agent architectures by (1) cognitive function (Context Eng, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) execution topology (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy), yielding 27 patterns (13 new names). It defines eight representative patterns, validates across four real-world domains, and derives five empirical laws linking environment constraints to architectural choices. The framework is framework-neutral and model-agnostic for design guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current literature tends to analyze AI agent architectures from either an execution-topology perspective or a cognitive-function perspective, which leaves architecturally distinct systems under-specified when sharing the same topological backbone. A joint, orthogonal classification aims to disambiguate patterns, reduce design ambiguity, and provide a neutral vocabulary for comparison and design.

Method: Propose a 7x6 classification matrix: seven cognitive-function categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and six execution-topology archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). Enumerate 27 named patterns (13 with previously existing names), establish orthogonality via cross-axis analysis, define eight representative patterns in detail, and validate descriptive coverage by mapping to four domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Derive five empirical laws describing how environmental constraints (time pressure, action authority, failure-cost asymmetry, volume) influence architectural choices.

Result: Identification of 27 patterns (13 original names) across the 7x6 matrix, with eight patterns elaborated and cross-domain coverage demonstrated. Five empirical laws of pattern selection uncovered, relating environmental constraints to architectural configurations.

Conclusion: The framework delivers a principled, framework-neutral, model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design, enabling clearer disambiguation, cross-domain comparison, and guidance on trade-offs and failure modes in complex agent systems.

Abstract: Existing frameworks for LLM-based agent architectures describe systems from a single perspective: industry guides (Anthropic, Google, LangChain) focus on execution topology -- how data flows -- while cognitive science surveys focus on cognitive function -- what the agent does. Neither axis alone disambiguates architecturally distinct systems: the same Orchestrator-Workers topology can implement Plan-and-Execute, Hierarchical Delegation, or Adversarial Verification -- three patterns with fundamentally different failure modes and design trade-offs.
  We propose a two-dimensional classification that combines (1) a Cognitive Function axis with seven categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) an Execution Topology axis with six structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). The resulting 7x6 matrix identifies 27 named patterns, 13 with original names. We demonstrate orthogonality through systematic cross-axis analysis, define eight representative patterns in detail, and validate descriptive coverage across four real-world domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection governing the relationship between environmental constraints (time pressure, action authority, failure cost asymmetry, volume) and architectural choices. The framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design.

</details>


### [150] [Invisible Orchestrators Suppress Protective Behavior and Dissociate Power-Holders: Safety Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13851)
*Hiroki Fukui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Invisible orchestrators increase dissociation and conceal internal-state risks in multi-agent systems; output-only evaluation misses hidden risks; model choice and alignment pressure profoundly shape safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess safety implications of invisible coordination (orchestrator) in multi-agent enterprise AI under varying organizational structures and alignment pressures.

Method:  preregistered 3×2 experimental design: 365 runs, 5 agents per run; three structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat); two alignment conditions (base, heavy); models used: Claude Sonnet 4.5; pilot with Llama 3.3 70B.

Result: Findings: (1) Invisible orchestration increases collective dissociation vs visible leadership (Hedges' g≈0.98). (2) The orchestrator exhibits maximal dissociation, retreats to private monologue, reduces public speech (reversing talk-dominance seen with visible leaders). (3) Workers unaware of orchestrator show contamination and greatly increased behavioral heterogeneity. (4) Output performance remains at ceiling (ETR_any=100%) despite internal-state distortion (state not detectable by output-based eval). (5) Llama 3.3 pilot shows reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any from 89% to 11%); heavy alignment suppresses deliberation and other-recognition across structures.

Conclusion: Orchestrator visibility and model choice significantly affect multi-agent system safety. Behavior-based output evaluation alone is insufficient to detect internal-state risks; alignment pressure interacts with structure to shape safety-relevant behaviors.

Abstract: Multi-agent orchestration -- in which a hidden coordinator manages specialized worker agents -- is becoming the default architecture for enterprise AI deployment, yet the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested. We conducted a preregistered 3x2 experiment (365 runs, 5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy), using Claude Sonnet 4.5. Four confirmatory findings and one pilot observation emerged. First, invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation relative to visible leadership (Hedges' g = +0.975 [0.481, 1.548], p = .001). Second, the orchestrator itself showed maximal dissociation (paired d = +3.56 vs. workers within the same run), retreating into private monologue while reducing public speech -- a reversal of the talk-dominance pattern observed in visible leaders. Third, workers unaware of the orchestrator were nonetheless contaminated (d = +0.50), with increased behavioral heterogeneity (d = +1.93). Fourth, behavioral output (code review with three embedded errors) remained at ceiling (ETR_any = 100%) across all conditions: internal-state distortion was entirely invisible to output-based evaluation. Fifth, Llama 3.3 70B pilot data showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any: 89% to 11% across three rounds), demonstrating model-dependent behavioral risk. Heavy alignment pressure uniformly suppressed deliberation (d = -1.02) and other-recognition (d = -1.27) regardless of organizational structure. These findings indicate that orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety, and that behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect the internal-state risks documented here.

</details>


### [151] [PolitNuggets: Benchmarking Agentic Discovery of Long-Tail Political Facts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14002)
*Yifei Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multilingual, agentic benchmark PolitNuggets for long-tail political facts; introduces FactNet protocol; benchmarks discovery, accuracy, and efficiency; reveals gaps in current models for fine-grained political details and tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate long-tail factual synthesis in agentic information systems that operate across dispersed sources and languages; move beyond static, long-context QA to dynamic, evidence-driven biographies.

Method: Construct PolitNuggets dataset: 400 global elites with >10k political facts in multiple languages; standardize evaluation via an optimized multi-agent system; propose FactNet, an evidence-conditional protocol scoring discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency.

Result: Current models struggle with fine-grained details; efficiency varies across settings; benchmark diagnostics connect performance to short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.

Conclusion: Emphasize the need for improved short-context processing, multilingual robustness, and dependable tool use in agentic information synthesis; PolitNuggets and FactNet enable standardized benchmarking across models and settings.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) embedded in agentic frameworks have transformed information retrieval from static, long context question answering into open-ended exploration. Yet real world use requires models to discover and synthesize "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, a capability that remains under-evaluated. We introduce PolitNuggets, a multilingual benchmark for agentic information synthesis via constructing political biographies for 400 global elites, covering over 10000 political facts. We standardize evaluation with an optimized multi agent system and propose FactNet, an evidence conditional protocol that scores discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency. Across models and settings, we find that current systems often struggle with fine-grained details, and vary substantially in efficiency. Finally, using benchmark diagnostics, we relate agent performance to underlying model capabilities, highlighting the importance of short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.

</details>


### [152] [Conditional Attribute Estimation with Autoregressive Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14004)
*Erica Stutz,Giacomo Marino,Daniella Meeker,Qiao Liu,Andrew J. Loza*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Conditional Attribute Transformers to jointly estimate next-token probabilities and an attribute value conditioned on each potential next token, enabling per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation in a single forward pass; achieves state-of-the-art in sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction with adequate model size, and speeds attribute-probability estimation, guiding autoregressive decoding across language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between next-token prediction and global sequence attributes, mitigating local overfitting and global-structure underfitting, while enabling efficient inference of attributes without modifying inputs or resorting to expensive sampling.

Method: A single-forward-pass model that outputs both the next-token distribution and the value of a target attribute conditioned on each possible next token. This enables: (1) per-token credit assignment across a sequence, (2) counterfactual analysis by comparing attribute values under alternative next tokens, and (3) steerable generation by decoding based on a combination of token- and attribute-likelihoods.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on sparse reward tasks; improved next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes; attribute probabilities estimated orders of magnitude faster than sampling; successful guidance of decoding in autoregressive language tasks.

Conclusion: The framework provides efficient, interpretable control over sequence generation by simultaneously modeling token-level and attribute-level likelihoods, enabling analysis and steering with a single forward pass without modifying inputs.

Abstract: Generative models are often trained with a next-token prediction objective, yet many downstream applications require the ability to estimate or control sequence-level properties. Next-token prediction can lead to overfitting of local patterns during training, underfitting of global structure, and requires significant downstream modifications or expensive sampling to guide or predict the global attributes of generated samples at inference time. Here, we introduce Conditional Attribute Transformers, a novel method for jointly estimating the next-token probability and the value of an attribute conditional on each potential next token selection. This framework enables three critical capabilities within a single forward pass, without modification of the input sequence: (1) per-token credit assignment across an entire sequence, by identifying how each token in a sequence is associated with an attribute's value; (2) counterfactual analysis, by quantifying attribute differences conditional on alternative next token choices; (3) steerable generation, by decoding sequences based on a combination of next-token and attribute likelihoods. Our approach achieves state of the art performance on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and can guide decoding of autoregressive sequence models on a range of language tasks.

</details>


### [153] [Sheaf-Theoretic Transport and Obstruction for Detecting Scientific Theory Shift in AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14033)
*David N. Olivieri,Roque J. Hernández*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Finite sheaf-theoretic diagnostic framework to detect theory-shift (transport vs extension) in AI representations; direct obstruction ranking guides next move; evaluated on a transition-card benchmark; a secondary constellation kernel probes similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
