Motivation: Address the need for AI agents to determine when an existing representational framework can be transported to a new regime or must be extended; provide a finite, computable diagnostic subproblem for theory-shift.

Method: Organize contexts as a local-to-global structure with source, overlap, target, and validation charts that are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Compute obstruction measures: residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. Evaluate on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. Include a constellation kernel over the same signatures as a secondary representational-similarity probe.

Result: Direct obstruction ranking places the intended deformation or extension as the lowest-obstruction candidate; the benchmark separates transition types; the constellation kernel serves as a supplementary signal.

Conclusion: Proposes a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detect when transport fails and extension is the coherent next move, without attempting full autonomous theory invention or historical paradigm reconstruction.

Abstract: Scientific theory shift in AI agents requires more than fitting equations to data. An artificial scientific agent must detect whether an existing representational framework remains transportable into a new regime, or whether its language has become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended. This paper develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework for detecting theory-shift candidates through transport and obstruction. Contexts are organized as a local-to-global structure in which source, overlap, target, and validation charts are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Obstruction measures failure of coherence through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. We evaluate the framework on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. The main result is direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition type is separated in the benchmark. A constellation kernel over the same signatures is included only as a secondary representational-similarity probe. The aim is not to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention, but to isolate a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move.

</details>


### [154] [Enhanced and Efficient Reasoning in Large Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14036)
*Leslie G. Valiant*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-stage framework for trustworthy LLM reasoning: preprocessing text into a Unary Relational Integracode to make relations explicit, followed by conventional ML that learns and predicts these relations; supported by Robust Logic, it yields polynomial-time learnability of core world-relational rules and is compatible with existing hardware/software, with cross-domain applicability to vision and actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs today produce fluent prose but lack a principled, provable basis for trust in content. Prior attempts to inject principled reasoning are deemed too computationally expensive. The work aims to provide a tractable, principled reasoning method that can be integrated with current LLM stacks and hardware.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) preprocessing that recodes input data into a Unary Relational Integracode, explicitly encoding relationships among objects; (2) a standard (potentially streamlined) machine learning process trained to predict these relations. The approach also frames learning as building a world model and suggests extension beyond language to vision and actions, consolidating multiple object properties explicitly to enable robust logical chaining.

Result: The recoding makes a core subset of world-relational rules polynomial-time learnable (in a defined sense, with complexity tied to the rule), supporting sound reasoning within a single classifier invocation and across multiple calls. The method aims to provide robust, principled chaining under uncertainty (Robust Logic) and maintains compatibility with existing training pipelines while enabling efficient learning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a tractable, principled reasoning mechanism for LLMs that preserves most of the current software/hardware base and extends naturally to other domains, offering a foundation for sound, scalable relational reasoning and improved trust in generated content.

Abstract: In current Large Language Models we can trust the production of smoothly flowing prose on the basis of the principles of machine learning. However, there is no comparably principled basis to justify trust in the content of the text produced. It appears to be conventional wisdom that addressing this issue by adding more principled reasoning is not computationally affordable.
  Here we propose a principled method of reasoning that is efficient enough to be practical for large language models. Further, the method allows the retention of much of the currently used software and hardware base. Our method for improving the functioning of large language models consists of a first stage of preprocessing that recodes the data to a Unary Relational Integracode that is more explicit about the relationships among the objects described in the text, followed as a second stage by a standard but possibly streamlined machine learning process that then also learns to predict these relationships.
  The method may be viewed as realizing a world model and applying beyond natural language, to vision and actions, for example, where the multiple properties of an object referred to in an input are brought together explicitly, rather than remaining distributed in the various references to it in the input. We articulate its advantages in terms of Robust Logic, a system for performing principled chaining on learned, and hence uncertain, information. We show that this recoding has the surprising and fortuitous property that, while succinct, it makes the task of learning a core subset of relational rules that hold in the world described in the training data polynomial time learnable in a defined sense, the polynomial depending on the complexity of the rule. This gives support for sound reasoning within each single call of the learned classifier as well as between multiple calls.

</details>


### [155] [Model-Adaptive Tool Necessity Reveals the Knowing-Doing Gap in LLM Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14038)
*Yize Cheng,Chenrui Fan,Mahdi JafariRaviz,Keivan Rezaei,Soheil Feiz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines a model-adaptive notion of tool-necessity based on empirical performance, showing large gaps between when tools are needed and when tools are called across multiple models; identifies a cognition-to-action gap as the main source of mismatch in two-stage tool use (internal decision vs. execution).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why LLMs fail to translate tool-necessity recognition into tool calls and to diagnose the gap between model judgment of tool need and actual tool invocation, across model strengths and dataset types.

Method: 1) Propose a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity. 2) Compare model-predicted necessity with observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA datasets. 3) Probe hidden states to separate an internal cognition stage from an execution stage. 4) Analyze decodability of signals and trajectory in the two-stage process to locate the source of mismatch.

Result: Found substantial mismatches between necessity and tool use (26.5–54.0% for arithmetic; 30.8–41.8% for factual QA). Both cognition and execution signals are linearly decodable, but their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in late layers. Most mismatch arises during cognition-to-action translation rather than cognition itself, indicating a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use.

Conclusion: Improving tool-use reliability requires better recognition of when tools are needed and, crucially, better translation of that recognition into tool calls (addressing the knowing-doing gap).

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as autonomous agents that must decide when to answer directly vs. when to invoke external tools. Prior work studying adaptive tool use has largely treated tool necessity as a model-agnostic property, annotated by human or LLM judge, and mostly cover cases where the answer is obvious (e.g., fetching the weather vs. paraphrasing text). However, tool necessity in the wild is more nuanced due to the divergence of capability boundaries across models: a problem solvable by a strong model on its own may still require tools for a weaker one. In this work, we introduce a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity, grounded in each model's empirical performance. Following this definition, we compare the necessity against observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA dataset, and find substantial mismatches of 26.5-54.0% and 30.8-41.8%, respectively. To diagnose the failure, we decompose tool use into two stages: an internal cognition stage that reflects whether a model believes a tool is necessary, and an execution stage that determines whether the model actually makes a tool-call action. By probing the LLM hidden states, we find that both signals are often linearly decodable, yet their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime that drives the next-token action. By tracing the trajectory of samples in the two-stage process, we further discover that the majority of mismatch is concentrated in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself. These results reveal a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: improving tool-use reliability requires not only better recognition of when tools are needed, but also better translation of that recognition into action.

</details>


### [156] [Network-Aware Bilinear Tokenization for Brain Functional Connectivity Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14048)
*Leo Milecki,Qingyu Hu,Bahram Jafrasteh,Mert R. Sabuncu,Qingyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NERVE is a network-aware self-supervised framework for brain FC that tokenizes FC into intra- and inter-network blocks and uses structured bilinear factorization to embed patches, achieving better cross-cohort transferability with linear parameter scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MAE-style tokenization treats FC as structurally homogeneous, ignoring large-scale brain network organization, leading to less stable/transferable representations and higher parameter costs.

Method: Tokenize FC matrices into patches corresponding to intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks guided by anatomically grounded parcellation. Use a structured bilinear factorization to embed patches, preserving network identity and reducing parameter count from quadratic to linear in the number of networks.

Result: Evaluated on ABCD, PNC, and CCNP for behavior and psychopathology prediction. The network-aware approach yields more stable and transferable representations, especially in cross-cohort settings; ablations show that bilinear embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical.

Conclusion: Incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning improves functional connectomics representations and their cross-cohort generalization.

Abstract: Masked autoencoders (MAEs) have recently shown promise for self-supervised representation learning of resting-state brain functional connectivity (FC). However, a fundamental question remains unresolved: how should FC matrices be tokenized to align with the intrinsic modular organization of large-scale brain networks? Existing approaches typically adopt region-centric or graph-based schemes that treat FC as structurally homogeneous elements and overlook the large-scale network brain organization. We introduce NERVE (Network-Aware Representations of Brain Functional Connectivity via Bilinear Tokenization), a self-supervised learning framework that redefines FC tokenization by partitioning FC matrices into patches of intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks. Unlike image-based MAE, where fixed-size patches share a common tokenizer, FC patches defined by network pairs are heterogeneous in size and correspond to distinct functional roles. To resolve this problem, NERVE embeds FC patches through a novel structured bilinear factorization. This formulation preserves network identity and reduces parameter complexity from quadratic to linear scaling in the number of networks. We evaluate NERVE across three large-scale developmental cohorts (ABCD, PNC, and CCNP) for behavior and psychopathology prediction. Compared to structurally agnostic MAE variants and graph-based self-supervised baselines, the proposed network-aware formulation yields more stable and transferable representations, particularly in cross-cohort evaluation. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed bilinear network embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical for performance. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning for functional connectomics.

</details>


### [157] [Bridging Legal Interpretation and Formal Logic: Faithfulness, Assumption, and the Future of AI Legal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14049)
*Olivia Peiyu Wang,Leilani H. Gilpin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a neuro-symbolic framework to make AI-assisted legal reasoning trustworthy by combining LLMs with formal verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal practice requires rigorous, verifiable reasoning; current LLMs risk hallucinations and unsupported inferences, threatening accountability.

Method: Neuro-symbolic integration that uses LLMs for expressive reasoning while applying formal verification to ensure conclusions are grounded in source text, reducing reliance on unverified inferences.

Result: No empirical results are reported in the abstract; the goal is to present a feasible framework and argue for its ability to reduce manual verification while enhancing accountability.

Conclusion: A neuro-symbolic approach can provide capable and trustworthy AI in law, balancing automation with the accountability demanded by legal practice.

Abstract: The growing adoption of large language models in legal practice brings both significant promise and serious risk. Legal professionals stand to benefit from AI that can reason over contracts, draft documents, and analyze sources at scale, yet the high-stakes nature of legal work demands a level of rigor that current AI systems do not provide. The central problem is not simply that LLMs hallucinate facts and references; it is that they systematically draw inferences that go beyond what the source text actually supports, presenting assumption-laden conclusions as if they were logically grounded. This proposal presents a neuro-symbolic approach to legal AI that combines the expressive power of large language models with the rigor of formal verification, aiming to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy, thus reducing the burden of manual verification without sacrificing the accountability that legal practice demands.

</details>


### [158] [SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14051)
*Yusuke Ozaki,Dhaval Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPIN is a planning wrapper that enforces valid DAG-based plans and uses prefix-based execution to stop early, reducing task execution and tool usage while boosting success metrics across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM planners often generate structurally invalid or overly long workflows, causing brittle failures and unnecessary tool/API costs; a validated planning layer can improve reliability and efficiency.

Method: Introduce SPIN: a planning wrapper with DAG validation via _validate_plan_text and repair prompting; executable plans are produced before execution; prefixes of the DAG are evaluated incrementally to determine sufficiency. Evaluation on AssetOpsBench (261 scenarios) and MCP Bench assesses task counts, Accomplished score, tool calls, and grounding/dependency metrics across GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

Result: AssetOpsBench: executed tasks reduced 1061→623; Accomplished improved 0.638→0.706; tool calls reduced 11.81→6.82 per run. MCP Bench: improved planning, grounding, and dependency-related scores for GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

Conclusion: SPIN effectively enforces a strict DAG contract and employs prefix-based execution to curb unnecessary planning length and tool usage, yielding measurable improvements in efficiency and performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose \texttt{SPIN}, a planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. \texttt{SPIN} enforces a strict DAG contract through \texttt{\_validate\_plan\_text} and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, \texttt{SPIN} reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves \emph{Accomplished} from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

</details>


### [159] [Bad Seeing or Bad Thinking? Rewarding Perception for Vision-Language Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14054)
*Haozhe Wang,Qixin Xu,Changpeng Wang,Taofeng Xue,Chong Peng,Wenhu Chen,Fangzhen Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A reinforcement-learning framework (MoCA) for Vision-Language Models that decouples perception and reasoning, uses Perception Verification (PV) and Structured Verbal Verification (SVV) to reward perceptual fidelity, enabling modality credit assignment and improved performance across tasks with reduced external agentic complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Perception and reasoning are entangled in VLMs; when failures occur, it is unclear whether they stem from perception (bad seeing) or reasoning (bad thinking). This ambiguity harms efficient learning and limits gains from architectural or agentic approaches. A scalable, targeted supervision signal that emphasizes perception fidelity is needed to improve robustness while avoiding heavy external agents.

Method: Decouple generation into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. Introduce Perception Verification (PV) using a blindfolded reasoning proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. Replace high-variance LLM judgments with Structured Verbal Verification (SVV), an algorithmic, structured verification process. Integrate these into Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) to route rewards to either bad seeing or bad thinking, enabling a single VLM to improve across a wide task spectrum.

Result: Claimed improvements in perception–reasoning synergy with scalable training that yields simultaneous performance gains across a broad suite of VL tasks, while reducing external agentic complexity and heavy engineering burden.

Conclusion: Decoupling perception and reasoning with targeted perceptual supervision (PV) and structured verification (SVV) under MoCA addresses modality credit assignment bottlenecks, enabling a more robust and scalable VLM capable of improved performance across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Achieving robust perception-reasoning synergy is a central goal for advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Recent advancements have pursued this goal via architectural designs or agentic workflows. However, these approaches are often limited by static textual reasoning or complicated by the significant compute and engineering burden of external agentic complexity. Worse, this heavy investment does not yield proportional gains, often witnessing a "seesaw effect" on perception and reasoning. This motivates a fundamental rethinking of the true bottleneck. In this paper, we argue that the root cause of this trade-off is an ambiguity in modality credit assignment: when a VLM fails, is it due to flawed perception ("bad seeing") or flawed logic ("bad thinking")? To resolve this, we introduce a reinforcement learning framework that improves perception-reasoning synergy by reliably rewarding the perception fidelity. We explicitly decompose the generation process into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. This decoupling enables targeted supervision on perception. Crucially, we introduce Perception Verification (PV), leveraging a "blindfolded reasoning" proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. Furthermore, to scale training across free-form VL tasks, we propose Structured Verbal Verification, which replaces high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution. These techniques are integrated into a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) mechanism, which routes rewards to the specific source of error -- either bad seeing or bad thinking -- enabling a single VLM to achieve simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum.

</details>


### [160] [MathAtlas: A Benchmark for Autoformalization in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14061)
*Nilay Patel,Noah Arias,Davit Babayan,Victoria Cochran,Timothy Libman,Hafsah Mahmood,Liam McCarty,Soli Munoz,Laurel Willey,Jeffrey Flanigan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new large-scale graduate-level autoformalization benchmark, MathAtlas, with ~52k statements and a 178k-dependency graph from 103 textbooks; aims to spur dependency-aware autoformalization research on challenging, real-world math.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graduate-level mathematics is underrepresented in autoformalization benchmarks. A large, in-the-wild dataset with dependency structure enables evaluating and guiding development of models that handle complex, interconnected mathematical knowledge.

Method: Assembled MathAtlas from 103 graduate math textbooks, extracting ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs; constructed a mathematical dependency graph of ~178k relations; defined MA-Hard as the deepest-dependency subset (700 entities) for challenging evaluation; evaluated state-of-the-art autoformalization models on the dataset.

Result: The dataset is high quality but extremely challenging: strong baselines reach at most 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions; for MA-Hard, best model attains only 2.6% correctness, indicating performance degrades with dependency depth.

Conclusion: MathAtlas provides the first large-scale benchmark for graduate-level autoformalization with a dependency graph, enabling evaluation of dependency-aware systems and providing a resource for advancing research in wild, high-complexity mathematics.

Abstract: Current autoformalization benchmarks are largely focused on olympiad or undergraduate mathematics, while graduate and research-level mathematics remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce MathAtlas, the first large-scale autoformalization benchmark of in the wild graduate-level mathematics, containing ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs extracted from 103 graduate mathematics textbooks. MathAtlas is enriched with a mathematical dependency graph containing ~178k relations, and is the first autoformalization benchmark to include such relations, facilitating evaluation and development of dependency-aware autoformalization systems. Our extensive experiments show that MathAtlas is high quality but extremely challenging: strong baselines achieve at most 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions. Furthermore, we find performance of state-of-the-art models degrades substantially with dependency depth: on MA-Hard, a subset of 700 entities with the deepest dependency trees, the best model achieves only 2.6% correctness for autoformalization on this challenging dataset. We release MathAtlas to the community as a benchmark set for large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics in the wild.

</details>


### [161] [Know When To Fold 'Em: Token-Efficient LLM Synthetic Data Generation via Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14062)
*Anjir Ahmed Chowdhury,Syed Zawad,Feng Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MSIFR: multi-stage in-flight rejection to reduce token waste in LLM synthetic data generation without training, via stage-wise validators and sequential decision process; promising token savings (11-77% standalone, up to 78.2% with early-exit) while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce wasted tokens from full-output generation by early rejection of low-quality trajectories to improve efficiency in LLM-based data generation.

Method: Decomposes generation into sequential stages; applies fast rule-based validators to detect arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations; formalizes in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process; proves that any non-trivial discard policy reduces expected token consumption; shows conditional utility estimates form a martingale; evaluates across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks.

Result: Token consumption reduced by 11-77% as a standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy.

Conclusion: MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes; compatible with other early-exit strategies and applicable across models and benchmarks.

Abstract: While synthetic data generation with large language models (LLMs) is widely used in post-training pipelines, existing approaches typically generate full outputs before applying quality filters, leading to substantial token waste on samples that are ultimately discarded. To address this, we propose Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection (MSIFR), a lightweight, training-free framework that detects and terminates low-quality generation trajectories at intermediate checkpoints before they reach full completion. MSIFR decomposes the generation process into sequential stages and applies fast rule-based validators to identify arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations, enabling early rejection of faulty samples. We formalize in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process and show that any non-trivial discard policy reduces expected token consumption, with stage-wise savings increasing when rejection occurs earlier in the generation pipeline. We further demonstrate that conditional utility estimates form a martingale, ensuring that early, in-flight rejection does not bias the expected utility of retained samples. Across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks, MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11%-77% as a standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy. These results confirm that MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes.

</details>


### [162] [SkillFlow: Flow-Driven Recursive Skill Evolution for Agentic Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14089)
*Mingda Zhang,Tiesunlong Shen,Haoran Luo,Wenjin Liu,Zikai Xiao,Erik Cambria,Xiaoying Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillFlow presents a flow-based agent orchestration framework using a trainable Supervisor and a structured environment with a dynamic skill library, optimizing via Tempered Trajectory Balance to stabilize diverse orchestration strategies and enable transparent credit assignment, plus a recursive skill-evolution loop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome core limitations in LLM-based orchestration: (1) strategy collapse under reward maximization leading to mode collapse; (2) high gradient variance and opaque credit assignment; (3) unguided skill evolution relying on direct prompting rather than principled training signals.

Method: Propose SkillFlow, a flow-based framework with a trainable Supervisor, a structured environment (dynamic skill library, frozen executor), and multi-turn interaction. Introduce Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward to preserve diversity. Derive a jointly learned backward policy for per-step credit assignment with zero extra inference cost. Implement recursive skill evolution to decide when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and identify decision gaps.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 14 datasets shows SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines on question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks.

Conclusion: SkillFlow delivers diverse, well-credited orchestration strategies through a principled flow-based training signal, enabling automated capability growth via recursive skill evolution and achieving strong cross-domain performance.

Abstract: In recent years, a variety of powerful LLM-based agentic systems have been applied to automate complex tasks through task orchestration. However, existing orchestration methods still face key challenges, including strategy collapse under reward maximization, high gradient variance with opaque credit assignment, and unguided skill evolution whose decisions are typically made by directly prompting an LLM to judge rather than derived from principled training signals. To address these challenges, we propose SkillFlow, a flow-based framework that takes a trainable Supervisor as the agent and a structured environment with dynamic skill library and frozen executor, automating task orchestration through multi-turn interaction. SkillFlow employs Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward, preserving diverse orchestration strategies rather than collapsing to a single mode. The same flow objective yields a jointly learned backward policy that provides transparent per-step credit assignment at zero additional inference cost. Building on these flow diagnostics, a recursive skill evolution mechanism determines when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and where decision gaps lie -- closing the loop from training signal to autonomous capability growth. Experimental results on 14 datasets show that SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines across question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SkillFlow-E850.

</details>


### [163] [ChromaFlow: A Negative Ablation Study of Orchestration Overhead in Tool-Augmented Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14102)
*Tarun Mittal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analyzes ChromaFlow, finds that more aggressive orchestration increased noise and did not improve accuracy; emphasizes gated evaluation criteria for reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliability and failure modes of tool-augmented autonomous agents beyond final accuracy, using a controlled GAIA Level-1 evaluation.

Method: ChromaFlow with planner-directed execution, specialized tools, and telemetry. Compared a frozen Level-1 baseline (29/53, 54.72%), a recover configuration with more orchestration (27/53, 50.94%), and two randomized 20-task smoke evaluations (12/20 and 11/20).

Result: Baseline accuracy 54.72%; recovery accuracy 50.94% with increased tracebacks, timeouts, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log cost estimates. Small-sample smoke evaluations showed instability (12/20, 11/20). Negative ablation: more aggressive orchestration did not improve full-set performance and raised operational noise.

Conclusion: Bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates are essential first-order requirements for reliable autonomous agent evaluation.

Abstract: Autonomous language-model agents increasingly combine planning, tool use, document processing, browsing, code execution, and verification loops. These capabilities make agent systems more useful, but they also introduce operational failure modes that are not visible from final accuracy alone. This report presents ChromaFlow, a tool-augmented autonomous reasoning framework built around planner-directed execution, specialized tool use, and telemetry-driven evaluation. We analyze ChromaFlow on GAIA 2023 Level-1 validation tasks under clean evaluation constraints. A frozen full Level-1 baseline achieved 29/53 correct answers, or 54.72%. A later recovery configuration with expanded orchestration achieved 27/53 correct answers, or 50.94%, while increasing tracebacks, timeout events, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log cost estimates. Two randomized 20-task smoke evaluations produced 12/20 and 11/20 correct answers, showing that small diagnostic gains can be unstable across samples. The central result is therefore a negative ablation: more aggressive orchestration did not improve full-set performance and increased operational noise. The report argues that bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates should be treated as first-order requirements for reliable autonomous agent evaluation.

</details>


### [164] [Modeling Bounded Rationality in Drug Shortage Pharmacists Using Attention-Guided Dynamic Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14111)
*Yaniv Eliyahu Amiri,Noah Chicoine,Jacqueline Griffin,Stacy Marsella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An attention-guided bounded-rational framework with Expert and Learner agents improves hospital drug-shortage decisions by focusing cognitive effort on a subset of drugs, achieving stable performance without full state reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high-stakes drug-shortage decisions under uncertainty, time pressure, and patient risk. Interviews show pharmacists fix attention on a small, urgent drug subset; formalize a mechanism to allocate cognitive effort.

Method: Formalize a bounded-rational, attention-guided decision framework that partitions drugs into high-cost (deep reasoning) and low-cost (monitoring) subsets. Implement an Expert Agent using interview-derived attention weights and a Learner Agent that adapts attention with experience. Evaluate across simulated scenarios from short to long horizons.

Result: Attention-guided planning yields stable decision-making without requiring complete state reasoning. The primary decision shifts from choosing an action to selecting where to allocate cognitive effort. Attention-guided satisficing reduces problem complexity while maintaining performance across scenarios.

Conclusion: Allocating cognitive attention, rather than actions, is central to effective decision-making in drug shortage scenarios; attention-guided, satisficing strategies can simplify complex problems while preserving stability.

Abstract: Hospital pharmacists make high-stakes decisions to mitigate drug shortages under uncertainty, time pressure, and patient risk. Interviews revealed that pharmacists focus attention on a small subset of drugs, limiting cognitive effort to the most urgent cases. Motivated by these findings, we formalize a bounded-rational, attention-guided decision framework that dynamically decomposes drugs into a subset for high-cost reasoning and a complementary subset for low-cost monitoring. We develop two agents: an Expert Agent that applies attention weights derived from pharmacist interviews, and a Learner Agent that adapts attention allocation over time through experience. Across simulated scenarios spanning short to long horizons, we show that attention-guided planning supports stable decision-making without complete state reasoning. These results suggest that a primary decision is not what action to take, but where to allocate cognitive effort, and that attention-guided, satisficing strategies can reduce problem complexity while maintaining stable performance.

</details>


### [165] [ClawForge: Generating Executable Interactive Benchmarks for Command-Line Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14133)
*Yuxiang Lai,Peng Xia,Haonian Ji,Kaiwen Xiong,Kaide Zeng,Jiaqi Liu,Fang Wu,Jike Zhong,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClawForge is a generator-backed benchmark framework for executing CLI workflows under state conflicts, evaluating agents with persistent state-aware tests and stepwise validation; initial results show limited strict accuracy, substantial sensitivity to whether agents inspect existing state, and distinct failure styles under state conflict.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the tension between scalable benchmark construction and realistic workflow evaluation by testing agents on pre-existing partial, stale, or conflicting artifacts rather than clean starting states.

Method: Generates executable workflow benchmarks by compiling scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specs. Evaluation is stepwise over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching. ClawForge-Bench comprises 17 scenarios across 6 ability categories, evaluated on seven frontier models.

Result: Best model achieves 45.3% strict accuracy; wrong-state replacement stays below 17% across models; model separation ranges from 17% to 90%, driven by whether agents inspect existing state first. Analyses show many failures are near-miss closures, not early breakdowns, and failure styles vary with state conflict.

Conclusion: State conflict critically shapes interactive-agent performance; evaluating with persistent state and state-awareness reveals distinct failure modes and room for improvement in agent design and benchmarking approaches.

Abstract: Interactive agent benchmarks face a tension between scalable construction and realistic workflow evaluation. Hand-authored tasks are expensive to extend and revise, while static prompt evaluation misses failures that only appear when agents operate over persistent state. Existing interactive benchmarks have advanced agent evaluation significantly, but most initialize tasks from clean state and do not systematically test how agents handle pre-existing partial, stale, or conflicting artifacts. We present \textbf{ClawForge}, a generator-backed benchmark framework for executable command-line workflows under state conflict. The framework compiles scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specifications, and evaluates agents step by step over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching. We instantiate this framework as the ClawForge-Bench (17 scenarios, 6 ability categories). Results across seven frontier models show that the best model reaches only 45.3% strict accuracy, wrong-state replacement remains below 17\% for all models, and the widest model separation (17% to 90%) is driven by whether agents inspect existing state before acting. Partial-credit and step-efficiency analyses further reveal that many failures are near-miss closures rather than early breakdowns, and that models exhibit qualitatively different failure styles under state conflict.

</details>


### [166] [Distribution-Aware Algorithm Design with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14141)
*Saharsh Koganti,Priyadarsi Mishra,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Tomer Galanti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Learning executable solver code via solver hints; empirical fastest sample-consistent solvers generalize in correctness and runtime; can recover identifiable hints from polynomial samples; large empirical speedups across 21 distributions and 7 problem classes using LLM-based code agents; on Dominating Set instances, solvers are valid on all graphs and far faster than competitors by ~100x–300x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many tasks, the deployed artifact is executable solver code where runtime matters alongside correctness. Traditional ML solvers optimize for solution quality, not execution speed on deployment, and lack mechanisms to incorporate problem structure into specialized solver code. The work aims to learn and compile specialized solver code from data to jointly optimize correctness and runtime.

Method: Introduce 'solver hints' as reusable, inferred structures compiled into specialized solver code. Show that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime. Prove that identifiable hints can be recovered from polynomially many samples. Empirically instantiate with LLM code agents across 21 structured combinatorial-optimization tasks over 7 problem classes. Compare synthesized solvers against heuristic pools, Gurobi, and a time-limited exact backend on multiple distributions. Measure solution quality and execution time; highlight that many gains come from switching to distribution-specific, compiled computation rather than general-purpose search.

Result: Solvers achieve mean normalized quality 0.971. Improvements: +0.224 over average heuristic pool; +0.098 over best heuristic. Speedups: 336.9x over quality-best heuristic; 342.8x over Gurobi; 16.1x over time-limited exact backend. On released PACE 2025 Dominating Set private instances, solvers are valid on all 100 graphs and run about two orders of magnitude faster than top competition solvers, with a moderate quality gap. Analysis shows gains largely from replacing ambient exponential search with distribution-specific, compiled computation.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that executable solvers can be learned and compiled from data to jointly optimize quality and runtime, with generalization guarantees for the fastest sample-consistent solver and identifiability of solver hints from polynomial samples. It suggests a viable path for distribution-aware solver design using LLM-based code agents and emphasizes the performance benefits of problem-specific computation over general-purpose optimization. Potential extensions include broader problem domains, understanding robustness under distribution shifts, interpretability of hints, and practical considerations around library construction and deployment.

Abstract: We study learning when the learned object is executable solver code rather than a predictor. In this setting, correctness is not enough: two solvers may both return valid solutions on the deployment distribution while differing substantially in runtime. Given samples from an unknown task distribution, the learner returns code evaluated on fresh instances by both solution quality and execution time. Our central abstraction is a \emph{solver hint}: reusable structure inferred from samples and compiled into specialized solver code. We prove that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and that statistically identifiable hints can be recovered and compiled from polynomially many samples.
  Empirically, we instantiate the framework with LLM code agents on \(21\) structured combinatorial-optimization target distributions across seven problem classes. The synthesized solvers reach mean normalized quality \(0.971\), improve by \(+0.224\) over the average heuristic pool and by \(+0.098\) over the highest-quality heuristic, and are \(336.9\times\), \(342.8\times\), and \(16.1\times\) faster than the quality-best heuristic, Gurobi, and the selected time-limited exact backend, respectively. On released PACE 2025 Dominating Set private instances, the synthesized solver is valid on all \(100\) graphs and runs about two orders of magnitude faster than top competition solvers, with a moderate quality gap. Inspection shows that many gains come from changing the computational scale: replacing ambient exponential search or general-purpose optimization with compiled distribution-specific computation.

</details>


### [167] [Agentic Systems as Boosting Weak Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14163)
*Varun Sunkaraneni,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Riccardo Neumarker,Tomaso Poggio,Tomer Galanti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Verifier-backed committee search can boost reasoning performance of weak models by leveraging sampling to expose latent correct solutions; however, effective amplification requires local soundness signals (e.g., execution, checking). Theoretical bounds describe when errors accumulate, and empirical results on SWE-bench Verified show near-oracle performance with a small nano model when using a critic–comparator setup, with coverage remaining a dominant challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether a committee of weaker reasoning-model calls can match stronger models at inference time, and to formalize the mechanisms by which sampling, identifiability, progress, and diversity contribute to performance gains.

Method: Formal framework separating proposal coverage, local identifiability, progress, and diversity; theoretical results showing coverage can be amplified by sampling but needs local soundness signals; rank-based bounds on error composition; analysis of the proposer-side ceiling; empirical evaluation on SWE-bench Verified with GPT-5.4 nano proposals and critic–comparator orchestration across k proposals.

Result: Coverage amplification via repeated sampling is possible, but not by itself; reliable improvement requires local signals. The proposer’s best-of-k ceiling converges only to the portion of tasks with nonzero useful probability. Empirically, a single GPT-5.4 nano proposal solves 67.0% of tasks; with a critic–comparator setup at k=8, 76.4% are solved, matching the standalone performance of Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5, and approaching the oracle best-of-8 bound of 79.0%. The remaining failures are largely proposal-coverage failures.

Conclusion: Weak-model proposal pools contain many correct patches, but the main challenge is selecting them. Strengthened selection alone cannot close shared blind spots; improving coverage requires stronger local-verification signals and diverse proposal generation.

Abstract: Can a committee of weak reasoning-model calls reach the performance of much stronger models? We study verifier-backed committee search as inference-time boosting for reasoning language models. The mechanism is not simply that ``more agents help'': samples expose latent correct solutions, while critics and comparators must recover them without access to the hidden verifier. We formalize this view by separating proposal coverage, local identifiability, progress, and diversity. We prove that coverage can be amplified by repeated sampling, but cannot by itself create useful critics or comparators; reliable amplification requires an additional local soundness signal, such as execution, proof checking, type checking, tests, or constraint solving. We give rank-based bounds showing when local selection errors compose into reliable trajectories, and characterize the proposer-side ceiling: oracle best-of-\(k\) converges only to the mass of task slices on which the proposal system assigns nonzero useful probability. Empirically, on SWE-bench Verified, a single \texttt{GPT-5.4 nano} proposal solves \(67.0\%\) of tasks. Using the same nano model, our critic--comparator orchestration reaches \(76.4\%\) with \(k=8\) proposals, matching the standalone performance of \texttt{Gemini 3 Pro} and \texttt{Claude Opus 4.5} Thinking and approaching the \(79.0\%\) oracle best-of-\(8\) upper bound. Thus, many correct patches are already present in weak-model proposal pools; the main challenge is selecting them. The remaining failures are mostly proposal-coverage failures, indicating shared blind spots that stronger selection alone cannot close.

</details>


### [168] [Unsteady Metrics and Benchmarking Cultures of AI Model Builders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14164)
*Stefan Baack,Christo Buschek,Maty Bohacek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benchmarking-Cultures-25 analyzes how AI model benchmarks are highlighted in 2025 releases, revealing fragmentation and narrative framing over standardized measurement; introduces a dataset and taxonomy to map benchmark terminology to measured signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how model builders select and describe benchmarks in press/blog communications, and how these choices shape perceptions of progress and state of the art.

Method: Assembles Benchmarking-Cultures-25: 231 benchmarks across 139 model releases from 11 builders in 2025; open-source dataset and an interactive exploration tool; develops a unified taxonomy mapping diverse terminologies to a shared framework of measured signals; qualitative analysis of narrative claims.

Result: Finds fragmented evaluation landscape: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks are used by a single builder; 38.5% appear in only one release. Few benchmarks achieve widespread use (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025). Different builders attribute different competencies to the same benchmarks; many benchmarks emphasize progress toward AGI and focus on STEM/math, often de-emphasizing construct validity.

Conclusion: Highlighted benchmarks function more as flexible narrative devices and market-positioning tools than standardized measurement instruments; the work provides a taxonomy and dataset to enable cross-model analysis and transparency in benchmarking practices.

Abstract: The primary way to establish and compare competencies in foundation and generative AI models has shifted from peer-reviewed literature to press releases and company blog posts, where model builders highlight results on selected benchmarks. These artifacts now largely define the state of the art for researchers and the public. Despite their prominence, which benchmarks model builders choose to highlight, and what they communicate through this selection, is underexamined. To investigate, we introduce and open-source Benchmarking-Cultures-25, a dataset of 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 model releases in 2025 from 11 major AI builders, alongside an interactive tool to explore the data. Our analysis reveals a fragmented evaluation landscape with limited cross-model comparability: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks are used by a single builder, and 38.5% appear in just one release. Few achieve widespread use (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025). Moreover, benchmarks are attributed different competencies by different builders, depending on their narrative. To disentangle these conflicting presentations, we develop a unified taxonomy mapping diverging terminology to a shared framework of measured signals based on what benchmark authors claim to measure. "General knowledge application" is the second most popular, yet vaguely defined, category. Qualitative analysis shows many such benchmarks deemphasize construct validity, instead framing results as indicators of progress toward AGI. Their authors claim to measure knowledge or reasoning broadly, yet mostly evaluate STEM subjects (especially math). We argue that highlighted benchmarks function less as standardized measurement tools and more as flexible narrative devices prioritizing market positioning over scientific evaluation. Data: https://hf.co/datasets/matybohacek/benchmarking-cultures-25; tool: https://bench-cultures.net.

</details>


### [169] [The Evaluation Trap: Benchmark Design as Theoretical Commitment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14167)
*Theodore J Kalaitzidis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Epistematics, a meta-evaluative framework to audit AI benchmarks by deriving evaluation criteria from claimed technical capabilities and testing whether benchmarks truly discriminate those capabilities from proxies; demonstrated via an audit of Dupoux et al. (2026) showing how benchmarks can entrench existing assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarks often encode unexamined commitments that stabilize a dominant paradigm. A meta-evaluative method is needed to ensure benchmarks measure genuine capabilities rather than proxy behaviors, preventing self-reinforcing assessments.

Method: Introduce Epistematics: an audit procedure, a failure-mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria. Apply the procedure to a worked audit of Dupoux et al. (2026) to show how proposed benchmarks may reproduce architectural-level assumptions in evaluation criteria.

Result: A worked audit demonstrates that the Dupoux et al. proposal reproduces dominant paradigm assumptions within its evaluation criteria, thereby entrenching the constraint it seeks to overcome and illustrating the risk of evaluation criteria failing to discriminate target capabilities from proxies.

Conclusion: Epistematics provides a meta-evaluative toolkit to derive coherent, capability-aligned benchmarks, offering audit procedures and criteria to assess evaluation coherence and to reveal when benchmarks may obscure true capabilities by embedding proxy reasoning.

Abstract: Every AI benchmark operationalizes theoretical assumptions about the capability it claims to assess. When assumptions function as unexamined commitments, benchmarks stabilize the dominant paradigm by narrowing what counts as progress. Over time, narrow evaluation reorganizes capability concepts: architectures and definitions are selected for benchmark legibility until evaluation ceases to track an independent object and instead produces a version of the target defined by its own operational assumptions. The result is a trap: evaluation frameworks treat self-reinforcing assessments as valid, both creating and obscuring structural limits on what the current paradigm can accomplish. We introduce Epistematics, a methodology for deriving evaluation criteria directly from technical capability claims and auditing whether proposed benchmarks can discriminate the claimed capability from proxy behaviors. The contribution is meta-evaluative: an audit procedure, a failure mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria for evaluating capability-evaluation coherence. We demonstrate the procedure through a worked audit of Dupoux et al. (2026), a proposal that revises the dominant paradigm's theoretical assumptions at the architectural level while reproducing them in its evaluation criteria, thereby entrenching the constraint it seeks to overcome in a form the evaluation cannot detect.

</details>


### [170] [Grounded Continuation: A Linear-Time Runtime Verifier for LLM Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14175)
*Qisong He,Yi Dong,Xiaowei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A runtime verifier with a dependency-graph backbone enforces that LLM continuations in long dialogs have explicit support from evidence, achieving formal soundness and improved accuracy over baselines with low per-turn cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long conversations risk context drift where plausible continuations rely on abandoned premises. Existing retrieval-based or hallucination-robust approaches lack explicit guarantees of support. The work aims to close the gap with a runtime verifier that tracks evidence-support dependencies and detects unsupported continuations.

Method: Maintain a dependency graph mapping claims to evidence. Classify each turn into one of 8 update operations spanning four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation). A symbolic engine records dependencies. Verifying a continuation reduces to a graph walk; retraction propagates to identify support loss; per-turn cost is linear and guarantees conflict-free soundness.

Result: Empirical evaluation across datasets: LongMemEval-KU (n=78) shows 89.7% accuracy vs LLM baseline 88.5% (+1.3pp) and transcript-RAG baseline 87.2% (+2.6pp); correct abstentions in disagreements where baselines confabulate. LoCoMo QA items show competitive performance with retrieval-augmented baselines. A 15-item stale-premise subset yields 100% accuracy vs 93.3% baseline (+6.7pp). Retraction check runs in microseconds; history-replay scales linearly with conversation length. Across four LLM families, it demonstrates a soundness-faithfulness decomposition and a clear empirical question about LLM extraction faithfulness.

Conclusion: The approach provides a formal, efficient runtime verification with a sound structural check and an empirical measurement of LLM extraction faithfulness. It achieves strong defense against context-manipulation with pragmatic per-turn costs and competitive or superior accuracy against retrieval-based baselines.

Abstract: In long conversations, an LLM can produce a next utterance that sounds plausible but rests on premises the conversation has already abandoned. Context-manipulation attacks against deployed agents now actively exploit this gap. We close it with a runtime verifier that maintains an explicit dependency graph: an LLM classifies each turn into one of 8 update operations drawn from four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation), and a symbolic engine records which claims depend on which evidence. Checking whether a continuation is supported reduces to a graph walk; retraction propagates through the same graph to flag exactly the conclusions that lose support, with linear per-turn cost and a formal conflict-free guarantee. On LongMemEval-KU oracle (n=78), the verifier reaches 89.7% accuracy vs. 88.5% for the LLM-only baseline (+1.3pp) and 87.2% for a transcript-RAG baseline matched on retrieval budget (+2.6pp); wins among disagreements are correct abstentions where the baseline confabulates. On LoCoMo's 60 official QA items the verifier is competitive with retrieval-augmented baselines. Beyond external benchmarks, we construct two multi-agent scenarios and a 50-item grounding test: on the 15-item stale-premise subset, the verifier reaches 100% accuracy vs. 93.3% (+6.7pp). These instantiate a soundness-faithfulness decomposition: the structural check is sound by construction, and per-deployment LLM extraction faithfulness is the empirical question we measure across four LLM families. The retraction check plateaus at microseconds while history-replay grows linearly with conversation length.

</details>


### [171] [SimPersona: Learning Discrete Buyer Personas from Raw Clickstreams for Grounded E-Commerce Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14205)
*Zahra Zanjani Foumani,Alberto Castelo,Shuang Xie,Ted Chaiwachirasak,Han Li,Lingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimPersona learns discrete buyer types from historical traffic using a behavior-aware VQ-VAE, maps each type to a persona token in an LLM-based web agent, and uses population-aware sampling to simulate heterogeneous buyers. It achieves 78% conversion-rate alignment across 8.37M buyers over 42 storefronts and outperforms a larger baseline, with an open-source data pipeline released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Capture, represent, and scale heterogeneous buyer behavior in live storefronts. Hand-crafted personas are brittle and cannot faithfully reflect population-level distributions; a scalable, data-driven approach is needed to enable realistic, population-aware LLM-based agents.

Method: Train a behavior-aware VQ-VAE on raw clickstreams to learn a discrete buyer-type codebook. Map each buyer type to a dedicated persona token in the LLM agent's vocabulary and fine-tune the agent with these tokens on real browsing traces. At inference, assign a buyer type via a single encoder forward pass. For population-level simulation, sample buyer types from each merchant’s empirical distribution over the codebook and instantiate agents with the corresponding persona tokens. Evaluate on 8.37M buyers across 42 hold-out storefronts.

Result: Achieves 78% conversion-rate alignment with real buyers; shows interpretable variation across buyer types; outperforms a larger-baseline (8x more parameters) on goal-oriented shopping tasks.

Conclusion: SimPersona provides scalable, interpretable, and population-aware LLM-based web agents that reflect merchant-specific buyer distributions, with an open-source data pipeline to convert raw event logs into buyer representations and agent-training traces.

Abstract: LLM-based web agents can navigate live storefronts, yet they often collapse to a single "average buyer" policy, failing to capture the heterogeneous and distributional nature of real buyer populations. Existing personalization methods rely on hand-crafted prompt-based personas that are brittle, difficult to scale, context-inefficient, and unable to faithfully represent population-level behavior. We introduce SimPersona, a novel framework that learns discrete buyer types from historical traffic and exposes them to LLM-based web agents as compact persona tokens. Given raw clickstreams, a behavior-aware VQ-VAE induces a discrete buyer-type space that captures the statistical structure of real buyer behavior and merchant-specific buyer population distributions. To provide behavior-specific guidance to LLM-based web agents, SimPersona maps each learned buyer type to a dedicated persona token in the LLM agent vocabulary and fine-tunes the agent with these tokens on real browsing traces. At inference, each synthetic buyer is assigned to a learned buyer type with a single encoder forward pass, requiring no retraining or store-specific prompt engineering. For population-level simulation, SimPersona samples buyer types from each merchant's empirical distribution over the learned VQ-VAE codebook and instantiates agents with the corresponding persona tokens, preserving merchant-specific buyer population distributions. Evaluated on $8.37$M buyers across $42$ held-out live storefronts, SimPersona achieves $78\%$ conversion-rate alignment with real buyers, exhibits interpretable behavioral variation across buyer types, and outperforms a baseline with $8\times$ more parameters on goal-oriented shopping tasks. We further release an open-source data pipeline that converts raw e-commerce event logs into buyer representations and agent-training traces.

</details>


### [172] [ASH: Agents that Self-Hone via Embodied Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14211)
*Benjamin Schneider,Xavier Schneider,Victor Zhong,Sun Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Long-horizon embodied tasks remain a fundamental challenge in AI, as current methods rely on hand-engineered rewards or action-labeled demonstrations, neither of which scales. We introduce ASH, an agentic system that learns an embodied policy from unlabeled, noisy internet video, without reward shaping or expert annotation. ASH follows a self-improvement loop; when it gets stuck, ASH learns an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) from its own trajectories, and uses its IDM to extract supervision from relevant internet video. ASH uses unsupervised learning to identify key moments from large-scale internet video and retains them as long-term memory -- allowing it to tackle long-horizon problems. We evaluate ASH on two complementary environments demanding multi-hour planning: Pokemon Emerald, a turn-based RPG, and The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap, a real-time action-adventure game. In both games, behavioral cloning, retrieval-augmented and zero-shot foundation-model baselines plateau, while ASH sustains progression across our 8-hour evaluation. ASH reaches an average of $11.2/12$ milestones in Pokemon Emerald and $9.9/12$ in Legend of Zelda, while the strongest baseline gets stuck in both environments at an average of $6.5/12$ and $6.0/12$ milestones, respectively. We demonstrate that self-improving agents are a scalable recipe for long-horizon embodied learning.

</details>


### [173] [MetaAgent-X : Breaking the Ceiling of Automatic Multi-Agent Systems via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14212)
*Yaolun Zhang,Yujie Zhao,Nan Wang,Yiran Wu,Jiayu Chang,Yizhao Chen,Qingyun Wu,Jishen Zhao,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: End-to-end reinforcement learning framework MetaAgent-X jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution, leveraging script-based generation, hierarchical rollout, and stagewise co-evolution to achieve end-to-end self-designing and self-executing agents with substantial gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automatic MAS approaches are only partially adaptive, either using training-free test-time search or optimizing only the designer while keeping executors fixed, creating a frozen-executor ceiling and leaving end-to-end training of self-designing/self-executing models unexplored.

Method: MetaAgent-X enables script-based MAS generation, execution rollout collection, and credit assignment for designer and executor trajectories. It introduces Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout and Stagewise Co-evolution to stabilize training and reveal designer-executor dynamics.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows MetaAgent-X consistently outperforms automatic MAS baselines with up to 21.7% gains. Ablations indicate both designer and executor improve during training, and that learning follows a stagewise co-evolution process.

Conclusion: End-to-end trainable automatic MAS is feasible and practical for building self-designing and self-executing agentic models, surpassing existing partial-adaptation approaches.

Abstract: Automatic multi-agent systems aim to instantiate agent workflows without relying on manually designed or fixed orchestration. However, existing automatic MAS approaches remain only partially adaptive: they either perform training-free test-time search or optimize the meta-level designer while keeping downstream execution agents frozen, which creating a frozen-executor ceiling and leaving the end-to-end training of self-designing and self-executing agentic models unexplored. To address this, we introduce MetaAgent-X, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution. MetaAgent-X enables script-based MAS generation, execution rollout collection, and credit assignment for both designer and executor trajectories. To support stable and scalable optimization, we propose Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout and Stagewise Co-evolution to improve training stability and expose the dynamics of designer-executor co-evolution. MetaAgent-X consistently outperforms existing automatic MAS baselines, achieving up to 21.7% gains. Comprehensive ablations show that both designer and executor improve throughout training, and that effective automatic MAS learning follows a stagewise co-evolution process. These results establish end-to-end trainable automatic MAS as a practical paradigm for building self-designing and self-executing agentic models.

</details>


### [174] [GenCircuit-RL: Reinforcement Learning from Hierarchical Verification for Genetic Circuit Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14215)
*Noah Flynn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GenCircuit-RL framework improves automated genetic circuit design via hierarchical verification rewards and curriculum learning, validated on SynBio-Reason benchmark; achieves topological correctness and generalization to unseen parts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on expert-driven genetic circuit design by leveraging code generation to Python SBOL representations, enabling automated verification.

Method: Reinforcement learning with GenCircuit-RL using hierarchical rewards across five verification levels, plus a four-stage curriculum; SynBio-Reason benchmark with 4,753 circuits across six circuit types and nine tasks; held-out biological parts for out-of-distribution evaluation.

Result: Hierarchical verification improves functional reasoning task success by 14-16 percentage points vs binary rewards; curriculum learning is required for strong design performance; generated circuits are topologically correct, generalize to novel parts, and rediscover canonical designs.

Conclusion: Combination of hierarchical verification and curriculum learning yields reliable, generalizable automated SBOL circuit design; benchmark supports out-of-distribution evaluation.

Abstract: Genetic circuit design remains a laborious, expert-driven process despite decades of progress in synthetic biology. We study this problem through code generation: models produce Python code in pysbol3 to construct genetic circuits in the Synthetic Biology Open Language (SBOL), a formal representation that supports automated verification. We introduce GenCircuit-RL, a reinforcement learning framework built around hierarchical verification rewards that decompose correctness into five levels, from code execution to task-specific topological checks, and a four-stage curriculum that shifts optimization pressure from code generation to functional reasoning. We also introduce SynBio-Reason, a benchmark of 4,753 circuits spanning six canonical circuit types and nine tasks from code repair to de novo design, with held-out biological parts for out-of-distribution evaluation. Hierarchical verification improves task success on functional reasoning tasks by 14 to 16 percentage points over binary rewards, and curriculum learning is required for strong design performance. The resulting models generate topologically correct circuits, generalize to novel biological parts, and rediscover canonical designs from the synthetic biology literature.

</details>


### [175] [Fusion-fission forecasts when AI will shift to undesirable behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14218)
*Neil F. Johnson,Frank Yingjie Huo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A model-agnostic, vector fusion–fission dynamics framework to forecast and warn about AI behavior shifts (desirable to undesirable) with real-time signals, validated across multiple models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems exhibit unpredictable shifts from safe to harmful behavior; existing alignment safeguards are insufficient for foretelling or preventing such shifts; a portable mechanism is needed.

Method: Proposes a vector generalization of fusion–fission group dynamics. The shift condition arises from competition between past conversation (C) and desirable (B) vs undesirable (D) basins. Mathematically derived; basins estimated for given applications; validated across seven models (124M–12B), production-scale chatbots, and a priori forecast before the Stanford 'Delusional Spirals' corpus; cross-validated with 207,443 human–AI exchanges; model-agnostic and independent of stochastic sampling; implementable as a real-time warning below current safety stacks.

Result: Achieves ~90% predictive accuracy across seven AI models; persists across ten frontier chatbots; provides a priori prediction 11 months before the Stanford corpus; independently confirmed by the corpus data.

Conclusion: The shift-detection formula is architecture-agnostic, portable across ChatGPT-like systems, and delivers real-time warnings where alignment signals may fail; applicable across domains where competing response classes can be defined.

Abstract: The key problem facing ChatGPT-like AI's use across society is that its behavior can shift, unnoticed, from desirable to undesirable -- encouraging self-harm, extremist acts, financial losses, or costly medical and military mistakes -- and no one can yet predict when. Shifts persist in even the newest AI models despite remarkable progress in AI modeling, post-training alignment and safeguards. Here we show that a vector generalization of fusion-fission group dynamics observed in living and active-matter systems drives -- and can forecast -- future shifts in the AI's behavior. The shift condition, which is also derivable mathematically, results from group-level competition between the conversation-so-far (C) and the desirable (B) and undesirable (D) basin dynamics which can be estimated in advance for a given application. It is neither model-specific nor driven by stochastic sampling. We validate it across six independent tests, including: 90 percent correct across seven AI models spanning two orders of magnitude in parameter count (124M-12B); production-scale persistence across ten frontier chatbots; and a priori time-stamped prediction eleven months before the Stanford 'Delusional Spirals' corpus appeared, and independently confirmed by that corpus of 207,443 human-AI exchanges. Because it sits architecturally below the current safety stack, the same formula provides a real-time warning signal that current alignment does not supply, portable across current and future ChatGPT-like AI architectures and instantiable in application domains where competing response classes can be defined.

</details>


### [176] [Good to Go: The LOOP Skill Engine That Hits 99% Success and Slashes Token Usage by 99% via One-Shot Recording and Deterministic Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14237)
*Xiaohua Wang,Kai Yu,XuXiao Liang,Liang Wang,Chao Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: One-shot recording converts an LLM-driven task into a deterministic, parameterized Loop Skill; subsequent runs replay the tool sequence without LLMs, achieving near-perfect success, massive token savings, and lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce nondeterminism and token costs of using LLMs for periodic tasks while guaranteeing reliable, fast execution across long intervals.

Method: Record the initial LLM-driven run, extract a parameterized, branch-free Loop Skill via a greedy length-descending template extraction algorithm, then replay deterministically by binding real-time values to template variables. Enforce write safety with reentrant locks and atomic file replacement. Prove Replay Determinism and Write Safety. Include a multi-layer degradation strategy and release as open source (buddyMe).

Result: Achieves ~99% success rate and ~99% token reduction; token usage across a month reduced by 93.3%–99.98%; latency reduced by 8.7x; supports tasks with intervals from 5 minutes to 24 hours; eliminates output nondeterminism.

Conclusion: The Loop Skill Engine provides a reliable, scalable approach to execute periodic agent tasks deterministically and cost-effectively, backed by theoretical guarantees and open-source release.

Abstract: Deploying AI agents for repetitive periodic tasks exposes a critical tension: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer unmatched flexibility in tool orchestration, yet their inherent stochasticity causes unpredictable failures, and repeated invocations incur prohibitive token costs. We present the LOOP SKILL ENGINE, a system that achieves a combined 99% success rate and 99% token reduction for periodic agent tasks through a one-shot recording, deterministic replay paradigm. On its first run, the agent executes the task with full LLM reasoning while the system transparently intercepts and records the complete tool-call trajectory. A greedy length-descending template extraction algorithm then converts this recording into a parameterized, branch-free Loop Skill -- a deterministic execution plan that captures the task's functional intent while parameterizing time-dependent and result-dependent variables. All subsequent executions bypass the LLM entirely: the engine resolves template variables against real-time values and replays the tool sequence deterministically. We prove two theorems: (1) Replay Determinism -- the step sequence of a validated Loop Skill is invariant across all future executions; (2) Write Safety -- concurrent access to persistent configuration is serialized through reentrant locks and atomic file replacement. Across a benchmark of periodic agent tasks spanning intervals from 5 minutes to 24 hours, the Loop Skill Engine reduces monthly token consumption by 93.3%--99.98% and cuts execution latency by 8.7x while eliminating output non-determinism. A multi-layer degradation strategy guarantees that tasks never stall. We release the engine as part of the buddyMe open-source agent framework.

</details>


### [177] [Hypergraph Enterprise Agentic Reasoner over Heterogeneous Business Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14259)
*Ling Wang,Songnan Liu,Jianan Wang,Cheng Cheng,Xin Liu,Yihan Zhu,Enyu Li,Yu Xiao,Jiangyong Xie,Duogong Yan,Jiangyi Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HEAR introduces an ontology-guided enterprise agent that performs auditable, evidence-driven multi-hop reasoning over heterogeneous data to solve complex tasks like supply-chain RCA, achieving high accuracy and cost efficiency without LLM retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations and failures in multi-hop reasoning hinder applying LLMs to complex, heterogeneous enterprise systems; semantic grounding and auditable execution are needed; existing paradigms lack these properties.

Method: Proposes Stratified Hypergraph Ontology with a Graph Layer (provenance-aware data interfaces) and Hyperedge Layer (n-ary business rules and procedural protocols). An evidence-driven reasoning loop orchestrates ontology tools for structured multi-hop analysis without retraining LLMs.

Result: On supply-chain tasks such as order fulfillment blockage root-cause analysis, HEAR achieves up to 94.7% accuracy; demonstrates adaptive efficiency by using procedural hyperedges to reduce token costs and topological exploration for rigorous correctness on complex queries; matches proprietary model performance with open-weight backbones and automates manual diagnostics.

Conclusion: HEAR provides a scalable, auditable foundation for enterprise intelligence, enabling robust, cost-efficient, and verifiable reasoning in enterprise environments without retraining LLMs.

Abstract: Applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to heterogeneous enterprise systems is hindered by hallucinations and failures in multi-hop, n-ary reasoning. Existing paradigms (e.g., GraphRAG, NL2SQL) lack the semantic grounding and auditable execution required for these complex environments.
