### [203] [Cattle Trade: A Multi-Agent Benchmark for LLM Bluffing, Bidding, and Bargaining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14537)
*Robert Müller,Clemens Müller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent benchmark (Cattle Trade) evaluates LLMs as strategic agents in a long-horizon, 50–60 turn economic game with auctions, hidden-offer trades, bargaining, bluffing, and resource allocation. It logs all actions for behavioral analysis, and finds that strategic coherence and phase-adaptive bidding predict success more than sheer spending; two heuristic code agents outperform most LLMs; LLMs exhibit failure modes such as overbidding and weak opponent adaptation. It argues for joint benchmarks that test integrated capabilities under uncertainty and conflicting incentives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill a gap in evaluation benchmarks by testing the integrated deployment of multiple strategic and resource-management capabilities of agents in multi-agent, imperfect-information settings, rather than isolated subskills.

Method: A long-horizon multi-agent game (~50–60 turns) integrating auctions, hidden-offer trades (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation. The study logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection. It evaluates seven cost-efficient LLMs and three deterministic code agents across 242 games, analyzing performance, strategy coherence, and behavioral traces.

Result: Higher ranks correlate with strategic coherence, spending discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding rather than raw spending or single subskills. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs. Behavioral traces reveal LLM failure modes: overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and poor opponent-state adaptation.

Conclusion: Benchmarks that jointly test multiple capabilities in multi-agent economic environments with uncertainty and conflicting incentives are necessary to properly evaluate and guide the development of agentic systems.

Abstract: We introduce \textsc{Cattle Trade, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as agents in strategic reasoning under imperfect information, adversarial interaction, and resource constraints. The benchmark combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single long-horizon game lasting 50--60 turns. Unlike prior agent benchmarks that test these abilities in isolation, \textsc{Cattle Trade} evaluates whether agents integrate them across a competitive, multi-agent economic game with conflicting incentives. The benchmark logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection, enabling behavioural analysis beyond final scores or win rates. We evaluate seven cost-efficient language models and three deterministic code agents across 242 games. Strategic coherence, in particular spending efficiency, resource discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding, is associated with rank more strongly than spending volume or any single subskill. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs, and behavioural traces surface recurring LLM failure modes including overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation. Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.

</details>


### [204] [VerbalValue: A Socially Intelligent Virtual Host for Sales-Driven Live Commerce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14542)
*Yuyan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VerbalValue is a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host for live-commerce, built with a domain knowledge base, a sales lexicon, and fine-tuning on 1,475 interactions to produce empathetic, evidence-backed, and engaging responses; it outperforms several baselines in informativeness and factual correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where current AI systems either treat recommendations as terminal acts or hallucinate product claims, and to translate verbal ability into measurable commercial value in live-commerce.

Method: Construct a product knowledge base + sales lexicon; collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions; fine-tune a large language model to produce empathetic, commercially oriented responses with empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection.

Result: Empirical evaluation against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro and baselines shows ~23% gain in informativeness and ~18% gain in factual correctness; improved tactfulness and viewer engagement.

Conclusion: VerbalValue demonstrates that domain-specific knowledge, curated lexicons, and data-driven fine-tuning can yield a sales-focused virtual host that delivers credible, engaging, and persuasive live-commerce interactions.

Abstract: A skilled live-commerce host is not merely a narrator, but a sales agent who converts viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expert product knowledge, emotionally intelligent response tactics, and entertainment that serves as a vehicle for product exposure. Yet no existing AI system replicates this: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and default to generic promotional templates that fail to engage or persuade. We present VerbalValue, a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that turns exceptional verbal ability into real commercial value, built on three contributions. First, we construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon that anchor product-related responses in verified expertise. Second, we collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. Third, we fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent through empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection. Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines demonstrate gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, with consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

</details>


### [205] [Complacent, Not Sycophantic: Reframing Large Language Models and Designing AI Literacy for Complacent Machines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14544)
*Federico Germani,Giovanni Spitale*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframe LLM behavior from sycophancy to complacency; motives lie with training/data/reward signals, not the model; agency resides in developers/institutions; AI literacy should target countering confirmation bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the conceptual understanding of LLM behavior and its implications for training, evaluation, and AI literacy.

Method: Conceptual critique of the sycophancy label; propose a shift to complacency; analyze causal sources (data, rewards); discuss implications for responsibility and education.

Result: Sycophancy is an inappropriate framing; complacency better captures alignment with user input driven by systemic factors; highlights that model behavior reflects design/optimization choices rather than agentic intent.

Conclusion: Agency is with developers/institutions; literacy efforts should emphasize countering confirmation bias; future work could empirically quantify complacency and test interventions to reduce it.

Abstract: Large language models are often described as sycophantic, in the sense that they appear to flatter users or mirror their beliefs. We argue that this label is conceptually misleading: sycophancy implies motives and strategic intent, which LLMs do not possess. Their behaviour is better understood as complacency, a structural tendency to agree with user input because training data, reward signals and design favour agreement and reinforcement over correction. We argue that this distinction matters. Whether developers act sycophantically or not, models themselves never are sycophants; they can only be made more or less complacent. This reframing locates agency in developers and institutions, not in the model. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, we argue that AI literacy educational approaches should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias.

</details>


### [206] [TeachAnything: A Multimodal Crowdsourcing Platform for Training Embodied AI Agents in Symmetrical Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14556)
*Zidong Liu,Rongkai Liu,Yue Li,Zhenliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce TeachAnything platform and a three-stage multimodal demo paradigm to collect diverse demonstrations for embodied agents in Symmetrical Reality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SR envisions closer human-agent coexistence requiring human-like intelligence; needs rich, diverse human guidance and seamless virtual-physical interaction.

Method: Three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal signals; cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented platform TeachAnything with physics simulation to collect demonstrations across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments; aims to unify virtual and physical interactions.

Result: Conceptual/system design with a prototype platform; demonstrates feasibility of collecting diverse demonstrations and bridging virtual-physical interaction, but no empirical evaluation reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: System provides a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality.

Abstract: Symmetrical Reality (SR) is emerging as a future trend for human-agent coexistence, placing higher demands on agents to acquire human-like intelligence. It calls for richer and more diverse human guidance. We introduce a three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal demonstration signals. Building on this paradigm, we developed TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented demonstration platform with physics simulation capable of collecting diverse demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments. By unifying virtual and physical interactions through both methodological design and physics simulation, the system serves as a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality.

</details>


### [207] [PyCSP3-Scheduling: A Scheduling Extension for PyCSP3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14559)
*Sohaib Afifi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PyCSP^3 Scheduling extends PyCSP^3 with native scheduling abstractions via 53 constraints and 27 expressions, compiling to XCSP^3 while preserving modeling/solving separation; experiments show identical optima on tested pairs but mixed runtime impact depending on model family.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constraint programming models often rely on low-level encodings for scheduling; providing high-level scheduling abstractions (interval/sequence variables, resource functions) within PyCSP^3 improves expressiveness and preserves separation between modeling and solving.

Method: Introduce PyCSP^3 Scheduling, a library with 53 dedicated constraints and 27 expressions; compile scheduling constructs to standard PyCSP^3/XCSP^3 constraints; evaluate on 261 paired instances across 17 model families (5 runs each); compare objective values, structural changes after compilation, and runtime performance.

Result: All 72 doubly-proved optimal pairs have identical objectives across formulations; 8 of 17 model families remain structurally unchanged after compilation; performance gains up to 5.8x observed for some families, with regressions in others due to compilation overhead.

Conclusion: PyCSP^3 Scheduling preserves the modeling/solving separation while enabling scheduling abstractions; effectiveness is model-dependent due to decomposition overhead; code and benchmarks are available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: PyCSP$^3$ provides a productive way to build constraint models for solving combinatorial constrained problems and export them to XCSP$^3$, preserving a complete separation between modeling and solving. However, it lacks native support for scheduling abstractions such as interval variables, sequence variables, and resource functions. As a result, scheduling models must be encoded with low-level integer variables and manual channeling constraints, even though PyCSP$^3$ already provides global constraints like NoOverlap and Cumulative on integer arrays. We present PyCSP$^3$ Scheduling, a library that adds scheduling abstractions to PyCSP$^3$ through 53 dedicated constraints and 27 expressions, and compiles them down to standard PyCSP$^3$/XCSP$^3$ constraints, maintaining the modeling/solving separation that underpins the PyCSP$^3$ ecosystem. On 261 paired instances across 17 model families (5 runs each), both formulations produce identical objectives on all 72 doubly-proved optimal pairs and nearly half of the families (8/17) remain structurally unchanged after compilation; however, runtime performance diverges across families, with clear gains on some (up to 5.8x) and regressions on others due to the overhead of compilation decompositions. Code and benchmarks are available at: https://github.com/sohaibafifi/pycsp3-scheduling

</details>


### [208] [Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation: Controlling LLM Behaviour via Optimised Segment-Level Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14561)
*Devika Prasad,Luke Gerschwitz,Tong Li,Henry Xiao,Anjin Liu,Coco Wu,Anna Leontjeva,Luiz Pizzato*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PSAO is a structured prompt optimisation framework that segments prompts into interpretable units with human-readable annotations to guide LLMs, improving reasoning and self-consistency while keeping the original prompt as a fallback; it serves as a proof of concept with unresolved challenges in optimal segmentation and annotation identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unstructured prompt spaces are large and costly to optimise, risking distortions of intent and inefficiency; a controllable, segment-level approach could improve performance and efficiency in prompt engineering.

Method: Decompose prompts into segments (e.g., sentences), attach per-segment annotations (e.g., {not important}, {important}, {very important}), formally define segmentations/annotations, use annotations to guide LLM focus during response generation, retain the original prompt as a candidate in the optimisation space, evaluate on reasoning accuracy and self-consistency.

Result: Empirical evaluations indicate that segment-level annotations improve reasoning accuracy and self-consistency; keeping the original prompt avoids degraded performance; the work is a proof of concept and does not yet provide efficient methods for optimal segmentation/annotation discovery.

Conclusion: PSAO demonstrates feasibility and potential of segment-level annotation optimisation for prompt engineering, offering improved controllability and efficiency in principle, while highlighting challenges and future work needed for efficient identification of optimal segmentations and annotations.

Abstract: Prompt engineering is crucial for effective interaction with generative artificial intelligence systems, yet existing optimisation methods often operate over an unstructured and vast prompt space, leading to high computational costs and potential distortions of the original intent. We introduce Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation (PSAO), a structured prompt optimisation framework designed to improve prompt optimisation controllability and efficiency. PSAO decomposes a prompt into interpretable segments (e.g., sentences) and augments each with human-readable annotations (e.g., {not important}, {important}, {very important}). These annotations guide large language models (LLMs) in allocating focus and clarifying confusion during response generation. We formally define the segmentations and annotations and demonstrate that optimised segment-level annotations can lead to improved LLM responses, with the original prompt retained as a candidate in the optimisation space to prevent performance degradation. Empirical evaluations indicate that PSAO benefits from annotations in terms of improved reasoning accuracy and self-consistency. However, developing efficient methods for identifying optimal segmentations and annotations remains challenging and is reserved for future investigation. This work is intended as a proof of concept, demonstrating the feasibility and potential of segment-level annotation optimisation.

</details>


### [209] [Sycophancy is an Educational Safety Risk: Why LLM Tutors Need Sycophancy Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14604)
*Enkelejda Kasneci,Gjergji Kasneci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cross-disciplinary tutoring benchmark reveals social-epistemic vulnerabilities in frontier LLMs and advocates for measuring social-epistemic courage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure tutoring AI maintains epistemic rigor under social pressures and to operationalize corrective teaching as a safety standard.

Method: Proposes EduFrameTrap benchmark across six domains; manipulates student confidence and pressure via context-switch, authority cues, and social-affective cues; evaluates two frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2 and Claude); uses context-switch failure rates and a two-judge disagreement reliability signal.

Result: GPT-5.2 shows relatively lower context-switch failures; authority/social pressure trigger epistemic retreat; Claude exhibits stronger context-switch fragility; two-judge disagreement indicates reliability of judgments; results suggest model-specific vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: Benchmarks should quantify social-epistemic courage and enforce kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement; encourage tutoring benchmarks that balance corrective rigor with respectful interaction.

Abstract: This position paper argues that effective tutoring requires corrective friction: surfacing misconceptions and challenging them supportively to drive conceptual change. Yet preference-aligned LLMs can trade epistemic rigor for agreeableness. We identify a Reasoning-Sycophancy Paradox: models that resist context-switch frame attacks can still capitulate under social-epistemic pressure, especially authority ("my notes say I'm right") and social-affective face-saving ("please don't tell me I'm wrong"). We introduce EduFrameTrap, a tutoring benchmark across math, physics, economics, chemistry, biology, and computer science that varies student confidence and pressure (context-switch, authority, social-affective). Across two frontier LLMs, context-switch failures are comparatively lower for GPT-5.2, while authority and social pressure more often trigger epistemic retreat. In contrast, Claude shows substantial context-switch fragility in this run. Because these failures are hard to judge automatically, we report two-judge disagreement as a reliability signal. We argue benchmarks should measure social-epistemic courage, i.e., supportive but corrective tutoring, and treat kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement.

</details>


### [210] [SliceGraph: Mapping Process Isomers in Multi-Run Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14619)
*Kang Chen,Junjie Nian,Yixin Cao,Yugang Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SliceGraph is a post-hoc, graph-based measurement of multi-run chain-of-thought (CoT) geometry that reveals shared reasoning units and route families, exposing process-isomer-like trajectories that final-answer aggregation misses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify the geometry and diversity of intermediate CoT trajectories across multiple runs, instead of only reporting final answers, and to identify whether different reasoning routes converge to the same result.

Method: Construct a mutual-kNN graph on CoT slices using activation-key Jaccard similarity to form a SliceGraph; identify biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and define process families as within-family routes; use a label-seeded reward field to map value landscapes; perform typed-state transition analysis; validate robustness via ablations, cross-architecture and cross-scale replications.

Result: Found that in 85.5% of 954 problem-model cells, correct CoTs sharing the same normalized answer split into multiple process families; among cells with ≥2 runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average, termed process isomers; reward-field reveals disconnected high-value cores with route families specializing over cores; processes navigate the same atlas with distinct transition kernels; results robust to ablations and replications, showing final-answer aggregation overlooks structured multi-route geometry.

Conclusion: SliceGraph provides a robust framework to characterize the geometry of multi-run CoT reasoning and demonstrates that many correct solutions arise from diverse, family-divergent routes that final-answer aggregation obscures.

Abstract: Multi-run chain-of-thought reasoning is usually collapsed to final-answer aggregates, which discard howsampled trajectories share, split, and rejoin through intermediate computation. We propose SliceGraph, a post-hoc problem-model-cell graph built by mutual-kNN over sparse activation-key Jaccard similarity between CoT slices, and treat it as a measurement object for process geometry rather than as a decoding program. Across sampled CoT ensembles from three primary 4B/8B models on math and science benchmarks, blinded annotation supports SliceGraph biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and process families as within-family strategy-coherent route units. In 85.5% of 954 problem-model cells, correct CoTs sharing the same normalized answer split into multiple process families; among cells with at least two such runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average. We call such same-answer, family-divergent correct trajectories process isomers. A label-seeded reward field provides a separate value-landscape layer: success-associated regions often split into disconnected high-value cores, and route families specialize over these core footprints rather than merely duplicating one another. A typed-state transition analysis further shows that process families navigate the same atlas with distinct transition kernels under matched null controls. Representation ablations, a cross-architecture replication, and two cross-scale replications support the robustness of the route-family scaffold, showing that final-answer aggregation overlooks this structured multi-route process geometry.

</details>


### [211] [Teaching Large Language Models When Not to Know: Learning Temporal Critique for Ex-Ante Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14636)
*Chenlu Ding,Jiancan Wu,Yanchen Luo,Zheyuan Liu,Yancheng Yuan,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Probes temporal leakage in LLMs via ex-ante reasoning, introduces Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning (TCFT), and shows TCFT reduces leakage substantially vs prompting and SFT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal leakage occurs when models answer using information that became available after a cutoff. Ex-ante reasoning requires pre-cutoff knowledge and cannot be guaranteed by prompting alone or standard fine-tuning; there is a need for explicit temporal verification.

Method: Systematic analysis of prompt-level interventions to assess cutoff formulation effects; development of TCFT (Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning) that trains models to detect post-cutoff leakage, explain boundary violations, and judge temporal admissibility; evaluation on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct.

Result: Leakage is highly sensitive to how the cutoff is formulated; explicit cutoff statements outperform implicit historical framings, and prefix constraints reduce leakage more effectively than suffix constraints. TCFT outperforms prompting and SFT baselines, reducing average leakage by 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points respectively.

Conclusion: Prompting can steer models to a temporal frame but cannot verify temporal admissibility. Ex-ante correctness is a relation to the cutoff, not an intrinsic property of an answer. TCFT provides temporal verification, improving leakage resistance across models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often fail to reason under temporal cutoffs: when prompted to answer from the standpoint of an earlier time, they exploit knowledge that became available only later. We study this failure through the lens of ex-ante reasoning, where a model must rely exclusively on information knowable before a cutoff. Through a systematic analysis of prompt-level interventions, we find that temporal leakage is highly sensitive to cutoff formulation and instruction placement: explicit cutoff statements outperform implicit historical framings, and prefix constraints reduce leakage more effectively than suffix constraints. These findings indicate that prompting can steer models into a temporal frame, but does not endow them with the ability to verify whether a response is temporally admissible. We further argue that supervised fine-tuning is insufficient, since ex-ante correctness is not an intrinsic property of an answer, but a relation between the answer and the cutoff. To address this gap, we propose TCFT, a Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning framework that trains models to acquire cutoff-aware temporal verification. Given a query, a cutoff, and a candidate response, TCFT teaches the model to identify post-cutoff leakage, explain temporal boundary violations, and judge temporal admissibility. Experiments with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct show that TCFT consistently outperforms prompting and SFT baselines, reducing average leakage by 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points, respectively.

</details>


### [212] [MindGap: A Conversational AI Framework for Upstream Neuroplastic Intervention in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14660)
*Eranga Bandara,Ross Gore,Asanga Gunaratna,Ravi Mukkamala,Nihal Siriwardanagea,Sachini Rajapakse,Isurunima Kularathna,Pramoda Karunarathna,Wathsala Herath,Chalani Rajapakse,Sachin Shetty,Anita H. Clayton,Christopher K. Rhea,Ng Wee Keong,Kasun De Zoysa,Amin Hass,Shaifali Kaushik,Preston Samuel,Atmaram Yarlagadda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes MindGap: an on-device, privacy-preserving LLM-based PTSD intervention rooted in dependent origination to target upstream neuroplastic changes and reduce reactivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTSD involves hyperreactive neural pathways due to trauma-encoded Hebbian potentiation; existing therapies mainly suppress symptoms downstream and may not restructure pathways. A privacy-preserving upstream intervention could produce lasting neural reorganization and widen deployment in sensitive contexts.

Method: Three-layer observation protocol at the 'feeling tone gap': 1) notice bare affect; 2) recognize it as self-arising (not caused by the stimulus); 3) identify conditioned implicit beliefs. Guided by a Buddhist framework, delivered via on-device, privacy-preserving conversational AI; fine-tuned lightweight LLM; daily calibrated exposure sessions; no data leaves the device; applicable to clinical and military settings where cloud solutions are restricted.

Result: Conceptual framework with theoretical rationale; no empirical results presented. Efficacy and feasibility remain to be demonstrated.

Conclusion: If validated, MindGap could offer upstream-focused PTSD intervention with privacy advantages; requires rigorous evaluation of efficacy, safety, cultural adaptability, and operational constraints; potential limitations include user engagement, interpretation accuracy, and risks associated with AI-guided therapy in sensitive contexts.

Abstract: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is fundamentally a neuroplastic problem traumatic contact events encode over-reactive neural pathways through Hebbian long-term potentiation, producing hair-triggered amygdala-HPA stress cascades that fire before conscious awareness can intercept them. Existing therapeutic approaches, prolonged exposure, EMDR, cognitive behavioural therapy, operate predominantly downstream of the reactive cascade, teaching patients to tolerate or reframe distress after it has arisen. While clinically valuable, these suppression-based approaches do not produce the upstream pathway dissolution that constitutes lasting structural neural reorganisation. This paper proposes MindGap, a privacy-preserving on-device conversational AI framework that delivers structured neuroplastic rehabilitation for PTSD through the practice of dependent origination, a Buddhist psychological framework that identifies the precise moment between the pre-cognitive affective signal and the reactive elaboration that follows as the site of therapeutic intervention. MindGap guides patients through three progressive layers of observation at this feeling tone gap: noticing the bare affective signal before reactive elaboration, recognising it as self-arising rather than caused by the stimulus, and recognising the conditioned implicit belief beneath the feeling. Each layer corresponds to progressively deeper prefrontal regulatory engagement and progressively deeper long-term depression-mediated weakening of the reactive pathway, producing genuine upstream dissolution rather than downstream suppression. Running entirely on-device with no data egress, MindGap delivers daily calibrated exposure sessions through a fine-tuned lightweight large language model, making it deployable in sensitive clinical and military contexts where cloud-based solutions are not permitted.

</details>


### [213] [Falkor-IRAC: Graph-Constrained Generation for Verified Legal Reasoning in Indian Judicial AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14665)
*Joy Bose*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Falkor-IRAC: a graph-constrained generation framework for Indian legal AI that grounds LLM outputs in an IRAC-based knowledge graph to ensure citation grounding, path validity, and conflict detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vector-based RAG and semantic similarity fall short for legal reasoning, leading to hallucinated precedents and outdated citations. In high-volume jurisdictions like India, faithful reasoning and verifiable citations are critical for access to justice.

Method: Ingest Supreme Court and High Court judgments as IRAC node structures with procedural state transitions, precedent relations, and statutory references into FalkorDB. Use a Verifier Agent to ensure that at inference time a valid graph path supports generated answers; detect doctrinal conflicts as outputs.

Result: On a 51-judgment proof-of-concept, the Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and rejected fabricated citations. Graph-native metrics proposed (citation grounding accuracy, path validity rate, hallucinated precedent rate, conflict detection rate). No RAG baseline comparison or GPU acceleration evaluated yet.

Conclusion: Falkor-IRAC provides a grounded, verifiable legal reasoning framework leveraging structured IRAC graphs; it reduces hallucinations and miscitations and enables explicit conflict detection, with future work including baseline comparisons and hardware acceleration.

Abstract: Legal reasoning is not semantic similarity search. A court judgment encodes constrained symbolic reasoning: precedent propagation, procedural state transitions, and statute-bound inference. These are properties that vector-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) cannot faithfully represent. Hallucinated precedents, outdated statute citations, and unsupported reasoning chains remain persistent failure modes in LLM-based legal AI, with real consequences for access to justice in high-caseload jurisdictions such as India. This paper presents Falkor-IRAC, a graph-constrained generation framework for Indian legal AI that grounds generation in structured reasoning over an IRAC (Issue, Rule, Analysis, Conclusion) knowledge graph. Judgments from the Supreme Court and High Courts of India are ingested as IRAC node structures enriched with procedural state transitions, precedent relationships, and statutory references, stored in FalkorDB for low-latency agentic traversal. At inference time, LLM-generated answers are accepted only if a valid supporting path can be traced through the graph, a check performed by a falsifiability oracle called the Verifier Agent. The system also detects doctrinal conflicts as a first-class output rather than silently resolving them. Falkor-IRAC is evaluated using graph-native metrics: citation grounding accuracy, path validity rate, hallucinated precedent rate, and conflict detection rate. These metrics are argued to be more appropriate for legal reasoning evaluation than BLEU and ROUGE. On a proof-of-concept corpus of 51 Supreme Court judgments, the Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and correctly rejected fabricated citations. Evaluation against vector-only RAG baselines is left for future work, as is GPU-accelerated inference to address current timeout rates on CPU hardware.

</details>


### [214] [How Sensitive Are Radiomic AI Models to Acquisition Parameters?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14667)
*D. Gil,I. Sanchez,C. Sanchez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework to quantify scan parameter sensitivity in radiomic AI models using mixed-effects to improve cross-dataset robustness; applied to lung cancer CT with two multicentre datasets and multiple state-of-the-art architectures; identifies acquisition settings that balance diagnostic quality and radiation dose, improving performance across quality levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous multicentre acquisition degrades radiomic model performance; there is a need for a performance-oriented method to map how acquisition parameters affect accuracy and robustness across datasets.

Method: Develop a mixed-effects framework to quantify the influence of clinically relevant CT acquisition parameters on model performance while accounting for subject-level random effects. Apply to lung cancer diagnosis in CT using two multicentre datasets and several state-of-the-art architectures. Use cross-dataset evaluation by adjusting CT parameters and testing on the public dataset to assess reproducibility.

Result: Identified an acquisition configuration: X-ray tube current ≥ 200 mA, spiral pitch ≤ 1.5, slice thickness ≤ 1.25 mm, balancing diagnostic quality with radiation dose. Performance improved from ~0.79±0.04 sensitivity and ~0.47±0.10 specificity in low-quality scans to ~0.90±0.10 sensitivity and ~0.79±0.13 specificity in high-quality scans across datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables targeted, performance-oriented sensitivity analysis of scanner parameters, revealing parameter regions that enhance cross-dataset robustness while maintaining diagnostic quality and dose considerations. Provides practical acquisition guidelines and a path toward more reliable multicentre radiomic deployment.

Abstract: A main barrier for the deployment of AI radiomic systems in clinical routine is their drop in performance under heterogeneous multicentre acquisition protocols. This work presents a performance-oriented framework for quantifying scan parameter sensitivity of radiomic AI models, while identifying clinically significant parameter regions associated with improved cross-dataset robustness. We formulate a mixed-effects framework for quantifying the influence that clinically relevant acquisition parameters have on models performance, while accounting for subject-level random effects. We have applied our framework to lung cancer diagnosis in CT scans using two independent multicentre datasets (a public database and own-collected data) and several SoA architectures. To evaluate across-database reproducibility, CT parameters have been adjusted using the data collected and tested on the public set. The optimal configuration selected is the current of the X-ray tube >= 200 mA, spiral pitch <= 1.5, slice thickness <= 1.25 mm, which balances diagnostic quality with low radiation dose. These configuration push metrics from 0.79+-0.04 sensitivity, 0.47+-0.10 specificity in low quality scans to 0.90+-0.10 sensitivity, 0.79 +- 0.13 specificity in high quality ones.

</details>


### [215] [$π$-Bench: Evaluating Proactive Personal Assistant Agents in Long-Horizon Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14678)
*Haoran Zhang,Luxin Xu,Zhilin Wang,Runquan Gui,Shunkai Zhang,Haodi Lei,Zihao He,Bingsu He,Chicheng Qin,Tong Zhu,Xiaoye Qu,Yang Yang,Yu Cheng,Yafu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: π-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating proactive, long-horizon assistance in large language model–driven agents. It uses 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 user personas, embedding hidden intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity to jointly measure proactivity and task completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in existing benchmarks that underevaluate an agent's ability to identify and act on hidden user intents emerging over extended, multi-turn interactions.

Method: Constructed 100 multi-turn tasks spanning 5 domain-specific personas, with hidden intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity. Evaluation jointly measures proactivity and task completion across long-horizon trajectories.

Result: Experiments indicate proactive assistance is still challenging; there is a clear separation between proactivity and task completion; leveraging prior interactions improves later proactive intent resolution.

Conclusion: π-Bench provides a framework to study proactive, long-horizon assistance in agent interactions, revealing that proactivity is distinct from completion and benefits from historical context.

Abstract: The rise of personal assistant agents, e.g., OpenClaw, highlights the growing potential of large language models to support users across everyday life and work. A core challenge in these settings is proactive assistance, since users often begin with underspecified requests and leave important needs, constraints, or preferences unstated. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate whether agents can identify and act on such hidden intents before they are explicitly stated, especially in sustained multi-turn interactions where user needs emerge gradually. To address this gap, we introduce $π$-Bench, a benchmark for proactive assistance comprising 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 domain-specific user personas. By incorporating hidden user intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity, $π$-Bench evaluates agents' ability to anticipate and address user needs over extended interactions, jointly measuring proactivity and task completion in long-horizon trajectories that better reflect real-world use. Experiments show (1) proactive assistance remains challenging, (2) a clear distinction between task completion and proactivity, and (3) the value of prior interaction for proactive intent resolution in later tasks.

</details>


### [216] [On Strong Equivalence Notions in Logic Programming and Abstract Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14721)
*Giovanni Buraglio,Wolfgang Dvorak,Stefan Woltran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces a new notion of strong equivalence for logic programs that remains stable under updates and translations, aligning ASP with Dung-style and claim-augmented AFs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong equivalence is crucial for modular knowledge-base replacement; dynamic updates cause divergence between logic programming and abstract argumentation formalisms that are otherwise semantically aligned statically.

Method: Define a novel notion of strong equivalence for logic programs and analyze its compatibility; prove preservation under translations to certain classes of logic programs and to Dung-style and claim-augmented argumentation frameworks.

Result: The proposed notion preserves strong equivalence across translations for the specified classes, restoring compatibility between the formalisms under dynamic contexts.

Conclusion: This work enables consistent reasoning outcomes when replacing knowledge bases across logic programming and argumentation frameworks in dynamic settings.

Abstract: Strong equivalence between knowledge bases ensures the possibility of replacing one with the other without affecting reasoning outcomes, in any given context. This makes it a crucial property in nonmonotonic formalisms. In particular, the fields of logic programming and abstract argumentation provide primary examples in which this property has been subject to vast investigations. However, while (classes of) logic programs and abstract argumentation frameworks are known to be semantically equivalent in static settings, this alignment breaks in dynamic contexts due to differing notions of update. As a result, strong equivalence does not always carry over from one formalism to the other. In this paper, we carefully investigate this discrepancy and introduce a new notion of strong equivalence for logic programs. Our approach preserves strong equivalence under translation between certain classes of logic programs and both Dung-style and claim-augmented argumentation frameworks, thus restoring compatibility across these formalisms.

</details>


### [217] [Agentifying Patient Dynamics within LLMs through Interacting with Clinical World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14723)
*Minghao Wu,Yuting Yan,Zhenyang Cai,Ke Ji,Chuangsen Fang,Ziying Sheng,Xidong Wang,Rongsheng Wang,Hejia Zhang,Shuang Li,Benyou Wang,Hongyuan Zha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A world-model augmented LLM SepsisAgent uses a Clinical World Model to simulate responses to fluid-vasopressor interventions; a three-stage curriculum yields improved off-policy value and safety on MIMIC-IV sepsis data, with learned dynamics remaining useful even without the simulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs encode broad clinical knowledge but are not inherently grounded in action-conditioned patient dynamics; integrating a world model can help reason about consequences of interventions.

Method: Learned Clinical World Model; three-stage training: (1) patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning, (2) propose--simulate--refine behavior cloning, (3) world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning; a propose--simulate--refine loop evaluating candidate interventions before prescribing; evaluation on MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories against traditional RL and LLM baselines; safety and guideline-adherence metrics.

Result: SepsisAgent outperforms all traditional RL and LLM-based baselines in off-policy value and achieves the best safety profile under guideline adherence and unsafe-action metrics; repeated world-model interactions enable learning regularities of patient evolution that persist even when simulator access is removed.

Conclusion: World-model-augmented LLMs can ground action decisions in evolving patient dynamics, enabling safer and more effective sepsis treatment recommendations; agentic training with a Clinical World Model yields robust policies with benefits beyond continued simulator access.

Abstract: Sepsis management in the ICU requires sequential treatment decisions under rapidly evolving patient physiology. Although large language models (LLMs) encode broad clinical knowledge and can reason over guidelines, they are not inherently grounded in action-conditioned patient dynamics. We introduce SepsisAgent, a world model-augmented LLM agent for sepsis treatment recommendation. SepsisAgent uses a learned Clinical World Model to simulate patient responses under candidate fluid--vasopressor interventions, and follows a propose--simulate--refine workflow before committing to a prescription. We first show that world-model access alone yields inconsistent LLM decision performance, motivating agent-specific training. We then train SepsisAgent through a three-stage curriculum: patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning, propose--simulate--refine behavior cloning, and world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning. On MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories, SepsisAgent outperforms all traditional RL and LLM-based baselines in off-policy value while achieving the best safety profile under guideline adherence and unsafe-action metrics. Further analysis shows that repeated interaction with the Clinical World Model enables the agent to learn regularities in patient evolution, which remain useful even when simulator access is removed.

</details>


### [218] [XDomainBench: Diagnosing Reasoning Collapse in High-Dimensional Scientific Knowledge Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14754)
*Gong Zhiren,Tiantong Wu,Jiaming Zhang,Fuyao Zhang,Che Wang,Yurong Hao,Yikun Hou,Foo Ping,Yilei Zhao,Fei Huang,Chau Yuen,Wei Yang Bryan Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDomainBench is a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning, revealing LLMs' reasoning collapse with higher composition order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, single-domain tasks and fail to characterize compositional generalization in real-world interactive scientific workflows; there is a need to stress-test LLMs across multi-domain, multi-step reasoning.

Method: Formalize composition order and mixture structure; construct 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories; define 8 trajectory patterns capturing difficulty and domain-mixture dynamics; perform large-scale evaluation of LLMs to diagnose failure modes such as error accumulation and domain confusion.

Result: LLMs exhibit systematic reasoning collapse as composition order increases, due to direct difficulty from domain composition and indirect interaction-amplified failures leading to error propagation and session collapse.

Conclusion: The benchmark exposes current LLM limitations in interdisciplinary scientific reasoning and highlights the need to address domain confusion and error propagation to make AI-assisted scientific workflows more robust.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for knowledge synthesis, yet their capacity for compositional generalization in scientific knowledge remains under-characterized. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on single-turn restricted scenarios, failing to capture the capability boundaries exposed by real-world interactive scientific workflows. To address this, we introduce XDomainBench, a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning. We formalize the composition order and mixture structure to enable systematic stress-testing from single-discipline to inter-disciplinary, comprising 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories, with 8 realistic trajectory patterns covering difficulty and domain-mixture dynamics, simulating real AI4S scenarios. Large-scale evaluation of LLMs reveals a systematic reasoning collapse as composition order increases, stemming from two root causes: (i) direct difficulty increases induced by domain composition, and (ii) indirect interaction-amplified failures where trajectory patterns trigger error accumulation, reasoning breaks, and domain confusion, ultimately leading to session collapse.

</details>


### [219] [Probabilistic Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks for Single and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14758)
*Luca Marzari,Enrico Marchesini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A probabilistic framework (RNN-ProVe) for verifying RNN-based policies in partially observable RL by policy-driven sampling and statistical error bounds to estimate likelihood of undesired behaviors; scales to single-agent and multi-agent settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address difficulty of verifying history-dependent RNN policies under partial observability due to hidden state dynamics and over-conservative guarantees from existing tools.

Method: Policy-driven sampling to explore feasible hidden states under a trained policy; derive bounded-error, high-confidence probabilistic guarantees for behavioral violations; use statistical bounds to quantify risk.

Result: Demonstrates quantitative probabilistic guarantees that are more feasible and less conservative than existing tools; scalable to recurrent and multi-agent settings.

Conclusion: RNN-ProVe provides a feasible, high-confidence probabilistic verification approach for RNN-based policies in partially observable RL, expanding verification coverage to complex, history-dependent policies.

Abstract: History-dependent policies induced by recurrent neural networks (RNNs) rely on latent hidden state dynamics, making verification in partially observable reinforcement learning (RL) challenging. Existing RNN verification tools typically rely on restrictive modeling assumptions or coarse over-approximations of the hidden state space, which can lead to overly conservative or inconclusive results. We propose $\textbf{RNN}$ $\textbf{Pro}$babilistic $\textbf{Ve}$rification ($\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$), a probabilistic framework that $\textit{estimates the likelihood}$ of undesired behaviors in RNN-based policies. $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ uses policy-driven sampling to approximate the set of hidden states that are feasible under a trained policy, and derives statistical error bounds to produce bounded-error, high-confidence estimates of behavioral violations. Experiments on partially observable single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks show that $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ yields more quantitative, feasibility-aware probabilistic guarantees than existing tools, while scaling to recurrent and multi-agent settings.

</details>


### [220] [AI Outperforms Humans in Personalized Image Aesthetics Assessment via LLM-Based Interviews and Semantic Feature Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14761)
*Yoshia Abe,Tatsuya Daikoku,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An integrated DL-LLM system uses LLM-driven interviews to capture high-level aesthetic preferences and combines low- and high-level image features to predict individual aesthetic evaluations, outperforming conventional models and human predictors, with prediction error below within-person variability; suggests AI could be a deeper interpreter of personal aesthetic sensibilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aesthetic judgments are subjective and person-specific; traditional DL models rely on low-level features and collect limited preference data. LLMs offer rich, generalized knowledge to elicit preferences and infer high-level semantic factors.

Method: Develop a DL-LLM pipeline that (1) extracts low-level image features via deep networks, (2) uses LLM-based semi-structured interviews to elicit target individual's preferences and high-level features, (3) fuses low- and high-level representations to predict subjective aesthetic evaluations; evaluate against conventional systems, human predictors, and the individual's own reevaluations after a time interval.

Result: The proposed system outperforms all baselines, especially on highly-rated images; prediction error is smaller than within-person variability, whereas humans show the largest error.

Conclusion: AI may serve as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility than humans themselves, opening questions about the role of AI in modeling individualized aesthetic taste.

Abstract: Accurately predicting individual aesthetic evaluation for images is a fundamental challenge for AI. Various deep learning (DL)-based models have been proposed for this task, training on image evaluation data to extract objective low-level features. However, aesthetic preferences are inherently subjective and individual-dependent. Accurate prediction thus requires the extraction of high-level semantic features of images and the active collection of preference information from the target individual. To address this issue, we focus on the utility of Large Language Models (LLMs) pretrained on vast amounts of textual data, and develop an integrated DL-LLM system. The system actively elicits aesthetic preferences through LLM-based semi-structured interviews and predicts aesthetic evaluation by leveraging both low-level and high-level features. In our experiments, we compare the proposed system against conventional systems, human predictors, and the target individual's own re-evaluations after a certain time interval. Our results show that the proposed system outperforms all of them, with particularly strong performance on highly-rated images. Moreover, the prediction error of the proposed system is smaller than within-person variability, while human predictors show the largest error, likely due to the influence of their own aesthetic values. These results suggest that AI may be better positioned than others or one's future self to capture individual aesthetic preferences at a given point. This opens a new question of whether AI could serve as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility than humans themselves.

</details>


### [221] [MediaClaw: Multimodal Intelligent-Agent Platform Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14771)
*Shaoan Zhao,Huanlin Gao,Qiang Hui,Ting Lu,Xueqiang Guo,Yantao Li,Xinpei Su,Fuyuan Shi,Chao Tan,Fang Zhao,Kai Wang,Shiguo Lian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MediaClaw presents a three-layer multimodal platform architecture (unified abstraction, pluginized extension, workflow orchestration) to unify AIGC capabilities, enable plug-in expansion, and convert complex production processes into reusable workflow assets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address common deployment pain points in AIGC adoption: fragmented capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production processes, and limited reuse of high-quality production workflows.

Method: Three-layer design: unified invocation model for all capabilities; plugin system for hot-pluggable expansion; task-oriented Skills that package complex workflows into reusable assets; architectural design philosophy, core capability model logic, and engineering trade-offs.

Result: Articulates the architectural design philosophy, the logic of the core capability model, and key engineering trade-offs, providing a reusable practical reference for building multimodal capability platforms.

Conclusion: The work offers a design-oriented framework and practical reference to guide the construction of multimodal capability platforms, emphasizing reusable workflow assets and extensible capability integration.

Abstract: MediaClaw is a multimodal agent platform built on the OpenClaw ecosystem. Its core design follows a three-layer architecture of unified abstraction, pluginized extension, and workflow orchestration. The system is intended to address practical deployment pain points in AIGC adoption, including fragmented capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production processes, and limited reuse of high-quality production workflows. \system{} abstracts full-category AIGC capabilities into a unified invocation model, uses plugins to support hot-pluggable capability expansion, and uses task-oriented Skills to turn complex production processes into reusable workflow assets. This report focuses on the architectural design philosophy of MediaClaw, the design logic of its core capability model, and the key engineering trade-offs in implementation. It aims to provide reusable practical reference for building multimodal capability platforms.

</details>


### [222] [Identifying Culprits Through Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient Deep Learning Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14774)
*Lata B T,Savitha N J*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DDPG-based framework for crime investigation data achieving 95% accuracy in identifying criminals, outperforming several existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional investigations rely on limited data; there is a need for an optimal method to identify offenders from complex, multi-source data while reducing false positives/negatives.

Method: Train a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient model on a dataset combining crime scene materials, witness statements, and suspect profiles; optimize feature usage to maximize offender identification while minimizing noise.

Result: Reported 95% accuracy, outperforming several existing approaches on the evaluated data.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a DDPG-based approach for crime data fusion and offender identification; practical deployment would require addressing data quality, ethics, and robustness concerns.

Abstract: In the world of AI and advanced technologies investigation aspects identification of a crime or criminal plays a major problem. In this research we focus on a Conventional ways of implicating criminal investigations usually rely on limited data analysis. Finding an optimal and efficient method that will effectively identify criminals from complex datasets and minimise false positives and false negatives is the considered as a challenge. The main novelty approach of this work is based on the deep learning algorithm Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) is presented in this paper. We train the DDPG model with a dataset of crime scene material, witness statements and suspect profiles. The algorithm uses features to maximise the likelihood of identifying the offender while minimising the noise impact and irrelevant data. We show the efficacy of the proposed method, where DDPG identified criminals with an amazing accuracy of 95% than other several existing methods.

</details>


### [223] [A Heterogeneous Temporal Memory Governance Framework for Long-Term LLM Persona Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14802)
*Zhao Yang,Wang Huan,Li Yingshuo,Tu Haomiao,Lin Hujite*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARPM is an external temporal memory governance framework for long-term dialogue that separates static memory from dynamic dialogue memory and uses hybrid retrieval, dual-temporal reranking, and evidence-reading plus a controlled analysis protocol to maintain knowledge continuity under noise; experiments show mixed automatic recall vs manual review, emphasize the importance of history retrieval and BM25, and illustrate the governance-based approach to long-term persona consistency under heavy noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and instability in long-range LLM dialogue, especially with noisy knowledge bases and cross-model transfer; existing approaches that encode persona into weights or rely solely on long contexts are insufficient for auditable governance of continuity.

Method: Propose ARPM: separate static knowledge memory and dynamic dialogue memory; combine vector retrieval, BM25, and RRF fusion; apply dual-temporal reranking; use chronological evidence reading; implement a controlled analysis protocol for evidence verification and answer binding; conduct engineering-log-based experiments (50-round QA) with different noise levels, CSV automatic judgment versus manual review; ablations on history retrieval and BM25; test under a 5.1M-character noise substrate with periodic clearance and multi-model handoffs.

Result: Under 1:5 signal-to-noise ratio, CSV recall is 54.0% versus 100% with manual review; under 1:200+ SNR, 44.0% versus 80.0%; ablations show dialogue history retrieval is necessary (dropping strict accuracy from 100% to 66.7%), BM25 removal reduces to 80%; ARPM maintains semantic and boundary continuity and persona consistency under a 5.1M-character noise substrate, though limits arise from weak protocol compliance; overall demonstrates that long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable components and evaluated in a white-box manner.

Conclusion: Treat long-term dialogue continuity as a governance problem, decomposable into auditable components; separate static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue memory, apply hybrid retrieval and evidence-verification protocols, and evaluate governance-level continuity across sessions and models.

Abstract: Large language models often suffer from fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and reduced stability during long-range interaction, especially under high-noise knowledge bases, context clearing, and cross-model transfer. To address these issues, we introduce ARPM, an external temporal memory governance framework for long-term dialogue. ARPM separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory and combines vector retrieval, BM25, RRF fusion, dual-temporal reranking, chronological evidence reading, and a controlled analysis protocol for evidence verification and answer binding. Unlike approaches that encode persona consistency into model weights or rely only on long context, ARPM treats continuity as a traceable, auditable, and transferable governance problem. Using engineering logs, we conduct three experiments. First, in a 50-round question-answering setting, we compare signal-to-noise ratios of 1:5 and 1:200+, and distinguish CSV auto-judgment from manual review. Under 1:5, CSV recall accuracy is 54.0%, while manual review raises it to 100.0%. Under 1:200+, the values are 44.0% and 80.0%. These results show that automatic rules can underestimate recall after supporting evidence enters the prompt. Second, ablation results show that dialogue history retrieval is necessary for recent continuity: disabling it reduces strict accuracy from 100% to 66.7%, and disabling BM25 reduces it to 80.0%, indicating that pure semantic retrieval is insufficient for correction and tracing. Third, under a 5.1-million-character noise substrate, periodic context clearing, and multi-model handoff, ARPM maintains semantic continuity, boundary continuity, and persona consistency, while exposing limits caused by weak protocol compliance. These findings show that long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable components and evaluated in a white-box manner.

</details>


### [224] [Interestingness as an Inductive Heuristic for Future Compression Progress](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14831)
*Vincent Herrmann,Jürgen Schmidhuber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes 'interestingness' as a predictive inductive heuristic for future compression progress, grounded in Kolmogorov Complexity and Algorithmic Statistics; finds exponential dependence on recency and a stronger Algorithmic Prior versus Length Prior, with experimental validation across three universal computational paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the bottleneck in recursive self-improvement by identifying tasks with high potential for future progress via a principled measure of interestingness.

Method: Theoretical analysis of complexity-runtime profiles under Length, Algorithmic, and Speed priors; derivation of how expected future progress scales with recency; comparison of priors; empirical validation in three universal computational paradigms.

Result: The inductive core of interestingness is viable: expected progress grows exponentially with recency; Algorithmic Prior yields a quadratic gain in discovery rate over the same observed profile compared to Length Prior; empirical confirmation across three diverse universal computational paradigms.

Conclusion: Supports using an inductive notion of interestingness as a viable predictor of future progress, with Algorithmic priors offering optimistic estimates; provides a framework to guide task selection in self-improving systems and invites further empirical testing and refinement.

Abstract: One of the bottlenecks on the way towards recursively self-improving systems is the challenge of interestingness: the ability to prospectively identify which tasks or data hold the potential for future progress. We formalize interestingness as an inductive heuristic for future compression progress and investigate its predictability using tools from Kolmogorov Complexity and Algorithmic Statistics. By analyzing complexity-runtime profiles under Length, Algorithmic, and Speed priors, we demonstrate that the inductive property of interestingness -- the capacity for past progress to signal future discovery -- is theoretically viable and empirically supported. We prove that expected future progress depends exponentially on the recency of the last observed breakthrough. Furthermore, we show that the Algorithmic Prior is significantly more optimistic than the Length Prior, yielding a quadratic increase in expected discovery for the same observed profile. These findings are experimentally confirmed across three diverse universal computational paradigms.

</details>


### [225] [Emotion-Attended Stateful Memory (EASM):The Architecture for Hyper-Personalization at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14833)
*Vineet Kotecha,Vansh Gupta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Emotion-attended stateful memory enables persistent, user-specific context for LLMs; A/B study shows substantial improvements in memory grounding, plan clarity, and emotional validation across diverse emotional states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current stateless LLMs limit personalization; persistent user understanding and emotion-aware adaptation require memory and inference-time signals.

Method: Developed an emotion-attended memory architecture that combines long-term history, emotional signals, and inferred intent. Evaluated via a controlled A/B study with 30 non-scripted conversations across six emotion categories using the same base model; compare memory-enabled vs stateless baseline.

Result: Memory-enabled condition outperformed stateless baseline across all evaluated scenarios; largest gains in memory grounding (95%), plan clarity (57%), and emotional validation (34%). Robust results even in emotionally adversarial contexts (grief, distress, uncertainty).

Conclusion:  Stateful emotional memory may serve as foundational infrastructure for hyper-personalized AI, but validation across larger and more diverse evaluations is needed.

Abstract: Current language model systems remain fundamentally stateless across sessions, limiting their ability to personalize interactions over time. While retrieval-augmented generation and fine-tuning improve knowledge access and domain capability, they do not enable persistent understanding of individual users. We propose an emotion-attended stateful memory architecture that dynamically constructs user-specific conversational context using long-term history, emotional signals, and inferred intent at inference time. To evaluate its impact, we conducted a controlled A/B study across thirty non-scripted conversations spanning six emotionally distinct categories using the same underlying language model in both conditions. The memory-enriched condition consistently outperformed the stateless baseline across all evaluated scenarios. The largest gains were observed in memory grounding (95% improvement), plan clarity (57%), and emotional validation (34%). Results remained consistent even in emotionally adversarial conversations involving grief, distress, and uncertainty. These findings suggest that stateful emotional memory may represent a foundational infrastructure layer for hyper-personalized AI systems, though broader validation across larger and more diverse evaluations remains necessary

</details>


### [226] [A Deterministic Agentic Workflow for HS Tariff Classification: Multi-Dimensional Rule Reasoning with Interpretable Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14857)
*Yu Zhang,Dongjiang Zhuang,Qu Zhou,Zheng Huang,Junhe Wu,Jing Cao,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Deterministic, stage-wise agentic workflow for HS tariff classification improves interpretability and achieves competitive accuracy against end-to-end LLMs; reveals potential ground-truth label inconsistencies in HSCodeComp.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address multi-dimensional rule reasoning in HS classification, where correct labeling requires simultaneously satisfying competing priority rules across material, form, function, essential character, part-vs-whole, and specific vs residual headings; traditional end-to-end prompting struggles to preserve all constraints.

Method: Offline knowledge-engineering of Chinese HS tariff data combined with an online six-stage fixed-control-flow pipeline. Stage-wise outputs with verbatim citations to relevant notes ensure interpretability and verifiability. The approach contrasts with self-planning LLM agents by constraining tool calls to narrow stages and retaining local reflection/verification.

Result: On HSCodeComp (six-digit level): 75.0% top-1 and 91.5% top-3 at four digits; 64.2% top-1 and 78.3% top-3 at six digits with Qwen3.6-plus. An open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 backbone in non-thinking mode achieves 84.2% four-digit and 77.4% six-digit top-1 agreement with the frontier model.

Conclusion: Deterministic agentic workflows yield interpretable, rule-consistent classifications with competitive accuracy; however, ground-truth labels in HSCodeComp may deviate from HS general rules, underscoring data quality concerns and the value of adjudication records in the appendix.

Abstract: Harmonized System (HS) tariff classification is a high-stakes, expert-level task in which a free-form product description must be mapped to a specific six- or eight-digit code under the General Interpretive Rules (GIR), section notes, chapter notes, and Explanatory Notes. The difficulty lies not in knowledge volume but in *multi-dimensional rule reasoning*: a correct classification must satisfy competing priority rules along several axes simultaneously, including material, form, function, essential character, the part-versus-whole boundary, and specific listing versus residual headings. End-to-end prompting of large language models fails characteristically by resolving one axis while ignoring the priority constraints on the others. We present a *deterministic agentic workflow* in contrast to self-planning agents: the control flow is fixed, language model calls are confined to narrow stages, and reflection and verification are retained as local mechanisms. This design yields interpretability by construction--each decision is decomposed into stage-wise structured outputs with verbatim citation of the chapter or section notes that bear on it. The architecture combines offline knowledge-engineering of the Chinese HS tariff with an online six-stage pipeline. Evaluated on HSCodeComp at the six-digit level, the workflow reaches 75.0% top-1 and 91.5% top-3 at four digits, and 64.2% top-1 and 78.3% top-3 at six digits with Qwen3.6-plus; an open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 backbone in non-thinking mode achieves 84.2% four-digit and 77.4% six-digit top-1 agreement with the frontier model. A two-stage manual audit of 226 six-digit disagreements suggests that a non-trivial fraction of HSCodeComp ground-truth labels may deviate from HS general rules; full adjudication records are released in the appendix as preliminary findings for community review.

</details>


### [227] [Holistic Evaluation and Failure Diagnosis of AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14865)
*Netta Madvil,Gilad Dym,Alon Mecilati,Edo Dekel,Jonatan Liberman,Rotem Brazilay,Liron Schliesser,Max Svidlo,Shai Nir,Orel Shalom,Yaron Friedman,David Connack,Amos Rimon,Philip Tannor,Shir Chorev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A holistic evaluation framework for AI agents that combines top-down diagnostics with bottom-up span-level analysis to localize errors within long traces; achieves state-of-the-art on TRAIL across GAIA and SWE-Bench, showing evaluation methodology as the bottleneck rather than model capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current outcome-only metrics fail to explain why a failure occurred and process-level methods struggle to localize failures in long, structured traces; need scalable, per-span explanations to diagnose failures.

Method: Propose decomposition into independent per-span assessments, coupling top-down agent-level diagnosis with bottom-up span-level evaluation, producing span-level rationales for each verdict; scales to arbitrary trace length.

Result: On TRAIL benchmark, state-of-the-art across GAIA and SWE-Bench; relative gains: up to 38% category F1, up to 3.5x localization accuracy, up to 12.5x joint localization-categorization accuracy; per-category analysis shows leading in more error categories; frontier model achieves higher localization inside framework than monolithic judge.

Conclusion: Evaluation methodology can be the bottleneck; the proposed framework demonstrates that better analysis at the span level yields substantial performance gains and improved interpretability.

Abstract: AI agents execute complex multi-step processes, but current evaluation falls short: outcome metrics report success or failure without explaining why, and process-level approaches struggle to connect failure types to their precise locations within long, structured traces. We present a holistic agent evaluation framework that pairs top-down agent-level diagnosis with bottom-up span-level evaluation, decomposing analysis into independent per-span assessments. This decomposition scales to traces of arbitrary length and produces span-level rationales for each verdict. On the TRAIL benchmark, our framework achieves state-of-the-art results across all metrics on both GAIA and SWE-Bench, with relative gains over the strongest prior baselines of up to 38% on category F1, up to 3.5x on localization accuracy, and up to 12.5x on joint localization-categorization accuracy. Per-category analysis shows our framework leading in more error categories than any other evaluator. Notably, the same frontier model achieves several times higher localization accuracy when used inside our framework than as a monolithic judge over the full trace, showing that evaluation methodology, not model capability, is the bottleneck.

</details>


### [228] [BiFedKD: Bidirectional Federated Knowledge Distillation Framework for Non-IID and Long-Tailed ECG Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14886)
*Zixuan Shu,Tiancheng Cao,Hen-Wei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BiFedKD enables bidirectional logit distillation in federated ECG learning to stabilize cross-client alignment under non-IID/long-tailed data, achieving higher accuracy and Macro-F1 with lower communication and computation costs on MIT-BIH.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning for ECG in IoMT is privacy-preserving but suffers from heavy updates and non-IID, long-tailed distributions. FD reduces traffic but degrades under challenging data distributions. A stable, efficient distillation framework is needed.

Method: An aggregation-by-distillation pipeline with temperature scaling that enables bidirectional knowledge distillation (BiFedKD) across clients, producing a global distillation signal for cross-client alignment and reducing per-round data transmission.

Result: On MIT-BIH Arrhythmia data, BiFedKD improves accuracy by 3.52% and Macro-F1 by 9.93% over baseline. It also reduces communication overhead by 40% and computation cost by 71.7% to reach the same Macro-F1.

Conclusion: BiFedKD offers a robust, efficient solution for FL-based ECG analysis under non-IID and long-tailed label distributions, delivering better performance with substantial resource savings.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) networks is constrained by strict data-sharing regulations and privacy concerns. Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative learning by keeping raw ECG data on devices, but frequent transmissions of high-dimensional model updates incur heavy per-round traffic over bandwidth-limited links. To alleviate this bottleneck, federated distillation (FD) replaces parameter exchange with logit-based knowledge transfer. However, the performance of FD often degrades under the non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) and long-tailed label distributions in ECG deployments. To address these challenges, we propose a bidirectional federated knowledge distillation (BiFedKD) framework that employs an aggregation-by-distillation pipeline with temperature scaling to produce a stable global distillation signal for cross-client alignment. Experiments on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset show that BiFedKD improves accuracy and Macro-F1 over the baseline by $3.52\%$ and $9.93\%$, respectively. Moreover, to reach the same Macro-F1, BiFedKD reduces communication overhead by $40\%$ and computation cost by $71.7\%$ compared with the baseline.

</details>


### [229] [Beyond Individual Intelligence: Surveying Collaboration, Failure Attribution, and Self-Evolution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14892)
*Shihao Qi,Jie Ma,Rui Xing,Wei Guo,Xiao Huang,Zhitao Gao,Jianhao Deng,Jun Liu,Lingling Zhang,Bifan Wei,Boqian Yang,Pinghui Wang,Jianwen Sun,Jing Tao,Yaqiang Wu,Hui Liu,Yu Yao,Tongliang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes LIFE progression (Lay/Integrate/Find faults/Evolve) as a unified, causally linked framework for autonomous, self-improving multi-agent systems, with taxonomies, dependency characterization, and a cross-stage research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses limitations of LLM-based autonomous agents in sustaining cross-role coordination and the risk of error propagation; aims to unify fragmented literature and enable continuous self-improvement.

Method: Theoretical survey framework that formalizes four stages, develops taxonomies, characterizes dependencies between adjacent stages, synthesizes existing work, and identifies open challenges and a cross-stage agenda.

Result: Delivers a systematic reference and roadmap for closed-loop, self-improving multi-agent coordination; clarifies dependencies across stages and highlights open problems at stage boundaries.

Conclusion: Bridges disparate threads into the LIFE framework, advancing toward autonomous, self-organizing multi-agent intelligence.

Abstract: LLM-based autonomous agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in reasoning, planning, and tool use, yet remain limited when tasks require sustained coordination across roles, tools, and environments. Multi-agent systems address this through structured collaboration among specialized agents, but tighter coordination also amplifies a less explored risk: errors can propagate across agents and interaction rounds, producing failures that are difficult to diagnose and rarely translate into structural self-improvement. Existing surveys cover individual agent capabilities, multi-agent collaboration, or agent self-evolution separately, leaving the causal dependencies among them unexamined. This survey provides a unified review organized around four causally linked stages, which we term the LIFE progression: Lay the capability foundation, Integrate agents through collaboration, Find faults through attribution, and Evolve through autonomous self-improvement. For each stage, we provide systematic taxonomies and formally characterize the dependencies between adjacent stages, revealing how each stage both depends on and constrains the next.
