Result: In closed-loop evaluations, the model generalizes reliably to unseen conditions, maintains stable control, and completes scenarios that differ substantially from training. The BEV representation and flow-matching formulation are cited as key factors improving robustness to distribution shift.

Conclusion:  BEV-based, flow-matching planning yields robust, low-latency autonomous driving control with strong generalization to out-of-distribution environments; supports real-time applications and suggests that geometry-centric perception plus smooth vector-field learning enhances stability under distribution shifts.

Abstract: We present a flow-matching planner for autonomous driving that directly outputs actionable control trajectories defined by acceleration and curvature profiles. The model is conditioned on a bird's-eye-view (BEV) raster of the surrounding scene and generates control sequences in a small number of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODE) integration steps, enabling low-latency inference suitable for real-time closed-loop re-planning. We train exclusively on urban scenarios (real urban city streets, intersections and roundabouts of the city of Parma, Italy) collected from a 2D traffic simulator with reactive agents, and evaluate in closed-loop on both in-distribution and markedly out-of-distribution environments, including multi-lane highways and unseen urban scenarios. Our results show that the model generalizes reliably to these unseen conditions, maintaining stable closed-loop control and successfully completing scenarios that differ substantially from the training distribution. We attribute this to the BEV representation, which provides a geometry-centric view of the scene that is inherently less sensitive to distributional shifts, and to the flow-matching formulation, which learns a smooth vector field that degrades gracefully under distribution shift. We provide video demonstrations of closed-loop behavior at https://marcelloceresini.github.io/DirectControlFlowMatching.

</details>


### [257] [Chrono-Gymnasium: An Open-Source, Gymnasium-Compatible Distributed Simulation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14911)
*Bocheng Zou,Harry Zhang,Khailanii Slaton,Jingquan Wang,Derrick Ruan,Huzaifa Mustafa Unjhawala,Radu Serban,Dan Negrut*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Ray-based distributed wrapper for Chrono physics with a Gymnasium interface to accelerate high-fidelity simulations for RL and optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity physics are computationally expensive; need scalable, reproducible, ML-ready infrastructure to close the sim-to-real gap.

Method: Chrono-Gymnasium built on Ray; provides Gymnasium-compatible API; built-in synchronization and messaging; two case studies: RL navigation in complex terrains; Bayesian optimization of lander design parameters.

Result: Reduces wall-clock time of high-fidelity simulations without sacrificing physical accuracy; enables scalable RL and optimization workflows; demonstrates practical gains in two domains.

Conclusion: A scalable path to integrate high-fidelity physics with ML pipelines, enabling faster design/control iterations in robotics and mechanical systems.

Abstract: High-fidelity physics simulation is essential for closing the sim-to-real gap in robotics and complex mechanical systems. However, the computational overhead of high-fidelity engines often limits their use in data-intensive tasks like Reinforcement Learning (RL) and global optimization. We introduce Chrono-Gymnasium, a distributed computing framework that scales the high-fidelity multi-body dynamics of Project Chrono across large-scale computing clusters. Built upon the Ray framework, Chrono-Gymnasium provides a standardized Gymnasium interface, enabling seamless integration with modern machine learning libraries while providing built-in synchronization and messaging primitives for distributed execution. We demonstrate the framework's capabilities through two distinct case studies: (1) the training of an RL agent for autonomous robotic navigation in complex terrains, and (2) the Bayesian Optimization of a planetary lander's design parameters to ensure landing stability. Our results show that Chrono-Gymnasium reduces wall-clock time for high-fidelity simulations without sacrificing physical accuracy, offering a scalable path for the design and control of complex robotic systems.

</details>


### [258] [FU-MPC: Frontier- and Uncertainty-Aware Model Predictive Control for Efficient and Accurate UAV Exploration with Motorized LiDAR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14920)
*Jianping Li,Pengfei Wan,Zhongyuan Liu,Yi Wang,Yiheng Chen,Xinhang Xu,Rui Jin,Boyu Zhou,Lihua Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A UAV platform with an independently actuated rotating LiDAR and a hierarchical planner optimizes both exploration and localization, using a global frontier-based planner and a local FU-MPC controller to steer LiDAR orientation along the flight path for better coverage and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient exploration in unknown environments requires rapid coverage expansion while maintaining accurate, robust localization. Conventional LiDAR on UAVs couples sensing to pose, limiting exploration efficiency; rotating LiDAR introduces an extra sensing degree of freedom that can decouple sensing from motion but lacks explicit integration into exploration/localization decisions.

Method: 1) Global planner organizes frontiers into representative viewpoints and sequences them with topology-aware transition costs. 2) Local receding-horizon FU-MPC controller jointly optimizes LiDAR rotation along the predicted flight trajectory, considering frontier-aware exploration utility and direction-dependent localization uncertainty. 3) Lightweight surrogate evaluation enables real-time onboard execution.

Result: Experiments in complex environments show improved exploration efficiency while maintaining robust localization compared with fixed-pattern scanning and uncertainty-only baselines; the approach demonstrates the benefit of treating LiDAR rotation as an explicit decision variable linked to exploration progress and localization quality.

Conclusion: Actively controllable LiDAR orientation can enhance exploration efficiency without sacrificing localization robustness. The proposed hierarchical planner plus FU-MPC controller provides a practical, real-time framework for coordinated sensing and navigation on UAVs with rotating LiDAR.

Abstract: Efficient UAV exploration in unknown environments requires rapid coverage expansion while maintaining accurate and reliable localization, since safe navigation in complex scenes depends on consistent mapping and pose estimation. However, for conventional LiDAR-equipped UAVs, the observable region is tightly coupled with the UAV pose and motion. Expanding coverage often requires additional translational or rotational maneuvers, which can reduce exploration efficiency and increase the risk of localization degradation in geometrically challenging environments. Motorized rotating LiDARs provide a promising solution by actively adjusting the sensor viewing direction without changing the UAV motion, thereby introducing an additional sensing degree of freedom. Nevertheless, existing exploration systems rarely exploit this scanning freedom as an explicit decision variable linked to both exploration progress and localization quality. To address this gap, we develop a UAV platform equipped with an independently actuated rotating LiDAR and propose a hierarchical exploration framework. The global planner organizes frontiers into representative viewpoints and sequences them using topology-aware transition costs. Built upon this planner, FU-MPC serves as a local receding-horizon scan controller that optimizes LiDAR rotation along the predicted flight trajectory. The controller jointly considers frontier-aware exploration utility and direction-dependent localization uncertainty, while lightweight surrogate evaluation enables real-time onboard execution. Experiments in complex environments demonstrate that the proposed system improves exploration efficiency while maintaining robust localization performance compared with fixed-pattern scanning and uncertainty-only baselines. The project page can be found at https://kafeiyin00.github.io/FU-MPC/.

</details>


### [259] [Behavioral Data-Driven Optimal Trajectory Generation for Rotary Cranes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14944)
*Iskandar Khemakhem,Manuel Zobel,Johannes Schüle,Oliver Sawodny,Naoki Uchiyama,Abdallah Farrage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Data-driven open-loop trajectory generation for rotary cranes using Willems' lemma to suppress load sway while reducing operation time and energy, avoiding explicit modeling and requiring limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety and efficiency in crane operations amid skilled-labor shortages, by reducing reliance on accurate models and heavy data requirements of learning-based methods.

Method: Utilize Willems' fundamental lemma and its generalizations to construct a nonparametric representation of crane dynamics from measured input-output data; generate smooth, optimal open-loop slewing trajectories via convex optimization, bypassing explicit system identification.

Result: Lab validation on a rotary crane showing substantial performance gains: up to 35% reduction in load sway, 43% reduction in tracking error, and 50% reduction in travel time compared with a model-based approach.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a data-driven, model-free trajectory generation framework for rotary cranes that reduces need for expert modeling and achieves notable efficiency and safety improvements; further work could address robustness and generalization to broader operating conditions.

Abstract: With the growth of the construction industry and the global shortage of skilled labor, the automation of crane control has become increasingly important for safe and efficient operations. A central challenge in automatic crane control is the reduction of load oscillations during motion, which is primarily addressed through appropriate slewing trajectories. In this context, classical model-based control methods rely on accurate dynamical models and expert tuning, and often struggle to meet safety and precision requirements, while many learning-based approaches require large data sets and significant computational resources. This paper proposes a behavioral data-driven framework for generating open-loop slewing trajectories for rotary cranes that suppress load sway while reducing operation time and energy consumption. The approach builds on Willems' fundamental lemma and its generalizations, to bypass explicit system modeling and operate directly on measured input-output data. A practical workflow is presented in this paper to reduce the need for expert knowledge. Despite the underactuated nature of the crane dynamics, the method identifies a nonparametric representation of the system behavior and generates smooth, optimal trajectories using limited data and convex optimization. The proposed trajectory generation method is validated on a laboratory crane setup and compared against an established model-based approach, achieving up to 35% reduction in load sway, 43% reduction in tracking error, and 50% reduction in travel time.

</details>


### [260] [A Prototyping Framework for Distributed Control of Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15049)
*Junaid Ahmed Memon,Allan Andre Do Nascimento,Kostas Margellos,Antonis Papachristodoulou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Prototyping SPMD framework for validating distributed multi-robot control/optimization across simulations and hardware, demonstrated on four quadrotors with a non-cooperative game-theoretic algorithm; shows low-cost, scalable validation across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge theory and practice in distributed control/optimization for multi-robot systems; provide a cheap, scalable platform to test algorithms before hardware deployment.

Method: An SPMD-based framework that emulates distributed control on a single computer, with each core running the same algorithm and communicating with neighbors. Apply to four-quadrotor position-swapping task using a non-cooperative game-theoretic distributed algorithm. Validate across three dynamics levels: point-mass, high-fidelity quadrotor, and Crazyflie hardware.

Result: Comparative computational time and trajectory data across dynamics levels; validation demonstrates the framework's low-cost accessibility for validating distributed algorithms.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical, scalable pathway to prototype and validate distributed optimization algorithms for multi-robot systems, effectively bridging theory and hardware testing.

Abstract: This paper presents a prototyping framework for distributed control of multi-robot systems, aimed at bridging theory and practical testing of distributed optimization algorithms. Using the Single Program, Multiple Data (SPMD) paradigm, the framework emulates distributed control on a single computer, with each core running the same algorithm using local states and neighbour-to-neighbour communication. We demonstrate the framework on a four-quadrotor position-swapping task using a non-cooperative game-theoretic distributed algorithm. Computational time and trajectory data are compared across the supported dynamics levels: a point-mass model, a high-fidelity quadrotor model, and an experimental hardware testbed using Crazyflie quadcopters. The results show that the framework provides a low-cost and accessible approach for validating distributed algorithms.

</details>


### [261] [SOCC-ICP: Semantics-Assisted Odometry based on Occupancy Grids and ICP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15074)
*Johannes Scherer,Sebastian Hirt,Henri Meeß*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SOCC-ICP jointly performs semantics-assisted occupancy grid mapping and LiDAR odometry, using voxel-level geometric and semantic statistics to enable adaptive ICP and dynamic-object filtering within a single, downstream-ready map framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce fragmentation of map representations in LiDAR odometry and reward robustness in unseen or degenerate environments by integrating semantic occupancy grids that support both accurate pose estimation and subsequent tasks such as planning.

Method: Construct a 3D semantic occupancy grid where each voxel stores geometric (e.g., planarity) and semantic statistics. Use adaptive ICP (point-to-point or point-to-plane) guided by local planarity, and perform raycasting-based free-space updates to suppress dynamic objects. Integrate semantic labels to influence map construction, downsampling, and correspondence weighting; jointly update odometry and the semantic map.

Result: SOCC-ICP achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry across diverse scenarios, maintaining robustness in geometrically degenerate environments even without semantic cues. When semantics are available, they yield additional accuracy gains. The unified representation eliminates redundant maps and provides an immediately useful map for downstream robotic tasks.

Conclusion: A unified semantics-assisted occupancy-grid approach can match or surpass traditional LiDAR odometry while offering stronger downstream utility, robustness to degeneracies, and straightforward incorporation of semantic information.

Abstract: Reliable pose estimation in previously unseen environments is a fundamental capability of autonomous systems. Existing LiDAR odometry methods typically employ point-, surfel-, or NDT-based map representations, which are distinct from the semantic occupancy grids commonly used for downstream tasks such as motion planning. We introduce SOCC-ICP, a semantics-assisted odometry framework that jointly performs Semantic OCCupancy grid mapping and LiDAR scan alignment. Each map voxel encodes geometric and semantic statistics, enabling adaptive point-to-point or point-to-plane ICP based on local planarity. Further, the occupancy grid naturally filters dynamic objects through raycasting-based free-space updates. Across diverse evaluation scenarios, SOCC-ICP achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry and remains robust in geometrically degenerate environments, even in the absence of semantic cues. When semantic labels are available, integrating them into map construction, downsampling, and correspondence weighting yields further accuracy gains. By unifying odometry and semantic occupancy grid mapping within a single representation, SOCC-ICP eliminates redundant map structures and directly provides a map suitable for downstream robotic applications.

</details>


### [262] [CLOVER: Closed-Loop Value Estimation \& Ranking for End-to-End Autonomous Driving Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15120)
*Sining Ang,Yuguang Yang,Canyu Chen,Yan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CLOVER is a closed-loop, scorer-driven generator–solver framework for end-to-end autonomous driving planning that improves proposal coverage and ranking via evaluator-guided pseudo-experts and self-distillation, achieving state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM/NavHard and competitive nuScenes open-loop metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation-learning for end-to-end driving often trains on a single logged trajectory while evaluation uses rule-based planning metrics. This leads to a training–evaluation mismatch, particularly harming proposal-selection planners that rely on candidate sets and scorer quality.

Method: A generator produces diverse candidate trajectories and a scorer predicts planning-metric sub-scores to rank them at inference. The framework creates evaluator-filtered pseudo-expert trajectories to broaden proposals, and trains the generator with set-level coverage supervision. Conservative closed-loop self-distillation is used: the scorer is fitted to true evaluator sub-scores on generated proposals; the generator is updated toward teacher-selected top-k and vector-Pareto targets with stability regularization. The paper analyzes when an imperfect scorer helps, showing reliability when targets enriched by the true evaluator and updates remain conservative.

Result: On NAVSIM, CLOVER achieves 94.5 PDMS and 90.4 EPDMS (state of the art). On the more challenging NavHard split, it obtains 48.3 EPDMS (matching the strongest reported result). In supplementary nuScenes open-loop evaluation, CLOVER shows the lowest L2 error and collision rate among compared methods.

Conclusion: CLOVER mitigates training–evaluation mismatch by expanding proposal coverage and leveraging scorer-driven refinement within a conservative closed loop, achieving strong state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks and open-source code release.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving planners are commonly trained by imitating a single logged trajectory, yet evaluated by rule-based planning metrics that measure safety, feasibility, progress, and comfort. This creates a training--evaluation mismatch: trajectories close to the logged path may violate planning rules, while alternatives farther from the demonstration can remain valid and high-scoring. The mismatch is especially limiting for proposal-selection planners, whose performance depends on candidate-set coverage and scorer ranking quality. We propose CLOVER, a Closed-LOop Value Estimation and Ranking framework for end-to-end autonomous driving planning. CLOVER follows a lightweight generator--scorer formulation: a generator produces diverse candidate trajectories, and a scorer predicts planning-metric sub-scores to rank them at inference time. To expand proposal support beyond single-trajectory imitation, CLOVER constructs evaluator-filtered pseudo-expert trajectories and trains the generator with set-level coverage supervision. It then performs conservative closed-loop self-distillation: the scorer is fitted to true evaluator sub-scores on generated proposals, while the generator is refined toward teacher-selected top-$k$ and vector-Pareto targets with stability regularization. We analyze when an imperfect scorer can improve the generator, showing that scorer-mediated refinement is reliable when scorer-selected targets are enriched under the true evaluator and updates remain conservative. On NAVSIM, CLOVER achieves 94.5 PDMS and 90.4 EPDMS, establishing a new state of the art. On the more challenging NavHard split, it obtains 48.3 EPDMS, matching the strongest reported result. On supplementary nuScenes open-loop evaluation, CLOVER achieves the lowest L2 error and collision rate among compared methods. Code data will be released at https://github.com/WilliamXuanYu/CLOVER.

</details>


### [263] [CoCo-InEKF: State Estimation with Learned Contact Covariances in Dynamic, Contact-Rich Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15122)
*Michael Baumgartner,David Müller,Agon Serifi,Ruben Grandia,Espen Knoop,Markus Gross,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoCo-InEKF uses a differentiable invariant EKF with learned continuous contact covariances to robustly estimate velocity in dynamic legged robots, outperforming binary-contact methods without requiring ground-truth contact labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust state estimation in highly dynamic, contact-rich legged locomotion is hindered by reliance on binary contact states, which fail to capture partial contact, directional slippage, or transient loss of contact.

Method: Introduce CoCo-InEKF, an invariant EKF that replaces binary contact indicators with continuous contact velocity covariances. A lightweight neural network predicts these covariances for predefined contact candidate points using a state-error loss, enabling end-to-end training without heuristic ground-truth contact labels. Includes an automated contact candidate selection procedure and design that is insensitive to exact placement.

Result: On a bipedal robot, the approach achieves a better accuracy-efficiency tradeoff for linear velocity estimation and improved filter consistency relative to baselines, enabling robust dancing and complex ground interactions in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Learning continuous contact covariances within a differentiable invariant EKF enhances robustness and efficiency of state estimation in dynamic legged locomotion, reducing dependence on ground-truth contact labels and accommodating nuanced contact conditions.

Abstract: Robust state estimation for highly dynamic motion of legged robots remains challenging, especially in dynamic, contact-rich scenarios. Traditional approaches often rely on binary contact states that fail to capture the nuances of partial contact or directional slippage. This paper presents CoCo-InEKF, a differentiable invariant extended Kalman filter that utilizes continuous contact velocity covariances instead of binary contact states. These learned covariances allow the method to dynamically modulate contact confidence, accounting for more nuanced conditions ranging from firm contact to directional slippage or no contact. To predict these covariances for a set of predefined contact candidate points, we employ a lightweight neural network trained end-to-end using a state-error loss. This approach eliminates the need for heuristic ground-truth contact labels. In addition, we propose an automated contact candidate selection procedure and demonstrate that our method is insensitive to their exact placement. Experiments on a bipedal robot demonstrate a superior accuracy-efficiency tradeoff for linear velocity estimation, as well as improved filter consistency compared to baseline methods. This enables the robust execution of challenging motions, including dancing and complex ground interactions -- both in simulation and in the real world.

</details>


### [264] [Pelican-Unified 1.0: A Unified Embodied Intelligence Model for Understanding, Reasoning, Imagination and Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15153)
*Yi Zhang,Yinda Chen,Che Liu,Zeyuan Ding,Jin Xu,Shilong Zou,Junwei Liao,Jiayu Hu,Xiancong Ren,Xiaopeng Zhang,Yechi Liu,Haoyuan Shi,Zecong Tang,Haosong Sun,Renwen Cui,Kuishu Wu,Wenhai Liu,Yang Xu,Yingji Zhang,Yidong Wang,Senkang Hu,Jinpeng Lu,Nga Teng Chan,Yechen Wu,Yong Dai,Jian Tang,Xiaozhu Ju*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pelican-Unified 1.0 presents an embodied foundation model using a single shared VLM to unify understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action, yielding strong cross-domain performance without sacrificing specialist capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome fragmentation across separate expert systems by training a single model that jointly handles scene understanding, reasoning, imagination (future planning), and action generation.

Method: A single Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as both understanding and unified reasoning module, autoregressively producing chains of thought in one pass and projecting the final hidden state into a dense latent variable. A Unified Future Generator (UFG) conditions on this latent and jointly generates future videos and actions via two modality-specific heads within a single denoising process. Language, video, and action losses backpropagate into the shared representation for joint optimization across understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action.

Result: With one checkpoint, Pelican-Unified 1.0 attains 64.7 on eight VLM benchmarks (best among comparable-scale models), 66.03 on WorldArena (ranked first), and 93.5 on RoboTwin (second-best average among action methods).

Conclusion: The unified approach preserves specialist strengths while integrating understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action, demonstrating that unification does not incur performance trade-offs.

Abstract: We present Pelican-Unified 1.0, the first embodied foundation model trained according to the principle of unification. Pelican-Unified 1.0 uses a single VLM as a unified understanding module, mapping scenes, instructions, visual contexts, and action histories into a shared semantic space. The same VLM also serves as a unified reasoning module, autoregressively producing task-, action-, and future-oriented chains of thought in a single forward pass and projecting the final hidden state into a dense latent variable. A Unified Future Generator (UFG) then conditions on this latent variable and jointly generates future videos and future actions through two modality-specific output heads within the same denoising process. The language, video, and action losses are all backpropagated into the shared representation, enabling the model to jointly optimize understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action during training, rather than training three isolated expert systems.
  Experiments demonstrate that unification does not imply compromise. With a single checkpoint, Pelican-Unified 1.0 achieves strong performance across all three capabilities: 64.7 on eight VLM benchmarks, the best among comparable-scale models; 66.03 on WorldArena, ranking first; and 93.5 on RoboTwin, the second-best average among compared action methods. These results show that the unified paradigm succeeds in preserving specialist strength while bringing understanding, reasoning, imagination, and action into one model.

</details>


### [265] [Hand-in-the-Loop: Improving Dexterous VLA via Seamless Interventional Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15157)
*Zhuohang Li,Liqun Huang,Wei Xu,Zhengming Zhu,Nie Lin,Xiao Ma,Xinjun Sheng,Ruoshi Wen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HandITL blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to prevent gesture jumps during takeover in bimanual dexterous manipulation. It significantly reduces takeover jitter, improves post-takeover robustness, and yields higher-quality data for policy refinement, with policies trained on HandITL data outperforming teleoperation-based data across multiple long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous manipulation with high-DoF hands suffers from compounding errors in Vision-Language-Action models. Interactive Imitation Learning can help via human takeover data, but a mismatch between human commands and policy execution at takeover causes gesture jumps, destabilizing manipulation and hindering learning.

Method: Hand-in-the-Loop (HandITL) is a seamless intervention framework that blends human corrective input with autonomous policy execution to suppress abrupt configuration changes ('gesture jumps') during takeover, enabling smooth bimanual manipulation. Evaluation compares HandITL against direct teleoperation takeover on tasks requiring bimanual coordination, tool use, and long-horizon manipulation; it also uses HandITL-collected data to refine policies.

Result: HandITL reduces takeover jitter by 99.8% and preserves robust post-takeover manipulation, decreasing grasp failures by 87.5% and mean completion time by 19.1%. Policies trained on HandITL-collected data outperform those trained on standard teleoperation data by 19% on average across three long-horizon dexterous tasks.

Conclusion: HandITL provides a practical solution to the gesture-jump problem in high-DoF manipulation, enabling smoother takeover and more effective data collection for policy refinement. The approach demonstrates robust performance across bimanual tasks and suggests broad applicability for IIL-based improvement of dexterous policies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are prone to compounding errors in dexterous manipulation, where high-dimensional action spaces and contact-rich dynamics amplify small policy deviations over long horizons. While Interactive Imitation Learning (IIL) can refine policies through human takeover data, applying it to high-degree-of-freedom (DoF) robotic hands remains challenging due to a command mismatch between human teleoperation and policy execution at the takeover moment, which causes abrupt robot-hand configuration changes, or "gesture jumps". We present Hand-in-the-Loop (HandITL), a seamless human-in-the-loop intervention method that blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to avoid gesture jumps during bimanual dexterous manipulation. Compared with direct teleoperation takeover, HandITL reduces takeover jitter by 99.8% and preserves robust post-takeover manipulation, reducing grasp failures by 87.5% and mean completion time by 19.1%. We validate HandITL on tasks requiring bimanual coordination, tool use, and fine-grained long-horizon manipulation. When used to collect intervention data for policy refinement, HandITL yields policies that outperform those trained with standard teleoperation data by 19% on average across three long-horizon dexterous tasks.

</details>


### [266] [Let Robots Feel Your Touch: Visuo-Tactile Cortical Alignment for Embodied Mirror Resonance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14571)
*Tianfang Zhu,Ning An,Rui Wang,Jiasi Gao,Qingming Luo,Anan Li,Guyue Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Mirror Touch Net enables robotic cross-modal visuo-tactile resonance by aligning visual and tactile representations across semantic, distributional, and geometric constraints, enabling millimeter-scale tactile prediction from RGB images and reflexive responses to observed touch; extends to human-hand observations; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To operationalize mirro touch-like visuo-tactile resonance in robots, bridging cortical correspondence theory with practical autonomous perception and empathic interaction.

Method: Develop Mirror Touch Net with multi-level constraints enforcing semantic, distributional, and geometric alignment between visual and tactile manifolds; predict tactile signals for 1,140 taxels from RGB; use manifold analysis to show geometry reshaping; extend to cross-domain human hand observations.

Result: Accurate prediction of tactile signals across 1,140 taxels from RGB images; evidence that alignment reduces cross-modal mapping complexity; demonstration of tactile prediction and reflexive responses in cross-domain human hands; code availability.

Conclusion: Links neural visuo-tactile resonance to robotic perception, providing an explainable framework for anticipatory touch and empathetic HRI; opens avenue for tactile-enabled perception in robots.

Abstract: Observing touch on another's body can elicit corresponding tactile sensations in the observer, a phenomenon termed mirror touch that supports empathy and social perception. This visuo-tactile resonance is thought to rely on structural correspondence between visual and somatosensory cortices, yet robotic systems lack computational frameworks that instantiate this principle. Here we demonstrate that cortical correspondence can be operationalized to endow robots with mirror touch. We introduce Mirror Touch Net, which imposes semantic, distributional and geometric alignment between visual and tactile representations through multi-level constraints, enabling prediction of millimetre-scale tactile signals across 1,140 taxels on a robotic hand from RGB images. Manifold analysis reveals that these constraints reshape visual representations into geometry consistent with the tactile manifold, reducing the complexity of cross-modal mapping. Extending this alignment framework to cross-domain observations of human hands enables tactile prediction and reflexive responses to observed human touch. Our results link a neural principle of visuo-tactile resonance to robotic perception, providing an explainable route towards anticipatory touch and empathic human-robot interaction. Code is available at https://github.com/fun0515/Mirror-Touch-Net.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [267] [Vision-Based Runtime Monitoring under Varying Specifications using Semantic Latent Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13923)
*Bardh Hoxha,Oliver Schön,Hideki Okamoto,Lars Lindemann,Georgios Fainekos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents a reusable, certified runtime monitor for past-time STL from images under partial observability, introducing semantic-basis robustness as the minimal prediction target and a rolling predictor; provides conformal guarantees and empirical validation on driving data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need safety-certified monitoring from visual data with finite-sample guarantees that generalize across a fragment of ptSTL, avoiding per-formula retraining while handling partial observability.

Method: Proves that the semantic-basis vector of atom robustness is the minimal target within a class of monotone, 1-Lipschitz reusable interfaces; maps formula evaluation to a deterministic parse-tree decoder; uses a single conformal calibration pass per fragment; introduces a rolling prediction monitor that reconstructs history online.

Result: Rolling predictor yields tighter bounds at short horizons; semantic-basis monitor is up to 4× tighter at long horizons; both satisfy conformal coverage empirically on Waymo driving data and on a pedestrian-crossroad benchmark.

Conclusion: Provides a practical, reusable framework for certified monitoring of ptSTL from visual data under partial observability, enabling fragment-wide certification without retraining and with empirical conformal guarantees.

Abstract: We study certified runtime monitoring of past-time signal temporal logic (ptSTL) from visual observations under partial observability. The monitor must infer safety-relevant quantities from images and provide finite-sample guarantees, while being \emph{reusable}: once trained and calibrated, it should certify any formula in a target fragment without per-formula retraining. For fragments induced by a finite dictionary of temporal atoms, we prove that the \emph{semantic basis}, the vector of atom robustness scores, is the minimum prediction target within the class of monotone, 1-Lipschitz reusable interfaces: any formula is evaluated by a deterministic decoder derived from the parse tree, and a single conformal calibration pass certifies the entire fragment with no union bound. We also introduce a \emph{rolling prediction monitor} that predicts only current predicate values and reconstructs temporal history online; this is easier to learn but grows conservative at long horizons. On a pedestrian-crossroad benchmark, rolling achieves tighter certified bounds at short horizons while the semantic-basis monitor is up to 4-times tighter at long horizons. We validate the presented monitors on real-world Waymo driving data, where both monitors satisfy the conformal coverage guarantee empirically.

</details>


### [268] [Towards the Next Frontier of LLMs, Training on Private Data: A Cross-Domain Benchmark for Federated Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13936)
*Daniel M. Jimenez-Gutierrez,Enrique Zuazua,Georgios Kellaris,Joaquin del Rio,Oleksii Sliusarenko,Xabi Uribe-Etxebarria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated PEFT enables privacy-preserving LLM adaptation across private, non-IID data silos; QLoRA/IA3 improve efficiency with minimal accuracy loss, approaching centralized training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlock valuable private domain data (healthcare/finance) for LLM adaptation while preserving privacy and managing non-IID data across institutions.

Method: Federated fine-tuning on the Sherpa.ai FL platform; compare LoRA, QLoRA, IA3 across MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, and FiQA-SA; cross-domain, non-IID settings; evaluate across pretrained backbones; consider efficiency (Green AI).

Result: Federated fine-tuning achieves performance close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning; QLoRA and IA3 offer efficiency gains with limited accuracy degradation.

Conclusion: Federated PEFT is a viable approach for adapting LLMs when data cannot be shared, enabling domain-specific utility in regulated sectors.

Abstract: The recent success of large language models (LLMs) has been largely driven by vast public datasets. However, the next frontier for LLM development lies beyond public data. Much of the world's most valuable information is private, especially in highly regulated sectors such as healthcare and finance, where data include patient histories or customer communications. Unlocking this data could represent a major leap forward, enabling LLMs with deeper domain expertise and stronger real-world utility. Yet, these data cannot be shared because they are distributed across institutions and constrained by privacy, regulatory, and organizational barriers. Moreover, institutional datasets are typically non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID), differing across sites in population characteristics, data modalities, documentation patterns, and task-specific label distributions.
  In this paper, we demonstrate a practical approach to unlocking private and distributed institutional data for LLM adaptation through federated collaboration across data silos. Built on the Sherpa.ai Federated Learning platform, our framework enables nodes to jointly fine-tune a shared LLM without exchanging private data. We evaluate this approach through a cross-domain benchmark in healthcare and finance, using four closed-ended question answering and classification datasets: MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, and FiQA-SA. We compare three parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies-LoRA, QLoRA, and IA3-across pretrained backbones under non-IID settings reflecting institutional data heterogeneity. Our results show that federated fine-tuning performs close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning. From a Green AI perspective, QLoRA and IA3 improve efficiency with limited accuracy degradation, supporting federated PEFT as a viable approach for adapting LLMs where data cannot be shared.

</details>


### [269] [EvolveMem:Self-Evolving Memory Architecture via AutoResearch for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13941)
*Jiaqi Liu,Xinyu Ye,Peng Xia,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Mingyu Ding,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-evolving memory (EvolveMem) co-evolves stored knowledge and retrieval configuration via an LLM-powered diagnosis loop, enabling autonomous optimization and showing cross-benchmark gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the rigidity of fixed retrieval infrastructure in long-term memory for multi-session LLM agents by enabling co-evolution of knowledge and retrieval mechanisms through an AutoResearch loop.

Method: Expose retrieval configuration as a structured action space; use an LLM-powered diagnosis module to read per-question failure logs, identify root causes, and propose targeted configuration adjustments; apply changes via a guarded meta-analyzer with revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards; start from a minimal baseline and autonomously converge, discovering new configuration dimensions beyond the original action space; results in transferable configurations across benchmarks.

Result: On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and by 78.0% relative to the minimal baseline; on MemBench, it exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer positively across benchmarks, suggesting universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Self-evolution via AutoResearch yields effective retrieval strategies and cross-benchmark transfer, indicating that co-evolution of memory and retrieval configuration can outperform static baselines and generalize beyond individual benchmarks.

Abstract: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that operate across multiple sessions, yet existing memory systems treat retrieval infrastructure as fixed: stored content evolves while scoring functions, fusion strategies, and answer-generation policies remain frozen at deployment. We argue that truly adaptive memory requires co-evolution at two levels: the stored knowledge and the retrieval mechanism that queries it. We present EvolveMem, a self-evolving memory architecture that exposes its full retrieval configuration as a structured action space optimized by an LLM-powered diagnosis module. In each evolution round, the module reads per-question failure logs, identifies root causes, and proposes targeted configuration adjustments; a guarded meta-analyzer applies them with automatic revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards. This closed-loop self-evolution realizes an AutoResearch process: the system autonomously conducts iterative research cycles on its own architecture, replacing manual configuration tuning. Starting from a minimal baseline, the process converges autonomously, discovering effective retrieval strategies including entirely new configuration dimensions not present in the original action space. On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and achieves a 78.0% relative improvement over the minimal baseline. On MemBench, EvolveMem exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer across benchmarks with positive rather than catastrophic transfer, indicating that the self-evolution process captures universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleMem.

</details>


### [270] [Collider-Bench: Benchmarking AI Agents with Particle Physics Analysis Reproduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13950)
*Darius A. Faroughy,Sofia Palacios Schweitzer,Ian Pang,Siddharth Mishra-Sharma,David Shih*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Collider-Bench is a new benchmark to test whether LLM agents can reproduce LHC experimental analyses using only public papers and open software. It converts published analyses into executable simulation-and-selection pipelines, evaluates predicted yields with histogram metrics, and uses an LLM judge to detect failures. Across agents, no one reliably beats a physicist-in-the-loop baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducibility and automation gaps in real scientific workflows: public toolchains approximate internal software and papers omit implementation details, making faithful reconstruction difficult. There is a need for long-horizon, tool-using benchmarks that reflect authentic experimental analyses.

Method: Provide a containerized sandbox with event-simulation tools; tasks derived from LHC searches require turning published analyses into an executable pipeline that predicts collision event yields in specified signal regions. Evaluation uses standard histogram metrics, reports computational cost per task, and employs an LLM judge to detect fabrications, hallucinations, and duplications. An initial task set is released, and experiments are run across a ladder of general-purpose coding agents.

Result: On average, no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop solution. The benchmark reveals the current limitations of autonomous LLM agents in reproducing complex experimental analyses, despite structured evaluation and tooling.

Conclusion: Reproducing real-world experimental analyses with automated agents remains challenging. Collider-Bench provides a principled, reproducible framework for assessing and guiding improvements in long-horizon, domain-specific agent reasoning and tool use, highlighting the gap to reach parity with human experts.

Abstract: Autonomous language-model agents are increasingly evaluated on long-horizon tool-use tasks, but existing benchmarks rarely capture the complexity and nuance of real scientific work. To address this gap, we introduce Collider-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can reproduce experimental analyses from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) using only public papers and open scientific software. Such analyses are often difficult to reproduce because the public toolchain only approximates the software used internally by the experimental collaborations, while the published papers inevitably omit implementation details needed for a faithful reconstruction. Agents must therefore rely on physical reasoning, domain knowledge, and trial-and-error to fill these gaps. Each task requires the agent to turn a published analysis into an executable simulation-and-selection pipeline and submit predicted collision event yields in specified signal regions. These predictions are evaluated with standard histogram metrics that provide continuous fidelity scores without a hand-written rubric. We also report the computational cost incurred by each agent per task. Finally, we evaluate the codebase and full session trace using an LLM judge to catch qualitative failure modes such as fabrications, hallucinations and duplications. We release an initial set of tasks drawn from LHC searches, together with a containerized sandbox and event simulation tools. We evaluate across a capability ladder of general purpose coding agents. Our results show that on average no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop solution.

</details>


### [271] [WarmPrior: Straightening Flow-Matching Policies with Temporal Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13959)
*Sinjae Kang,Chanyoung Kim,Kaixin Wang,Li Zhao,Kimin Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Replacing Gaussian sources with WarmPrior (history-based prior) improves success and sample efficiency in diffusion/flow-based visuomotor control; straighter probability paths and better exploration in prior-space RL; highlights source distribution as a key design axis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve generative control for robotics by using a temporally grounded, history-derived prior to guide diffusion-based policies, addressing inefficiencies and exploration challenges.

Method: Introduce WarmPrior built from recent action history, integrate it as the source distribution in diffusion/flow matching frameworks for visuomotor control; compare against Gaussian sources; analyze probability-path geometry; extend to prior-space RL.

Result: Consistent gains in robotic manipulation success rates; observation of straighter probability paths akin to optimal-transport couplings; improved sample efficiency and final performance in prior-space RL.

Conclusion: The choice of source distribution is a meaningful, underexplored design axis in generative robot control; WarmPrior provides a simple, effective way to bias generation toward favorable trajectories and exploration patterns.

Abstract: Generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching have become a dominant paradigm for visuomotor robotic control. We show that replacing the standard Gaussian source distribution with WarmPrior, a simple temporally grounded prior constructed from readily available recent action history, consistently improves success rates on robotic manipulation tasks. We trace this gain to markedly straighter probability paths, echoing the effect of optimal-transport couplings in Rectified Flow. Beyond standard behavior cloning, WarmPrior also reshapes the exploration distribution in prior-space reinforcement learning, improving both sample efficiency and final performance. Collectively, these results identify the source distribution as an important and underexplored design axis in generative robot control.

</details>


### [272] [Towards Resource-Efficient LLMs: End-to-End Energy Accounting of Distillation Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13981)
*Katherine Lambert,Sasha Luccioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents an end-to-end energy accounting framework for distillation pipelines in NLP, measuring full GPU energy across stages (including teacher workloads), comparing logit-based distillation and synthetic-data fine-tuning, and releasing open-source tools to standardize energy-aware distillation research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address rising GPU demand and electricity concerns in large language models, noting that much of distillation's claimed efficiency ignores end-to-end energy costs such as data generation, logit caching, and evaluation.

Method: Stage-wise logging of GPU power consumption to separate energy use across distillation phases; empirical measurement of two distillation strategies (logit-based knowledge distillation and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning); construction of energy-quality Pareto frontiers; derivation of design rules under energy and budget constraints; release of open-source measurement harness and accounting protocol for reproducible evaluation.

Result: Quantified end-to-end energy and emissions for two common distillation methods, exposed trade-offs via energy-quality Pareto frontiers, and provided actionable design rules; open-source harness and protocol to standardize energy accounting in distillation research.

Conclusion: End-to-end energy accounting is essential for selecting distillation methods and hyperparameters under energy constraints; the work enables reproducible, energy-aware distillation research and informs practical deployment decisions.

Abstract: The rise in deployment of large language models has driven a surge in GPU demand and datacenter scaling, raising concerns about electricity use, grid stress, and the impacts of modern AI workloads. Distillation is often promoted as one of the most effective paths to obtain cheaper, more efficient models, yet these claims rarely account for the full end-to-end energy and resource costs, including crucial teacher-side workloads such as data generation, logit caching, and evaluation. We present a comprehensive energy accounting framework that measures the complete computational cost of distillation pipelines via detailed stage-wise tracking of GPU device power consumption. In our experiments, we separate and log empirical energy use across distinct phases and systematically measure the energy and emissions of two common distillation methods: the classic logit-based knowledge distillation and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning, constructing energy-quality Pareto frontiers that expose the previously ignored costs. From these measurements and analyses, we derive practical design rules for selecting distillation methods and hyperparameters under energy and budget constraints, and release an open-source measurement harness and accounting protocol to provide a standardized foundation for comparable, reproducible distillation research, explicitly accountable for complete pipeline energy impact.

</details>


### [273] [TabPFN-3: Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13986)
*Léo Grinsztajn,Klemens Flöge,Oscar Key,Felix Birkel,Philipp Jund,Brendan Roof,Mihir Manium,Shi Bin,Hoo,Magnus Bühler,Anurag Garg,Dominik Safaric,Jake Robertson,Benjamin Jäger,Simone Alessi,Adrian Hayler,Vladyslav Moroshan,Lennart Purucker,Philipp Singer,Alan Arazi,Julien Siems,Jan Hendrik Metzen,Georg Grab,Nick Erickson,Siyuan Guo,Eliott Kalfon,Simon Bing,David Salinas,Clara Cornu,Lilly Charlotte Wehrhahn,Diana Kriuchkova,Kursat Kaya,Lydia Sidhoum,Marie Salmon,Jerry Chen,Madelon Hulsebos,Yann LeCun,Samuel Müller,Bernhard Schölkopf,Sauraj Gambhir,Noah Hollmann,Frank Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-3 scales tabular foundation models to 1M-row datasets with synthetic pretraining, achieving state-of-the-art speed and accuracy across tabular, relational, and tabular-text tasks; TabPFN-3-Plus and related variants provide test-time compute scaling and further dominance over non-TabPFN models, with substantial speedups and scalable inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome scalability and inference bottlenecks in tabular foundation models while preserving or improving predictive performance across diverse tabular data modalities (standard tabular data, relational data, and tabular-text).

Method: Pretraining exclusively on synthetic data; architectural/algorithmic improvements in TabPFN-3; test-time compute scaling via TabPFN-3-Plus; reduced KV cache and row-chunking enabling 1M-row inference; specialized checkpoints (TabPFN-TS-3); SHAP acceleration.

Result: TabPFN-3 achieves SOTA on TabArena, beating tuned/enesmbles and dominating speed/performance frontier; strong performance on datasets with many classes; outperforms 8-hour-tuned GBDT baselines up to 1M rows and 200 features; TabPFN-3-Plus beats all non-TabPFN models on TabArena by 200+ Elo (420 Elo on largest subset), and outperforms AutoGluon 1.5 Extreme while being 10x faster; extends SOTA to RelBenchV1 and TabSTAR; SHAP computation up to 120x faster; 20x speedup over TabPFN-2.5; scalable to 1M rows on a single H100 with fast inference.

Conclusion: TabPFN-3 markedly advances tabular foundation models in both scalability and predictive performance, enabling SOTA results across tabular, relational, and tabular-text data with substantial practical speedups and memory efficiency; the approach shows strong potential for real-world deployment at large scales.

Abstract: Tabular data underpins most high-value prediction problems in science and industry, and TabPFN has driven the foundation model revolution for this modality. Designed with feedback from our users, TabPFN-3 builds on this foundation to scale state-of-the-art performance to datasets with 1M training rows and substantially reduce training and inference time. Pretrained exclusively on synthetic data from our prior, TabPFN-3 dramatically pushes the frontier of tabular prediction and brings substantial gains on time series, relational, and tabular-text data. On the standard tabular benchmark TabArena, a forward pass of TabPFN-3 outperforms all other models, including tuned and ensembled baselines, by a significant margin, and pareto-dominates the speed/performance frontier. On more diverse datasets, TabPFN-3 ranks first on datasets with many classes, and beats 8-hour-tuned gradient-boosted-tree baselines on datasets up to 1M training rows and 200 features. TabPFN-3 introduces test-time compute scaling to tabular foundation models. Our API offering TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) exploits this to beat all non-TabPFN models by over 200 Elo on TabArena, rising to 420 Elo on the largest data subset, and outperforms AutoGluon 1.5 extreme while being 10x faster, without using LLMs, real data, internet search or any other model besides TabPFN. TabPFN-3 extends the capabilities of our models, enabling SOTA prediction on relational data (new SOTA foundation model on RelBenchV1) and tabular-text data (SOTA on TabSTAR via TabPFN-3-Plus); and improves existing integrations: a specialized checkpoint, TabPFN-TS-3, ranks 2nd on the time-series benchmark fev-bench, and SHAP-value computation is up to 120x faster. TabPFN-3 achieves this performance while being up to 20x faster than TabPFN-2.5. In addition, a reduced KV cache and row-chunking scale to 1M rows on one H100 with fast inference speed.

</details>


### [274] [Neural Fields for NV-Center Inverse Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13988)
*Zhixuan Zhao,Tao Zhong,Yixun Hu,Nathalie P. de Leon,Christine Allen-Blanchette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeTMY: a physics-faithful neural inverse for NV-center noise-sensing using a corrected tensor forward operator; it overcomes center-collapse in density-space optimizations and achieves superior localization on synthetic sparse reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forward models for NV-based noise spectroscopy are nonlinear and spectrally coupled; hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks often fail under these physics; a robust, physics-faithful inverse method is needed.

Method: Introduce NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to a differentiable NV forward model with a corrected tensor dipolar operator; employs annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses; optimization-based, not supervised.

Result: On synthetic reconstructions generated using the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves best localization and distributional metrics across sparse scenarios.

Conclusion: NV quantum sensing is a valuable testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems; NeTMY mitigates center-collapse by smoothing updates via its parameterization.

Abstract: Inverse problems in scientific sensing are often solved with either hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks trained on simulated labels, yet both can fail when the forward model is nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate. We study this issue for noise sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, where a quantum sensor measures magnetic-noise spectra generated by sparse spin sources. We show that replacing a common scalar/coherent forward approximation with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape and exposes a center-collapse failure mode in free-density optimization. We propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses. Across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics in the tested benchmark. Mechanism experiments show that NeTMY does not directly execute the raw density-space gradient; its parameterization smooths and redistributes updates, mitigating the center-collapse pathology. These results position NV quantum sensing as a useful testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

</details>


### [275] [HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13997)
*Tao Zhong,Dongzhe Zheng,Christine Allen-Blanchette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identify and exploit the harmonic kernel of the MoE's interaction graph to enable learning-free and hybrid compressions that respect higher-order compatibility among experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse MoE inference is costly; naive pairwise scoring of expert compatibility fails due to higher-order (triple) obstruction. A graph/topology-based obstruction prevents naive compressions. The paper motivates a topology-aware compression method that uses higher-order signals.

Method: Model experts as vertices of a 2-complex; edges carry KL merge barriers, faces carry triplet barriers. Compute the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian to isolate the kernel component of the barrier signal via Hodge decomposition. Turn this into a selection objective (HodgeCover) that greedily covers harmonic-critical edges and triangles; combine with off-the-shelf weight pruning for survivors.

Result: Across three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines for expert reduction, leads on aggressive-compression frontier in the hybrid axis, and achieves balanced retention across the four Hodge components.

Conclusion: Exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure shifts which compressor wins in the regime that matters, demonstrating the value of topology-aware, higher-order analysis for model compression.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers route tokens through a handful of experts, and learning-free compression of these layers reduces inference cost without retraining. A subtle obstruction blocks every existing compressor in this family: three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible cycle when merged together, so any score that ranks experts on pairwise signals is structurally blind to which triples are jointly mergeable. We show the obstruction is a precise mathematical object, the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry KL merge barriers, and whose faces carry triplet barriers; Hodge-decomposing the edge-barrier signal isolates the kernel exactly. We turn the diagnostic into a selection objective: HodgeCover greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and a hybrid variant of HodgeCover pairs it with off-the-shelf weight pruning on survivors. On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on the expert-reduction axis, leads on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid axis, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components. These results show that exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure changes which compressor wins at the regime that matters most.

</details>


### [276] [Support Before Frequency in Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13999)
*Adrian Müller,Antoine Gonon,Zebang Shen,Ya-Ping Hsieh,Niao He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Discrete diffusion models exhibit a learning hierarchy: in the small-noise regime, reverse denoising edits decompose into a leading scale (validity) and a finer coefficient (within-scale frequencies). Uniform diffusion yields a three-way validity change (improving/preserving/worsening); absorbing diffusion concentrates mass on validity-improving moves. Empirical results show support localization precedes within-support frequency ranking, with rate separation matching the predicted mechanism-dependent behavior, implying models learn data support before frequencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how discrete diffusion model training objectives organize learning, specifically whether and how learning progresses from global data validity (support) to finer frequency information, and how diffusion mechanism (uniform vs absorbing) influences this ordering.

Method: Perform a theoretical analysis in the small-noise regime for uniform and absorbing diffusion, showing that a single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale and a finer coefficient. Derive a trichotomy for uniform diffusion and a leading-order mass on validity-improving moves for absorbing diffusion. Validate predictions with experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks.

Result: The reverse process is hierarchical: recovering data validity (support) requires estimating only the scale order of reverse probabilities, whereas recovering data frequencies requires coefficient-level estimation. Uniform diffusion exhibits a trichotomy of edits (validity-improving, -preserving, -worsening), absorbing diffusion emphasizes validity-improving moves. Empirically, support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking; the observed rate separation aligns with the theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: Discrete diffusion models tend to learn and locate data support before modeling data frequencies, with the ordering and rate of this learning depending on the diffusion mechanism (uniform vs absorbing).

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models are increasingly competitive for language modeling, yet it remains unclear how their denoising objectives organize learning. Although these objectives target the full data distribution, we show that the exact reverse process induces a hierarchy between coarse support information and finer frequency information. For uniform and absorbing (a.k.a. masking) diffusion, we prove that, in the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps, each single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale, determined by whether it moves toward the data support (e.g., grammatically valid sentences), and a finer coefficient, determining relative probabilities within the same scale. Thus, recovering validity structure only requires learning the correct order of magnitude of reverse probabilities, whereas recovering data frequencies requires coefficient-level estimation. The separation is mechanism-dependent: uniform diffusion exhibits a trichotomy into validity-improving, validity-preserving, and validity-worsening edits, while absorbing diffusion places its leading-order mass on validity-improving moves. Experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks support these predictions: support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking, and the contrast between uniform and absorbing diffusion matches the predicted rate separation. Together, our results suggest that discrete diffusion models learn data support before data frequencies.

</details>


### [277] [Dywave: Event-Aligned Dynamic Tokenization for Heterogeneous IoT Sensing Signal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14014)
*Tomoyoshi Kimura,Denizhan Kara,Jinyang Li,Hongjue Zhao,Yigong Hu,Yizhuo Chen,Xiaomin Ouyang,Shengzhong Liu,Tarek Abdelzaher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic wavelet-based tokenization (Dywave) for IoT sensing; constructs compact, event-aligned tokens via hierarchical wavelet decomposition, adaptive boundary detection, and redundancy compression; yields up to 12% accuracy gains and up to 75% shorter input tokens across five real-world datasets, with robustness to domain shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT sensing signals are non-stationary and multi-scale, making standard tokenization ineffective at preserving semantic temporal structure; there is a need for representations that align with intrinsic events and physical processes.

Method: Employ wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition to capture temporal scales; identify meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to semantic events; adaptively compress redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence; generate compact input tokens for mainstream sequence models.

Result: Across five IoT datasets covering activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection, Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy and reduces input token lengths by up to 75%, while improving robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

Conclusion: Dywave provides a dynamic, wavelet-driven tokenization that aligns with the intrinsic temporal structure of IoT signals, enhancing accuracy and efficiency across diverse sensing tasks and demonstrating robustness to domain variation; potential trade-offs include parameter choices and boundary-detection overhead.

Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) systems continuously collect heterogeneous sensing signals from ubiquitous sensors to support intelligent applications such as human activity analysis, emotion monitoring, and environmental perception. These signals are inherently non-stationary and multi-scale, posing unique challenges for standard tokenization techniques. This paper proposes Dywave, a dynamic tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals that constructs compact input representations aligned with intrinsic temporal structures and underlying physical events. Dywave leverages wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition, identifies meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to underlying semantic events, and adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence. Extensive evaluations on five real-world IoT sensing datasets across activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection demonstrate that Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy, while improving computational efficiency by reducing input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models. Moreover, Dywave exhibits improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

</details>


### [278] [R2R2: Robust Representation for Intensive Experience Reuse via Redundancy Reduction in Self-Predictive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14026)
*Sanghyeob Song,Donghyeok Lee,Jinsik Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: R2R2 adds a non-centered redundancy-reduction regularizer to Self-Predictive Learning to stabilize representations under high Update-to-Data (UTD) regimes in data-scarce RL, addressing zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties; validated on TD7 and SimbaV2-SPL with broad 11-task experiments; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In data-scarce domains (e.g., real-world robotics), heavy data reuse causes overfitting. While prior work focuses on critic bias, representation-level instability in SPL under high UTD remains underexplored. A robust, SPL-compatible representation objective is needed.

Method: Introduce R2R2 (robust representation via redundancy reduction) with a non-centered redundancy-reduction objective aligned with SPL's spectral properties. Theoretical analysis shows conflicts with standard zero-centering are resolved by a non-centered formulation. Apply R2R2 to SPL-native TD7 and extend SimbaV2 with an SPL module (SimbaV2-SPL) to demonstrate orthogonality.

Result: Empirical evaluation across 11 continuous control tasks shows R2R2 mitigates overfitting under UTD=20, improving TD7 by ~22% and yielding additional gains when combined with SimbaV2-SPL. SimbaV2-SPL alone achieves new state-of-the-art. Code available at the project repo.

Conclusion: R2R2 provides a principled, SPL-compatible regularization that stabilizes representations under high data reuse, boosting performance in existing SPL-based and SPL-augmented baselines and advancing state-of-the-art in continuous control benchmarks.

Abstract: For reinforcement learning in data-scarce domains like real-world robotics, intensive data reuse enhances efficiency but induces overfitting. While prior works focus on critic bias, representation-level instability in Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) under high Update-to-Data (UTD) regimes remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2), a regularization method within SPL. We theoretically identify that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties and design a non-centered objective accordingly. We verify R2R2 on SPL-native algorithms like TD7. Furthermore, to demonstrate its orthogonality to prior advancements, we extend the state-of-the-art SimbaV2, which originally lacks SPL, by integrating a tailored SPL module, termed SimbaV2-SPL. Experiments across 11 continuous control tasks confirm that R2R2 effectively mitigates overfitting; specifically, at a UTD ratio of 20, it improves TD7 by ~22% and provides additional gains on top of SimbaV2-SPL, which itself establishes a new state-of-the-art. The code can be found at: https://github.com/songsang7/R2R2

</details>


### [279] [Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention: Learning When to Write by Predicting Future Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14037)
*Gergely Szilvasy,Manuel Faysse,Maria Lomeli,Matthijs Douze,Pierre-Emmanuel Mazaré,Loïc Cabannes,Wen-tau Yih,Hervé Jégou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SP-KV introduces a learnable, dynamic KV pruning mechanism that predicts the utility of past key-value pairs to reduce transformer KV cache size, achieving 3–10x compression with little performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long sequences in modern LLMs are constrained by KV cache memory footprint and bandwidth; fixed compression is suboptimal; a dynamic, input-adaptive approach can substantially reduce memory and speed up decoding while maintaining accuracy.

Method: A lightweight utility predictor scores each key-value pair. Recent KVs remain in a local window; older KVs are cached and only used in global attention if their predicted utility exceeds a threshold. The LLM and predictor are trained jointly end-to-end via next-token prediction loss, starting from pretrained checkpoints, enabling dynamic sparsification without fixed compression ratios.

Result: KV cache size is typically reduced by 3–10× on longer sequences, with substantial gains in memory usage and decoding speed. Validation loss and downstream task performance show little to no degradation. The analysis also reveals structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns, suggesting implications for designing hybrid local-global attention architectures.

Conclusion: SP-KV is an effective, trainable KV-cache reduction mechanism that also provides insights into sparsity patterns useful for future attention design, enabling more scalable long-sequence processing in transformers.

Abstract: Under modern test-time compute and agentic paradigms, language models process ever-longer sequences. Efficient text generation with transformer architectures is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value cache memory footprint and bandwidth. To address this limitation, we introduce Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV), a mechanism designed to predict future KV utility in order to reduce the size of the long-term KV cache. This strategy operates at a fine granularity: a lightweight utility predictor scores each key-value pair, and while recent KVs are always available via a local window, older pairs are written in the cache and used in global attention only if their predicted utility surpasses a given threshold. The LLM and the utility predictor are trained jointly end-to-end exclusively through next-token prediction loss, and are adapted from pretrained LLM checkpoints.
  Rather than enforcing a fixed compression ratio, SP-KV performs dynamic sparsification: the mechanism adapts to the input and typically reduces the KV cache size by a factor of $3$ to $10\times$, longer sequences often being more compressible. This leads to vast improvements in memory usage and decoding speed, with little to no degradation of validation loss nor performance on a broad set of downstream tasks. Beyond serving as an effective KV-cache reduction mechanism, our method reveals structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns that we can use to guide the design of hybrid local-global attention architectures.

</details>


### [280] [Reliability-Gated Source Anchoring for Continual Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14063)
*Vikash Singh,Debargha Ganguly,Weicong Chen,Sabyasachi Sahoo,Sreehari Sankar,Biyao Zhang,Mohsen Harir,Shouren Wang,Osama Zafar,Christian Gagné,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Blind anchoring in continual test-time adaptation under unreliable sources; introduced RMemSafe, a reliability-gated ROID extension using the source posterior entropy to attenuate source-coupled objectives; yields robust improvements and graceful decay across matched continual-corruption tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CTTA relies on a fixed frozen-source anchor; when the source deteriorates (as in CCC-Hard with ResNet-50), fixed anchor harms performance. Need adaptive mechanism to detect source reliability and gate anchor usage.

Method: RMemSafe augments ROID with a reliability gate based on the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy. When entropy is high (posterior near uniform), the gate closes, removing the source anchor and agreement filter; the objective becomes source-agnostic, using ROID base losses plus calibration. Combined with ASR. Evaluated on 9 matched-split continual-corruption cells; includes a controlled source-degradation sweep.

Result: RMemSafe achieves the best performance in 8/9 cells and is the best reset-based method on all 9, outperforming ROID+ASR by 1.05 percentage points on ResNet-50 and 0.48 points on ViT-B/16. Demonstrates 1.13x shallower harm slope under source degradation, consistent with graceful decay; entropy gate detects high-entropy collapse and avoids relying on confidently wrong low-entropy cases.

Conclusion: Reliability-gated source reliance effectively mitigates blind anchoring in CTTA, enabling graceful degradation and improved accuracy across continual-corruption benchmarks. The entropy gate's scope—detecting high-entropy collapses rather than confidently wrong predictions—should be emphasized; potential for broader adoption in source-anchored continual adaptation with reliability gates.

Abstract: Continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) updates a pretrained model online on an unlabeled, non-stationary stream while anchoring it to a frozen source checkpoint. This anchor is useful only when the source remains reliable. On CCC-Hard, however, a ResNet-50 source falls to approximately $1.3\%$ top-$1$ accuracy, while existing source-anchored CTTA methods continue applying the same anchor strength. We call this failure mode blind anchoring and propose RMemSafe, a reliability-gated extension of ROID that uses the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy to attenuate all explicit source-coupled uses in the objective. When the source posterior approaches uniformity, the gate closes: the source anchor and agreement filter vanish, and the objective reduces to a source-agnostic fallback comprising ROID's base losses plus marginal calibration. Combined with ASR, RMemSafe achieves the lowest error on $8$ of $9$ matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all $9$, improving ROID+ASR by $1.05$~pp on ResNet-50 and $0.48$~pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled source-degradation sweep shows a $1.13{\times}$ shallower harm slope than ROID+ASR, consistent with the graceful-decay prediction. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse, not confidently wrong low-entropy sources; this scope is explicitly evaluated and discussed.

</details>


### [281] [SurF: A Generative Model for Multivariate Irregular Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14069)
