*Mohammad R. Rezaei,Tejas Balaji,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SurF provides a unified, scalable generative model for irregular multivariate event streams by using the Time Rescaling Theorem as a learnable bijection to iid exponential noise, with scalable intensity parameterizations and a Transformer-based pretraining encoder, achieving state-of-the-art or near-baseline performance across six datasets and strong cross-dataset generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irregularly sampled, multivariate event streams are hard for generative models: tokenization-based methods break with large inter-event intervals and neural temporal point processes struggle with long sequences and heterogeneous datasets. A unified approach that generalizes across datasets and scales toward foundation-model capabilities is needed.

Method: Propose SurF: use the Time Rescaling Theorem as a learnable bijection between event sequences and iid unit-rate exponential noise; provide three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity for long sequences; employ a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining.

Result: Empirical evaluation on six real-world benchmarks shows SurF achieving the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao, and within trial-level noise of the top specialist on the other three; under strict leave-one-out, the held-out checkpoint beats every classical and neural-autoregressive baseline on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake.

Conclusion: The work marks an initial step toward foundation models for asynchronous event streams, demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalization and scalable performance that approach specialist models on diverse tasks.

Abstract: Irregularly sampled multivariate event streams remain a stubbornly difficult modality for generative modeling: tokenization-based approaches break down when inter-event intervals vary by orders of magnitude, and neural temporal point processes are bottlenecked by window-level numerical quadrature. We (i) propose SurF, a generative model that uses the Time Rescaling Theorem (TRT) as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d.\ unit-rate exponential noise, enabling a single model to be trained across heterogeneous event-stream datasets; (ii) three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity that scale to long sequences; and (iii) a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining. On six real-world benchmarks, SurF achieves the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao, and is within trial-level noise of the strongest specialist on the remaining three. Under a strict leave-one-out protocol, the held-out checkpoint beats every classical and neural-autoregressive baseline on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake, an initial step toward foundation models over asynchronous event streams.

</details>


### [282] [AttnGen: Attention-Guided Saliency Learning for Interpretable Genomic Sequence Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14073)
*Rayhaneh Shabani Nia,Ali Karkehabadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AttnGen introduces attention-guided masking during training to embed interpretability, achieving higher accuracy and faster convergence on a 200-nt genome sequence task, with 10–20% masking providing best interpretability/accuracy trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate interpretability into genomic sequence models by focusing the model on a compact, informative set of positions rather than noisy elements, improving both performance and explainability.

Method: An attention mechanism computes nucleotide-level importance; during training, low-contribution positions are progressively masked/suppressed (attention-guided masking), encouraging the model to rely on informative regions.

Result: On the demo_human_or_worm benchmark (200-nt sequences): validation accuracy with moderate masking is 96.73%, higher than CNN baseline at 95.83%; also exhibits faster convergence and improved training stability. Perturbation by removing high-saliency nucleotides reduces accuracy from 96.9% to near chance on a 3,000-sequence evaluation set, indicating reliance on a small subset of positions. Masking 10–20% of positions provides the best trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability.

Conclusion: Attention-guided masking not only boosts classification performance but also steers the distribution of importance across sequence positions, potentially extendable to longer or more complex interpretable sequence modeling tasks.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in genomic sequence classification; however, relating their predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns remains challenging. In this work, we present AttnGen, an attention-guided training framework that embeds interpretability directly into the optimization process. AttnGen computes nucleotide-level importance scores using an attention mechanism and progressively suppresses low-contribution positions during training. This encourages the model to focus its predictions on a compact set of informative regions while reducing reliance on noisy sequence elements. We evaluate AttnGen on the standardized demo_human_or_worm benchmark, a binary classification task over 200-nucleotide sequences. With moderate masking, AttnGen achieves a validation accuracy of 96.73%, outperforming a conventional CNN baseline with 95.83% accuracy, while also exhibiting faster convergence and improved training stability. To assess whether the learned importance scores reflect functionally relevant signal, we conduct perturbation-based analysis by removing high-saliency nucleotides. This causes accuracy to drop from 96.9% to near chance level on a 3,000-sequence evaluation set, indicating that the model relies on a relatively small subset of informative positions. Our analysis shows that masking 10--20% of positions provides the most favorable trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability. These results suggest that attention-guided masking not only improves classification performance but also reshapes how models distribute importance across sequence positions. Although this study focuses on short genomic sequences, the proposed approach may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling settings.

</details>


### [283] [Fair and Calibrated Toxicity Detection with Robust Training and Abstention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14074)
*Mokshit Surana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibration disparities across identity subgroups are a hidden fairness violation in toxicity classification; training-time interventions reshape but do not fix fairness across ranking, calibration, and abstention; post-hoc methods inherit training biases; abstention can exacerbate unequal harms; a multi-axis fairness framework is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how training-time interventions (ERM, reweighting, Group DRO) and post-hoc safety mechanisms interact across three fairness axes (ranking, calibration, abstention) in toxicity classification and to evaluate their effectiveness using subgroup metrics.

Method: Compare ERM, instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across ranking, calibration, and abstention axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup ECE with bootstrap CIs (n=1000).

Result: (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation: ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration (ECE ≈0.013) but substantial subgroup miscalibration (+0.029 to +0.134). (2) Training interventions reshape disparity: Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC +0.06 to +0.12) but widens calibration fairness gap by up to +0.232; Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity but induces uniform global miscalibration (ECE ≈0.118). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failures: Temperature scaling fails due to non-uniform miscalibration; confidence-based abstention works under ERM but fails under Group DRO, where risk-coverage curves rise with deferral. (4) Abstention itself is unfair: confidence-based deferral benefits background content more than identity-mentioning content. Conclusion: Achieving SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework; similar aggregate ranking can mask divergent failure modes that cause real-world harm.

Conclusion: A multi-axis SRAI fairness framework is essential. Evaluations must jointly consider ranking, calibration, and abstention; improvements in aggregate ranking do not guarantee reductions in real-world harms, and post-hoc fixes may propagate training-time biases. 

Abstract: Fairness in toxicity classification involves three integrated axes: ranking, calibration, and abstention. Training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms cannot be evaluated independently because the former determines the efficacy of the latter. We compare Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across these axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap CIs ($n = 1000$).
  We report four findings. (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation. ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration ($0.013$) but is significantly miscalibrated across all identity subgroups ($+0.029$ to $+0.134$). (2) Training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparity. Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC $+0.06$ to $+0.12$) but worsens the calibration-fairness gap by up to $+0.232$. Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity but only by becoming uniformly miscalibrated globally (ECE $0.118$). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failure modes. Temperature scaling fails because miscalibration is non-uniform. Confidence-based abstention works under ERM but breaks under DRO, where the risk-coverage curve rises with deferral. (4) Abstention itself is unfair. Confidence-based deferral helps background content far more than identity-mentioning content. We argue that SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework: methods that differ only in aggregate ranking can differ sharply in failure modes that determine real-world harm.

</details>


### [284] [Rethinking Layer Relevance in Large Language Models Beyond Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14075)
*Cristian Hinostroza,Rodrigo Toro Icarte,Christ Devia,Andres Carvallo De Ferari,Eugenio Herrera-Berg,Denis Parra,Jorge F Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cosine similarity is an unreliable proxy for layer importance in LLMs; actual accuracy drop upon layer removal is a more robust, albeit costly, metric, with weak-to-moderate empirical correlation, suggesting a shift from cosine-based interpretability to performance-based pruning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gaps in mechanistic interpretability by evaluating layer relevance with a metric that aligns with model performance rather than geometric similarity.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing possible misalignment between cosine similarity and importance; extensive experiments across multiple LLMs measuring cosine similarity versus accuracy drop when removing layers; proposes and tests an accuracy-drop metric for layer relevance.

Result: Cosine similarity often fails to track the true impact of removing a layer; the accuracy-drop metric provides a more accurate assessment of layer relevance and can inform pruning, with consistent findings across models.

Conclusion: Advocate moving beyond cosine similarity for layer relevance; adopt performance-based metrics for interpretable and efficient models, accepting higher computational cost.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing. Understanding their internal mechanisms is crucial for developing more interpretable and optimized architectures. Mechanistic interpretability has led to the development of various methods for assessing layer relevance, with cosine similarity being a widely used tool in the field. On this work, we demonstrate that cosine similarity is a poor proxy for the actual performance degradation caused by layer removal. Our theoretical analysis shows that a layer can exhibit an arbitrarily low cosine similarity score while still being crucial to the model's performance. On the other hand, empirical evidence from a range of LLMs confirms that the correlation between cosine similarity and actual performance degradation is often weak or moderate, leading to misleading interpretations of a transformer's internal mechanisms. We propose a more robust metric for assessing layer relevance: the actual drop in model accuracy resulting from the removal of a layer. Even though it is a computationally costly metric, this approach offers a more accurate picture of layer importance, allowing for more informed pruning strategies and lightweight models. Our findings have significant implications for the development of interpretable LLMs and highlight the need to move beyond cosine similarity in assessing layer relevance.

</details>


### [285] [Mini-JEPA Foundation Model Fleet Enables Agentic Hydrologic Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14120)
*Mashrekur Rahman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fleet of small, sensor-specific JEPA models (Mini-JEPAs) with a routing LLM improves geospatial reasoning and hydrologic predictions over a single generic AlphaEarth model, while maintaining low compute and strong reconstruction of various sensor signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalist planetary-scale foundation models may underperform on specialized hydrologic signals and are costly/less accessible. The work proposes compact, sensor-specialized embeddings (Mini-JEPAs) plus a routing mechanism to selectively leverage them for specific questions.

Method: Train five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs sharing a ViT backbone, identical JEPA recipe, and a 64-d output space, using diverse data streams (Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, topography-soil). Each model reconstructs the variable corresponding to its sensor. Evaluate via cross-validated R^2 for elevation, temperature, precipitation; analyze manifold geometry (global participation ratios 8.9–20.2; local intrinsic dimensionalities 2.3–9.0). Integrate topography-soil and phenology jointly for additive value on soil moisture, aridity, precipitation (ΔR^2 up to 0.031). Use a routing LLM to read per-modality references and select sensors; evaluate routing with perfect hit rate on curated questions. In paired LLM-as-Judge setup, retrieval from AlphaEarth plus routed fleet outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched questions (Cohen’s d = 1.10, p = 0.031). Demonstrate feasibility of locally-trained Mini-JEPAs with modest compute for hydrologic intelligence.

Result: Five Mini-JEPAs achieve high sensor-reconstruction fidelity (R^2 ≈ 0.97 for elevation and temperature, ≈0.81 for precipitation). They exhibit distinct geometric properties (global participation ratios 8.9–20.2; local intrinsic dimensionalities 2.3–9.0). Joint topography-soil and phenology models add predictive value for soil moisture, aridity, and precipitation (ΔR^2 up to 0.031). A routing LLM accurately selects sensors; combined retrieval (AlphaEarth + routed fleet) outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched tasks (Cohen’s d = 1.10, p = 0.031). Locally-trained Mini-JEPAs are viable with modest compute.

Conclusion: Small, sensor-specialized embeddings coupled with a routing mechanism can rival or surpass a generalist planetary model on physics-aligned tasks, while offering lower compute and greater accessibility for hydrologic intelligence.

Abstract: Geospatial foundation models compress multispectral observations into dense embeddings increasingly used in natural-language environmental reasoning systems. A single planetary-scale model, e.g. Google AlphaEarth, handles broad characterization well but may compromise on specialized hydrologic signals. Such generalist models are also often inaccessible, expensive, and require large-scale compute. We propose Mini-JEPAs: a fleet of small sensor-specialized Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) foundation models consulted by a routing agent for specialized questions. We pretrained five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs sharing an identical Vision Transformer backbone, JEPA recipe, and 64-d output space, using Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, and a topography-soil stack. Each Mini-JEPA reconstructs the variable matched to its sensor, with cross-validated $R^2$ reaching 0.97 for elevation, 0.97 for temperature, and 0.81 for precipitation. The five manifolds differ in geometric structure, with global participation ratios from 8.9 to 20.2 and local intrinsic dimensionalities from 2.3 to 9.0. Joint topography-soil and phenology models add predictive value beyond AlphaEarth alone for soil moisture, aridity, and precipitation ($ΔR^2$ up to 0.031). A router LLM reads per-modality references and selects appropriate sensors with a perfect hit rate over a curated question set. In paired LLM-as-Judge evaluation, dual retrieval over AlphaEarth and the routed fleet outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched questions (Cohen's $d = 1.10$, $p = 0.031$). Locally-trained Mini-JEPAs can be operationalized for hydrologic intelligence with modest compute.

</details>


### [286] [Reinforcement Learning for Tool-Calling Agents in Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14126)
*Marius S. Knorr,Robert Müller,Jan P. Bremer,Nils Schweingruber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL post-training of a multi-turn CodeAct agent with an LLM judge improves FHIR QA accuracy from 50% to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench, enforcing data-integrity constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address failures of tool-augmented LLMs that select wrong resources or violate graph traversal constraints when querying structured healthcare data.

Method: Model: multi-turn CodeAct agent; RL post-training with a custom environment/harness/tools; execution-grounded rewards from an LLM Judge; benchmark FHIR-AgentBench; compare to prompt-based baselines; use Qwen3-8B model.

Result: Accuracy improves from 50% to 77% on o4-mini; end-to-end pipeline demonstrated; robust improvement in multi-turn reasoning over structured clinical graphs.

Conclusion: RL post-training with execution-grounded feedback is effective for constraint-aware QA over FHIR graphs; provides scalable pipeline for better structured clinical reasoning.

Abstract: Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) is the dominant standard for interoperable exchange of healthcare data. In FHIR, electronic health records form a directed graph of resources. Answering clinically meaningful questions over FHIR requires agents to perform multi-step reasoning, filtering, and aggregation across multiple resource types. Prior work shows that even tool-augmented LLM agents (retrieval, code execution, multi-turn planning) often select the wrong resources or violate traversal constraints. We study this problem in the context of FHIR-AgentBench, a benchmark for realistic question answering over real-world hospital data, and frame reasoning on FHIR as a sequential decision-making problem over a queryable structured graph. We implement a multi-turn CodeAct agent and post-train it with reinforcement learning using a custom harness and tools. A LLM Judge provides execution-grounded rewards. Compared to prompt-based, closed-model baselines, RL post-training improves performance while enforcing data-integrity constraints. Empirically, our approach improves answer correctness from 50% (o4-mini) to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench using a smaller and cheaper Qwen3-8B model. We present an end-to-end post-training pipeline (environment building, harness construction, model training and custom evaluation) that reliably improves multi-turn reasoning over structured clinical graphs.

</details>


### [287] [bde: A Python Package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles via MILE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14146)
*Vyron Arvanitis,Angelos Aslanidis,Emanuel Sommer,David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents bde, a JAX-based Bayesian Deep Ensemble library for tabular data using Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles, with scikit-learn style APIs for fast training and uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide scalable, user-friendly Bayesian ensembling with reliable uncertainty quantification for tabular data.

Method: Utilizes Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE) as a sampling-based inference method implemented in JAX; offers scikit-learn compatible estimators enabling fast training, efficient MCMC sampling, and uncertainty quantification for regression and classification.

Result: Delivers a practical workflow with fast training, scalable Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, and calibrated predictive uncertainty on tabular datasets.

Conclusion: BDE offers an accessible, scalable tool for Bayesian deep ensembles on tabular data, with a familiar API aligned to scikit-learn to facilitate adoption.

Abstract: bde is a user-friendly Python package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles with a particular focus on tabular data. Built on an efficient JAX implementation of the sampling-based inference method Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE), it provides scikit-learn compatible estimators for fast training, efficient Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, and uncertainty quantification in both regression and classification tasks.

</details>


### [288] [Uncovering Trajectory and Topological Signatures in Multimodal Pediatric Sleep Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14156)
*Scott Ye,Harlin Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal pediatric PSG embeddings analyzed for session-wide diagnostic signals; geometry/topology and EHR augment embeddings to improve predictive performance and calibration, with late fusion outperforming simple baselines and full fusion yielding best calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and leverage latent structure in multimodal embeddings of pediatric sleep data, and to assess whether geometric, topological, and clinical features add value for diagnostic tasks under severe class imbalance.

Method: Embed 30-second PSG epochs via a multimodal masked autoencoder. Augment embeddings with PHATE-based per-epoch coordinates, whole-night movement descriptors, and persistent homology summaries. Train simple linear models and MLPs for interpretability; compare late fusion vs full fusion. Evaluate on four binary tasks (desaturation, EEG arousal, hypopnea, apnea) using AUPRC, Brier score, and Expected Calibration Error.

Result: AUPRC improvements across tasks when using augmented features and late-/full-fusion models (desaturation 0.26→0.34; EEG arousal 0.31→0.48; hypopnea 0.09→0.22; apnea 0.05→0.14). Full fusion yielded best calibration across tasks; feature importance was task-dependent; latent geometry/topology and EHR supply complementary signals improving calibration and robustness under severe class imbalance.

Conclusion: Latent geometric/topological features and EHR provide interpretable, complementary information beyond embeddings, enhancing calibration and robustness; late fusion offers gains, with full fusion delivering the strongest calibration benefits.

Abstract: While generative models have shown promise in pediatric sleep analysis, the latent structure of their multimodal embeddings remains poorly understood. This work investigates session-wide diagnostic information contained in the sequences of 30-second pediatric PSG epochs embedded by a multimodal masked autoencoder. We test whether augmenting embeddings with PHATE-derived per-epoch coordinates and whole-night movement descriptors, persistent homology summaries of the embedding cloud, and EHR yields task-relevant signals. Simple linear and MLP models, chosen for interpretability rather than state-of-the-art performance, show that geometric, topological, and clinical features each provide complementary gains. For binary predictions, feature importance is task-dependent, and more expressive late-fusion models generally perform better, with AUPRC improving from 0.26 to 0.34 for desaturation, 0.31 to 0.48 for EEG arousal, 0.09 to 0.22 for hypopnea, and 0.05 to 0.14 for apnea. We also report Brier score and Expected Calibration Error, where the full fusion model yields the best calibration across all four binary tasks. Our study reveals that latent geometry/topology and EHR offer complementary, interpretable signals beyond embeddings, improving calibration and robustness under extreme imbalance.

</details>


### [289] [Finite Sample Bounds for Learning with Score Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14168)
*Devin Smedira,Abhijith Jayakumar,Sidhant Misra,Marc Vuffray,Andrey Y. Lokhov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-asymptotic, polynomial-in-dimension sample complexity bounds for learning the structure of exponential families of polynomials via score matching; first such non-asymptotic results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of non-asymptotic theoretical guarantees for score matching in continuous exponential families with unbounded support; provide statistical guarantees for high-dimensional settings.

Method: Derives a non-asymptotic sample complexity analysis for learning the structure of exponential families of polynomials using score matching, establishing polynomial dependence on model dimension.

Result: First non-asymptotic bounds on the sample complexity for score matching in this setting; the bounds depend polynomially on the model dimension, contrasting prior asymptotic results.

Conclusion: The work provides theoretical justification for score matching in high-dimensional polynomial exponential families and demonstrates polynomial-dimensional scalability, filling a gap in the literature.

Abstract: Learning of continuous exponential family distributions with unbounded support remains an important area of research for both theory and applications in high-dimensional statistics. In recent years, score matching has become a widely used method for learning exponential families with continuous variables due to its computational ease when compared against maximum likelihood estimation. However, theoretical understanding of the statistical properties of score matching is still lacking. In this work, we provide a non-asymptotic sample complexity analysis for learning the structure of exponential families of polynomials with score matching. The derived sample bounds show a polynomial dependence on the model dimension. These bounds are the first of its kind, as all prior work has shown only asymptotic bounds on the sample complexity.

</details>


### [290] [Slot-MPC: Goal-Conditioned Model Predictive Control with Object-Centric Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14937)
*Jonathan Spieler,Angel Villar-Corrales,Sven Behnke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Slot-MPC, an object-centric world model that enables planning via differentiable MPC; demonstrates superior performance and planning efficiency over non-object-centric baselines in offline robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the generalization limits of reactive, fixed policies by leveraging object-centric representations within a model-based planning framework, enabling adaptation to unseen situations and efficient gradient-based planning.

Method: Use vision encoders to extract slot-based representations that correspond to individual scene objects; train an action-conditioned, object-centric dynamics model; apply gradient-based Model Predictive Control (MPC) for action planning; compare against gradient-free, sampling-based MPC; evaluate in simulated robotic manipulation tasks under offline data regimes; provide code and results.

Result: Slot-MPC achieves higher task performance and more efficient planning than non-object-centric baselines; gradient-based MPC outperforms gradient-free MPC in the offline, data-limited setting; underscores the benefit of explicit object-centric structure for controllable decision-making.

Conclusion: Structured, object-centric representations provide strong inductive bias that supports controllable and generalizable planning; Slot-MPC enables adaptive planning in unseen scenarios, with potential for real-world validation and scaling.

Abstract: Predictive world models enable agents to model scene dynamics and reason about the consequences of their actions. Inspired by human perception, object-centric world models capture scene dynamics using object-level representations, which can be used for downstream applications such as action planning. However, most object-centric world models and reinforcement learning (RL) approaches learn reactive policies that are fixed at inference time, limiting generalization to novel situations. We propose Slot-MPC, an object-centric world modeling framework that enables planning through Model Predictive Control (MPC). Slot-MPC leverages vision encoders to learn slot-based representations, which encode individual objects in the scene, and uses these structured representations to learn an action-conditioned object-centric dynamics model. At inference time, the learned dynamics model enables action planning via MPC, allowing agents to adapt to previously unseen situations. Since the learned world model is differentiable, we can use gradient-based MPC to directly optimize actions, which is computationally more efficient than relying on gradient-free, sampling-based MPC methods. Experiments on simulated robotic manipulation tasks show that Slot-MPC improves both task performance and planning efficiency compared to non-object-centric world model baselines. In the considered offline setting with limited state-action coverage, we find that gradient-based MPC performs better than gradient-free, sampling-based MPC. Our results demonstrate that explicitly structured, object-centric representations provide a strong inductive bias for controllable and generalizable decision-making. Code and additional results are available at https://slot-mpc.github.io.

</details>


### [291] [CSI-JEPA: Towards Foundation Representations for Ubiquitous Sensing with Minimal Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14171)
*Xuanhao Luo,Zhizhen Li,Yuchen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-supervised predictive representation learning (CSI-JEPA) for label-efficient, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing; pretrains a temporal-spectral encoder via masked region prediction on CSI; uses channel variation-aware masking and tokenized channel-response windows; reports strong cross-task gains and high label efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CSI sensing models rely on task-specific supervised training and large labeled datasets per task/device/environment, hindering scalability. Unlabeled CSI is abundant; there is a need for reusable representations that generalize across tasks with limited labels.

Method: Tokenize channel-response amplitude windows along time and subcarrier dimensions to form spatial-temporal tokens. Use a channel variation-aware masking strategy that targets regions with strong local temporal/subcarrier variation and predict latent features of masked regions from visible context (self-supervised pretraining). After pretraining, freeze the encoder as a backbone and add lightweight task-specific adapters for downstream sensing tasks.

Result: Pretrained model yields reusable representations that improve downstream performance across seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks, outperforming competitive baselines. Reported gains include up to 10.64 percentage points in mean accuracy and up to 98.0% label savings compared to supervised baselines with budgeted labels.

Conclusion: CSI-JEPA demonstrates effective self-supervised pretraining for CSI sensing, enabling task-agnostic, reusable representations that reduce labeling requirements while maintaining strong cross-task performance across diverse deployment settings.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI) provides a widely available sensing modality for human and environment perception, but existing CSI sensing models usually rely on task-specific supervised training and require substantial labeled data for each task, device, user, or environment. This limits their scalability in practical deployments where unlabeled CSI is abundant but labeled data is costly to collect. In this paper, we present CSI-JEPA, a self-supervised predictive representation learning framework for label-efficient, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing. CSI-JEPA learns reusable temporal-spectral representations from unlabeled CSI samples by predicting latent features of masked channel regions from visible context. To better match the physical structure of CSI, CSI-JEPA tokenizes channel-response amplitude windows along the time and subcarrier dimensions. It then introduces a channel variation-aware masking strategy that samples predictive targets from regions with stronger local temporal and subcarrier-domain variations. After pretraining, the encoder is frozen and used as a backbone, with lightweight task-specific adapters added for downstream sensing tasks. We evaluate CSI-JEPA on seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks spanning diverse objectives and deployment settings. The results show that CSI-JEPA improves downstream sensing performance over competitive baselines, achieving up to 10.64 percentage points mean accuracy gain over state-of-the-art supervised Transformer and matched-budget label savings of up to 98.0%.

</details>


### [292] [LLMs Know When They Know, but Do Not Act on It: A Metacognitive Harness for Test-time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14186)
*Qi Cao,Yufan Wang,Peijia Qin,Shuhao Zhang,Pengtao Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Metacognitive harness for LLMs uses pre-solve FOK and post-solve JOL signals to control reasoning, improving accuracy without fine-tuning across text, code, and multimodal tasks; surpasses leaderboard on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs' self-monitoring signals exist but are not exploited for control; leveraging latent metacognition could enhance decision-making and accuracy at test time, aligning with Nelson–Narens theory.

Method: Introduce a separate monitoring module that outputs FOK before solving and JOL after attempts; a control policy uses these signals to decide to trust, retry with compact feedback, or delegate to an aggregator; applied to Claude Sonnet-4.6 without parameter updates or benchmark-specific fine-tuning.

Result: Pooled accuracy improves from 48.3 to 56.9 on public benchmarks; outperforms top leaderboard entries on HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V; demonstrates metacognition can be harnessed to control reasoning.

Conclusion: Strong LLMs may possess latent metacognitive abilities that, when given an explicit control interface, yield substantial performance gains without training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often expose useful signals of self-monitoring: before solving a problem, they can estimate whether they are likely to succeed, and after solving it, they can judge whether their answer is likely to be correct. However, these signals are typically measured or elicited in isolation, rather than used to control inference. In this work, we ask whether LLMs possess latent metacognitive ability that can be turned into effective test-time control. Inspired by the Nelson--Narens theory from cognitive psychology, we propose a metacognitive harness that separates monitoring from reasoning. For each problem, the model first reports a pre-solve feeling-of-knowing (FOK) signal; after each solve attempt, it reports a post-solve judgment-of-learning (JOL) signal. Rather than treating these signals as passive confidence estimates, the harness turns them into an explicit control interface for reasoning: it decides when to trust the current solution, when to retry with compact metacognitive feedback, and when to pass multiple attempts to a final aggregator. Across text, code, and multimodal reasoning benchmarks, our harness substantially improves a fixed Claude Sonnet-4.6 base model without parameter updates or benchmark-specific fine-tuning. On the evaluated public benchmark snapshots, it raises pooled accuracy from 48.3 to 56.9 and exceeds the strongest listed leaderboard entries on the three primary evaluation settings: HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V. These results suggest that strong LLMs may already possess useful metacognitive ability, but require an explicit control harness to act on it during reasoning.

</details>


### [293] [How to Scale Mixture-of-Experts: From muP to the Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14200)
*Leena Chennuru Vankadara,Moritz Haas,Luke Hayward,Sebastian Bordt,Alessandro Breccia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DMFT-based scaling analysis of MoE architectures yields MSSP, a scale-stable parameterization for SGD/Adam across three scaling regimes, resolving μP shortcomings and enabling monotonic improvement and rate transfer with scale, providing a complete MoE scaling recipe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no principled understanding of how MoE hyperparameters (N, Ne, M, K, L) should scale to maintain stability and performance at scale. The work aims to derive a principled, scalable parameterization that preserves stability and enables transfer across widths, depths, and expert configurations.

Method: Analyze three scaling regimes (I: N ~ Ne; II: N ~ M ~ K; III: full proportional scaling) using Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) to model limiting training dynamics under SGD and Adam. Derive a unique μ-desiderata-based parameterization (μP), identify pathologies, introduce a refined maximal scale stability principle, derive Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) for both SGD and Adam in all regimes, and characterize the limiting dynamics via DMFT. Validate MSSP experimentally.

Result: μP fails to guarantee monotonic improvement with scale or robust learning-rate transfer due to scale-dependent observables in aggregation dynamics. MSSP, grounded in maximal scale stability, recovers learning-rate transfer and monotonic scaling across regimes and provides a complete scaling prescription as a function of width, depth, Ne, M and K; dynamics under MSSP differ qualitatively from μP. Experiments support robust scaling behavior.

Conclusion: MSSP provides a robust, scale-stable optimization framework for MoEs, completing the scaling theory in conjunction with depth-scaling results and enabling principled design of MoE models across width, depth, expert width, and number of experts.

Abstract: Recent frontier large language models predominantly rely on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Despite empirical progress, there is still no principled understanding of how hyperparameters should scale with network width $N$, expert width $N_e$, number of experts $M$, sparsity $K$, and depth $L$ to ensure both stability and optimal performance at scale. We take a principled step toward resolving this gap by analyzing three different scaling regimes: (I) co-scaling $N\asymp N_e$, (II) co-scaling $N\asymp M\asymp K$, and (III) full proportional scaling of $N, N_e, M$, and $K$. For each regime, we develop a novel Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) description of the limiting training dynamics of MoEs that provides a formal foundation for our analysis. Within this framework, we derive the unique parameterization for SGD and Adam satisfying all maximal-update ($μ$) desiderata. We then show that the resulting $μ$P prescription does not reliably induce monotonic improvement with scale or robust learning-rate transfer. We trace these pathologies to scale-dependent observables in the aggregation dynamics, which motivates a refined set of desiderata that we term maximal scale stability. Guided by this principle, we derive a Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) for both SGD and Adam in all three scaling regimes, and characterize the corresponding limiting dynamics - qualitatively distinct from the $μ$P limit - through a separate DMFT analysis. Experiments verify that MSSP robustly recovers learning rate transfer and monotonic improvement with scale across regimes. Combined with existing depth-scaling theory, these results provide a complete scaling prescription for MoE architectures as a function of width, depth, expert width, and number of experts.

</details>


### [294] [Towards Fine-Grained and Verifiable Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14210)
*Yingying Fang,Haijie Xu,Shuang Wu,Mariathasan Anish,Guang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fine-grained, grounded Concept Bottleneck Model (CBM) framework that localizes each concept's visual evidence, yielding verifiable, information-complete concept representations with predictive performance comparable to standard CBMs and improved transparency, especially in medical imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBMs offer interpretability but may not verify that predicted concepts align with actual visual evidence, risking reliance on spurious correlations. There is a need for verifiable interpretability that ties concepts to localized evidence, crucial for clinical trust and actionability.

Method: Ground each concept in localized visual evidence and allow inspection of where/how concepts are encoded; ensure information completeness; compare to standard CBMs; validate both presence and correctness of concept representations; supports principled human-model interaction at the concept level.

Result: Predictive performance on par with standard CBMs; the learned concept space is information-complete; significantly enhanced transparency; evidence grounding validates both presence and correctness of concept representations; avoids reliance on post-hoc attribution.

Conclusion: Enhances trustworthiness of CBMs, enabling principled human-model interaction at the concept level and advancing clinically actionable, reliable concept-based learning systems.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer interpretable alternatives to black-box predictors by introducing human-relatable concepts before the final output. However, existing CBMs struggle to verify whether predicted concepts correspond to the correct visual evidence, limiting their reliability. We propose a fine-grained CBM framework that grounds each concept in localized visual evidence, enabling direct inspection of where and how concepts are encoded. This design allows users to interpret predictions and verify that the model learns intended concepts rather than spurious correlations. Experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show that our learned concept space is information-complete and achieves predictive performance comparable to standard CBMs, while substantially improving transparency. Unlike post-hoc attribution methods, our framework validates both the presence and correctness of concept representations, bridging interpretability with verifiability. Our approach enhances the trustworthiness of CBMs and establishes a principled mechanism for human-model interaction at the concept level, paving the way toward more reliable and clinically actionable concept-based learning systems.

</details>


### [295] [PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for efficient inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14217)
*Andrew Lanpouthakoun,Aryaman Arora,Zhengxuan Wu,Dhruv Pai,Ben Keigwin,Dan Jurafsky,Christopher Potts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prefill-only finetuning (PreFT) applies adapters only to prefill tokens, discarding them afterwards, to boost throughput for multi-adapter serving. It delivers up to 1.9× throughput with 512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B, with modest SFT cost (reducible by higher rank) and RL performance nearing standard PEFTs, offering a favorable accuracy-throughput tradeoff for personalized serving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome throughput bottlenecks when personalizing LLMs with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, which suffer from a prefill–decode throughput mismatch during multi-adapter serving. The goal is to optimize serving throughput (tokens per second) rather than parameter count.

Method: Introduce PreFT (Prefill-only Finetuning) which applies adapters only during the prefill phase and discards them afterward. Implement two prefill-only PEFTs (LoRA and ReFT) on the vLLM inference engine. Evaluate throughput for multi-user serving (512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B) and compare prefill-only vs all-token adapters across supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) tasks across model scales.

Result: Multi-user PreFTs yield higher throughput than traditional PEFTs (1.9× for 512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B). On SFT tasks, PreFTs incur higher evaluation loss than standard PEFTs, but this can be offset by increasing the adapter rank with minimal throughput impact. On RL tasks, PreFTs approach parity with standard PEFTs. Overall, Prefill-only adaptation provides a more favorable accuracy-throughput tradeoff for personalized serving.

Conclusion: Prefill-only adaptation (PreFT) is a viable and advantageous alternative to traditional PEFTs for scalable, personalized serving of LLMs, delivering substantially higher throughput with comparable RL performance and manageable SFT tradeoffs when rank is increased.

Abstract: Large language models can now be personalised efficiently at scale using parameter efficient finetuning methods (PEFTs), but serving user-specific PEFTs harms throughput, even with specialised kernels and memory management techniques. This is because, theoretically and empirically, a mismatch exists between prefill (processing a large number of tokens at once) and decode (generating a single token autoregressively): the latter has far lower throughput when serving multiple adapters. Rather than optimising performance relative to parameter count, for efficient multi-adapter serving, we instead ought to optimise performance relative to serving throughput. We therefore propose PreFT (Prefill-only Finetuning), wherein we only apply the adapter to prefill tokens and discard it afterwards. PreFT significantly increases throughput with minimal effect on performance. We develop and release an efficient implementation of two prefill-only PEFTs, LoRA and ReFT, on the vLLM inference engine. We first show that serving multi-user PreFTs is more efficient than traditional PEFTs ($1.9\times$ the throughput when serving $512$ adapters on Llama 3.1 70B). Then, we compare the performance of prefill-only vs. all-token adapters on a variety of supervised finetuning and reinforcement learning tasks with LMs at varying scales. On SFT, we observe that the evaluation loss of PreFTs is higher than PEFTs, but can be compensated by increasing rank with nearly no reduction in throughput. On RL, we consistently find that PreFTs approach parity with standard PEFTs. Together, this work validates prefill-only adaptation of LLMs as a more favourable accuracy-throughput tradeoff than existing PEFTs for personalised serving.

</details>


### [296] [Diagnosing Training Inference Mismatch in LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14220)
*Tianle Zhong,Neiwen Ling,Yifan Pi,Zijun Wei,Tianshu Yu,Geoffrey Fox,Peng Wu,Xiao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TIM is a systemic issue in LLM RL: small token-level numerical disagreements between rollout and training cause training collapse; VeXact isolates TIM; remedies identified; TIM should be treated as a first-order factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate why rollout-generation and policy-optimization stages diverge in LLM RL, and isolate Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM) from off-policy drift and stabilization effects.

Method: Introduce VeXact, a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting to isolate TIM; quantify effects of small token-level numerical disagreements; analyze how TIM alters the optimization objective; propose potential remedies.

Result: Showing that tiny numerical disagreements can trigger training collapse; TIM is a non-benign, system-level perturbation that changes the effective optimization problem; identifies remedies to mitigate TIM.

Conclusion: TIM is a first-order factor in LLM RL stability; future work should design pipelines to minimize TIM and account for it in stability analyses.

Abstract: Modern LLM RL systems separate rollout generation from policy optimization. These two stages are expected to produce token probabilities that match exactly. However, implementation differences can make them assign different values to the same sequence under the same model weights, inducing Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM). TIM is difficult to inspect because it is entangled with off-policy drift and common stabilization mechanisms. In this work, we isolate TIM in a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting (VeXact), and show that small token-level numerical disagreements can independently cause training collapse. We further show that TIM changes the effective optimization problem, and identify a set of remedies that could mitigate TIM. Our results suggest that TIM is not benign numerical noise, but a systems-level perturbation that should be treated as a first-order factor in analyzing LLM RL stability.

</details>


### [297] [DT-Transformer: A Foundation Model for Disease Trajectory Prediction on a Real-world Health System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14227)
*Yunying Zhu,Andrew R Weckstein,Kueiyu Joshua Lin,Jie Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DT-Transformer: a foundation model trained on large-scale, multi-hospital EHR data to predict next clinical events with broad disease coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-hospital or curated cohorts may not reflect routine care; real-world deployment requires models trained on diverse, multi-center data to capture full trajectory complexity.

Method: Pretraining the DT-Transformer on 57.1 million structured EHR entries from 1.7 million patients across 11 Mass General Brigham hospitals and affiliated clinics; evaluation on held-out and prospective data with next-event prediction across 896 disease categories; reported age- and sex-stratified AUC.

Result: Median age- and sex-stratified AUC of 0.871 across 896 disease categories (all categories > 0.5) in next-event prediction; strong discrimination in held-out and prospective validation.

Conclusion: Health-system-scale training can produce foundation models well-suited for real-world clinical forecasting across diverse patient trajectories.

Abstract: Accurate disease trajectory prediction is critical for early intervention, resource allocation, and improving long-term outcomes. While electronic health records (EHRs) provide a rich longitudinal view of patient health in clinical environments, models trained on curated research cohorts may not reflect routine deployment settings, and those trained on single-hospital datasets capture only fragments of each patient's trajectory. This highlights the importance of leveraging large, multi-hospital health systems for training and validation to better reflect real-world clinical complexity. In this work, we develop DT-Transformer, a foundation model trained on 57.1M structured EHR entries over 1.7M patients from Mass General Brigham (MGB), spanning 11 hospitals and a broad network of outpatient clinics. DT-Transformer achieves strong discrimination in both held-out and prospective validation settings. Next-event prediction achieves a median age- and sex-stratified AUC of 0.871 across 896 disease categories, with all categories exceeding AUC 0.5. These results support health system-scale training as a path toward foundation models suited to real-world clinical forecasting.

</details>


### [298] [AudioMosaic: Contrastive Masked Audio Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14231)
*Hanxun Huang,Qizhou Wang,Xingjun Ma,Cihang Xie,Christopher Leckie,Sarah Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AudioMosaic is a contrastive SSL framework for audio that uses structured time-frequency masking on spectrogram patches to create positive pairs, enabling memory-efficient large-batch training and strong, transferable utterance-level representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the limitations of generative SSL in audio and the challenges of contrastive SSL, especially designing effective augmentations and scaling with large batch sizes, to learn robust, general-purpose audio representations.

Method: During pre-training, apply structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches to generate positive pairs for a contrastive objective, enabling memory efficiency and large-batch training. The encoder yields discriminative utterance-level representations and can be integrated into audio-language models.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard audio benchmarks in both linear probing and fine-tuning. Demonstrates strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. Improves performance on audio-language tasks when combined with audio-language models. Code available on GitHub.

Conclusion: Structured masking-based contrastive SSL effectively learns discriminative, transferable audio representations at the utterance level and scales with large-batch training, outperforming generative SSL on several benchmarks and enabling better multimodal integration.

Abstract: Audio self-supervised learning (SSL) aims to learn general-purpose representations from large-scale unlabeled audio data. While recent advances have been driven mainly by generative reconstruction objectives, contrastive approaches remain less explored, partly due to the difficulty of designing effective audio augmentations and the large batch sizes required for contrastive pre-training. We introduce \textbf{AudioMosaic}, a contrastive learning-based audio encoder for general audio understanding. During pre-training, AudioMosaic constructs positive pairs by applying structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches, which reduces memory usage and enables efficient large-batch training. Compared with generative approaches, the AudioMosaic encoder learns more discriminative utterance-level representations that demonstrate strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. Extensive experiments show that AudioMosaic achieves state-of-the-art performance on several standard audio benchmarks under both linear probing and fine-tuning. We further show that integrating the pretrained AudioMosaic encoder into audio-language models improves performance on audio-language tasks. The code is publicly available in our \href{https://github.com/HanxunH/AudioMosaic}{GitHub repository}.

</details>


### [299] [Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14235)
*Simranjeet Singh Dahia,Claudia Szabo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entangled QMARL shows quantum advantage in coordination tasks; entanglement is the active resource; entanglement structure matters; hybrid quantum-classical setups yield strongest performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously assess quantum advantage in quantum multi-agent reinforcement learning by introducing provable baselines and isolating entanglement as the active resource, addressing the lack of rigorous baselines in prior work.

Method: Decentralized QMARL with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors sharing entangled states; evaluated on the CHSH game and CoopNav; comparisons among entangled vs unentangled quantum circuits and classical baselines; analysis of different entanglement structures (Bell states).

Result: In CHSH, entangled QMARL approaches the Tsirelson limit of 0.854 (vs classical 0.75); unentangled circuits align with the classical baseline; entanglement structure matters (some Bell states help, others hurt). In CoopNav, QMARL without entanglement achieves ~2× improvement over classical MAA2C (≈0.85 vs ≈0.40); a hybrid quantum actor with classical centralized critic outperforms both fully classical and fully quantum solutions.

Conclusion: Entanglement is the principal coordination resource in QMARL, and its structure critically influences performance. Hybrid quantum-classical configurations can yield the strongest gains. The work motivates further exploration of entanglement design and scalability in QMARL.

Abstract: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

</details>


### [300] [Active Learners as Efficient PRP Rerankers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14236)
*Jeremías Figueiredo Paschmann,Juan Kaplan,Francisco Nattero Santiago Mauricio Barron Bucolo,Juan Wisznia,Luciano del Corro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframe pairwise ranking prompting as active learning to obtain robust, cost-efficient top-K rankings from noisy LLM-derived pairwise comparisons, using a randomized-direction oracle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-derived pairwise judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive; naive sorting to produce top-K under budget is unreliable. A method that handles noise and budget constraints is needed.

Method: Introduce active rankers as drop-in replacements for sorting and a randomized-direction oracle that requires only one LLM call per pair, converting systematic position bias into zero-mean noise.

Result: Active rankers improve NDCG@10 per call in call-constrained settings; the framework yields unbiased aggregate rankings with fewer calls.

Conclusion: Active learning–based PRP reranking with a randomized-direction oracle provides robust, budget-efficient rankings from noisy pairwise LLM judgments.

Abstract: Pairwise Ranking Prompting (PRP) elicits pairwise preference judgments from an LLM, which are then aggregated into a ranking, usually via classical sorting algorithms. However, judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive, so sorting assumptions do not match the setting. Because sorting aims to recover a full permutation, truncating it to meet a call budget does not produce a dependable top-K. We thus reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons and show that active rankers are drop-in replacements that improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime. Our noise-robust framework also introduces a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair. This approach converts systematic position bias into zero-mean noise, enabling unbiased aggregate ranking without the cost of bidirectional calls.

</details>


### [301] [Paraphrasing Attack Resilience of Various AI-Generated Text Detection Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14240)
*Andrii Shportko,Inessa Verbitsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Paraphrase robustness analysis shows Binoculars-based ensembles perform best for detection but are most vulnerable to paraphrase attacks, highlighting a performance-resilience trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how paraphrase attacks affect machine-generated text detectors and to compare multiple detection strategies (RoBERTa, Binoculars, text features) and their ensembles.

Method: Evaluate detectors under paraphrase attacks using three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis; combine via Random Forest ensembles; assess performance and resilience.

Result: Binoculars-based ensembles yield the strongest detection performance but experience the greatest degradation under paraphrase attacks; overall, a clear trade-off between detection accuracy and resilience.

Conclusion: AI-generated text detectors exhibit a performance-resilience dichotomy; reliability is limited by adversarial paraphrase. Future work should aim to enhance robustness (e.g., adversarial training, robust features, diversified ensembles) while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: The recent large-scale emergence of LLMs has left an open space for dealing with their consequences, such as plagiarism or the spread of false information on the Internet. Coupling this with the rise of AI detector bypassing tools, reliable machine-generated text detection is in increasingly high demand. We investigate the paraphrasing attack resilience of various machine-generated text detection methods, evaluating three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis, along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers. We discovered that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest results, but they also suffer the most significant losses during attacks. In this paper, we present the dichotomy of performance versus resilience in the world of AI text detection, which complicates the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

</details>


### [302] [Latency-Quality Routing for Functionally Equivalent Tools in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14241)
*Kexin Chu,Dawei Xiang,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers that emphasizes latency-quality matching to optimize provider selection under load; achieves gains in F1, accuracy, and NDCG over SW-UCB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle provider-routing in scenarios where multiple providers offer the same tool type, with runtime load causing latency and quality trade-offs and lacking gold labels at deployment; improve decision-making under heterogeneity and pressure.

Method: LQM-ContextRoute uses a capacity-aware score that ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle, combining query-specific quality estimation, LLM-as-judge feedback, and online adaptation to load and quality differences.

Result: Empirical improvements on main web-search load benchmark (F1 +2.18 pp over SW-UCB), StrategyQA with heterogeneous settings (accuracy up to +18 pp), and heterogeneous retriever pools (NDCG +2.91–+3.22 pp) while staying on the latency-quality frontier.

Conclusion: Treat latency as service capacity and use capacity-aware, quality-first routing to handle load and provider heterogeneity, avoiding additive-reward collapse and improving robustness across tasks.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents increasingly access the same tool type through multiple functionally equivalent providers, such as web-search APIs, retrievers, or LLM backends exposed behind a shared interface. This creates a provider-routing problem under runtime load: the router must choose among providers that differ in latency, reliability, and answer quality, often without gold labels at deployment time. We introduce LQM-ContextRoute, a contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers. Its key design is latency-quality matching: instead of letting low latency offset poor answers in an additive reward, the router ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines this capacity-aware score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback, allowing it to adapt online to both load changes and provider-quality differences. On the main web-search load benchmark, LQM-ContextRoute improves F1 by +2.18 pp over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In a high-heterogeneity StrategyQA setting, LQM-ContextRoute avoids additive-reward collapse and improves accuracy by up to +18 pp over SW-UCB; on heterogeneous retriever pools, it improves NDCG by +2.91--+3.22 pp over SW-UCB. These results show that same-function tool routing benefits from treating latency as service capacity, especially when runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity coexist.

</details>


### [303] [Artificial Intelligence-Assistant Cardiotocography: Unified Model for Signal Reconstruction, Fetal Heart Rate Analysis, and Variability Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14242)
*Xiaohua Wang,Kai Yu,XuXiao Liang,Liang Wang,Chao Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An AI-based fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring model (FHrCTG) reduces noise and reconstructs signals for FHR analysis. Pretrained on 558,412 unlabeled data points and fine-tuned on 7,266 expert-labeled entries. Introduces Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) to convert rate analysis into categorical judgments. Achieves high sensitivity and specificity for FHR decelerations (89.13% / 87.78%) and accelerations (62.5% / 92.04%), with AUCs of 0.7214 for periodicity verification and 0.9643 for amplitude variation per Fischer’s criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in traditional FHR monitoring caused by equipment performance, data transmission, and subjective interpretation, by delivering robust noise suppression, signal reconstruction, and objective decision support.

Method: A tailored AI-based FHR-CTG model trained on a large unlabeled dataset (558,412 points) and refined with 7,266 expert-labeled entries; uses Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) to translate rate analysis into categorical judgments.

Result: The model shows high sensitivity and specificity for detect­ing decelerations (89.13%/87.78%) and accelerations (62.5%/92.04%), with Fischer’s criterion AUCs of 0.7214 (periodicity) and 0.9643 (amplitude variation).

Conclusion: The FHrCTG AI framework with IOL demonstrates strong performance and potential for clinical application in FHR monitoring, though further validation across diverse datasets and integration considerations are needed.

Abstract: The monitoring of fetal heart rate (FHR) and the assessment of its variability are crucial for preventing fetal compromise and adverse outcomes. However, traditional methods encounter limitations arising from equipment performance, data transmission, and subjective assessments by doctors. We have developed a tailored AI-based FHrCTG model specifically for FHR monitoring, which effectively mitigates noise interference and precisely reconstructs signals. Our model was pre-trained on a massive dataset consisting of 558,412 unlabeled data points and further refined using 7,266 expert-reviewed entries. To validate FHR, we introduced the Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) approach, which transforms rate analysis into categorical judgments. Testing revealed that our model demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity in detecting critical FHR decelerations (89.13% and 87.78%, respectively) and accelerations (62.5% and 92.04%, respectively). Furthermore, based on Fischer's criteria for clinical application, our model achieved impressive AUC scores of 0.7214 and 0.9643 for verifying FHR periodicity and amplitude variation, respectively.

</details>


### [304] [Action-Conditioned Risk Gating for Safety-Critical Control under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14246)
*Yushen Liu,Yin-Jen Chen,Ziyi Chen,Tao Wang,Heng Huang,Xugui Zhou,Yanfu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight risk-gated RL approach for risk-sensitive control under partial observability using a compact history proxy and an action-conditioned near-term risk predictor, improving safety-performance tradeoffs with reduced computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Belief-space planning for risk-sensitive POMDPs provides principled safety guarantees but is computationally expensive and sensitive to model specification; practical systems need scalable, robust alternatives.

Method: Create a compact finite-history proxy state; learn an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violations. Use predicted risk as (1) a penalty during value learning and (2) a decision-time gate to blend optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates, biasing low-risk actions toward reward-seeking estimates and high-risk actions toward conservatism.

Result: In glucose regulation, the method improves overall glycemic tradeoffs and reduces runtime versus belief-space planning. In Safety-Gym navigation, it yields a more favorable reward-cost balance than unconstrained RL and standard safe-RL baselines.

Conclusion: Action-conditioned near-term risk signals can serve as an effective local surrogate for risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical.

Abstract: Many safety-critical control problems are modeled as risk-sensitive partially observable Markov decision processes, where the controller must make decisions from incomplete observations while balancing task performance against safety risk. Although belief-space planning provides a principled solution, maintaining and planning over beliefs can be computationally costly and sensitive to model specification in practical domains. We propose a lightweight risk-gated reinforcement learning approximation for risk-sensitive control under partial observability. The method constructs a compact finite-history proxy state and learns an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violation. This predicted candidate-action risk is used in two complementary ways: as a risk penalty during value learning, and as a decision-time gate that interpolates between optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates. As a result, low-risk actions are evaluated closer to reward-seeking estimates, while high-risk actions are evaluated more conservatively. We evaluate the approach in two safety-critical partially observable domains: automated glucose regulation and safety-constrained navigation. Across adult and adolescent glucose-control cohorts, the method improves overall glycemic tradeoffs and substantially reduces runtime relative to a belief-space planning baseline. On Safety-Gym navigation benchmarks, it achieves a more favorable reward-cost balance than unconstrained RL and several standard safe-RL baselines. These results suggest that action-conditioned near-term risk can provide an effective local signal for approximate risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical.

</details>


### [305] [Not All Timesteps Matter Equally: Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation for Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14252)
*Kai Sun,Peibo Duan,Yongsheng Huang,Guowei Zhang,Benjamin Smith,Nanxu Gong,Levin Kuhlmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SeAl-KD, a selective knowledge distillation framework for SNNs that aligns class logits only at erroneous timesteps and weights temporal alignment by confidence and inter-timestep similarity, outperforming uniform KD across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SNNs exhibit temporally evolving predictions; enforcing uniform per-timestep supervision can misguide learning and degrade temporal dynamics. A selective, corrective distillation strategy could improve final performance without erasing useful temporal structure.

Method: Propose Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation (SeAl-KD): (1) class-level logit alignment is applied only at timesteps identified as erroneous; (2) temporal alignment is reweighted by per-timestep confidence and inter-timestep similarity; (3) integrates class-level and temporal knowledge into the distillation objective. Evaluation on static-image and neuromorphic event-based datasets; code released at the provided GitHub URL.

Result: Empirically, SeAl-KD yields consistent improvements over existing distillation methods across the tested benchmarks, indicating that selective and confidence-aware alignment helps SNNs close the gap with ANNs.

Conclusion: Selective, target-aware distillation that preserves beneficial temporal dynamics while correcting incorrect timesteps offers a robust improvement for SNN training, with accompanying code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs), which are brain-inspired and spike-driven, achieve high energy efficiency. However, a performance gap between SNNs and artificial neural networks (ANNs) still remains. Knowledge distillation (KD) is commonly adopted to improve SNN performance, but existing methods typically enforce uniform alignment across all timesteps, either from a teacher network or through inter-temporal self-distillation, implicitly assuming that per-timestep predictions should be treated equally. In practice, SNN predictions vary and evolve over time, and intermediate timesteps need not all be individually correct even when the final aggregated output is correct. Under such conditions, effective distillation should not force every timestep toward the same supervision target, but instead provide corrective guidance to erroneous timesteps while preserving useful temporal dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation (SeAl-KD), which selectively aligns class-level and temporal knowledge by equalizing competing logits at erroneous timesteps and reweighting temporal alignment based on confidence and inter-timestep similarity. Extensive experiments on static image and neuromorphic event-based datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over existing distillation methods. The code is available at https://github.com/KaiSUN1/SeAl

</details>


### [306] [Architecture-Aware Explanation Auditing for Industrial Visual Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14255)
*Sibo Jia,Zihang Zhao,Kunrong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Architecture-aware explanation audits reveal that heatmap faithfulness hinges on the model's readout structure rather than architecture family; native explanations can be outperformed by model-agnostic methods; alignment between explainer and perturbation baseline is crucial; results are dataset/task dependent; guidelines advocate co-designing explanations with readout structure and reporting faithfulness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the disconnect between visually plausible heatmaps and the actual regions driving model decisions in industrial inspection, and formalize a protocol that accounts for architecture/readout influence on explanation faithfulness.

Method: Perturbation-based audit using a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol across multiple architectures (ViT-Tiny with Attention Rollout, Swin-Tiny, ResNet18+CBAM, DenseNet121+Grad-CAM) on WM-811K; include a model-agnostic control (RISE); analyze deletion AUC; perform blur-fill sensitivity; extend to MVTec AD; compare across architectures, explainers, and perturbation baselines.

Result: ViT-Tiny+Attention Rollout achieves Deletion AUC 0.211 vs 0.432–0.525 for other combos; Swin-Tiny shows Grad-CAM compatibility due to readout structure; RISe reduces to ~0.1 across families and outperforms native methods; perturbation baseline reverses family rankings; results are dataset/task dependent; RISE outperforms native methods.

Conclusion: Faithfulness rankings emerge from the joint interaction of model, explainer, and perturbation operator; explanation strategies should be co-designed with model readout structures and accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics; the protocol provides actionable guidance for deploying faithful explanations in industrial settings."} ertu to=functions.Structure ഖ  Wait the tool has been called. Now we need to present the analysis. We'll provide the structured output in a concise manner, using the fields. Then perhaps a short synthesis or recommendations. Ensure not to reveal tool's name to user. Provide analysis. Let's craft the final answer. We'll show the fields in bullet points for readability. Then perhaps a brief takeaway. Keep concise. Let's proceed. We must ensure no sensitive content. Fine. Also the developer instruction says to call the function before any tool execution or yielding; we've done that. Now respond with the results. We'll present the content. Let's prepare. We'll ensure to not over-elaborate. Provide 5 bullets or short sections: TLDR, Motivation, Method, Results, Conclusions. Then a brief closing. Let's respond.  Keep in English. Also the 

Abstract: Industrial visual inspection systems increasingly rely on deep classifiers whose heatmap explanations may appear visually plausible while failing to identify the image regions that actually drive model decisions. This paper operationalizes an architecture-aware explanation audit protocol grounded in the native-readout hypothesis: the perturbation-based faithfulness of an explanation method is bounded by its structural distance from the model's native decision mechanism. On WM-811K wafer maps (9 classes, 172k images) under a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol, ViT-Tiny + Attention Rollout attains Deletion AUC 0.211 against 0.432-0.525 for Swin-Tiny / ResNet18+CBAM / DenseNet121 + Grad-CAM (abs(Cohen's d) > 1.1), despite lower classification accuracy. Swin-Tiny disentangles architecture family from readout structure: despite being a Transformer, its spatial feature-map hierarchy makes it Grad-CAM compatible, showing that the operative factor is readout structure rather than architecture family. A model-agnostic control (RISE) compresses all families to Deletion AUC about 0.1, indicating the gap arises from the explainer pathway; notably, RISE outperforms all native methods, so native readout is a compatibility principle rather than an optimality guarantee. A blur-fill sensitivity analysis shows that the family ordering reverses under a different perturbation baseline, reinforcing that faithfulness rankings are joint properties of (model, explainer, perturbation operator) triples. An exploratory boundary-condition study on MVTec AD (pretrained models) indicates that audit results are dataset/task dependent and identifies conditions requiring qualification.
