The protocol yields actionable guidance: explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure, and deployed heatmaps should be accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics.

</details>


### [307] [Dynamics of the Transformer Residual Stream: Coupling Spectral Geometry to Network Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14258)
*Jesseba Fernando,Grigori Guitchounts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training induces a depth-aware spectral geometry in LLMs: a monotonic gradient from rotation-dominated early layers to near-symmetric late layers, together with a cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into few residual modes; these effects are learned and tied to the model's functional topology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how perturbations propagate through LLM layers by examining the full Jacobian spectrum rather than relying on scalar summaries or approximate linearizations.

Method: Compute full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production-scale LLMs; analyze spectral geometry across depth, non-normality, low-rank structure, and graph-community couplings; compare trained vs initialization; test effects of removing structured non-normality.

Result: Found a monotonic spectral gradient with depth and a cumulative low-rank bottleneck; the gradient and collapse are learned and largely disappear when structured non-normality is removed; graph community topology predicts Jacobian amplification vs suppression with coupling sign determined by local operator type; these patterns are tied to the network's functional topology.

Conclusion: Reveals a learned spectral geometry that links perturbation propagation and compression to architectural topology; training shapes dynamical properties of the residual stream in a depth-dependent manner.

Abstract: Large language models are remarkably capable, yet how computation propagates through their layers remains poorly understood. A growing line of work treats depth as discrete time and the residual stream as a dynamical system, where each layer's nonlinear update has a local linear description. However, previous analyses have relied on scalar summaries or approximate linearizations, leaving the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs unknown. We perform full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production--scale LLMs and show that training installs a monotonic spectral gradient through depth -- from non-normal, rotation-dominated early layers to near--symmetric late layers -- together with a cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream's effective dimensions. Our experiments reveal that this gradient and the dimensional collapse are learned rather than architectural, and is largely dissolved when structured non-normality is removed. We further show that the topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether the Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them, with the sign of the coupling determined by the local operator type, a relationship absent at initialization. These results map a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology.

</details>


### [308] [TILT: Target-induced loss tilting under covariate shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14280)
*Kakei Yamamoto,Martin J. Wainwright*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Target-Induced Loss Tilting (TILT) for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift by decomposing the predictor into f+b and penalizing b on target data; yields oracle-type guarantees and improved target performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address covariate shift in unsupervised domain adaptation by inducing target-specific weighting without explicit density ratios, achieving uniform-bounded estimand and end-to-end guarantees.

Method: Decompose source predictor into f+b; train using labeled source with an auxiliary penalty on b using unlabeled target inputs; derive population-level interpretation as relative importance weighting via b*_f; prove finite-sample oracle inequality; apply to sparse ReLU nets; experiments.

Result: Target-domain performance improves over source-only, exact importance weighting, and density-ratio baselines; stable with regularization; provides end-to-end guarantees.

Conclusion: TILT offers a theoretically grounded and practically effective approach to UDA under covariate shift, with robust generalization and stable tuning, applicable to shallow and deep models.

Abstract: We introduce and analyze Target-Induced Loss Tilting (TILT) for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift. It is based on a novel objective function that decomposes the source predictor as $f+b$, fits $f+b$ on labeled source data while simultaneously penalizing the auxiliary component $b$ on unlabeled target inputs. The resulting fit $f$ is deployed as the final target predictor. At the population level, we show that this target-side penalty implicitly induces relative importance weighting at the population level, but in terms of an estimand $b^*_f$ that is self-localized to the current error, and remains uniformly bounded for any source-target pair (even those with disjoint supports). We prove a general finite-sample oracle inequality on the excess risk, and use it to give an end-to-end guarantee for training with sparse ReLU networks. Experiments on controlled regression problems and shifted CIFAR-100 distillation show that TILT improves target-domain performance over source-only training, exact importance weighting, and relative density-ratio baselines, with a stable dependence on the regularization parameter.

</details>


### [309] [Smooth Multi-Policy Causal Effect Estimation in Longitudinal Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14284)
*Wenxin Chen,Weishen Pan,Kyra Gan,Fei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jointly estimating multiple dynamic treatment policies via a policy-aware Q-function reparameterization (PEQ-Net) improves finite-sample stability and predictive accuracy, outperforming ICE-based methods, especially for related policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional longitudinal causal inference estimates each policy in isolation, which induces a structurally unanalyzed second-order bias and inflated finite-sample variance. Shared information across counterfactuals could stabilize estimates and improve efficiency.

Method: Introduce a policy-aware reparameterization of ICE Q-functions to enable joint estimation. Implement PEQ-Net with a shared policy encoder trained through kernel mean embeddings to reflect population-level policy dissimilarities. Apply an LTMLE correction step to enforce a structural constraint on the second-order remainder, stabilizing variance.

Result: Empirical evaluation on semi-synthetic datasets shows PEQ-Net consistently outperforms ICE-based methods, achieving substantial reductions in RMSE, especially for closely related policies.

Conclusion: Policy-aware joint estimation via PEQ-Net reduces second-order bias and stabilizes finite-sample variance in multi-policy dynamic treatment settings, offering improved accuracy for policy evaluation.

Abstract: Comparative evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies is essential for healthcare and policy decisions, yet conventional longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each in isolation, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals. We demonstrate that this separate estimation paradigm induces a structurally uncontrolled second-order bias, inflating finite-sample variance even after standard debiasing with longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation(LTMLE). To address this, we propose a policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions that enables joint estimation through shared representations. We implement this approach in the Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net), an architecture centered on a shared policy encoder. The encoder is trained using kernel mean embeddings, ensuring that the learned representation space reflects population-level policy dissimilarities. After applying an LTMLE correction step, we prove this design imposes a structural constraint on the second-order remainder, thereby stabilizing finite-sample variance. Experiments on semi-synthetic datasets demonstrate that PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies.

</details>


### [310] [MetaMoE: Diversity-Aware Proxy Selection for Privacy-Preserving Mixture-of-Experts Unification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14289)
*Weisen Jiang,Shuhao Chen,Sinno Jialin Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaMoE introduces a privacy-preserving method to unify independently trained, domain-specialized MoE experts using public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. It uses diversity-aware proxy selection and a context-aware router to align and coordinate experts, achieving superior results over prior privacy-preserving MoE unification methods on CV and NLP tasks; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Private data are distributed across clients and cannot be shared, making centralized MoE training infeasible. A unified MoE is desirable for cross-domain collaboration, but requires access to representative data without violating privacy.

Method: Leverage public proxy data as surrogates for private data. Implement diversity-aware proxy selection to pick client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data. Use proxies to align expert training and coordinate their unification, and employ a context-aware router to select experts across heterogeneous inputs.

Result: Empirical results on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks show that MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods.

Conclusion: Public-data proxies combined with diversity-aware selection and a context-aware router enable effective privacy-preserving unification of domain-specialized MoE experts, improving coordination and performance across modalities.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale capacity by combining specialized experts, but most existing approaches assume centralized access to training data. In practice, data are distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making unified MoE training challenging. We propose MetaMoE, a privacy-preserving framework that unifies independently trained, domain-specialized experts into a single MoE using public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. Central to MetaMoE is diversity-aware proxy selection, which selects client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data to effectively approximate private data distributions and supervise router learning. These proxies are further used to align expert training, improving expert coordination at unification time, while a context-aware router enhances expert selection across heterogeneous inputs. Experiments on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks demonstrate that MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ws-jiang/MetaMoE.

</details>


### [311] [Minimal-Intervention KV Retention: A Design-Space Study and a Diversity-Penalty Survivor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14292)
*Libo Sun,Po-wei Harn,Peixiong He,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In small-budget KV-cache compression, seven mechanisms across representation, routing, cadence, decoding, and scoring were evaluated on long-form math reasoning with two distilled models; all were rejected. A minimal scoring modification to TriAttention (α) using greedy facility-location-inspired selection with a V-space redundancy penalty (λ) achieved significance in some settings under a preregistered, held-out evaluation, suggesting simple scoring changes can outperform heavier designs in budget-constrained regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify effective KV-cache compression strategies under tight budget constraints for long-form mathematical reasoning, comparing a broad design space and emphasizing rigorous, preregistered evaluation to distinguish genuine gains from architectural tinkering.

Method: Evaluate seven KV-cache compression mechanisms spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring, at budgets b ∈ {64,128}, on MATH-500 with two distilled models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill). Use matched memory. Implement α as a one-function modification to TriAttention retention scorer, replacing argmax-top-k with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by λ. A preregistered protocol tunes λ on a frozen development split and validates on a disjoint held-out split; assess significance with Bonferroni correction.

Result: Seven mechanisms were all rejected. α with λ=0.5 achieved Bonferroni-corrected significance in two of four (model,budget) cells (Qwen b=128 and Llama b=64); no cell was significantly negative; pre-registered Branch A triggered.

Conclusion: A minimal, scoring-based modification can outperform heavier structural redesigns in tight-budget KV-cache compression, as evidenced by a matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol that minimizes optimistic bias and reveals asymmetry favoring simple scoring changes over complex architecture changes.

Abstract: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. We study seven mechanisms across these five families under matched mean cache on long-form mathematical reasoning (MATH-500~\cite{hendrycks2021math}) with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill~\cite{deepseek2025r1}) at budgets $b \in \{64, 128\}$. All seven were rejected. We then propose $α$, a one-function modification to the TriAttention~\cite{mao2026triattention} retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-$k$ with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight $λ$. A pre-registered protocol tunes $λ$ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split; with $λ= 0.5$, $α$ clears Bonferroni on two of the four (model, budget) cells (Qwen $b{=}128$ and Llama $b{=}64$), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch~A triggers. The finding is asymmetric: a minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns in this regime, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that made the asymmetry visible.

</details>


### [312] [Policy Optimization in Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Action Spaces via Mixed Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14297)
*Matias Alvo,Daniel Russo,Yash Kanoria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO) blends pathwise (gradient-through-simulator) and score-function estimators to learn in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces. It reformulates discrete decisions into a hybrid structure, maintains unbiasedness, and shows empirical gains over PPO that grow with continuous-action dimensionality. Theoretical analysis indicates the cross-term of the mixed gradient vanishes near a discrete best response, enabling decentralized updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address credit-assignment and gradient quality issues in high-dimensional hybrid RL where discrete regimes interact with continuous actions, and to overcome limitations of purely SF-based or differentiable-simulator approaches in the presence of non-smooth dynamics.

Method: Backpropagate through the simulator wherever smooth; use a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise (when differentiable) and score-function components; reformulate action-discontinuity problems into a hybrid framework; analyze the mixed-gradient structure; evaluate on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems; provide open-source resources.

Result: Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on the tested tasks, with larger performance gaps as the continuous-action dimension grows.

Conclusion: The mixed gradient framework yields unbiased, variance-controlled updates; the cross-term becomes negligible near a discrete best response, enabling approximate decentralized updates for continuous and discrete components and broad applicability to problems with discrete decisions and non-smooth dynamics.

Abstract: We study reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, such as settings where the discrete component selects a regime (or index) and the continuous component optimizes within it -- a structure common in robotics, control, and operations problems. Standard model-free policy gradient methods rely on score-function (SF) estimators and suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional settings, leading to poor gradient quality. On the other hand, differentiable simulation largely sidesteps these issues by backpropagating through a simulator, but the presence of discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics yields biased or uninformative gradients. To address this, we propose Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO), which backpropagates through the simulator wherever smoothness permits, using a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise and SF gradients while maintaining unbiasedness. We also show how problems with action discontinuities can be reformulated in hybrid form, further broadening its applicability. Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as the continuous action dimension grows. Finally, we characterize the structure of the mixed gradient, showing that its cross term -- which captures how continuous actions influence future discrete decisions -- becomes negligible near a discrete best response, thereby enabling approximate decentralized updates of the continuous and discrete components and reducing variance near optimality. All resources are available at github.com/MatiasAlvo/hybrid-rl.

</details>


### [313] [Language-Induced Priors for Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14301)
*Qiyuan Chen,Jiayu Zhou,Raed Al Kontar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic framework for cold-start domain adaptation that uses expert textual descriptions to induce a Language-Induced Prior (LIP), guiding source-domain relevance via an EM procedure; compatible with any likelihood-based model, with theoretical guarantees and multi-task empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve negative transfer in cold-start domain adaptation by leveraging semantic target-domain descriptions, which are rich but underutilized resources.

Method: Translate semantic target descriptions into a Language-Induced Prior learned from a pretrained LLM, integrate into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify and weigh source relevance; the framework is model-agnostic as long as a likelihood is available and allows the LIP to guide source selection while updating with data.

Result: Theoretically, the estimator approximates an oracle cold-start MSE when the prior is correct and is asymptotically consistent regardless of the LIP quality. Empirically, validated on three tasks: Gaussian estimation (descriptive), C-MAPSS (predictive), and MuJoCo hopper (prescriptive).

Conclusion: The work offers a principled prior-informed approach to source selection in cold-start domain adaptation, with solid theoretical guarantees and cross-domain empirical validation, and demonstrates robustness to imperfect priors while remaining compatible with various likelihood-based models.

Abstract: Domain adaptation faces a fundamental paradox in the cold-start regime. When target data is scarce, statistical methods fail to distinguish relevant source domains from irrelevant ones, which often leads to negative transfer. In this paper, we address this challenge by leveraging expert textual descriptions of the target domain, a resource that is often available but overlooked. We propose a probabilistic framework that translates these semantic descriptions into a choice model, namely a Language-Induced Prior (LIP), that learns the preferences from a pretrained Large Language Model (LLM). The LIP is then integrated into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance. Methodologically, this framework is compatible with any parametric model where a likelihood is available. It allows the LIP to guide the selection of sources when target signals are weak, while gradually refining these choices as samples accumulate. Theoretically, we prove that the estimator roughly matches an oracle cold-start MSE under a correct prior, while remaining asymptotically consistent regardless of the quality of the LIP. Empirically, we validated the framework on a descriptive (Gaussian estimation), a predictive (C-MAPSS dataset), and a prescriptive task (MuJoCo hopper).

</details>


### [314] [Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning for Reusing Local Transition Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14304)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Carlee Joe-Wong,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning (MSRL) introduces PSD matrix descriptors to represent trajectory segments, enabling algebraic composition and transfer in a geometric matrix space; it comes with theoretical guarantees and improves finite-budget target AUC to 0.73.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit rich local transition geometry and shared structure for compositional generalization, addressing limitations of skill/predictive-model reuse that overlook local dynamics.

Method: Define trajectory-segment descriptors as positive semidefinite matrices aggregating first- and second-order stats of lifted one-step transitions; enable composition in matrix space; provide obstruction filtering; condition value functions on the descriptor; show invariance up to coordinate gauge and other theoretical properties; exhibit plug-in compatibility with standard RL methods; empirical evaluation on target AUC.

Result: Theoretically, descriptors are well-defined up to gauge, complete for a low-order additive signal class, additive under valid compositions, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. Empirically, MSRL attains best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL-from-scratch 0.65, TD-MPC-PT+FT 0.63, and TD-MPC 0.57.

Conclusion: MSRL provides a principled geometric abstraction for compositional generalization in sequential decision making, enabling transfer and bootstrap learning with compatibility to existing methods; demonstrated empirical gains.

Abstract: Compositional generalization in sequential decision-making requires identifying which parts of prior rollouts remain useful for new tasks. Existing methods reuse skills or predictive models, but often overlook rich local transition geometry and dynamics. We propose Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning (MSRL), a geometric abstraction that represents trajectory segments through positive semidefinite matrix descriptors aggregating first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. These descriptors expose shared hidden structure, support algebraic composition in an abstract matrix space, and reveal opportunities for transfer. We prove that the descriptor is well defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. We further show that conditioning value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation of action values, enabling source-learned matrix-to-value mappings to bootstrap learning in new tasks. MSRL is plug-in compatible with standard model-free and model-based methods, while obstruction filtering rejects implausible compositions. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).

</details>


### [315] [Beyond Binary: Reframing GUI Critique as Continuous Semantic Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14311)
*Yuchen Sun,Pei Fu,Shaojie Zhang,Anan Du,Xiuwen Xi,Ruoceng Zhang,Zhenbo Luo,Jian Luan,Chongyang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BBCritic reframes GUI action critique as metric learning, addressing two defects of binary critics (Affordance Collapse and Noise Sensitivity) via two-stage contrastive learning; introduces BBBench for evaluation; empirically, BBCritic-3B surpasses 7B binary SOTA and generalizes across platforms/tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI critics rely on binary action labeling, which collapses a rich, hierarchical action space and causes indistinguishable scores between valid actions and plausible distractors. This entanglement undermines fine-grained ranking needed for Test-Time Scaling (TTS).

Method: Two-stage contrastive learning that maps both instructions and actions into a shared Affordance Space under a four-level hierarchical taxonomy. The model is trained without extra annotation. Introduces BBBench, a benchmark pairing dense action spaces with hierarchical labels to enable fine-grained ranking evaluation.

Result: BBCritic-3B achieves superior performance over 7B-parameter binary models and shows strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks, supporting the view that GUI critique is a metric-learning problem rather than a pure classification task.

Conclusion: Critique in GUI settings benefits from treating it as metric learning within an Affordance Space and utilizing a structured hierarchy; this resolves information loss from binary labels and yields robust, transferable action ranking.

Abstract: Test-Time Scaling (TTS), which samples multiple candidate actions and ranks them via a Critic Model, has emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist GUI agents. Its efficacy thus hinges on the critic's fine-grained ranking ability. However, existing GUI critic models uniformly adopt binary classification. Our motivational analysis of these models exposes a severe entanglement: scores for valid actions and plausible-but-invalid distractors become indistinguishable. We attribute this failure to two structural defects: Affordance Collapse--the hierarchical affordance space is compressed into 0/1 labels; and Noise Sensitivity--binary objectives overfit to noisy decision boundaries. To resolve this, we introduce BBCritic (Beyond-Binary Critic), a paradigm shift grounded in the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis. Through two-stage contrastive learning, BBCritic aligns instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space, recovering the hierarchical structure that binary supervision flattens. We also present BBBench (Beyond-Binary Bench), the first GUI critic benchmark that pairs a dense action space with a hierarchical four-level taxonomy, enabling fine-grained ranking evaluation. Experimental results show that BBCritic-3B, trained without any extra annotation, outperforms 7B-parameter SOTA binary models. It demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks, supporting our methodological view: GUI critique is fundamentally a metric-learning problem, not a classification one.

</details>


### [316] [Guided Diffusion Sampling for Precipitation Forecast Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14317)
*Ayumu Ueyama,Kazuhiko Kawamoto,Hiroshi Kera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-guided diffusion sampling to reduce precipitation in diffusion-based weather forecasts; evaluates physical plausibility via vertical/variable profiles, latent-space trajectory deviation, and cross-model transferability; shows effective precipitation reduction with more plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations on WeatherBench2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where perturbation-based weather control via data-driven forecasting models has not been explored, and ensure interventions remain physically plausible rather than exploiting model artifacts.

Method: Introduce a gradient-based guidance framework that steers the diffusion sampling trajectory rather than perturbing atmospheric states; assess plausibility using (i) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (iii) cross-model transferability; validate on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 and compare against adversarial perturbations.

Result: The method achieves effective precipitation reduction while producing interventions that are more physically plausible than adversarial perturbations; demonstrations include evaluations of perturbation profiles, latent trajectory deviations, and cross-model transferability.

Conclusion: Gradient-guided diffusion sampling provides a physically consistent approach to precipitation reduction in diffusion-based weather forecasting, outperforming adversarial perturbations in plausibility; indicates potential for controlled interventions with attention to physical realism, and suggests further work on robustness and generalization across models.

Abstract: Extreme precipitation causes severe societal and economic damage, and weather control has long been discussed as a potential mitigation strategy. However, to the best of our knowledge, perturbation-based interventions for weather control using data-driven weather forecasting models have not yet been explored. While adversarial attacks also generate perturbations that alter forecasts, they aim to exploit model artifacts and do not account for physical plausibility. In this paper, we propose a gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation-reduction interventions through diffusion sampling in diffusion-based weather forecasting models. Instead of directly perturbing atmospheric states, our method steers the diffusion sampling trajectory, enabling precipitation reduction while maintaining consistency with the atmospheric distribution. To assess physical plausibility, we evaluate from three perspectives: (i) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (iii) cross-model transferability. Experiments on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 demonstrate that our method achieves effective precipitation reduction while yielding more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations.

</details>


### [317] [Dynamic Latent Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14323)
*Fangyuan Yu,Xin Su,Amir Abdullah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: General Dijkstra Search (GDS) enables globally optimal policy recovery via temporal concatenation of sub-policies in MDPs with time-varying rewards; Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR) learns discrete latent codes and routing and model parameters through dynamic search, showing gains on few-shot LM fine-tuning and revealing structured routing roles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve data-efficient, interpretable policy learning under time-varying rewards by decomposing policies into temporally concatenated sub-policies and by enabling dynamic, latent routing within language-model fine-tuning.

Method: GDS provides a theoretical framework in which globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be constructed by composing optimal sub-policies over time; DLR is a language-model post-training technique that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters via dynamic search in a single training stage.

Result: DLR matches or exceeds supervised fine-tuning in low-data regimes across four datasets and six models, with a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points; discrete-latent baselines underperform SFT; mechanistic analyses and ablations indicate learned structured routing with distinct causal roles.

Conclusion: DLR demonstrates effective latent routing for LM-based policy learning, and GDS provides a principled basis for temporal sub-policy composition; together, they offer data-efficient, interpretable routing and potential applicability to broader few-shot fine-tuning tasks.

Abstract: We investigate the temporal concatenation of sub-policies in Markov Decision Processes (MDP) with time-varying reward functions. We introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS), and prove that globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be recovered through temporal composition of intermediate optimal sub-policies. Motivated by the "search, select, update" principle underlying GDS, we propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR), a language-model post-training method that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search in a single training stage. In low-data fine-tuning settings, DLR matches or outperforms supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models, achieving a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points, while prior discrete-latent baselines consistently underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and targeted code ablations show that DLR learns structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.

</details>


### [318] [AIM-DDI: A Model-Agnostic Multimodal Integration Module for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14327)
*Yerin Park,Sangseon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular, architecture-agnostic multimodal integration module (AIM-DDI) improves unseen-drug DDI prediction by treating diverse drug signals as tokens in a shared latent space and fusing them with a model-agnostic fusion component; shows robust gains across diverse models, especially when both drugs are unseen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unseen-drug generalization in DDI prediction is essential but challenging; existing multimodal fusion methods often tie to specific prediction architectures, hindering reuse and transferability.

Method: Represent heterogeneous modalities (structural, chemical, semantic) as tokens in a shared latent space. Use a unified fusion module to model dependencies among modality tokens, enabling architecture-agnostic integration across different DDI predictors.

Result: Across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings, AIM-DDI yields consistent performance gains, with the largest improvements in the stringent both-unseen setting where neither drug in a test pair appeared during training.

Conclusion: Treating multimodal fusion as a reusable module, rather than a model-specific component, enhances robustness to unseen-drug DDI prediction and supports broader applicability across architectures.

Abstract: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction is a critical task in computational biomedicine, as adverse interactions between co-administered drugs can cause severe side effects and clinical risks. A key challenge is unseen-drug generalization, where interactions must be predicted for drugs not observed during training. Although multimodal DDI models exploit diverse drug-related information, their fusion mechanisms are often tied to specific prediction architectures, limiting their reuse across models. To address this, we propose AIM-DDI, an architecture-independent multimodal integration module that represents heterogeneous modality information as tokens in a shared latent space. By modeling dependencies across modality tokens through a unified fusion module, AIM-DDI enables model-agnostic integration of structural, chemical, and semantic drug signals across different DDI prediction architectures. Extensive evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show that AIM-DDI consistently improves prediction performance, with the strongest gains under the most challenging both-unseen setting where neither drug in a test pair is observed during training. These results suggest that treating multimodal integration as a reusable module, rather than a model-specific fusion component, is an effective strategy for robust unseen-drug DDI prediction.

</details>


### [319] [Nearest-Neighbor Radii under Dependent Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14343)
*Yuanyuan Gao,Yilong Hou,Zhexiao Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling with strong mixing; dependence does not ruin NN geometry; results depend on local intrinsic dimension rather than ambient dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nearest-neighbor methods are fundamental; in practice data exhibit dependence (e.g., time series). It is unclear whether dependence alters NN geometry; this work analyzes NN radii under dependent sampling.

Method: Theoretical analysis under strong mixing conditions. Proves distribution-free almost-sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. Bounding techniques emphasize local intrinsic dimension.

Result: Established distribution-free almost-sure convergence of NN radii under polynomial mixing. Derived sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. Bounds depend on local intrinsic dimension, not ambient dimension. Findings extend to high-dimensional data concentrated on low-dimensional manifolds. Empirical validation via synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks.

Conclusion: Nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependent sampling; dependence affects scale only through local intrinsic dimension, supporting robust use of NN methods in dependent data regimes.

Abstract: Nearest-neighbor methods are fundamental to classical and modern machine learning, yet their geometric properties are typically analyzed under independent sampling. In this paper, we study the nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling. We consider strong mixing dependent observations and ask whether dependence changes the scale of nearest-neighbor neighborhoods. We establish distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. The moment bounds depend on the local intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension, making the results applicable to high-dimensional data concentrated near lower-dimensional manifolds. Synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks support the theory, showing that nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependence sampling.

</details>


### [320] [Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14347)
*Jessica Rumbelow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exemplar Partitioning (EP) creates unsupervised, interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations via Voronoi partitions anchored by exemplars. It requires no prespecified dictionary size, enables cross-layer/checkpoint comparability, and offers causal interpretability, out-of-distribution detection, and strong latent-concept performance with orders-of-magnitude less compute than comparable SAEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide interpretable, directly comparable activation representations for large language models without supervised labels or heavy training, enabling cross-model and cross-checkpoint diagnostics and efficient evaluation.

Method: Streamed activations are clustered with a distance threshold using leader (Voronoi) partitioning. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that defines membership and intervention direction. The dictionary size emerges from data geometry rather than being prespecified, allowing direct cross-model comparability of dictionaries built from the same stream.

Result: EP regions are interpretable and support causal interventions. Cross-checkpoint matching distinguishes finetuning effects. EP regions and Gemma Scope SAE features decompose activation space differently but share a core (~20% match with SAE features at F1>0.5). One-hot probes retain ~97% of raw-activation probe accuracy with l0=1. Nearest-exemplar distance provides a free OOD signal. On AxBench/ Gemma-2-2B-it L20, EP achieves AUROC 0.881 (+0.126 over GemmaScope SAE leaderboard) and is within 0.030 of SAE-A’s 0.911, with ~1000x less build compute.

Conclusion: EP provides a scalable, interpretable, and cross-checkpoint-compatible dictionary of activation regions that supports causal interventions and competitive performance with far lower computation than standard SAE baselines, plus a built-in OOD signal.

Abstract: We introduce Exemplar Partitioning (EP), an unsupervised method for constructing interpretable feature dictionaries from large language model activations with $\sim 10^{3}\times$ fewer tokens than comparable sparse autoencoders (SAEs). An EP dictionary is a Voronoi partition of activation space, built by leader-clustering streamed activations within a distance threshold. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that serves as both its membership criterion and intervention direction; dictionary size is not prespecified, but determined by the activation geometry at that threshold. Because exemplars are observed rather than learned, dictionaries built from the same data stream are directly comparable across layers, models, and training checkpoints.
  We characterise EP as an interpretability object via targeted demonstrations of properties newly accessible through this construction, plus one head-to-head benchmark. In Gemma-2-2B, EP dictionary regions are interpretable and support causal interventions: refusal in instruction-tuned Gemma concentrates in a region whose exemplar ablation can collapse held-out refusal. Cross-checkpoint matching between base and instruction-tuned dictionaries separates the directions preserved through finetuning from those introduced by it. EP regions and Gemma Scope SAE features decompose activation space differently but agree on a shared core: $\sim 20\%$ of EP regions match an SAE feature at $F_{1} > 0.5$, and EP one-hot probes retain $\sim 97\%$ of raw-activation probe accuracy at $\ell_{0} = 1$. Nearest-exemplar distance provides a free out-of-distribution signal at inference. On AxBench latent concept detection at Gemma-2-2B-it L20, EP at $p_{1}$ reaches mean AUROC $0.881$, $+0.126$ over the canonical GemmaScope SAE leaderboard entry and within $0.030$ of SAE-A's $0.911$, at $\sim 10^{3}\times$ less build compute.

</details>


### [321] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Task Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14350)
*Nicholas E. Corrado,Wenyuan Huang,Josiah P. Hanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRATS addresses imbalanced data allocation in multi-task RL by adaptive task sampling to minimize the worst-case return gap, improving data efficiency and worst-task performance on MetaWorld MT10/MT50.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MTRL allocates equal interactions per task, which overfits easy tasks and underfits hard ones; existing gradient-based methods focus on gradient conflicts, not data allocation. Need to balance data distribution to accelerate learning across tasks.

Method: Proposes Distributionally Robust Adaptive Task Sampling (DRATS). Formulates MTRL as a feasibility problem and derives a minimax objective to minimize the worst-case return gap to a target; adaptively samples tasks that are furthest from being solved.

Result: Empirical results on MetaWorld MT10 and MT50 show DRATS improves data efficiency and increases worst-task performance relative to existing task-sampling baselines.

Conclusion: Adaptive, distributionally robust task sampling is a viable strategy for mitigating data allocation imbalance in MTRL; future work could extend to other domains and analyze theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) aims to train a single agent to efficiently optimize performance across multiple tasks simultaneously. However, jointly optimizing all tasks often yields imbalanced learning: agents quickly solve easy tasks but learn slowly on harder ones. While prior work primarily attributes this imbalance to conflicting task gradients and proposes gradient manipulation or specialized architectures to address it, we instead focus on a distinct and under-explored challenge: imbalanced data allocation. Standard MTRL allocates an equal number of environment interactions to each task, which over-allocates data to easy tasks that require relatively few interactions to solve and under-allocates data to hard tasks that require substantially more experience to solve. To address this challenge, we introduce Distributionally Robust Adaptive Task Sampling (DRATS), an algorithm that adaptively prioritizes sampling tasks furthest from being solved. We derive DRATS by formalizing MTRL as a feasibility problem from which we derive a minimax objective for minimizing the worst-case return gap, the difference between a desired target return and the agent's return on a task. In benchmarks like MetaWorld-MT10 and MT50, DRATS improves data efficiency and increases worst-task performance compared to existing task sampling algorithms.

</details>


### [322] [RQ-MoE: Residual Quantization via Mixture of Experts for Efficient Input-Dependent Vector Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14359)
*Zhengjia Zhong,Shuyan Ke,Zaizhou Lin,Jiaqi Song,Hongyi Lan,Hui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RQ-MoE unifies residual quantization and QINCo using a two-level mixture-of-experts with dual-stream quantization to enable input-dependent codebooks, enabling parallel decoding and 6x-14x faster decoding while preserving reconstruction/retrieval performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the expressiveness limits of static codebooks on heterogeneous data and the decoding bottlenecks of sequential dynamic quantizers; enable adaptive yet efficient vector quantization.

Method: Introduce a two-level Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with dual-stream (likely parallel) quantization that dynamically constructs codebooks conditioned on the input; decouple control/instruction from quantization to allow parallel decoding; show that RC and QINCo are constrained special cases of RQ-MoE; provide guidelines for setting expert dimensionality.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art or on-par reconstruction and retrieval performance; reports 6x-14x faster decoding compared with prior vector quantization methods; code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: RQ-MoE offers a general, efficient and theoretically grounded framework for vector quantization that extends existing methods, enabling dynamic, input-adaptive codebooks with parallel decoding and broad applicability.

Abstract: Vector quantization is a fundamental tool for compressing high-dimensional embeddings, yet existing multi-codebook methods rely on static codebooks that limit expressiveness under heterogeneous data geometry. While recent dynamic quantizers like QINCo adapt codebooks to individual inputs and improve expressiveness, their strict sequential dependencies create decoding bottlenecks. We propose Residual Quantization via Mixture of Experts (RQ-MoE), a framework combining a two-level MoE with dual-stream quantization to enable input-dependent codebook adaptation for efficient vector quantization. RQ-MoE enables dynamic codebook construction and decouples instruction from quantization, facilitating parallel decoding. Theoretically, we show that standard Residual Quantization and QINCo can be recovered as constrained special cases of RQ-MoE, and derive a guideline for setting expert dimensionality in RQ-MoE. Extensive experiments show that RQ-MoE achieves state-of-the-art or on-par performance in reconstruction and retrieval, while providing 6x-14x faster decoding than prior vector quantization methods. The implementation is available at https://github.com/KDEGroup/RQ-MoE.

</details>


### [323] [MoRe: Modular Representations for Principled Continual Representation Learning on Squantial Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14364)
*Jiaqi Sun,Boyang Sun,Mohamad Rasmy,Xiangchen Song,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoRe introduces a representation-centric continual learning framework that discovers a hierarchy of modular representations (fundamental and specific) within the model, enabling principled reuse, alignment, and expansion during adaptation while preserving existing modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the representational nature of continual learning by uncovering intrinsic modular structure in representations rather than modifying entire architectures or task-specific parameters; time-delayed dependencies provide a natural signal for organizing representations.

Method: Identify and separate representations into a hierarchy of modules (fundamental and specific) with identifiability guarantees; enable principled module reuse, alignment, and expansion during continual adaptation; preserve old modules by construction.

Result: Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real-world LLM activations reveal interpretable hierarchical modular structure and an improved plasticity-stability trade-off, supporting MoRe as a principled foundation for continual adaptation.

Conclusion: MoRe offers a representation-based, modular approach to continual learning with identifiability guarantees, enabling controlled adaptation and knowledge preservation through modular decomposition and reuse.

Abstract: Continual learning requires models to adapt to new data while preserving previously acquired knowledge. At its core, this challenge can be viewed as principled one-step adaptation: incorporating new information with minimal interference to existing representations. Most existing approaches address this challenge by modifying model parameters or architectures in a supervised, task-specific manner. However, the underlying issue is representational: tasks require distinct yet structured representations that can be selectively updated without disrupting representations, while structure should reflect intrinsic organization in the data rather than task boundaries. In sequential data, time-delayed dependencies provide a natural signal for uncovering this organization, revealing how fundamental representations give rise to more specific ones. Inspired by the modular organization of the human brain, we propose MoRe, a framework that identifies modularity in the representation itself rather than allocating it at the architectural level. MoRe decomposes knowledge into a hierarchy of fundamental and specific modules with identifiability guarantees, enabling principled module reuse, alignment, and expansion during adaptation while preserving old modules by construction. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real-world LLM activations demonstrate interpretable hierarchical structure, improved plasticity-stability trade-offs, suggesting MoRe as a principled foundation for continual adaptation

</details>


### [324] [LoMETab: Beyond Rank-1 Ensembles for Tabular Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14365)
*Changryeol Choi,Hyewon Park,Yujin Kwon,Gowun Jeong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoMETab generalizes multiplicative implicit ensembles to a rank-r identity-residual Hadamard modulation, parameterizing each member weight as W_k = W ⊙ (1 + A_k B_k^T) with shared W and member-specific low-rank factors. It introduces two diversity-controls—adapter rank r and initialization scale σ_init—and proves that for r ≥ 2 this expands the hypothesis class beyond rank-1 BatchEnsemble/TabM. Empirically, it demonstrates higher predictive diversity (via pairwise KL, argmax disagreement, and regression ambiguity) and dataset-dependent performance across the (r, σ_init) grid, validating LoMETab as a controllable implicit-ensemble family rather than a fixed rank-1 construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark cascades in tabular learning show tight performance clusters across diverse architectures, motivating understanding and control of mechanisms that render simple neural tabular models competitive; aim to develop tunable, implicit-ensemble mechanisms to increase diversity and robustness.

Method: Proposes LoMETab: a rank-r generalization where each member weight is W_k = W ⊙ (1 + A_k B_k^T), with shared W and member-specific low-rank factors A_k, B_k. Studies two diversity axes—adapter rank r and initialization scale σ_init. Proves that for r ≥ 2 the hypothesis class strictly enlarges BatchEnsemble. Conducts empirical evaluations on representative classification and regression datasets, measuring diversity via pairwise KL; analyzes output-level measures such as argmax disagreement (classification) and regression ambiguity; performs grid sweeps over (r, σ_init) to assess performance.

Result: LoMETab yields measurable predictive diversity beyond additive low-rank ablations, with pairwise KL spanning multiple orders of magnitude across configurations. Diversity translates to decision- and output-level variation (argmax disagreement and regression ambiguity). Performance is dataset-dependent across the (r, σ_init) grid, supporting LoMETab as a tunable, controllable implicit-ensemble family with richer expressivity than rank-1 constructions.

Conclusion: LoMETab extends multiplicative implicit ensembles to a rank-r, identity-residual Hadamard family, offering explicit controls over model diversity and capacity. The approach provides a principled, tunable alternative to fixed-rank schemes, with empirical evidence that diversity benefits depend on data and configuration, enabling more robust tabular learning.

Abstract: Recent tabular learning benchmarks increasingly show a tight performance cluster rather than a clear hierarchy among leading methods, spanning gradient boosted decision trees, attention-based architectures, and implicit ensembles such as TabM. As benchmark gains plateau, a complementary goal is to understand and control the mechanisms that make simple neural tabular models competitive. We propose LoMETab, a rank-$r$ generalization of multiplicative implicit ensembles. LoMETab lifts the rank-1 BatchEnsemble/TabM modulation to a rank-$r$ identity-residual Hadamard family by parameterizing each member weight as $W_k = W \odot (1 + A_kB_k^\top)$, where $W$ is shared and $(A_k, B_k)$ are member-specific low-rank factors. This exposes two practical diversity-control axes: the adapter rank $r$ and the initialization scale $σ_{\mathrm{init}}$, and we prove that for $r \ge 2$ this generalization strictly enlarges BatchEnsemble's hypothesis class. Empirically, we show that this added capacity manifests as measurable predictive diversity after training: on representative classification datasets, LoMETab sustains higher pairwise KL than an additive low-rank ablation, and $(r, σ_{\mathrm{init}})$ provides broad control over pairwise KL, varying by up to several orders of magnitude across configurations. The induced diversity is reflected in task-appropriate output-level measures: argmax disagreement for classification and ambiguity for regression, indicating that the control extends beyond pairwise KL to decision- and output-level member variation. Finally, experiments sweeping over adapter rank $r$ and initialization scale $σ_{\mathrm{init}}$ reveal that predictive performance is dataset-dependent over the $(r, σ_{\mathrm{init}})$ grid, supporting LoMETab as a controllable family of implicit ensembles rather than a fixed rank-1 construction.

</details>


### [325] [Turning Stale Gradients into Stable Gradients: Coherent Coordinate Descent with Implicit Landscape Smoothing for Lightweight Zeroth-Order Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14373)
*Chen Liang,Xiatao Sun,Qian Wang,Daniel Rakita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoCD is a deterministic, sample-efficient zeroth-order optimizer that uses coherent coordinate descent to leverage stale gradients, achieving O(1) query per step and improved stability over randomized ZO methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zeroth-order optimization is essential when backpropagation is unavailable or impractical (e.g., on-device learning, black-box settings). Existing methods trade off sample efficiency against variance; a deterministic, budget-aware approach could improve practicality.

Method: Formalizes gradient coherence and proves equivalence of CoCD to Block Cyclic Coordinate Descent with warm starts, turning historical gradients into assets. Develops a deterministic, coordinate-descent-like update with constant query complexity per step and global descent guarantees. Derives error bounds showing larger finite-difference steps can implicitly smooth the landscape, improving convergence stability.

Result: Empirical results on MLP, CNN, and ResNet (up to 270k parameters) show CoCD outperforms Block BCCD in sample efficiency and convergence loss/accuracy, with superior stability compared to randomized ZO baselines.

Conclusion: Deterministic, structure-aware updates can outperform randomization for lightweight zeroth-order optimization, enabling stable, efficient on-device or black-box learning with fixed, low query budgets.

Abstract: Zeroth-Order (ZO) optimization is pivotal for scenarios where backpropagation is unavailable, such as memory-constrained on-device learning and black-box optimization. However, existing methods face a stark trade-off: they are either sample-inefficient (e.g., standard finite differences) or suffer from high variance due to randomized estimation (e.g., random subspace methods). In this work, we propose Coherent Coordinate Descent (CoCD), a deterministic, sample-efficient, and budget-aware ZO optimizer. Theoretically, we formalize the notion of gradient coherence and demonstrate that CoCD is equivalent to Block Cyclic Coordinate Descent (BCCD) with ``warm starts,'' effectively converting historical (stale) gradients from a liability into a computational asset. This mechanism enables $O(1)$ query complexity per step while maintaining global descent directions. Furthermore, we derive error bounds revealing a counter-intuitive insight: larger finite-difference step sizes can induce an implicit smoothing effect on the optimization landscape by reducing the effective smoothness constant, thereby improving convergence stability. Experiments on MLP, CNN, and ResNet architectures (up to 270k parameters) demonstrate that CoCD significantly outperforms BCCD in terms of sample efficiency and convergence loss/accuracy, and exhibits superior stability over randomized ZO methods. Our results suggest that deterministic, structure-aware updates offer a superior alternative to randomization for lightweight ZO optimization.

</details>


### [326] [Optimal Pattern Detection Tree for Symbolic Rule-Based Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14374)
*Young-Chae Hong,Yangho Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPDT uses a mixed-integer programming approach to find a single optimal binary rule (pattern) that maximizes dataset coverage while minimizing false positives; BSC lets domain knowledge be encoded as constraints; claims optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide interpretable, rule-based learning instead of black-box models; enable integration of prior knowledge and compliance constraints.

Method: Formulate OPDT as an MILP to search for a single rule (pattern); define objective to maximize coverage and minimize false positives; introduce Branching Structure Constraints to inject domain knowledge and constraints; solve for optimality.

Result: Computational experiments show OPDT discovers a pattern with optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets within reasonable runtime.

Conclusion: OPDT with BSC offers interpretable, optimal pattern discovery and supports knowledge-encoded constraints; applicable across domains requiring transparency.

Abstract: Pattern discovery in data plays a crucial role across diverse domains, including healthcare, risk assessment, and machinery maintenance. In contrast to black-box deep learning models, symbolic rule discovery emerges as a key data mining task, generating human-interpretable rules that offer both transparency and intuitive explainability. This paper introduces the Optimal Pattern Detection Tree (OPDT), a rule-based machine learning model based on novel mixed-integer programming to discover a single optimal pattern in data through binary classification. To incorporate prior knowledge and compliance requirements, we further introduce the Branching Structure Constraints (BSC) framework, which enables decision makers to encode domain knowledge and constraints directly into the model. This optimization-based approach discovers a hidden underlying pattern in datasets, when it exists, by identifying an optimal rule that maximizes coverage while minimizing the false positive rate due to misclassification. Our computational experiments show that OPDT discovers a pattern with optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets within reasonable runtime.

</details>


### [327] [Data-Augmented Game Starts for Accelerating Self-Play Exploration in Imperfect Information Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14379)
*JB Lanier,Nathan Monette,Pierre Baldi,Roy Fox*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS) seeds RL data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline expert data to accelerate exploration in regularized policy-gradient methods for two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games. It improves exploitability within fixed compute budgets on long-horizon, analytically tractable games (Kuhn Poker variants, Goofspiel) but can bias equilibria; mitigated by multi-task observation flags. A new benchmark suite with higher exploration challenges is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational infeasibility of finding approximate equilibria in large-scale imperfect-information competitive games due to sparse rewards and long horizons; leverage offline human demonstrations to guide exploration toward strategically relevant subgames.

Method: Introduce Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS): initialize RL data collection from intermediate states sampled from offline data; integrate into regularized policy-gradient methods for 2p0s games; analyze bias in equilibria due to augmented starts; propose multi-task observation flags as a mitigation; evaluate on synthetic datasets and analytically tractable long-horizon variants of Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game.

Result: Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in challenging exploration settings; demonstrates that augmented starting state distributions can bias equilibria, mitigated by multi-task observation flags; provides a new set of benchmark environments with higher exploration challenges while keeping exploitability measurements analytically tractable.

Conclusion: DAGS can substantially accelerate exploration and improve approximation of equilibria in imperfect-information games, but (a) starting-state augmentation can bias equilibria and (b) simple mitigations like multi-task flags are effective. The paper also contributes new benchmark environments for future study.

Abstract: Finding approximate equilibria for large-scale imperfect-information competitive games such as StarCraft, Dota, and CounterStrike remains computationally infeasible due to sparse rewards and challenging exploration over long horizons. In this paper, we propose a multi-agent starting-state sampling strategy designed to substantially accelerate online exploration in regularized policy-gradient game methods for two-player zero-sum (2p0s) games. Motivated by an assumption that offline demonstrations from skilled humans can provide good coverage of high-level strategies relevant to equilibrium play, we propose the initialization of reinforcement learning data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline data to facilitate exploration of strategically relevant subgames. Referring to this method as Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS), we perform experiments using synthetic datasets and analytically tractable, long-horizon control variants of two-player Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game designed to penalize biased beliefs over hidden information. Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in games with significantly more challenging exploration. We show that augmenting starting state distributions when solving imperfect information games can lead to biased equilibria, and we provide a straightforward mitigation to this in the form of multi-task observation flags. Finally, we release a new set of benchmark environments that drastically increase exploration challenges and state counts in existing OpenSpiel games while keeping exploitability measurements analytically tractable.

</details>


### [328] [NodeSynth: Socially Aligned Synthetic Data for AI Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14381)
*Qazi Mamunur Rashid,Xuan Yang,Zhengzhe Yang,Yanzhou Pan,Erin van Liemt,Darlene Neal,Kshitij Pancholi,Jamila Smith-Loud*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NodeSynth is an evidence-grounded synthetic data framework that expands taxonomic granularity via TaG to produce socially relevant queries; it reveals higher LLM failure rates than human benchmarks and highlights gaps in guard models; code and data are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for sociotechnical nuance in synthetic data for evaluating AI in high-stakes, sensitive domains, beyond generic, unguided datasets.

Method: Fine-tune a taxonomy generator (TaG) anchored in real-world evidence to generate socially relevant synthetic queries; evaluate across four mainstream LLMs (e.g., Claude 4.5 Haiku); perform ablations to isolate the impact of granular taxonomic expansion; conduct independent validation of guard models.

Result: NodeSynth elicited failure rates up to five times higher than human-authored benchmarks; ablation shows granular taxonomic expansion drives these failure rates; independent validation reveals critical deficiencies in guard models (e.g., Llama-Guard-3).

Conclusion: Open-source end-to-end prototype and datasets enable scalable, high-stakes model evaluation and targeted safety interventions; code and data released at https://github.com/google-research/nodesynth.

Abstract: Recent advancements in generative AI facilitate large-scale synthetic data generation for model evaluation. However, without targeted approaches, these datasets often lack the sociotechnical nuance required for sensitive domains. We introduce NodeSynth, an evidence-grounded methodology that generates socially relevant synthetic queries by leveraging a fine-tuned taxonomy generator (TaG) anchored in real-world evidence. Evaluated against four mainstream LLMs (e.g., Claude 4.5 Haiku), NodeSynth elicited failure rates up to five times higher than human-authored benchmarks. Ablation studies confirm that our granular taxonomic expansion significantly drives these failure rates, while independent validation reveals critical deficiencies in prominent guard models (e.g., Llama-Guard-3). We open-source our end-to-end research prototype and datasets to enable scalable, high-stakes model evaluation and targeted safety interventions (https://github.com/google-research/nodesynth).

</details>


### [329] [Watch your neighbors: Training statistically accurate chaotic systems with local phase space information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14405)
*Joon-Hyuk Ko,Andrus Giraldo,Deok-Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bridges Jacobian- and statistics-focused chaotic dynamics modeling by learning surrogates with accurate local Jacobians and long-term statistics using a local attractor covering and MMD-based training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chaotic systems resist long-term exact predictions; a good surrogate should capture both local expansion/contraction (Jacobian) and long-term statistical behavior.

Method: Construct a local cover of a chaotic attractor in phase space, analyze expansion/contraction of these coverings under the dynamics, and train the surrogate by minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy between the pushforward distributions of the coverings under the surrogate and ground-truth dynamics.

Result: Significant improvement in Jacobian accuracy while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art methods that emphasize long-term statistics.

Conclusion: The framework integrates Jacobian-aware and statistically faithful dynamics learning, offering a more faithful surrogate for chaotic systems and providing a promising direction for unified dynamics modeling.

Abstract: Chaotic systems pose fundamental challenges for data-driven dynamics discovery, as small modeling errors lead to exponentially growing trajectory discrepancies. Since exact long-term prediction is unattainable, it is natural to ask what a good surrogate model for chaotic dynamics is. Prior work has largely focused either on reproducing the Jacobian of the underlying dynamics, which governs local expansion and contraction rates, or on training surrogate models that reproduce the ground-truth dynamics' long-term statistical behavior. In this work, we propose a new framework that aims to bridge these two paradigms by training surrogate dynamics models with accurate Jacobians and long-term statistical properties. Our method constructs a local covering of a chaotic attractor in phase space and analyzes the expansion and contraction of these coverings under the dynamics. The surrogate model is trained by minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy between the pushforward distributions of the coverings under the surrogate and ground-truth dynamics. Experiments show that our method significantly improves Jacobian accuracy while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art statistically accurate dynamics learning methods. Our code is fully available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/neighborwatch.

</details>


### [330] [GeoViSTA: Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer for Multimodal Environment Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14406)
*Yuhao Liu,Sadeer Al-Kindi,Ashok Veeraraghavan,Guha Balakrishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A vision-tabular transformer (GeoViSTA) unifies gridded imagery and tabular census data through cross-modal, geography-aware attention and self-supervised masked autoencoding, yielding transferable embeddings that improve disease mortality and fire hazard predictions across regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geospatial foundation models primarily leverage imagery and often ignore structured socioeconomic covariates stored in tabular form. Integrating these modalities should yield richer, more transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

Method: GeoViSTA is a vision-tabular transformer that uses bilateral cross-attention to exchange information between modalities. It employs a geography-aware attention mechanism to align continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. Training uses a self-supervised joint masked-autoencoding objective to recover missing image patches and tabular rows from local spatial context and cross-modal cues.

Result: Unified embeddings improve linear probing performance on downstream tasks, notably predictions of disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency in held-out regions, outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: Joint modeling of the physical environment and structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

Abstract: Large-scale pretraining on Earth observation imagery has yielded powerful representations of the natural and built environment. However, most existing geospatial foundation models do not directly model the structured socioeconomic covariates typically stored in tabular form. This modality gap limits their ability to capture the complete total environment, which is critical for reasoning about complex environmental, social, and health-related outcomes. In this work, we propose GeoViSTA (Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer), a vision-tabular architecture that learns unified geospatial embeddings from co-registered gridded imagery and tabular data. GeoViSTA utilizes bilateral cross-attention to exchange spatial and semantic information across modalities, guided by a geography-aware attention mechanism that aligns continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. We train GeoViSTA with a self-supervised joint masked-autoencoding objective, forcing it to recover missing image patches and tabular rows using local spatial context and cross-modal cues. Empirically, GeoViSTA's unified embeddings improve linear probing performance on high-impact downstream tasks, outperforming baselines in predicting disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency across held-out regions. These results demonstrate that jointly modeling the physical environment alongside structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

</details>


### [331] [MahaVar: OOD Detection via Class-wise Mahalanobis Distance Variance under Neural Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14413)
*Donghwan Kim,Hyunsoo Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MahaVar augments Mahalanobis OOD scores with a class-wise distance variance term; achieves state-of-the-art AUROC and FPR@95 on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet under OpenOOD v1.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable OOD detection is critical for safe DL systems. The abstract observes that ID samples exhibit a pronounced class-wise MD variance (sharp minimum structure), while OOD samples show lower variance. Grounded by Neural Collapse theory under relaxed assumptions, this variance difference motivates a variance-augmented score.

Method: Empirical analysis of class-wise Mahalanobis distances; theoretical grounding via relaxed Neural Collapse; propose MahaVar as a post-hoc detector that combines the Mahalanobis distance with a class-wise distance variance term; no retraining required; evaluated under OpenOOD v1.5 on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet.

Result: MahaVar achieves state-of-the-art performance, with consistent improvements in AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods across benchmarks.

Conclusion: A simple, post-hoc augmentation to the Mahalanobis score—adding a class-wise distance variance term—provides robust OOD detection and is supported by Neural Collapse-based theory.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a critical component for ensuring the reliability of deep neural networks in safety-critical applications. In this work, we present a key empirical observation: for in-distribution (ID) samples, class-wise Mahalanobis distances exhibit a pronounced sharp minimum structure, where the distance to the nearest class is small while distances to all other classes remain large, resulting in high variance across classes. In contrast, OOD samples tend to exhibit a less pronounced sharp minimum structure, producing comparatively lower variance across classes. We further provide a theoretical analysis grounding this observation in Neural Collapse geometry: under relaxed Neural Collapse assumptions on within-class compactness and inter-class separation, ID samples are shown to structurally exhibit high class-wise distance variance, offering a theoretical basis for its use as an OOD score. Motivated by this observation and its theoretical backing, we propose MahaVar, a simple and effective post-hoc OOD detector that augments the Mahalanobis distance with a class-wise distance variance term. Following the OpenOOD v1.5 benchmark protocol, MahaVar achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet, with consistent improvements in both AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods across all benchmarks.

</details>


### [332] [What if Tomorrow is the World Cup Final? Counterfactual Time Series Forecasting with Textual Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14422)
*Shuqi Gu,Yongxiang Zhao,Baoyu Jing,Kan Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, plus an evaluation framework for factual and counterfactual settings and a text-attribution mechanism to separate mutable from immutable factors, aiming to improve forecast accuracy under complex textual inputs; project page available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting must adapt to future events and counterfactual scenarios. Traditional methods rely on historical data or factual futures and simple conditions, which limits their ability to generalize to real-world, stochastic environments. A flexible, condition-aware framework and robust evaluation are needed.

Method: Define the counterfactual time series forecasting task with textual conditions. Propose a comprehensive evaluation framework that covers both factual and counterfactual settings, including scenarios where ground-truth future series are unavailable. Introduce a text-attribution mechanism that distinguishes mutable from immutable factors to guide forecasting under diverse and stochastic textual cues.

Result: Conceptual framework and method; demonstration of improved forecast accuracy under complex textual conditions via the proposed text-attribution mechanism. The evaluation framework enables assessing both factual and counterfactual scenarios, even when ground truth is missing.

Conclusion: Counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions is feasible and beneficial. The proposed approach provides more flexible, condition-aware forecasting and a robust evaluation pipeline, suitable for real-world, stochastic futures.

Abstract: Time series forecasting has become increasingly critical in real-world scenarios, where future sequences are influenced not only by historical patterns but also by forthcoming events. In this context, forecasting must dynamically adapt to complex and stochastic future conditions, which introduces fundamental challenges in both forecasting and evaluation. Traditional methods typically rely on historical data or factual future conditions, while overlooking counterfactual scenarios. Furthermore, many existing approaches are restricted to simple structured conditions, limiting their ability to generalize to the real-world complexities. To address these gaps, we introduce the task of counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, enabling more flexible and condition-aware forecasting. We propose a comprehensive evaluation framework that encompasses both factual and counterfactual settings, even in the absence of ground truth time series. Additionally, we present a novel text-attribution mechanism that distinguishes mutable from immutable factors, thereby improving forecast accuracy under sophisticated and stochastic textual conditions. The project page is at https://seqml.github.io/TADiff/

</details>
