### [333] [Collaborative Yet Personalized Policy Training: Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14423)
*Leo Muxing Wang,Pengkun Yang,Lili Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated actor-critic with a shared linear subspace and personalized local policies; proves finite-time convergence with linear speedup in the number of agents under heterogeneous Markovian dynamics; shows practical gains in federated PPO on Hopper-v5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses environmental heterogeneity and the need for personalization in collaborative policy training by learning a common subspace shared across agents while keeping personalized local critics and actors.

Method: Proposes a joint linear-approximation federated actor-critic framework where agents iteratively estimate a shared subspace, local critic heads, and local policies. Uses single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling. Develops perturbation analysis for projected subspace updates and QR decomposition, conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise, and fine-grained comparisons between Markovian sampling and temporally frozen policies.

Result: Establishes finite-time convergence: critic error converges as ~O~(1/((1-γ)^4 sqrt(TK))) and policy gradient norm as ~O~(1/((1-γ)^6 sqrt(TK))). Demonstrates linear speedup in K despite heterogeneity. Provides experimental gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO on federated Hopper-v5 with action-map heterogeneity and shows transfer from the shared trunk.

Conclusion: A federated subspace-based actor-critic with personalized local components yields provable convergence and practical performance gains in heterogeneous multi-agent RL, supporting scalability and transfer via a shared trunk.

Abstract: Despite the popularity of the actor-critic method and the practical needs of collaborative policy training, existing works typically either overlook environmental heterogeneity or give up personalization altogether by training a single shared policy across all agents. We consider a federated actor-critic framework in which agents share a common linear subspace representation while maintaining personalized local policy components, and agents iteratively estimate the common subspace, local critic heads, and local policies (i.e., actors). Under canonical single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling, we establish finite-time convergence via a novel joint linear approximation framework. Specifically, we show that the critic error converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^4\sqrt{TK}))$, and the policy gradient norm converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^6\sqrt{TK}))$, where $T$ is the number of rounds, $K$ is the number of agents, and $γ\in (0,1)$ is the discount factor. These results demonstrate linear speedup with respect to the number of agents $K$, despite heterogeneous Markovian trajectories under distinct transition kernels and coupled learning dynamics. To address these challenges, we develop a new perturbation analysis for the projected subspace updates and QR decomposition steps, together with conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise. Furthermore, to handle the additional complications induced by policy updates and temporal dependence, we establish fine-grained characterizations of the discrepancies between function evaluations under Markovian sampling and under temporally frozen policies. Experiments instantiate the framework within PPO on federated \texttt{Hopper-v5} action-map heterogeneity, showing gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO and downstream transfer from the learned shared trunk.

</details>


### [334] [FrontierSmith: Synthesizing Open-Ended Coding Problems at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14445)
*Runyuan He,Qiuyang Mang,Shang Zhou,Kaiyuan Liu,Hanchen Li,Huanzhi Mao,Qizheng Zhang,Zerui Li,Bo Peng,Lufeng Cheng,Tianfu Fu,Yichuan Wang,Wenhao Chai,Jingbo Shang,Alex Dimakis,Joseph E. Gonzalez,Alvin Cheung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FrontierSmith automates the creation of open-ended coding problems from closed-ended seeds, selects diverse variants via an idea-divergence metric, and uses agents to produce tests and verifiers; training on this synthesized data improves open-ended coding performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended coding tasks are underrepresented in training data, limiting LLMs' ability to handle long-horizon, design-oriented coding challenges; scalable generation of such data could boost generalization.

Method: Start from competitive programming problems; iteratively generate open-ended variants by altering goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs; score variants with a quantitative idea-divergence metric to foster diverse solver approaches; have agents produce test cases and verifiers for surviving candidates; train on the resulting synthesized data.

Result: Model training with synthesized open-ended problems yields substantial performance gains on two open-ended benchmarks (FrontierCS and ALE-bench) across model sizes; larger devices see similar gains; seeds enable longer reasoning with more turns and tokens.

Conclusion: Closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for scalable open-ended coding data generation, enabling long-horizon problem solving and improved model performance on open-ended tasks.

Abstract: Many real-world coding challenges are open-ended and admit no known optimal solution. Yet, recent progress in LLM coding has focused on well-defined tasks such as feature implementation, bug fixing, and competitive programming. Open-ended coding remains a weak spot for LLMs, largely because open-ended training problems are scarce and expensive to construct. Our goal is to synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. We introduce FrontierSmith, an automated system for iteratively evolving open-ended problems from existing closed-ended coding tasks. Starting from competitive programming problems, FrontierSmith generates candidate open-ended variants by changing the problems'goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. It then uses a quantitative idea divergence metric to select problems that elicit genuinely diverse approaches from different solvers. Agents then generate test cases and verifiers for the surviving candidates. On two open-ended coding benchmarks, training on our synthesized data yields substantial gains over the base models: Qwen3.5-9B improves by +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; Qwen3.5-27B improves by +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. The synthesized problems also make agents take more turns and use more tokens, similar to human-curated ones, suggesting that closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for long-horizon coding data.

</details>


### [335] [When Answers Stray from Questions: Hallucination Detection via Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14449)
*Siyang Yao,Erhu Feng,Yubin Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QAOD (Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition) is a single-pass white-box probing framework for hallucination detection in LLMs that separates question-aligned from question-orthogonal components in answer representations to achieve robust OOD transfer and strong in-domain discriminability. It uses diversity-penalized Fisher scoring to select informative layers and Fisher importance to identify discriminative neurons. Two complementary probes are proposed: a joint probe combining the orthogonal component with question context for in-domain discriminability, and an orthogonal-only probe for domain-agnostic factuality signals. Evaluation shows the joint probe achieves top in-domain AUROC across model-dataset pairs, while the orthogonal-only probe yields the strongest OOD transfer, outperforming a strong white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ with about 25% generation cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination detection in LLMs must balance accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Existing black-box methods are costly due to repeated inferences, while white-box probes often degrade under domain shift as they analyze answer representations in isolation. A single-pass, informative, and domain-robust probing method is needed.

Method: Compute a projection of the model's answer representation to remove the question-aligned direction, obtaining a question-orthogonal component. Use diversity-penalized Fisher scoring to select informative layers and Fisher importance to identify discriminative neurons. Develop two probes: (1) a joint probe that pairs the orthogonal component with question context to maximize in-domain discriminability, and (2) an orthogonal-only probe to preserve domain-agnostic signals for robust transfer. Evaluate across model-dataset pairs, report in-domain AUROC and OOD transfer metrics (e.g., AUROC), and compare to white-box baselines (notably BioASQ).

Result: Joint probe achieves the best in-domain AUROC across evaluated model-dataset pairs. Orthogonal-only probe delivers the strongest OOD transfer, surpassing the best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ with generation cost under 25% of baseline.

Conclusion: QAOD provides a compact, single-pass probing framework that enhances hallucination detection by removing question-aligned variance, enabling a domain-robust orthogonal signal for OOD generalization, while offering a jointly optimized probe for in-domain discriminability. Two complementary probing strategies cover both in-domain accuracy and cross-domain robustness with improved efficiency.

Abstract: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) requires balancing accu racy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Black-box consistency methods are effective but demand repeated inference; single-pass white-box probes are effi cient yet treat answer representations in isolation, often degrading sharply under domain shift. We propose QAOD (Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition), a single-pass framework that projects away the question-aligned direction from the answer representation to obtain a question-orthogonal component that suppresses domain-conditioned variation. To identify informative signals, QAOD further selects layers via diversity-penalized Fisher scoring and discriminative neurons via Fisher importance. To address both in-domain detection and cross-domain generalization, we design two complementary probing strategies: pairing the or thogonal component with question context yields a joint probe that maximizes in-domain discriminability, while using the orthogonal component alone preserves domain-agnostic factuality signals for robust transfer. QAOD's joint probe achieves the best in-domain AUROC across all evaluated model-dataset pairs, while the orthogonal-only probe delivers the strongest OOD transfer, surpassing the best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ at under 25% of generation cost.

</details>


### [336] [LiSA: Lifelong Safety Adaptation via Conservative Policy Induction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14454)
*Minbeom Kim,Lesly Miculicich,Bhavana Dalvi Mishra,Mihir Parmar,Phillip Wallis,Bharath Chandrasekhar,Kyomin Jung,Tomas Pfister,Long T. Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LiSA is a lifelong safety adaptation framework that enhances a fixed base guardrail with memory of past failures, conflict-aware rules, and evidence-aware confidence gating, enabling robust performance under sparse/noisy feedback in real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guardrails in AI agents face context- and environment-dependent risks that cannot be fully specified pre-deployment. Real-world feedback is sparse and noisy, making static guardrails brittle. A lifelong, memory-based adaptation is needed to prevent leaks, unsafe actions, and blocking legitimate work.

Method: LiSA builds a conservative policy induction framework with structured memory of reusable policy abstractions derived from occasional failures; it incorporates conflict-aware local rules to prevent overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts; and applies evidence-aware confidence gating via a posterior lower bound, so that memory reuse scales with accumulated evidence. The approach was evaluated across three testbeds (PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, AgentHarm).

Result: LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback, remains robust under noisy user feedback even at 20% label-flip rates, and achieves better latency–performance trade-offs than larger backbone models, demonstrating scalable, practical protection against diverse, real-world edge risks.

Conclusion: LiSA provides a practical, scalable path to secure AI agents by enabling adaptive, evidence-informed guardrails that evolve with deployment environments, reducing long-tail risk from real-world edge cases.

Abstract: As AI agents move from chat interfaces to systems that read private data, call tools, and execute multi-step workflows, guardrails become a last line of defense against concrete deployment harms. In these settings, guardrail failures are no longer merely answer-quality errors: they can leak secrets, authorize unsafe actions, or block legitimate work. The hardest failures are often contextual: whether an action is acceptable depends on local privacy norms, organizational policies, and user expectations that resist pre-deployment specification. This creates a practical gap: guardrails must adapt to their own operating environments, yet deployment feedback is typically limited to sparse, noisy user-reported failures, and repeated fine-tuning is often impractical. To address this gap, we propose LiSA (Lifelong Safety Adaptation), a conservative policy induction framework that improves a fixed base guardrail through structured memory. LiSA converts occasional failures into reusable policy abstractions so that sparse reports can generalize beyond individual cases, adds conflict-aware local rules to prevent overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts, and applies evidence-aware confidence gating via a posterior lower bound, so that memory reuse scales with accumulated evidence rather than empirical accuracy alone. Across PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, and AgentHarm, LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback, remains robust under noisy user feedback even at 20% label-flip rates, and pushes the latency--performance frontier beyond backbone model scaling. Ultimately, LiSA offers a practical path to secure AI agents against the unpredictable long tail of real-world edge risks.

</details>


### [337] [Focused PU learning from imbalanced data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14467)
*Elias Zavitsanos,Georgios Paliouras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a focused empirical risk estimator for PU learning in highly imbalanced data, achieving state-of-the-art performance under SCAR and SAR, with application to financial misstatement detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PU learning on imbalanced data is common in real-world problems (e.g., disease gene identification, fraud, marketing) but existing methods struggle when positives are scarce and resemble negatives; leveraging unlabeled data effectively under varying labeling mechanisms is needed.

Method: A focused empirical risk estimator that leverages both positive and unlabeled data to train binary classifiers; evaluates performance under two labeling mechanisms (SCAR and SAR); demonstrates effectiveness in both controlled benchmarks and a real-world financial misstatement detection task.

Result: Empirical evaluations show state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced PU benchmarks under SCAR and SAR; real-world demonstration in financial misstatement detection.

Conclusion: The proposed PU learning estimator effectively handles imbalanced PU problems and offers practical gains for applications where positives are scarce and hard to distinguish from negatives.

Abstract: We propose a new method of learning from positive and unlabeled (PU) examples in highly imbalanced datasets. Many real-world problems, such as disease gene identification, targeted marketing, fraud detection, and recommender systems, are hard to address with machine learning methods, due to limited labeled data. Often, training data comprises positive and unlabeled instances, the latter typically being dominated by negative, but including also several positive instances. While PU learning is well-studied, few methods address imbalanced settings or hard-to-detect positive examples that resemble negative ones. Our approach uses a focused empirical risk estimator, incorporating both positive and unlabeled examples to train binary classifiers. Empirical evaluations demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced datasets under two labeling mechanisms - selecting positives completely at random (SCAR) and selecting at random (SAR). Beyond these controlled experiments, we demonstrate the value of the proposed method in the real-world application of financial misstatement detection.

</details>


### [338] [Test-Time Learning with an Evolving Library](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14477)
*Weijia Xu,Alessandro Sordoni,Chandan Singh,Zelalem Gero,Michel Galley,Xingdi Yuan,Jianfeng Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EvoLib is a test-time learning framework that builds a shared library of abstractions from an LLM's inference trajectories to accumulate, reuse, and evolve knowledge without parameter updates or external supervision; it employs a weighting/consolidation mechanism to balance immediate utility and long-term value, enabling instance-level abstractions to become general over time, with strong gains on math, code, and multi-turn tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable continual improvement of large language models without gradient updates or external supervision by exploiting internal inference traces to extract reusable knowledge abstractions.

Method: Maintain a shared repository of modular skills and reflective insights automatically extracted from the model's inference trajectories; apply a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that optimizes for both immediate utility and long-term value; allow instance-specific abstractions to evolve into more general, reusable ones over time.

Result: Substantial improvements over top test-time scaling and learning methods without ground-truth feedback on benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments.

Conclusion: EvoLib demonstrates that test-time learning via an evolving library of abstractions can enhance LLM performance without updating model parameters or requiring external supervision, by balancing immediate usefulness with long-term value and promoting generalization through abstraction evolution.

Abstract: We introduce EvoLib, a test-time learning framework that enables large language models to accumulate, reuse, and evolve knowledge across problem instances without parameter updates or external supervision. Instead of adapting model parameters, our approach maintains a shared library of knowledge abstractions, including modular skills and reflective insights, automatically extracted from the model's own inference trajectories. To support continual improvement, we introduce a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that jointly optimizes for immediate utility and long-term value. This allows simple, instance-specific abstractions to evolve into more general and reusable ones over time. Across challenging benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments, EvoLib improves substantially over the top test-time scaling and learning methods without ground-truth feedback.

</details>


### [339] [A Novel Schur-Decomposition-Based Weight Projection Method for Stable State-Space Neural-Network Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14489)
*Sergio Vanegas,Lasse Lensu,Fredy Ruiz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Schur-based projection for stable discrete-time state-space models: enforce asymptotic stability via projecting the Schur factor onto the stable set, with a pre-factorized variant; achieves comparable accuracy with fewer parameters and mild computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable data-driven, black-box identification of dynamical systems with guaranteed asymptotic stability while maintaining trainability and efficiency.

Method: A stability-ensuring, backpropagation-compatible projection scheme using the real Schur decomposition of the state matrix; projects the quasi-triangular factor onto the nearest stable peer; also provides a pre-factorized formulation.

Result: On synthetic linear systems, the method attains accuracy and convergence rates comparable to state-of-the-art stable-system identification methods, with only a marginal increase in computation and reduced parameter count that aids training in stacked neural networks with static nonlinearities.

Conclusion: Schur-based projection offers a numerically robust framework for identifying complex dynamics under strict asymptotic-stability requirements, matching state-of-the-art performance while ensuring stability.

Abstract: Building black-box models for dynamical systems from data is a challenging problem in machine learning, especially when asymptotic stability guarantees are required. In this paper, we introduce a novel stability-ensuring and backpropagation-compatible projection scheme based on the Schur decomposition for the state matrix of linear discrete-time state-space layers, as well as an alternative pre-factorized formulation of the methodology. The proposed methods dynamically project the quasi-triangular factor of the state matrix's real Schur decomposition onto its nearest stable peer, ensuring stable dynamics with minimal overparameterization. Experiments on synthetic linear systems demonstrate that the method achieves accuracy and convergence rates comparable to those of state-of-the-art stable-system identification techniques, despite a marginal increase in computational complexity. Furthermore, the lower weight count facilitates convergence during training without sacrificing accuracy in stacked neural-network architectures with static nonlinearities targeting real-world datasets. These results suggest that the Schur-based projection provides a numerically robust framework for identifying complex dynamics on par with the State of the Art while satisfying strict asymptotic-stability requirements.

</details>


### [340] [ROAD: Adaptive Data Mixing for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning via Bi-Level Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14497)
*Letian Yang,Xu Liu,Yiqiang Lu,Jian Liu,Weiqiang Wang,Shuai Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR:  ROAD: adaptive, plug-and-play data-mixing for offline-to-online RL using bi-level optimization with a bandit surrogate; improves stability and asymptotic performance without manual tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary distribution shift between offline data and online policy; fixed replay strategies fail to adapt to environment and training dynamics.

Method: Formulates data mixing as a bi-level optimization: outer level optimizes policy performance via data selection; inner level performs Q-learning updates. Uses a practical multi-armed bandit surrogate objective that favors offline priors and mitigates value overestimation.

Result: Empirical results show ROAD consistently outperforms static replay methods across diverse datasets and removes need for manual context-specific tuning, achieving better stability and higher asymptotic performance.

Conclusion: Adaptive data mixing through ROAD yields robust, automatic balancing of offline and online data, improving both stability and long-term performance in offline-to-online RL.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning harnesses the stability of offline pretraining and the flexibility of online fine-tuning. A key challenge lies in the non-stationary distribution shift between offline datasets and the evolving online policy. Common approaches often rely on static mixing ratios or heuristic-based replay strategies, which lack adaptability to different environments and varying training dynamics, resulting in suboptimal tradeoff between stability and asymptotic performance. In this work, we propose Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Adaptive Data-mixing (ROAD), a dynamic plug-and-play framework that automates the data replay process. We identify a fundamental objective misalignment in existing approaches. To tackle this, we formulate the data selection problem as a bi-level optimization process, interpreting the data mixing strategy as a meta-decision governing the policy performance (outer-level) during online fine-tuning, while the conventional Q-learning updates operate at the inner level. To make it tractable, we propose a practical algorithm using a multi-armed bandit mechanism. This is guided by a surrogate objective approximating the bi-level gradient, which simultaneously maintains offline priors and prevents value overestimation. Our empirical results demonstrate that this approach consistently outperforms existing data replay methods across various datasets, eliminating the need for manual, context-specific adjustments while achieving superior stability and asymptotic performance.

</details>


### [341] [Enjoy Your Layer Normalization with the Computational Efficiency of RMSNorm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14521)
*Yuxin Guo,Yihao Yue,Yunhao Ni,Yizhou Ruan,Jie Luo,Wenjun Wu,Lei Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to detect and convert foldable Layer Normalizations into RMSNorm to speed up inference without changing model outputs, using CCC/CBWC constraints and a graph-based detector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Layer normalization adds per-sample centering, causing inference overhead. RMSNorm removes centering but can change model behavior; need to know when LN can be replaced without function change.

Method: Define foldable LNs via column-centered constraint (CCC) and column-based weight centering (CBWC); extend to arbitrary DNNs; develop a graph-based detection algorithm to locate foldable LNs; perform exact inference-time conversion by folding LN into preceding linear layers.

Result: Many LNs in popular architectures are foldable, enabling exact conversion and 2–12% end-to-end speedup without changing predictions; when exact equivalence is partially broken in practice, the method remains competitive with LN while improving efficiency.

Conclusion: The framework enables practical, exact or near-exact replacement of LN with RMSNorm in many networks, yielding tangible inference-time gains with minimal or no accuracy loss.

Abstract: Layer normalization (LN) is a fundamental component in modern deep learning, but its per-sample centering and scaling introduce non-negligible inference overhead. RMSNorm improves efficiency by removing the centering operation, yet this may discard benefits associated with centering. This paper propose a framework to determine whether an LN in an arbitrary DNN can be replaced by RMSNorm without changing the model function. The key idea is to fold LN's centering operation into upstream general linear layers by enforcing zero-mean outputs through the column-centered constraint (CCC) and column-based weight centering (CBWC). We extend the analysis to arbitrary DNNs, define such LNs as foldable LNs, and develop a graph-based detection algorithm. Our analysis shows that many LNs in widely used architectures are foldable, enabling exact inference-time conversion and end-to-end acceleration of 2% to 12% without changing model predictions. Experiments across multiple task families further show that, when exact equivalence is partially broken in practical training settings, our method remains competitive with vanilla LN while improving efficiency.

</details>


### [342] [Lang2MLIP: End-to-End Language-to-Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Development with Autonomous Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14527)
*Wenwen Li,Yuki Orimo,Nontawat Charoenphakdee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lang2MLIP uses an LLM-driven multi-agent system to develop MLIP pipelines from natural-language prompts, removing fixed pipelines and enabling self-correcting, end-to-end MLIP development; demonstrated on SEI, showing promise for non-experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lower the barrier to MLIP development, address heterogeneity across materials where optimal curricula are unknown, and enable adaptive, automated decision-making via large language models.

Method: A multi-agent framework where each agent observes the current dataset, model state, evaluation results, and execution logs, then automatically selects actions to improve the MLIP. The system operates from natural-language input and avoids a predefined pipeline, with agents capable of revisiting earlier subsystems to self-correct.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces indicates that LLM-based multi-agent systems can automate MLIP development and make it more accessible to non-experts.

Conclusion: LLM-driven multi-agent automation is a promising direction for end-to-end MLIP development across heterogeneous materials, with potential to generalize beyond the tested SEI system and reduce the need for specialized expertise.

Abstract: Developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for complex materials systems remains challenging because it requires expertise in atomistic simulations, machine learning, and workflow design, as well as iterative active learning procedures. Existing automated pipelines typically assume a fixed sequence of stages or depend on domain experts, which limits their adaptability to heterogeneous materials systems where the optimal curriculum is not known in advance. To lower the barrier to developing MLIPs for non-experts, we propose Lang2MLIP, a multi-agent framework that takes natural-language input and formulates end-to-end MLIP development as a sequential decision-making problem solved by large language models (LLMs). At each step, a decision-making agent observes the current dataset, model, evaluation results, and execution log, and then automatically selects an appropriate action to improve the model. This removes the need for a predefined pipeline and enables the agent to self-correct by revisiting earlier subsystems when new failures arise. We evaluate this approach on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces. These results suggest that LLM-based multi-agent systems are a promising direction for automating MLIP development and making it more accessible to non-experts.

</details>


### [343] [Exploring Geographic Relative Space in Large Language Models through Activation Patching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14535)
*Stef De Sabbata,Rahul Baiju,Stefano Mizzaro,Kevin Roitero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Examines how LLMs process relative geographic space using activation patching to advance mechanistic interpretability and safety considerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety and governance concerns about deploying LLMs in geography due to limited understanding of their inner workings; a need for mechanistic insights into how geographic information is represented and manipulated.

Method: Apply activation patching, a mechanistic interpretability technique, to LLMs to probe the processing of relative geographic space, identifying which internal components contribute to geographic representations in prompts.

Result: Demonstrates the feasibility of activation patching for investigating geographic-space processing in LLMs and reveals identifiable, interpretable patterns in the contributions of internal activations, with implications for safety and reliability of geographic analyses.

Conclusion: Activation patching is a viable approach for interrogating LLMs in geographic contexts; findings support safer, more transparent integration of LLMs into geography workflows by exposing how geographic space is represented internally.

Abstract: The increased use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in geography raises substantial questions about the safety of integrating these tools across a wide range of processes and analyses, given our very limited understanding of their inner workings. In this extended abstract, we examine how LLMs process relative geographic space using activation patching, an emerging tool for mechanistic interpretability.

</details>


### [344] [RxEval: A Prescription-Level Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Medication Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14543)
*Shuhao Chen,Weisen Jiang,Changmiao Wang,Xiaoqing Wu,Xuanren Shi,Yu Zhang,James T. Kwok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RxEval is a prescription-level benchmark for LLMs, using time-ordered patient trajectories and real prescriptions in a multiple-choice format; shows high difficulty and discriminative evaluation across 16 LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks use coarse, admission-level codes, missing per-timepoint prescribing dynamics; a realistic, granular evaluation is needed to gauge LLMs' prescribing capability and safety.

Method: RxEval provides 1,547 questions across 584 patients, 18 diagnostic categories, and 969 medications. Each item presents a detailed profile and trajectory; distractors are generated via reasoning-chain perturbation; outputs are chosen as medication-dose-route triples from real prescriptions; evaluated with 16 LLMs using F1 and Exact Match.

Result: F1 ranges 45.18–77.10 across models; best Exact Match 46.10%. Error analysis shows frontier models can miss patient information and fail to derive clinical conclusions, indicating task difficulty and discriminative power.

Conclusion: RxEval is a robust, discriminative benchmark that reveals gaps in current LLM prescribing performance and highlights the need for improved reasoning over patient trajectories, integration with clinical guidelines, and stronger safety considerations in AI-assisted inpatient prescribing.

Abstract: Inpatient medication recommendation requires clinicians to repeatedly select specific medications, doses, and routes as a patient's condition evolves. Existing benchmarks formulate this task as admission-level prediction over coarse drug codes with multi-hot diagnostic and procedure code inputs, failing to capture the per-timepoint, information-rich nature of real prescribing. We propose RxEval, a prescription-level benchmark that evaluates LLM prescribing capability by multiple-choice questions: each question presents a detailed patient profile and time-ordered clinical trajectory, requiring selection of specific medication-dose-route triples from real prescriptions and patient-specific distractors generated via reasoning-chain perturbation. RxEval comprises 1,547 questions spanning 584 patients, 18 diagnostic categories, and 969 unique medications. Evaluation of 16 LLMs shows that RxEval is both challenging and discriminative: F1 ranges from 45.18 to 77.10 across models, and the best Exact Match is only 46.10%. Error analysis reveals that even frontier models may overlook stated patient information and fail to derive clinical conclusions.

</details>


### [345] [Discovering Physical Directions in Weight Space: Composing Neural PDE Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14546)
*Pengkai Wang,Pengwei Liu,Yuanyi Wang,Guanyu Chen,Xingyu Ren,Xiaolong Li,Zhongkai Hao,Yuting Kong,Qixin Zhang,Dong Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning a shared neural PDE operator yields endpoint-specific updates that align along a physical direction; a post-hoc Calibration-Conditioned Merge (CCM) combines endpoints along this direction for interpolation/extrapolation across PDE regimes, improving extrapolation performance across multiple PDE benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how weight-space updates encode physical structure across regimes and develop a practical mechanism to transfer and interpolate neural PDE surrogates without retraining.

Method: Start from a shared family anchor, fine-tune low- and high-regime endpoint experts. Analyze updates and show they decompose into a family-shared adaptation plus a direction aligned with the governing physical parameter. Interpret endpoint updates as finite-difference probes of a local physical direction in weight space. Propose Calibration-Conditioned Merge (CCM), a post-hoc coordinate readout that, given physical metadata, a calibrated coordinate mapping, or a short rollout prefix, infers the target composition coordinate and deploys a single merged checkpoint for the remainder of the rollout.

Result: CCM achieves its strongest gains in extrapolative regimes on three PDE benchmarks: reaction–diffusion, viscosity-parameterized 2D Navier–Stokes, and radial dam-break dynamics. Relative to the family anchor, out-of-distribution rollout error is reduced by 54.2%, 42.8%, and 13.8%, respectively. Additional experiments across FNO scales and DPOT-style backbones with ablations indicate endpoint fine-tuning reflects a calibratable physical direction rather than mere checkpoint drift, enabling training-free transfer across PDE regimes.

Conclusion: Endpoint fine-tuning reveals a calibratable physical direction in weight space; CCM provides a robust, post-hoc method to compose neural PDE experts along this direction for improved extrapolation across PDE regimes with a single merged model.

Abstract: Recent advances in neural operators have made partial differential equation (PDE) surrogate modeling increasingly scalable and transferable through large-scale pretraining and in-context adaptation. However, after a shared operator is fine-tuned to multiple regimes within a continuous physical family, it remains unclear whether the resulting weight-space updates merely form isolated regime experts or reveal reusable physical structure. Starting from a shared family anchor, we fine-tune low- and high-regime endpoint experts and show that their updates can be separated into a family-shared adaptation and a direction aligned with the underlying physical parameter. This separation reinterprets endpoint experts as finite-difference probes of a local physical direction in weight space, explaining why static averaging can interpolate between regimes but attenuates endpoint-specific physics. Building on this perspective, we propose Calibration-Conditioned Merge (CCM), a post-hoc coordinate readout method for composing neural PDE experts along this physical direction. Given physical metadata, a calibrated coordinate mapping, or a short observed rollout prefix, CCM infers the target composition coordinate and deploys a single merged checkpoint for the remaining rollout. We evaluate CCM on the reaction--diffusion system, viscosity-parameterized two-dimensional Navier--Stokes equations, and radial dam-break dynamics. Across these benchmarks, CCM achieves its strongest gains in extrapolative regimes, reducing out-of-distribution rollout error relative to the family anchor by 54.2%, 42.8%, and 13.8%, respectively. Further experiments across FNO scales, a DPOT-style backbone, and ablations confirm that endpoint fine-tuning is not arbitrary checkpoint drift, but reveals a calibratable physical direction for training-free transfer across PDE regimes.

</details>


### [346] [Multi-Dimensional Model Integrity and Responsibility Assessment Index and Scoring Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14550)
*Phuc Truong Loc Nguyen,Thanh Hung Do,Truong Thanh Hung Nguyen,Hung Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MIRAI: a unified five-dimensional evaluation index for tabular models, aggregating explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, and sustainability with predictive performance into a single score to guide responsible model selection in high-stakes domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-stakes tabular domains, predictive accuracy alone is insufficient. Existing practice tends to evaluate explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, and sustainability in isolation, hindering principled comparisons.

Method: Define MIRAI by normalizing and direction-aligning dimension scores for five metrics (explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, sustainability), then aggregating into a single composite score. Assess models under a controlled comparison setting across healthcare, financial, and socioeconomic datasets.

Result: Higher predictive performance does not automatically translate to higher MIRAI scores; simpler models often balance the five dimensions better than complex deep tabular architectures, highlighting cross-dimensional tradeoffs.

Conclusion: MIRAI offers a compact, practical basis for responsible model selection in regulated environments by enabling direct, cross-model comparisons across multiple integrity and responsibility dimensions.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence in high-stakes tabular domains cannot be evaluated by predictive performance alone, yet current practice still assesses explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, and sustainability mostly in isolation. We propose the Model Integrity and Responsibility Assessment Index (MIRAI), a unified evaluation framework that measures tabular models across these five dimensions under a controlled comparison setting and aggregates them into a single score. MIRAI combines established metrics through normalized and direction-aligned dimension scores, which enables direct comparison across models with different architectural and computational profiles. Experiments on healthcare, financial, and socioeconomic datasets show that higher predictive performance does not necessarily imply better overall integrity and responsibility. In several cases, simpler models achieve a stronger cross-dimensional balance than more complex deep tabular architectures. MIRAI provides a compact and practical basis for responsible model selection in regulated settings.

</details>


### [347] [SeesawNet: Towards Non-stationary Time Series Forecasting with Balanced Modeling of Common and Specific Dependencies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14551)
*Hao Li,Lu Zhang,Liu Chong,Yankai Chen,Pengyang Wang,Yingjie Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeesawNet introduces Adaptive Stationary-Nonstationary Attention (ASNA) to balance common vs. instance-specific dependencies in non-stationary multivariate time series forecasting, alternating temporal and channel modeling to capture long-range and cross-variable relations, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary multivariate time series suffer from distribution shifts. Instance normalization can over-smooth critical instance-specific structures. The central challenge is adaptively modeling both common and instance-specific dependencies based on each instance.

Method: SeesawNet uses ASNA to extract common dependencies from normalized sequences and instance-specific dependencies from raw sequences, adaptively fusing them per instance. It alternates dedicated temporal and channel relationship modeling to jointly capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show SeesawNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: A unified architecture that dynamic balances common and instance-specific dependencies in both temporal and channel dimensions improves non-stationary forecasting performance, with ASNA as the key component.

Abstract: Instance normalization (IN) is widely used in non-stationary multivariate time series forecasting to reduce distribution shifts and highlight common patterns across samples. However, IN can over-smooth instance-specific structural information that is essential for modeling temporal and cross-channel heterogeneity. While prior methods further suppress distribution discrepancies or attempt to recover temporal specific dependencies, they often ignore a central tension: how to adaptively model common and instance-specific dependency based on each instance's non-stationary structures. To address this dilemma, we propose SeesawNet, a unified architecture that dynamically balances common and instance-specific dependency modeling in both temporal and channel dimensions. At its core is Adaptive Stationary-Nonstationary Attention (ASNA), which captures common dependencies from normalized sequences and specific dependencies from raw sequences, and adaptively fuses them according to instance-level non-stationarity. Built upon ASNA, SeesawNet alternates dedicated temporal and channel relationship modeling to jointly capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that SeesawNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [348] [Efficient Multi-objective Prompt Optimization via Pure-exploration Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14553)
*Donghao Li,Chengshuai Shi,Weijuan Ou,Cong Shen,Jing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes multi-objective prompt selection in LLMs via pure-exploration bandits; proposes algorithms for Pareto set recovery and best-feasible-arm identification with linear guarantees; shows empirical gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-metric prompt evaluation fails to capture trade-offs; a multi-objective, bandit-based approach can efficiently identify prompts that balance multiple criteria.

Method: Model as pure-exploration multi-objective bandits; adapt existing algorithms; introduce a new design for best-feasible-arm identification in structured (linear) bandits; provide theoretical error guarantees.

Result: Bandit-based methods outperform baselines across several LLMs, with significant improvements in identifying Pareto-optimal prompts and best-feasible prompts.

Conclusion: Establishes a principled, efficient framework for multi-objective prompt optimization with theoretical guarantees and practical gains.

Abstract: Prompt engineering has become central to eliciting the capabilities of large language models (LLMs). At its core lies prompt selection -- efficiently identifying the most effective prompts. However, most prior investigations overlook a key challenge: the inherently multi-faceted nature of prompt performance, which cannot be captured by a single metric. To fill this gap, we study the multi-objective prompt selection problem under two practical settings: Pareto prompt set recovery and best feasible prompt identification. Casting the problem into the pure-exploration bandits framework, we adapt provably efficient algorithms from multi-objective bandits and further introduce a novel design for best feasible arm identification in structured bandits, with theoretical guarantees on the identification error in the linear case. Extensive experiments across multiple LLMs show that the bandit-based approaches yield significant improvements over baselines, establishing a principled and efficient framework for multi-objective prompt optimization.

</details>


### [349] [Resolving Action Bottleneck: Agentic Reinforcement Learning Informed by Token-Level Energy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14558)
*Langzhou He,Junyou Zhu,Yue Zhou,Zhengyao Gu,Junhua Liu,Wei-Chieh Huang,Henry Peng Zou,David Wipf,Philip S. Yu,Qitian Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Token reweighting ActFocus addresses Action Bottleneck in agentic RL with LLMs by downweighting reasoning tokens and upweighting uncertain action tokens; achieves large gains over PPO/GRPO without extra cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform token-level credit assignment misallocates learning signals; action tokens disproportionately influence reward variance despite small proportion of trajectory; need targeted credit assignment.

Method: Energy-based analysis of token-level gradients via correlation with reward variance across rollouts; ActFocus reweights token gradients, with an energy-based redistribution to emphasize high-uncertainty action tokens; simple, no extra runtime/memory.

Result: Across four environments and model sizes, ActFocus outperforms PPO and GRPO; final-step gains up to 65.2 pp and 63.7 pp, respectively; no additional cost.

Conclusion: A simple, principled reweighting scheme addressing Action Bottleneck yields substantial improvements, suggesting token-level credit assignment should be biased toward actions to enhance agentic RL with LLMs.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning trains large language models using multi-turn trajectories that interleave long reasoning traces with short environment-facing actions. Common policy-gradient methods, such as PPO and GRPO, treat each token in a trajectory equally, leading to uniform credit assignment. In this paper, we critically demonstrate that such uniform credit assignment largely misallocates token-level training signals. From an energy-based modeling perspective, we show that token-level training signals, quantified by their correlations with reward variance of different rollouts sampled from a given prompt, concentrate sharply on action tokens rather than reasoning tokens, even though action tokens account for only a small fraction of the trajectory. We refer to this phenomenon as the Action Bottleneck. Motivated by this observation, we propose an embarrassingly simple token reweighting approach, ActFocus, that downweights gradients on reasoning tokens, along with an additional energy-based redistribution mechanism that further increases the weights on action tokens with higher uncertainty. Across four environments and different model sizes, ActFocus consistently outperforms PPO and GRPO, yielding final-step gains of up to 65.2 and 63.7 percentage points, respectively, without any additional runtime or memory cost.

</details>


### [350] [Woodelf++: A Fast and Unified Partial Dependence Plot Algorithm for Decision Tree Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14578)
*Ron Wettenstein,Alexander Nadel,Udi Boker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Woodelf++ unifies and accelerates PDPs, Joint-PDPs, and Any-Order-PDIVs for decision-tree ensembles, delivering large speedups and introducing Full PDPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability of tree ensembles requires scalable tools for feature effects and interactions; existing PDP/PDIV methods are computationally intensive, especially for high-order interactions; a unified, efficient approach building on Woodelf and SHAP can substantially reduce cost.

Method: Derives metrics over pseudo-Boolean functions and extends Woodelf to compute PDPs (exact/approximate), Joint-PDPs, Any-Order-PDIVs, and Full PDPs in a unified framework; pure Python implementation with GPU acceleration; uses model split thresholds to capture behavior across feature values.

Result: On a 400k-row dataset, PDP and Joint-PDP computations are up to 6x faster than the state of the art and up to 5 orders of magnitude faster than scikit-learn. For Any-Order-PDIVs, Woodelf++ computes all interactions in about 5 minutes, whereas the state of the art would take an estimated >1,000,000 years.

Conclusion: Woodelf++ provides a unified, highly efficient framework for a broad set of explainability tools in decision-tree ensembles, enabling scalable interpretation on large datasets and across many interaction orders; supports pure Python and GPU acceleration, with substantial practical speedups.

Abstract: Partial Dependence Plots (PDPs) visualize how changes in a single feature affect the average model prediction. They are widely used in practice to interpret decision tree ensembles and other machine learning models. Joint-PDPs extend this idea to pairs of features, revealing their combined effect. Partial Dependence Interaction Values (PDIVs) measure feature interactions. The Any-Order-PDIVs task computes these interactions for every feature subset across all rows of the dataset.
  We introduce Woodelf++, a unified and efficient approach for computing all these useful explainability tools on decision tree ensembles, building on Woodelf, an algorithm for efficient SHAP computation. By deriving suitable metrics over pseudo-Boolean functions, Woodelf++ can compute PDPs (exact and approximate), Joint-PDPs, and Any-Order-PDIVs in a unified framework. Our method delivers substantial complexity improvements over the state of the art, including an exponential gain for Any-Order-PDIVs. Additionally, we introduce and efficiently compute Full PDPs, which leverage the model's split thresholds to faithfully capture its behavior across all possible feature values.
  Woodelf++ is implemented in pure Python and supports GPU acceleration. On a dataset with 400,000 rows, Woodelf++ computes PDP and Joint-PDP up to 6x faster than the state of the art and up to five orders of magnitude faster than scikit-learn. For Any-Order-PDIVs, the gap is even larger: Woodelf++ computes all interaction values in 5 minutes, while the state of the art is estimated to require over 1,000,000 years.

</details>


### [351] [Angel or Demon: Investigating the Plasticity Interventions' Impact on Backdoor Threats in Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14587)
*Oubo Ma,Ruixiao Lin,Yang Dai,Jiahao Chen,Chunyi Zhou,Linkang Du,Shouling Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Among 14,664 DRL intervention scenarios, most interventions mitigate backdoor risk, while SAM uniquely worsens it via backdoor gradient amplification; two key contributions are a framework SCC for the interaction of interventions and backdoors and using abnormal loss-landscape sharpness as a backdoor detector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor vulnerabilities in DRL are known, but how built-in plasticity interventions affect these vulnerabilities is poorly understood, risking deployment of insecure DRL agents.

Method: Large-scale empirical study (14,664 cases) examining representative DRL plasticity interventions under various backdoor attack scenarios; pathological analysis to identify mechanisms; synthesis into conceptual framework and detection indicator.

Result: All interventions except SAM reduce backdoor threats; SAM amplifies backdoor gradients, increasing vulnerability. Activation pathway disruption and representation-space compression contribute to mitigation. These findings yield two insights: (1) SCC framework clarifies the mechanistic interplay between interventions and backdoors; (2) abnormal loss-landscape sharpness serves as a detection signal for DRL backdoors.

Conclusion: Security-aware design of DRL interventions is necessary. The SCC framework helps reason about when and why interventions affect backdoors, and loss-landscape sharpness can be used to detect backdoors in DRL systems; future work should validate across more tasks and quantify detection thresholds.

Abstract: Extensive research has highlighted the severe threats posed by backdoor attacks to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). However, prior studies primarily focus on vanilla scenarios, while plasticity interventions have emerged as indispensable built-in components of modern DRL agents. Despite their effectiveness in mitigating plasticity loss, the impact of these interventions on DRL backdoor vulnerabilities remains underexplored, and this lack of systematic investigation poses risks in practical DRL deployments. To bridge this gap, we empirically study 14,664 cases integrating representative interventions and attack scenarios. We find that only one intervention (i.e., SAM) exacerbates backdoor threats, while other interventions mitigate them. Pathological analysis identifies that the exacerbation is attributed to backdoor gradient amplification, while the mitigation stems from activation pathway disruption and representation space compression. From these findings, we derive two novel insights: (1) a conceptual framework SCC for robust backdoor injection that deconstructs the mechanistic interplay between interventions and backdoors in DRL, and (2) abnormal loss landscape sharpness as a key indicator for DRL backdoor detection.

</details>


### [352] [Silent Collapse in Recursive Learning Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14588)
*Zhipeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Silent collapse in recursive learning: internal distributions contract while standard metrics remain stable; three precursors precede degradation; introduces MTR metacognitive loop to monitor trajectory statistics, estimate a slow trust variable, and regulate learning to prevent collapse, even with no pristine data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recursive learning is used in LLMs, autonomous agents, and self-supervised systems; standard metrics miss early degradation; need early-warning signals and a data-efficient regulator that works with private/contaminated data.

Method: Identify trajectory-level precursors (anchor entropy contraction, representation drift freezing, tail coverage erosion); define MTR framework with Monitor, Trust, Regulator components; lightweight metacognitive loop estimating slow-timescale trust to modulate effective learning intensity.

Result: Precursors reliably precede degradation by multiple generations; MTR provides early warning and can prevent silent collapse without pristine data, offering robustness in constrained data settings.

Conclusion: Silent collapse is addressable via trajectory-aware monitoring and adaptive learning regulation; MTR offers a practical tool for maintaining internal distributional health in recursive learning systems.

Abstract: Recursive learning -- where models are trained on data generated by previous versions of themselves -- is increasingly common in large language models, autonomous agents, and self-supervised systems. However, standard performance metrics (loss, perplexity, accuracy) often fail to detect internal degradation before it becomes irreversible. Here we identify a phenomenon we call silent collapse: under broad recursive conditions, model internal distributions -- predictive entropy, representational diversity, and tail coverage -- progressively contract even as conventional metrics appear stable or improving.
  We discover that silent collapse is not abrupt. Its onset is reliably preceded by three trajectory-level precursors: (1) contraction of anchor entropy, (2) freezing of representation drift, and (3) erosion of tail coverage. These signals manifest multiple generations before any degradation in standard validation metrics, enabling early warning.
  Based on these precursors, we propose the MTR (Monitor--Trust--Regulator) framework, a lightweight metacognitive loop that monitors trajectory statistics, estimates a slow-timescale trust variable, and adaptively modulates the effective learning intensity. MTR provides early warning and actively prevents silent collapse without requiring access to pristine real data -- a critical advantage when original data is unavailable, contaminated, or private.

</details>


### [353] [Fast Rates for Inverse Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14599)
*Andreas Schlaginhaufen,Maryam Kamgarpour*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equivalence between MLE and entropy-regularized Min-Max IRL at population level, with empirical equivalence under deterministic dynamics; fast (n^{-1}) convergence in trajectory KL and Hessian-norm parameter error for misspecified models; extends to general Borel spaces and derives reward-parameter derivatives of soft-optimal value.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Establish fundamental structural and statistical guarantees for entropy-regularized Min-Max IRL with linear reward classes in finite-horizon MDPs on general spaces, ensuring identifiability, consistency, and sample efficiency without requiring exploration.

Method: Analytical derivation showing population-level MLE and Min-Max-IRL equivalence, and, under deterministic dynamics, empirical equivalence. Use pseudo-self-concordance of the Min-Max-IRL loss to prove fast convergence rates for trajectory KL divergence and Hessian-norm parameter error. Extend results to misspecified models, general Borel spaces, and compute derivatives of the soft-optimal value with respect to reward parameters.

Result: MLE and Min-Max-IRL are equivalent at the population level; empirical equivalence under deterministic dynamics. Trajectory KL divergence and parameter error in Hessian norm decay at rate O(n^{-1}). Guarantees hold under misspecification and without exploration assumptions. Reward identifiability extended to general Borel spaces; derivatives of soft-optimal value with respect to reward parameters characterized.

Conclusion: The work provides robust theoretical guarantees for entropy-regularized Min-Max IRL in broad settings, establishing identifiability, consistency, and sample efficiency, and offering tools for sensitivity analysis via reward-parameter derivatives, with potential applicability to general spaces and misspecified models.

Abstract: We establish novel structural and statistical results for entropy-regularized min-max inverse reinforcement learning (Min-Max-IRL) with linear reward classes in finite-horizon MDPs with Borel state and action spaces. On the structural side, we show that maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and Min-Max-IRL are equivalent at the population level, and at the empirical level under deterministic dynamics. On the statistical side, exploiting pseudo-self-concordance of the Min-Max-IRL loss, we prove that both the trajectory-level KL divergence and the squared parameter error in the Hessian norm decay at the fast rate $\mathcal{O}(n^{-1})$, where $n$ is the number of expert trajectories. Our guarantees apply under misspecification and require no exploration assumptions. We further extend reward-identifiability results to general Borel spaces and derive novel results on the derivatives of the soft-optimal value function with respect to reward parameters.

</details>


### [354] [An Amortized Efficiency Threshold for Comparing Neural and Heuristic Solvers in Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14624)
*Sohaib Afifi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines Amortized Efficiency Threshold (AET) to decide deployment-volume at which neural solvers become energy/carbon-efficient compared to CPU metaheuristics under a fixed solution quality. Demonstrates a deployment crossover in MTVRP experiments and presents a reusable framework and protocol for measurement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenging the common view that neural combinatorial-optimization solvers are net energy-inefficient due to training costs by analyzing energy use in deployment, and formalizing a deployment-volume threshold to compare methods fairly.

Method: Introduce AET, derive that the cumulative energy ratio converges to a constant below 1 when the neural solver wins per instance, independent of training-cost measurement; amortize embodied carbon; instantiate on MTVRP (n=20, 19 variants, 5 seeds) with HGS/PyVRP as baseline; measure energy usage and deployment crossover (~1.58e5 instances; per-instance ratio 0.41); provide open instrumentation and measurement protocol.

Result: The per-instance energy/carb ratio favors the neural solver when deployed beyond the AET, with a measured crossover near 1.58×10^5 deployments and a per-instance ratio of 0.41. The cumulative-energy ratio tends to a constant below 1 when the network wins per instance, and this limit is robust to how training cost is measured. Structural convergence at larger sizes remains to be demonstrated.

Conclusion: Proposes a general framework, open instrumentation, and measurement protocol for fair energy/performance comparisons of neural solvers and heuristics. Demonstrates that training cost does not preclude deployment efficiency under sufficient volume; notes that empirical convergence at larger problem sizes warrants future work.

Abstract: A common critique of neural combinatorial-optimization solvers is that they are less energy-efficient than CPU metaheuristics, given the operational energy cost of training them on GPUs. This paper examines the inferential step from "training is expensive" to "neural solvers are net-inefficient", which is where the critique actually goes wrong. Training the network costs a large fixed amount of GPU energy; running the metaheuristic costs a small amount of CPU energy on every instance, repeated as long as the solver is deployed. The two are not commensurable until a deployment volume is fixed. We define the Amortized Efficiency Threshold (AET) as the deployment volume above which a neural solver breaks even with a heuristic baseline in total energy or carbon, under an explicit constraint on solution quality. We show that the cumulative-energy ratio between the two solvers tends to a constant strictly below one whenever the network wins per-instance, and that this limit does not depend on how the training cost was measured. An embodied-carbon term amortizes hardware fabrication symmetrically on both sides. We instantiate the framework on the Multi-Task VRP (MTVRP) environment at n=20 customers across 19 problem variants and five training seeds, with HGS via PyVRP as the heuristic baseline. The measured crossover sits near $1.58 \times 10^5$ deployed instances; the per-instance ratio is 0.41, reflecting the moderate size of the instances tested. The contribution is the framework, the open instrumentation, and the measurement protocol; structural convergence of the ratio at larger problem sizes is left to future empirical work.

</details>


### [355] [Action-Inspired Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14631)
*Eshwar R. A.,Debnath Pal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AGMs add a lightweight scalar potential Vφ to score bridge samples and modulate the drift with importance weights, using a stop-gradient barrier to prevent adverse feedback. Vφ is tiny (~1.4% of parameters), requires no inference overhead, and is discarded at test; results show improved generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge-matching methods currently apply uniform regression weights to all stochastic transitions, treating informative and degenerate paths equally. This can hamper learning by rewarding uninformative or degenerate transitions. A small, online-scored potential can bias training toward structurally coherent transport paths while avoiding destabilizing feedback.

Method: Introduce Vφ, a learnable scalar potential that scores bridge samples online. It modulates the drift objective via importance weights derived through a stop-gradient barrier to decouple the two networks. Vφ comprises ~1.4% of the primary drift network, adds no inference overhead, and requires no iterative half-bridge fitting or auxiliary SDE solvers. It is plug-and-play within any bridge-matching training loop. At inference, Vφ is discarded and standard Euler–Maruyama integration of the EMA drift is used.

Result: Selective penalization of uninformative transport paths through the learned potential yields consistent improvements in generation quality across fidelity and coverage metrics.

Conclusion: A lightweight, plug-and-play enhancement to bridge-matching training that biases learning toward informative transport paths with a small parameter footprint and no inference cost, improving generation quality without altering inference.

Abstract: We introduce Action-Inspired Generative Models (AGMs), a dual-network generative framework motivated by the observation that existing bridge-matching methods assign uniform regression weight to every stochastic transition in the transport landscape, regardless of whether a given bridge sample lies along a structurally coherent trajectory or a degenerate one. We address this by introducing a lightweight learned scalar potential $V_φ$ that scores bridge samples online and modulates the drift objective via importance weights derived through a stop-gradient barrier -- preventing adversarial feedback between the two networks whilst preserving $V_φ$'s guiding signal. Crucially, $V_φ$ comprises only $\sim$1.4% of the primary drift network's parameter count, adds no overhead to the inference graph, and requires no iterative half-bridge fitting or auxiliary stochastic differential equation (SDE) solvers: it is a plug-and-play enhancement to any bridge-matching training loop. At inference, $V_φ$ is discarded entirely, leaving standard Euler-Maruyama integration of the exponential moving average (EMA) drift. We demonstrate that selectively penalising uninformative transport paths through the learned potential yields consistent improvements in generation quality across fidelity and coverage metrics.

</details>


### [356] [DRL-STAF: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for State-Aware Forecasting of Complex Multivariate Hidden Markov Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14632)
*Manrui Jiang,Jingru Huang,Yong Chen,Chen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A DRL-based state-aware forecasting framework (DRL-STAF) that jointly forecasts next-step observations and infers hidden states for complex multivariate HMMs, addressing nonlinear emissions and reducing state-space explosion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine the interpretability and discrete state structure of HMMs with the expressive power and flexibility of deep learning, while handling nonlinear, nonstationary emissions and cross-sequence dependencies.

Method: Integrates deep neural networks to model nonlinear emissions and reinforcement learning to infer discrete hidden states, reducing reliance on predefined transition matrices and mitigating state-space explosion in multivariate HMMs.

Result: DRL-STAF achieves superior or comparable performance relative to HMM variants, standalone deep learning models, and DL-HMM hybrids in most cases, and yields reliable hidden-state estimates.

Conclusion: DRL-STAF provides a scalable, interpretable, and accurate state-aware forecasting framework for complex multivariate HMM processes by combining deep emissions modeling with RL-based state inference.

Abstract: Forecasting multivariate hidden Markov processes is challenging due to nonlinear and nonstationary observations, latent state transitions, and cross-sequence dependencies. While deep learning methods achieve strong predictive accuracy, they typically lack explicit state modeling, whereas Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) provide interpretable latent states but struggle with complex nonlinear emissions and scalability. To address these limitations, we propose DRL-STAF, a Deep Reinforcement Learning based STate-Aware Forecasting framework that jointly predicts next-step observations and estimates the corresponding hidden states for complex multivariate hidden Markov processes. Specifically, DRL-STAF models complex nonlinear emissions using deep neural networks and estimates discrete hidden states using reinforcement learning, reducing the reliance on predefined transition structures and enabling flexible adaptation to diverse temporal dynamics. In particular, DRL-STAF mitigates the state-space explosion encountered by typical multivariate HMM-based methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DRL-STAF outperforms HMM variants, standalone deep learning models, and existing DL-HMM hybrids in most cases, while also providing reliable hidden-state estimates.

</details>


### [357] [Unbiased and Second-Order-Free Training for High-Dimensional PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14643)
*Jaemin Seo,Surin Lee,Jae Yong Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unbiased, second-order-free BSDE training framework that removes Euler–Maruyama bias while keeping computational efficiency for high-dimensional PDEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forward/backward SDE-based solvers are effective for high-dimensional PDEs, but Euler–Maruyama discretization induces bias in the training loss. High-order schemes remove bias but introduce second-order spatial derivatives and heavy computation, creating a need for an unbiased, efficient alternative.

Method: Provide a principled analysis of EM-induced loss bias and propose an unbiased, second-order-free training framework that preserves BSDE methods' computational advantages.

Result: A framework is proposed that eliminates EM-induced bias without resorting to high-order spatial derivatives, maintaining computational efficiency; code is released.

Conclusion: The work clarifies the bias caused by EM discretization in BSDE-based PDE solvers and offers a practical, unbiased alternative that retains efficiency for high-dimensional problems.

Abstract: Deep learning methods based on backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) have emerged as competitive alternatives to physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs). By leveraging probabilistic representations, BSDE approaches can avoid the curse of dimensionality and often admit second-order-free training objectives that do not require explicit Hessian evaluations. It has recently been established that the commonly used Euler-Maruyama (EM) time discretization induces an intrinsic bias in BSDE training losses. While high-order schemes such as Heun can fully eliminate this bias, such schemes re-introduce second-order spatial derivatives and incur substantial computational overhead. In this work, we provide a principled analysis of EM-induced loss bias and propose an unbiased, second-order-free training framework that preserves the computational advantages of BSDE methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/seojaemin22/Un-EM-BSDE.

</details>


### [358] [Slower Generalization, Faster Memorization: A Sweet Spot in Algorithmic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14659)
*Shin So,Kyelim Lee,Albert No*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In a NW matrix-generation task, there exists a data-size sweet spot for fastest validation convergence; larger datasets can slow update-based convergence even as generalization remains possible; the rule-learning phase can speed up fitting beyond memorization; a simple multiplication baseline lacks this behavior; overall, generalization onset and update convergence are decoupled in structured-output tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether critical data-size accounts of grokking hold in structured-output tasks and disentangle rule discovery from memorization, using Needleman–Wunsch matrix generation as a controlled setting; examine how dataset size affects validation accuracy and gradient-update requirements.

Method: Train small Transformer models on NW matrix-generation with varying dataset sizes; monitor validation exact-match accuracy as a function of gradient updates; compare against a multiplication baseline; identify regimes where partial validation competence emerges and measure generalization vs. training convergence.

Result: Demonstrates a dataset-size sweet spot where validation accuracy is achieved fastest, not at the largest dataset. Beyond the sweet spot, generalization persists but requires more updates. In the regime where partial validation competence first emerges, larger datasets can reduce the number of updates needed to reach high training accuracy, indicating rule-structure accelerates fitting beyond memorization. A multiplication baseline does not show the post-threshold slowdown. The work separates the onset of generalization from update-convergence optimization and shows decoupling of rule learning from exact fitting in a structured-output task.

Conclusion: Shows that in structured-output tasks, the data size needed for generalization and the data size that optimizes update-based convergence can diverge. Learning the underlying rule can accelerate fitting beyond mere memorization, and larger datasets do not universally speed up optimization. This challenges simple critical-data-size intuitions in grokking and highlights the need to consider task structure when analyzing data-efficiency and convergence.

Abstract: Critical-data-size accounts of grokking suggest a natural post-threshold intuition: once training data is sufficient to identify the underlying rule, additional data should accelerate validation convergence. We show that this intuition can fail in a controlled structured-output task. In Needleman--Wunsch (NW) matrix generation, small Transformers reach high validation exact-match accuracy fastest at an intermediate dataset size, not at the largest one. Past this dataset-size sweet spot, generalization remains achievable but requires more gradient updates. Conversely, in the regime where partial validation competence first appears, larger datasets can require fewer updates to reach high training accuracy, suggesting that emerging rule structure can accelerate fitting beyond example-wise memorization. A multiplication baseline does not show the same post-threshold slowdown. These results separate the critical data size for the onset of generalization from the dataset size that optimizes update-based convergence, and identify structured-output tasks where learning the rule and completing exact-fitting can diverge.

</details>


### [359] [AQKA: Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition Under a Shot Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14672)
*Jian Xu,Chao Li,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AQKA delivers budget-aware shot allocation for quantum kernel learning, with a regime-decomposition framework and a closed-form pair-level acquisition; it achieves substantial empirical gains over baselines in budget-constrained regimes and demonstrates live hardware effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating N×N quantum kernels requires Θ(N^2 S) measurement shots; existing sub-sampling methods (Nyström-QKE, ShoFaR, kernel-target alignment) pick entries to measure but allocate shots uniformly within the subset, ignoring each entry’s impact on the downstream classifier. A principled, budget-aware allocation is needed.

Method: 1) Complete regime decomposition with AQKA: a principled menu of winners across budget regimes, dominating the budget-limited regime for sparse-sensitivity KRR; 2) Closed-form pair-level acquisition theory: s_ij^* ∝ |g_ij| sqrt(K_ij(1−K_ij)); gradient for KRR (|β_i α_j + β_j α_i| sqrt(K_ij(1−K_ij))) and for SVM via envelope theorem (|η_i^* η_j^*| sqrt(K_ij(1−K_ij))); corrected sparsity-aware rate ρ ≤ 2m/N; explicit plug-in regret bound (Theorem 2); and tighter SVM ceiling ρ^SVM ≤ m_sv^2 / N^2; plus first multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation on hardware.

Result: AQKA dominates the budget-limited regime in sparse-sensitivity KRR, with performance gap growing with N (from +8 to +25 points as N scales 225→1000) and reaching +26–32 points on ibm_pittsburgh. Nyström-QKE wins at saturating budgets on planted-sparse via low-rank reconstruction; ShoFaR competitive only at extreme low budgets. Hardware experiments show +17.0 ± 4.8 points at N=20 on ibm_aachen (3.5σ, 5 seeds) and +14.0 ± 8.5 at N=30 on ibm_berlin (higher budget, 5 seeds).
