TL;DR: Introduces a Two-Index framework to separate epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty in continuous regression with multimodal outputs; derives a tractable Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) for MDN ensembles as an acquisition function; MI between output and epistemic index is a principled active-learning objective that vanishes as data grows; MI-LB matches or outperforms baselines on multimodal benchmarks, uniquely robust across settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active learning for continuous regression struggles with multimodal outputs where variance underlevels modal disagreement and BALD-like criteria Target discrete outputs. A principled, tractable acquisition function for epistemic uncertainty in multimodal regression is needed.

Method: Two-Index framework: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic source), the second governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric source). Entropy decomposition identifies mutual information between the output and the epistemic index as the acquisition objective. For continuous outputs, MI is intractable; derive MI-LB as a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network (MDN) ensembles.

Result: On benchmarks with multimodal systems, MI-LB matches or surpasses all baselines; it is the only method that does so consistently. Geometric and Fisher-based baselines perform well only when the input space already encodes multimodality and otherwise collapse.

Conclusion: The proposed MI-LB acquisition function provides a principled, tractable approach for active learning in continuous regression with multimodal outputs by isolating epistemic uncertainty via a two-index/MI decomposition, with MI vanishing as data increases, reinforcing that the method captures resolvable uncertainty.

Abstract: Active learning for continuous regression has lacked an acquisition function that targets epistemic uncertainty when the predictive distribution is multimodal: variance misses modal disagreement, and information-theoretic targets like BALD are designed for discrete outputs. We introduce a Two-Index framework that makes this separation explicit: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic source), while a second governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric source). An entropy decomposition within the framework identifies the mutual information between the output and the epistemic index as a principled acquisition objective, and we prove this quantity vanishes as the model is trained on growing datasets, confirming that it captures exactly the uncertainty data can resolve. Because this mutual information is intractable for continuous outputs, we derive the Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) acquisition function, a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network ensembles. On benchmarks featuring multimodal systems, MI-LB matches or beats every baseline evaluated and is the only method to do so consistently -- geometric and Fisher-based baselines compete only when the input space already encodes the multimodality, and collapse otherwise.

</details>


### [385] [Learning with Shallow Neural Networks on Cluster-Structured Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14927)
*Elisabetta Cornacchia,Laurent Massoulié*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tractable model shows that when inputs are clustered and correlated with a few latent Boolean variables, layerwise gradient descent on shallow nets achieves sample complexity that scales with the number of latent variables and can be independent of input dimension (up to log terms) at high SNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data exhibit spatial correlations within the input space; unlike standard models that assume low-dim structure in the target, this work studies how input correlations affect learning with gradient descent.

Method: Propose a model where the target depends on a small set of latent Boolean variables; input features are grouped into clusters correlated with these latent variables; assume identifiability; analyze layerwise gradient-descent learning of shallow networks; derive sample complexity bounds.

Result: Under identifiability, sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables; with sufficiently high SNR, it becomes independent of input dimension up to logarithmic factors; experiments on synthetic and real data corroborate theory.

Conclusion: Shows that input-space correlations can relieve sample complexity in shallow nets under gradient descent, highlighting potential reasons for deep learning success on correlated real-world data.

Abstract: The success of deep learning in high-dimensional settings is often attributed to the presence of low-dimensional structure in real-world data. While standard theoretical models typically assume that this structure lies in the target function, projecting unstructured inputs onto a low-dimensional subspace, data such as images, text or genomic sequences exhibit strong spatial correlations within the input space itself. In this paper, we propose a tractable model to study how these correlations affect the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent on shallow neural networks. Specifically, we consider targets that depend on a small number of latent Boolean variables, and input features grouped into clusters and correlated with the latent variables. Under an identifiability assumption, we show that for a layerwise gradient-descent variant, the sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables and, when the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, is independent of the input dimension, up to logarithmic terms. We empirically test our theoretical findings on both synthetic and real data.

</details>


### [386] [A Hardware-Aware, Per-Layer Methodology for Post-Training Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14929)
*Earl Killian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training quantization (SOP) for LLM weights uses per-layer fixed/dynamic codebooks, per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and knapsack-based promotion to achieve 4.5–6 bpw fidelity with a hardware-friendly LUT format (HIF); FP6 point at 6.5 bpw outperforms FP8 baselines with 1.5 bpw storage savings, with full evaluation in a companion paper.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce memory footprint and energy of large language models while maintaining near-lossless fidelity. Current FP8 baselines are storage-inefficient at scale; block-scaled, codebook-based quantization with per-block adaptation and sparse residuals offers a hardware-efficient path.

Method: Per-layer search over fixed (NF4, BOF4, Split87, SH4) and dynamic (DD4) codebook pairs guided by a per-block selection bit; signed per-block scales; activation-weighted cosine-based codebook selection; multiple-choice knapsack to promote sensitive layers; outlier handling, sparse residual correction; per-layer codebooks stored in LUT SRAM; introduction of hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF); evaluation across six open-model families.

Result: The FP6 operating point (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than the FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) with 1.5 bpw less storage; full 4.5–6 bpw evaluation with layer promotion and sparse residual correction is reported in a companion paper.

Conclusion: Block-scaled small atoms with carefully chosen scale precision can replace conventional FP8, aided by a hardware-friendly LUT format; the approach yields superior fidelity at lower storage costs across multiple model families.

Abstract: Scaled Outer Product (SOP) is a post-training quantization methodology for large language model weights, designed to deliver near-lossless fidelity at 4.5--6 bits per weight on hardware with per-layer LUT decode. The methodology combines per-layer search of fixed and dynamic codebook pairs selected by a per-block selection bit, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and multiple-choice knapsack promotion of sensitive layers with outlier and sparse-residual correction. Fixed codebooks include NF4, BOF4, Split87, and SH4; per-layer optimized codebooks (DD4) are hosted in LUT SRAM. A new hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF) is proposed to improve performance, energy, and cost. Across six open model families, the recommended FP6 operating point (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than the conventional per-layer-POT FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) at 1.5 bpw lower storage cost, demonstrating that block-scaled small atoms with carefully chosen scale precision can replace conventionally-deployed FP8. Full evaluation across the 4.5--6 bpw range, including layer promotion and sparse residual correction, is reported in a companion paper.

</details>


### [387] [Octopus: History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization for Continual Learning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14938)
*Yuehao Liu,Shanyan Guan,Weijia Zhang,Xuanming Shang,Yanhao Ge,Wei Li,Chao Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Octopus is a two-stage continual learning framework for multimodal LLMs that uses History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO) to achieve gradient orthogonality without storing historical data, achieving state-of-the-art on UCIT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the key shortcomings of existing continual learning approaches in MLLMs: architecture-based methods incur extra compute and poor generalization; rehearsal-based methods raise privacy/storage concerns; regularization alone inadequately prevents parameter interference.

Method: A two-stage finetuning strategy that decouples task adaptation from regularization, combined with HiFGO, which enforces gradient-level orthogonality across tasks without using historical task data.

Result: On UCIT, Octopus outperforms previous SOTA by 2.14% in Avg and 6.82% in Last metrics.

Conclusion: Octopus provides a principled plasticity-stability balance via history-free gradient regularization and a decoupled two-stage optimization, achieving state-of-the-art performance in continual learning for MLLMs.

Abstract: Continual learning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) aims to sequentially acquire knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, yet existing methods face inherent limitations: architecture-based approaches incur additional computational overhead and often generalize poorly to new tasks, rehearsal-based methods rely on storing historical data, raising privacy and storage concerns, and conventional regularization-based strategies alone are insufficient to fully prevent parameter interference. We propose Octopus, a two-stage continual learning framework based on History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO), which enforces gradient-level orthogonality without historical task data. Our proposed two-stage finetuning strategy decouples task adaptation from regularization, achieving a principled balance between plasticity and stability. Experiments on UCIT show that Octopus establishes state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% and 6.82% in terms of Avg and Last.

</details>


### [388] [Not All Symbols Are Equal: Importance-Aware Constellation Design for Semantic Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14940)
*Albert Shaju,Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas,Mayukh Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A joint semantic-physical layer framework for goal-oriented transmission using a vector-quantized VAE to extract discrete concepts, an SCI to rate semantic relevance, and a RL-driven subset selection with a learned semantic-aware M-QAM constellation. Introduces SSV and SPP metrics, proving Gray-coded constellations are suboptimal under non-uniform semantic importance. Demonstrates near-100% SPP across 4–1024-QAM, high compression (21:1) with semantic quality >0.9, generalizing across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect task-relevant information during wireless transmission by jointly optimizing semantic encoding and physical-layer mapping, rather than treating them separately where all symbols are equally exposed to errors.

Method: A vector-quantized-variational autoencoder extracts discrete latent concepts; a semantic criticality indicator scores each concept by task relevance; a deep RL agent selects the transmission subset based on channel conditions; at the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M-QAM assigns symbol positions according to co-occurrence statistics and SCI scores; introduces semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) and semantic protection probability (SPP).

Result: The proposed constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders (4–1024-QAM) versus ~50% for standard constellations at high spectral efficiency; achieves 21:1 compression with semantic quality above 0.9; generalizes across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.

Conclusion: Joint semantic-physical layer design significantly improves protection for task-critical information, with Gray coding found strictly suboptimal under non-uniform semantic importance; the framework demonstrates strong semantic transmission performance and generalization across datasets, suggesting a new paradigm for semantic-aware communications.

Abstract: Semantic communication systems for goal-oriented transmission must protect task-relevant information not only through source compression but also via physical layer mapping. Existing approaches decouple constellation design and semantic encoding, exposing critical symbols to channel errors at the same rate as irrelevant ones. Contrary to this, in this paper, a joint semantic-physical layer framework is proposed, which is composed of a vector quantized-variational autoencoder that extracts discrete latent concepts, a semantic criticality indicator (SCI) that scores each concept by task relevance, and a deep reinforcement learning agent that dynamically selects the transmission subset based on instantaneous channel conditions. At the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M -QAM constellation assigns symbol positions according to joint co-occurrence statistics and SCI scores, departing from the uniform spacing and Gray coding of standard M -QAM which minimizes average BER without regard for semantic content. We introduce a novel semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) metric and a semantic protection probability (SPP) to quantify the exposure of task-critical symbols to decoding errors, and prove that any Gray-coded constellation is strictly suboptimal in SCI-Weighted SSV whenever the source exhibits non-uniform semantic importance and co-occurrence statistics. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders from 4-QAM to 1024-QAM versus 50% for standard constellations at high spectral efficiency, a 21:1 compression ratio with semantic quality above 0.9, generalizing across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.

</details>


### [389] [Efficient Online Conformal Selection with Limited Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14953)
*Sreenivas Gollapudi,Kostas Kollias,Kamesh Munagala,Ali Sinop*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) for conformal selection under bandit/semi-bandit feedback: updates a control/dual variable to achieve adversarial validity (target φ on average for any sequence) and sublinear efficiency regret under IID inputs; unifies limited-feedback online learning with distribution-free uncertainty quantification via Lyapunov analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal selection seeks a minimal subset achieving a target success probability φ with minimal resource use. Under severely limited feedback (bandit/semi-bandit), maintaining validity while being efficient is challenging, especially under distribution shifts. This work extends conformal inference to such settings.

Method: Apply the ACI update rule to a suitable control parameter or dual variable; develop a unifying algorithmic framework and Lyapunov-function-based analysis; model bandit and semi-bandit feedback in canonical setups to prove guarantees.

Result: Adversarial validity: the target success probability φ is met on average for any input sequence. Stochastic efficiency: sublinear efficiency regret for IID inputs relative to an appropriate stochastic benchmark. Results extend to more complex settings with reduced feedback and provide distribution-free uncertainty-quantification guarantees.

Conclusion: The work builds a theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free conformal inference, enabling robust validity guarantees and improved efficiency under minimal feedback and distribution shifts.

Abstract: We address the problem of conformal selection, where an agent must select a minimal subset of options to ensure that at least one ``success'' is identified with a pre-specified target probability $φ$. While traditional online conformal prediction focuses on maintaining validity for the observed sequence, minimizing the resource cost (efficiency) of such selections, especially under limited feedback, remains a significant challenge. In this work, we consider settings with the most limited ``bandit'' feedback, and demonstrate that the simple Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) update rule, when applied to the appropriate control parameter or dual variable, is both adversarially valid, ensuring the success target is met on average for any input sequence (and hence under distribution shifts), and stochastically efficient, achieving sublinear efficiency regret for $i.i.d.$ inputs against an appropriate stochastic benchmark. We show such guarantees under canonical models capturing bandit and semi-bandit feedback to the agent via a unifying algorithmic technique, and analytic framework involving Lyapunov functions. Our approach handles more complex settings than prior work, while requiring significantly less feedback, and our results provide a new theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free uncertainty quantification.

</details>


### [390] [InfoSFT: Learn More and Forget Less with Information-Aware Token Weighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14967)
*Mahdi Sabbaghi,George Pappas,Adel Javanmard,Hamed Hassani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InfoSFT introduces a simple, principled token-level weighting for supervised fine-tuning that emphasizes medium-confidence tokens, improving generalization and preserving base capabilities compared to vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT treats all samples equally, including low-likelihood ones, which can overfit to specific samples and degrade existing capabilities. Existing filters/down-weighting often suppress novel behaviors the model should learn.

Method: A one-line modification to the token-level loss that reweights training signals to focus on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens (neither too familiar nor too unlikely).

Result: Empirically improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks, with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.

Conclusion: A simple reweighting strategy (InfoSFT) effectively guides SFT toward informative samples, balancing learning of new behaviors with retention of existing capabilities.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) provides the standard approach for teaching LLMs new behaviors from offline expert demonstrations. However, standard SFT uniformly fits all samples -- including those with low likelihood under the base model -- which can disproportionately drive training updates toward overfitting specific samples rather than learning the target behavior. Moreover, adapting to these unlikely samples induces substantial policy shifts that degrade prior capabilities. Existing methods mitigate this by filtering, regenerating, or down-weighting low-likelihood data. In doing so, they often suppress precisely the novel behaviors the base model has yet to learn.
  We propose InfoSFT, a principled weighting scheme for the SFT objective that concentrates learning signals on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens -- those neither overly familiar to the base model nor too unlikely to cause instability. Requiring only a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss, InfoSFT demonstrably improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.

</details>


### [391] [Distance-Matrix Wasserstein Statistics for Scalable Gromov--Wasserstein Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14981)
*Ao Xu,Tieru Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distance-Matrix Wasserstein (DMW): a scalable, provably related proxy to Gromov–Wasserstein that samples distance matrices and transports their laws; provides a relaxation and lower bound to GW with convergence guarantees and intrinsic-dimensional finite-sample bounds; includes sliced/multi-scale variants and kernels; validated on synthetic tasks, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GW distances compare metric measure spaces without a common embedding but are nonconvex and scale poorly. There is a need for scalable, theoretically grounded proxies with interpretability and finite-sample guarantees.

Method: Sample n points from each space, record their pairwise distances to form random distance matrices, and transport the laws of these matrices via Wasserstein distance. DMW forms a hierarchy (including sliced and multi-scale versions). The authors prove DMW is a relaxation and a lower bound of GW, and derive a reverse approximation inequality bounding GW–DMW gap by the Wasserstein error of approximating each measure with n samples. Population DMW converges to GW as the sampled subspaces become dense. Finite-sample bounds with intrinsic-dimensional rates are established. For computation, they introduce sliced and multi-scale variants; for p=1, the sliced multi-scale dissimilarity yields positive-definite exponential kernels.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: DMW is a relaxation and a lower bound for GW; GW–DMW gap is controlled by sampling error; population DMW → GW with dense sampling; finite-sample bounds with intrinsic-dimensional rates depending on data manifold; scalable variants enable efficient computation; p=1 yields positive-definite kernels. Empirical validation on synthetic metric spaces, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing supports theory and demonstrates interpretability as a GW-style proxy for structural comparison.

Conclusion: DMW provides a scalable, theoretically grounded GW-style proxy for comparing geometric data, enabling efficient and interpretable structural analysis across graphs, shapes, and point clouds, with solid convergence and finite-sample guarantees and practical effectiveness.

Abstract: Gromov--Wasserstein (GW) distances compare graphs, shapes, and point clouds through internal distances, without requiring a common coordinate system. This invariance is powerful, but discrete GW is a nonconvex quadratic optimal transport problem and is difficult to estimate at scale. We propose \emph{Distance-Matrix Wasserstein} (DMW), a hierarchy of Wasserstein statistics comparing laws of random finite distance matrices. Rather than optimizing a global point-level alignment, DMW samples $n$ points from each space, records their pairwise distances, and transports the resulting matrix laws. We prove that DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, and establish a reverse approximation inequality: the GW--DMW gap is controlled by the Wasserstein error of approximating each original measure with $n$ samples. Hence population DMW converges to GW as sampled subspaces become dense. We further give finite-sample bounds, including intrinsic-dimensional rates that depend on the data manifold rather than the ambient matrix dimension $\binom n2$. For scalable computation, we introduce sliced and multi-scale DMW; for $p=1$, the sliced multi-scale dissimilarity yields positive-definite exponential kernels. Experiments on synthetic metric spaces, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing validate the theory and demonstrate an interpretable GW-style proxy for structural comparison.

</details>


### [392] [Second-Order Actor-Critic Methods for Discounted MDPs via Policy Hessian Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14982)
*Sanjeev Manivannan,Shuban V*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-timescale, Hessian-vector-product-based second-order actor-critic method for discounted RL is proposed, using a locally constant Q approximation to leverage curvature efficiently and stably.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To accelerate policy-gradient learning by incorporating curvature information (second-order updates) while addressing the computational challenges of Hessians in RL.

Method: Formulate a second-order actor update within a two-timescale actor-critic framework, justify treating the action-value function as locally constant w.r.t policy parameters, and compute updates via Hessian-vector products to avoid full Hessian calculation.

Result: Develops a computationally efficient and stable second-order actor-critic update for discounted rewards by exploiting HVPs and quasi-stationary critic dynamics in the faster timescale.

Conclusion: In the two-timescale setting, curvature-aware actor updates can be implemented efficiently and stably via HVP-based second-order updates, offering a principled acceleration over first-order methods.

Abstract: We address the discounted reward setting in reinforcement learning (RL). To mitigate the value approximation challenges in policy gradient methods, actor-critic approaches have been developed and are known to converge to stationary points under suitable assumptions. However, these methods rely on first-order updates. In contrast, second-order optimization provides principled curvature-aware updates that are proven to accelerate convergence, but its application in RL is limited by the computational complexity of Hessian estimation. In this work, we analyze second-order approximations for the actor update that leverage the full curvature information of the objective as much as possible. A stable approximation requires treating the action-value function as locally constant with respect to policy parameters, which does not generally hold in policy gradient methods. We show that this approximation becomes well-justified under a two-timescale actor-critic framework, where the critic evolves on a faster timescale and can be treated as quasi-stationary during actor updates. Building on this insight, we formulate a second-order actor-critic method for the discounted reward setting that leverages Hessian-vector product (HVP) computations, resulting in a computationally efficient and stable second-order update.

</details>


### [393] [DeepTokenEEG Enhancing Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimers Classification via Tokenized EEG Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15009)
*Thinh Nguyen-Quang,Minh Long Ngo,Ngoc-Son Nguyen,Nguyen Thanh Vinh,Huy-Dung Han,Bui Thanh Tung,Nguyen Quang Linh,Khuong Vo,Manoj Vishwanath,Hung Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight DeepTokenEEG model for AD diagnosis using EEG with 0.29M parameters; achieves high accuracy and outperforms baselines on a 274-subject dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses challenges in EEG-based AD detection: limited data, need for accurate yet efficient models, and reducing dependence on expert interpretation.

Method: Introduces DeepTokenEEG with spatial and temporal tokenizers to capture AD biomarkers in temporal and frequency domains; trained on 274 subjects (180 AD, 94 controls); model is compact (0.29M parameters).

Result: Achieves up to 100% accuracy on specific frequency bands; reports 1.41–15.35% improvement over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset.

Conclusion: Demonstrates potential for early detection and large-scale screening of AD with a lightweight, deployable EEG-based model.

Abstract: The detection of Alzheimers disease (AD) is considered crucial, as timely intervention can improve patient outcomes. Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based diagnosis has been recognized as a non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective approach for AD detection; however, it faces challenges related to data availability, accuracy of modern deep learning methods, and the time-consuming nature of expert-based interpretation. In this study, a novel lightweight and high-performance model, DeepTokenEEG, was designed for the diagnosis of AD and the classification of EEG signals from AD patients, individuals with other neurological conditions, and healthy subjects. Unlike traditional heavy-weight models, DeepTokenEEG ultilizes spatial and temporal tokenizer that effectively captures AD-related biomarkers in both temporal and frequency domain with only 0.29 million paramaters. Trained in a combined dataset of 274 subjects, including 180 AD cases, and 94 healthy controls, the proposed method achieves a maximum recorded accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands, representing an improvement of 1.41-15.35% over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset. These results indicate the potential of DeepTokenEEG for early detection and screening of AD, with promising applicability for deployment due to its compact size.

</details>


### [394] [Boosting Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards via Randomly Selected Few-Shot Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15012)
*Kai Yan,Alexander G. Schwing,Yu-Xiong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FEST is a few-shot demonstration-guided RLVR method that achieves strong performance with only 128 randomly selected demonstrations, significantly improving sample efficiency and matching full-data baselines on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR's sample efficiency is poor on hard tasks where correct rollouts are hard to obtain, and SFT data is expensive. There is a need for data-efficient demonstration-guided RLVR.

Method: FEST (FEw-ShoT) selects a small set (128) of demonstrations from an SFT dataset and integrates three components during training: a supervised signal, an on-policy signal, and decaying weights on the SFT data to mitigate overfitting from repeated training.

Result: FEST outperforms baselines using far less SFT data and can match the performance of baselines trained on the full dataset across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that extreme data efficiency in RLVR is achievable via a minimal demonstration set and a training regimen that combines supervised and on-policy signals with decaying SFT weights; three components are essential for success.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved great success in developing Large Language Models (LLMs) with chain-of-thought rollouts for many tasks such as math and coding. Nevertheless, RLVR struggles with sample efficiency on difficult problems where correct rollouts are hard to generate. Prior works propose to address this issue via demonstration-guided RLVR, i.e., to conduct Supervised FineTuning (SFT) when RL fails; however, SFT often requires a lot of data, which can be expensive to acquire. In this paper, we propose FEST, a FEw-ShoT demonstration-guided RLVR algorithm. It attains compelling results with only 128 demonstrations randomly selected from an SFT dataset. We find that three components are vital for the success: supervised signal, on-policy signal, and decaying weights on the few-shot SFT dataset to prevent overfitting from multiple-epoch training. On several benchmarks, FEST outperforms baselines with magnitudes less SFT data, even matching their performance with full dataset.

</details>


### [395] [Generalized Priority-Aware Shapley Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15018)
*Kiljae Lee,Ziqi Liu,Weijing Tang,Yuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generalizes priority-aware Shapley value to arbitrary directed weighted graphs where pairwise edges impose penalties for order violations, via a random-order value (GPASV); axiomatic foundation, computational methods, and a diagnostic are developed; demonstrated on LLM ensemble valuation in a cyclic preference graph, showing valuation sensitivity to balance between graph priorty and individual soft priority.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing priority-aware Shapley methods require binary, acyclic priority constraints. Real data (e.g., aggregated human preferences, multi-criterion comparisons) often contain cycles and graded priorities. A flexible, penalty-based framework is needed.

Method: Define GPASV as a random-order value on directed weighted priority graphs. Edges impose penalties for order violations rather than forbidding them. Establish axioms, derive GPASV, develop computational algorithms, and introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic (extension of PASV).

Result: GPASV encompasses classical models as boundary cases. It supports computation and diagnostics, enabling nuanced valuation. Empirical application to LLM ensembles on a cyclic Chatbot Arena preference graph shows valuations depend on the balance between pairwise graph priority and individual soft priority.

Conclusion: GPASV provides a principled, flexible valuation framework for settings with cyclic and graded priority constraints, enabling non-binary, penalized-order valuations and revealing that valuation outcomes are sensitive to how priority is modeled.

Abstract: Shapley value and its priority-aware extensions are widely used for valuation in machine learning, but existing methods require pairwise priority to be binary and acyclic, a restriction spectacularly violated in real-data examples such as aggregated human preferences and multi-criterion comparisons. We introduce the generalized priority-aware Shapley value (GPASV), a random order value defined on arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs, in which pairwise edges penalize rather than forbid order violations. GPASV covers a range of classical models as boundary cases. We establish GPASV through an axiomatic characterization, develop the associated computational methods, and introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic extending PASV's. We apply GPASV to LLM ensemble valuation on the cyclic Chatbot Arena preference graph, illustrating that priority-aware valuation is not a one-button operation: different balances of pairwise graph priority versus individual soft priority produce substantively different valuations of the same data.

</details>


### [396] [TopoPrimer: The Missing Topological Context in Forecasting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15035)
*Zara Zetlin,Kayhan Moharreri,Maria Safi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TopoPrimer introduces topology-informed inputs (persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates) to forecasting models, improving accuracy and stability, especially under seasonality spikes and cold-start, with per-token adapters; the topology coordinates drive most gains, providing robust gains across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting models struggle with regime shifts due to seasonal spikes and cold-start; leveraging the global topological structure of the series population can provide a domain-agnostic, informative signal to improve accuracy and stability.

Method: Compute domain-level topology features via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates once per domain; inject these features as per-token inputs for fully-trained models or as adapters for pre-trained backbones; empirical results indicate sheaf coordinates primarily drive gains.

Result: On Chronos and TimesFM benchmarks, up to 7.3% MSE improvement on ECL; topology benefits persist across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones; strongest gains in hard regimes; under peak season demand, traditional models degrade up to 50% while TopoPrimer stays within 10%; at cold start with no history, MAE reduced by 27% over topology-free baselines.

Conclusion: Topology-aware forecasting via TopoPrimer yields robust accuracy improvements and regime resilience; topology and per-series training offer complementary signals; precomputed topological coordinates enable lightweight adapters with strong performance gains.

Abstract: We introduce TopoPrimer, a framework that makes the global topological structure of the series population an explicit input
  to any forecasting model. TopoPrimer improves accuracy across diverse domains, stabilizes forecasts under seasonal demand
  spikes, and closes the cold-start gap. Precomputed once per domain via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates,
  TopoPrimer deploys per token for fully-trained models and as a lightweight adapter for pre-trained backbones. Of these two
  components, sheaf coordinates are the primary accuracy driver. Across four public benchmarks on Chronos and TimesFM,
  TopoPrimer consistently improves forecasting accuracy, with gains of up to 7.3% MSE on ECL. The topology advantage persists
  with near-identical magnitude across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones, suggesting topology and per-series training
  capture complementary signals. The gains are most pronounced in difficult regimes. Under peak seasonal demand, classical
  and zero-shot models degrade by up to 50%, while TopoPrimer stays within 10%. At cold start with no item history,
  TopoPrimer reduces MAE by 27% over a topology-free baseline.

</details>


### [397] [Separating Intrinsic Ambiguity from Estimation Uncertainty in Deep Generative Models for Linear Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15050)
*Yuxin Guo,Dongrui Deng,Pulkit Grover*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a cascade-based decomposition of posterior uncertainty for inverse problems, separating intrinsic ambiguity from forward-operator uncertainty, with calibration diagnostics validated on Gaussian, MRI, and EEG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Posterior uncertainty in deep generative inverse problems is confounded by ambiguity from the forward operator and by inference uncertainty. Interpretable uncertainty and robust diagnostics are crucial in high-stakes applications (medical imaging, scientific discovery).

Method: Proposes a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty and a cascade formulation to expose intrinsic ambiguity. Develops calibration analysis and simulation-based calibration tests to diagnose failures. Validates on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then applies to accelerated MRI and EEG source imaging.

Result: The decomposition isolates intrinsic ambiguity, enabling calibration-based diagnostics that reveal failure modes invisible when optimizing reconstruction quality alone. Demonstrated on Gaussian posterior, accelerated MRI, and EEG source imaging with relevant calibration insights.

Conclusion: A principled, calibration-ready framework for interpreting and diagnosing posterior uncertainty in deep generative inverse problems, applicable across imaging modalities and potentially beyond reconstruction quality metrics.

Abstract: Recently, deep generative models have been used for posterior inference in inverse problems, including high-stakes applications in medical imaging and scientific discovery, where the uncertainty of a prediction can matter as much as the prediction itself. However, posterior uncertainty is difficult to interpret because it can mix ambiguity inherent to the forward operator with uncertainty propagated through inference. We introduce a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. A cascade formulation makes this ambiguity accessible for calibration analysis, enabling qualitative diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests that reveal failure modes that remain hidden when models are selected by reconstruction quality alone. We first validate the approach on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then illustrate the decomposition on accelerated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and finally apply the calibration diagnostics to electroencephalography (EEG) source imaging.

</details>


### [398] [An Interpretable Latency Model for Speculative Decoding in LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15051)
*Linghao Kong,Megan Flynn,Michael Peng,Nir Shavit,Mark Kurtz,Alexandre Marques*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A latency model for speculative decoding in LLM serving, using Little's Law to estimate effective batch size; validated on vLLM; explains diminishing speedups under load; extends to mixture-of-experts; enables deployment guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand SD behavior in production serving where request load varies and effective batch size arises from the system rather than being fixed or observed.

Method: Propose a latency model that decomposes per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent terms across prefill, drafting, and verification; infer effective batch size via Little's Law from request rate; validate via extensive measurements on vLLM across verifier/drafter sizes, prefill/decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities; extend to mixture-of-experts.

Result: The model accurately describes observed latency, explains why speedups shrink with higher server load, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter size affect latency under different loads; demonstration across conditions; framework for deployment.

Conclusion: Provides a structured framework for understanding speculative decoding in real LLM serving systems and practical guidance for configuring SD in production; extends to mixture-of-experts settings.

Abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a smaller draft model to propose multiple tokens that are verified by a larger target model in parallel. While prior work demonstrates substantial speedups in isolated or fixed-batch settings, the behavior of SD in production serving systems remains poorly understood: request load varies over time, and effective batch size emerges from the serving system rather than being directly controlled or observed. In this work, we develop a simple and interpretable latency model for SD in LLM serving. We infer effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law and decompose per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification. We validate our model using extensive measurements from vLLM across verifier and drafter model sizes, prefill and decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities. The model accurately describes observed latency, explains why speedups often diminish as server load increases, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter size shape latency across serving conditions, with implications for configuring SD in deployed systems. We further show how the framework extends to mixture of experts models, where sparse expert activation changes the effective service costs across load regimes. Together, our results provide a structured framework for understanding SD in real LLM serving systems.

</details>


### [399] [TFGN: Task-Free, Replay-Free Continual Pre-Training Without Catastrophic Forgetting at LLM Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15053)
*Anurup Ganguli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFGN introduces an input-conditioned, parameter-efficient overlay for transformer LMs enabling continual pre-training across heterogeneous domains without replay or task labels; it achieves near-zero backward transfer, notable forward transfer, and supports a closed-loop meta-controller and an operator-level latent planner.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual pre-training of LLMs on heterogeneous domains without replay buffers or task identifiers is unresolved, due to catastrophic forgetting and inefficiencies; existing methods rely on replay, task IDs, penalties, or coarse evaluations and scale poorly.

Method: TFGN is an architectural overlay that enables input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates while leaving the base transformer unchanged. It uses a Read/Write decomposition where the forward pass is fully dense, but cross-domain parameter updates target prior-domain subspaces that are not written to. Evaluated across six domains, multiple model scales (≈398M, 739M, 9B), two regimes (From-Scratch and Retrofit), and two extensions (A: closed-loop meta-control; B: operator-level plan vector).

Result: Backward transfer is negligible (-0.007) at LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit; HellaSwag retention values ~0.506/0.504/0.510; >99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs; no replay, task IDs, or Fisher penalty. Positive cross-domain forward transfer: JavaScript PPL improves by 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch (driven by Python training). Extension A/B further reduce forgetting and reshape forward-pass behavior with high fidelity.

Conclusion: TFGN is claimed to be the first architecture that simultaneously eliminates catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, enables a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and provides an operator-level latent planner.

Abstract: Continually pre-training a large language model on heterogeneous text domains, without replay or task labels, has remained an unsolved architectural problem at LLM scale. Existing methods rely on replay buffers, task identifiers, regularization penalties that scale poorly, or sentence-classification-scale evaluation.
  We introduce TFGN, an architectural overlay for transformer language models that produces input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates while leaving the rest of the transformer unchanged. On six heterogeneous text domains (Prose, Python, Math, Biomedical, Chinese, JavaScript) at 1B tokens per phase across three model scales (~398M, ~739M, ~9B) and two regimes (From-Scratch and Retrofit), TFGN achieves backward transfer of -0.007 at LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit, HellaSwag retention 0.506/0.504/0.510, and >=99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs - with no replay, no task IDs, no Fisher penalty.
  The same matrices show positive cross-domain forward transfer: held-out JavaScript PPL drops 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch purely from Python training. Two extensions on the same substrate close further open problems. A closed-loop meta-control layer (Extension A) reduces forgetting by an additional 81% at ~398M, mapping onto the System A and System M roles of Dupoux et al. (arXiv:2603.15381). An operator-level plan vector (Extension B) reshapes forward-pass behavior at 99.96% cosine fidelity over 30 source->target pairs.
  The architectural insight is a Read/Write decomposition: the forward pass is fully dense, while cross-domain parameter updates are structured so prior-domain subspaces are not written to. To our knowledge, TFGN is the first architecture that simultaneously closes catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, realizes a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and carries an operator-level latent planner.

</details>


### [400] [DiffusionOPD: A Unified Perspective of On-Policy Distillation in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15055)
*Quanhao Li,Junqiu Yu,Kaixun Jiang,Yujie Wei,Zhen Xing,Pandeng Li,Ruihang Chu,Shiwei Zhang,Yu Liu,Zuxuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiffusionOPD introduces Online Policy Distillation for multi-task diffusion models, decoupling task-specific teachers from a unified student trained on the student's own rollout trajectories; derives a per-step KL objective for continuous-state processes, offering lower-variance gradients and improved efficiency vs PPO; claims state-of-the-art results across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend RL-assisted diffusion text-to-image modeling to multi-task settings, addressing cross-task interference, imbalance, and catastrophic forgetting inherent in joint or cascade RL

Method: 1) Train task-specific teachers independently. 2) Distill capabilities into a unified student using the student’s own rollout trajectories. 3) Derive a closed-form per-step KL objective for continuous-state Markov processes that unifies stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching. 4) Provide analytic gradient advantages over PPO-style policy gradients.

Result: Empirically, DiffusionOPD surpasses multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: DiffusionOPD enables efficient, scalable multi-task diffusion model training by decoupling single-task exploration from multi-task integration and providing a low-variance gradient via Online Policy Distillation with a unified continuous-state objective.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for improving diffusion-based text-to-image models, but existing methods are largely limited to single-task optimization. Extending RL to multiple tasks is challenging: joint optimization suffers from cross-task interference and imbalance, while cascade RL is cumbersome and prone to catastrophic forgetting. We propose DiffusionOPD, a new multi-task training paradigm for diffusion models based on Online Policy Distillation (OPD). DiffusionOPD first trains task-specific teachers independently, then distills their capabilities into a unified student along the student own rollout trajectories. This decouples single-task exploration from multi-task integration and avoids the optimization burden of solving all tasks jointly from scratch. Theoretically, we lift the OPD framework from discrete tokens to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies both stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching. We formally and empirically demonstrate that this analytic gradient provides lower variance and better generality compared to conventional PPO-style policy gradients. Extensive experiments show that DiffusionOPD consistently surpasses both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, while achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

</details>


### [401] [Proposal and study of statistical features for string similarity computation and classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15110)
*E. O. Rodrigues,D. Casanova,M. Teixeira,V. Pegorini,F. Favarim,E. Clua,A. Conci,Panos Liatsis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COM and RLM features from visual computing are adapted for language-agnostic string similarity; they outperform standard statistical features in synthetic tests and achieve best results on a real plagiarism dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop language-independent, purely statistical features for string similarity to handle words, phrases, codes and texts across languages, avoiding language-specific heuristics.

Method: Adapt co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM) features from visual computing to textual data. Compare with longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information, and edit distance. Evaluate on synthetic datasets and a real plagiarism corpus.

Result: In synthetic experiments, COM and RLM features outperform other statistics; in 3 of 4 cases, the COM/RLM methods are significantly better (P < 0.001). On a real text plagiarism dataset, RLM features yield the best performance.

Conclusion: COM and especially RLM features provide language-agnostic, effective similarity measures for strings, suitable for plagiarism detection and general text comparison.

Abstract: Adaptations of features commonly applied in the field of visual computing, co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM), are proposed for the similarity computation of strings in general (words, phrases, codes and texts). The proposed features are not sensitive to language related information. These are purely statistical and can be used in any context with any language or grammatical structure. Other statistical measures that are commonly employed in the field such as longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information and edit distances are evaluated and compared. In the first synthetic set of experiments, the COM and RLM features outperform the remaining state-of-the-art statistical features. In 3 out of 4 cases, the RLM and COM features were statistically more significant than the second best group based on distances (P-value < 0.001). When it comes to a real text plagiarism dataset, the RLM features obtained the best results.

</details>


### [402] [Learning from Language Feedback via Variational Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15113)
*Yang Li,Erik Nijkamp,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Rayhan Joty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VPD treats language-feedback learning as a variational EM problem that co-evolves a dynamic teacher and student to overcome sparse, verifiable rewards, outperforming RLVR and self-distillation baselines across reasoning and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR suffers from sparse outcome signals and exploration bottlenecks; fixed, passive teachers in on-policy self-distillation plateau as the student improves, limiting learning.

Method: Formulate learning as Variational EM. E-step: adaptively refine the teacher’s token-distribution via an adaptive trust-region update based on trajectory outcomes. M-step: the student internalizes this dense guidance during on-policy rollouts. The teacher and student co-evolve.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse diagnostic feedback sources for scientific reasoning and code generation shows VPD consistently outperforms standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines.

Conclusion: Dynamic teacher refinement mitigates the stagnation of passive distillation, establishing bounds for feedback-driven self-distillation versus environment-driven RL, with stress tests on rigid math reasoning and cold-start regimes to delineate these limits.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse outcome signals, creating severe exploration bottlenecks on complex reasoning tasks. Recent on-policy self-distillation methods attempt to address this by utilizing language feedback to generate dense, token-level supervision. However, these approaches rely on a fixed, passive teacher to interpret the feedback. As the student policy improves, the teacher's zero-shot assessment capabilities plateau, ultimately halting further learning. To overcome this, we propose Variational Policy Distillation (VPD), a framework that formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational Expectation-Maximization (EM) problem. VPD co-evolves both policies: in the E-step, the teacher is actively refined on trajectory outcomes via an adaptive trust-region update, translating textual feedback into a dynamically improved target token distribution. In the M-step, the student internalizes this dense distributional guidance on its own on-policy rollouts. By continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract actionable signals from textual critique, VPD overcomes the limitations of passive distillation. Evaluated across diverse sources of diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks, VPD consistently outperforms both standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines. Finally, by stress-testing our framework on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes, we illuminate the fundamental bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation compared to pure environment-driven RL.

</details>


### [403] [Natural Synthesis: Outperforming Reactive Synthesis Tools with Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15131)
*Frederik Schmitt,Matthias Cosler,Niklas Metzger,Julian Siber,Vladimir Krsmanovic,Mohamed Ghanem,Bernd Finkbeiner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic reactive synthesis with autoformalization enables superior benchmark performance and end-to-end natural-language to Verilog synthesis, extending to parameterized (undecidable) systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address both core bottlenecks of reactive synthesis: algorithmic complexity and the difficulty of crafting formal specifications, by integrating large reasoning models with model checking and by enabling natural-language specifications.

Method: A neuro-symbolic loop coupling large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback; an autoformalization step converts natural-language specifications into evaluation-friendly data using a hand-authored NL specification dataset.

Result: Outperforms the best dedicated synthesis tools on benchmarks and extends to parameterized systems; NL-based specifications achieve performance comparable to formal specs, supporting an end-to-end natural synthesis workflow.

Conclusion: Natural synthesis is viable as an end-to-end workflow, with neuro-symbolic synthesis and NL autoformalization advancing reactive synthesis and enabling parameterized systems.

Abstract: Reactive synthesis, the problem of automatically constructing a hardware circuit from a logical specification, is a long-standing challenge in formal verification. It is elusive for two reasons: It is algorithmically hard, and writing formal specifications by hand is notoriously difficult. In this paper, we tackle both sides of the problem. For the algorithmic side, we present a neuro-symbolic approach to reactive synthesis that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. Our approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated tools in the annual synthesis competition and extends to constructing parameterized systems, a problem known to be undecidable. On the specification side, we introduce an autoformalization step that shifts the specification task from temporal logic to natural language by introducing a hand-authored dataset of natural-language specifications for evaluation. We demonstrate performance comparable to that of starting from formal specifications, establishing natural synthesis as a viable end-to-end workflow.

</details>


### [404] [Causal Foundation Models with Continuous Treatments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15133)
*Christopher Stith,Medha Barath,Vahid Balazadeh,Jesse C. Cresswell,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A foundational model for continuous-treatment causal inference that meta-learns across tasks and reconstructs individual treatment-response curves from observational data, achieving state-of-the-art results without task-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous-treatment causal inference is under-explored and requires generalizing across a continuum of interventions; a model should infer causal effects for unseen tasks without retraining.

Method: Design a prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatments; train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves from observational data; leverage in-context learning to amortize Bayesian posterior inference.

Result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models trained specifically for those tasks.

Conclusion: Proposes a causal foundation model for the continuous-treatment setting that generalizes to unseen tasks (zero-shot) by combining meta-learning and transformer-based reconstruction of treatment-response curves.

Abstract: Causal inference, estimating causal effects from observational data, is a fundamental tool in many disciplines. Of particular importance across a variety of domains is the continuous treatment setting, where the variable of intervention has a continuous range. This setting is far less explored and represents a substantial shift from the binary treatment setting, with models needing to represent effects across a continuum of treatment values. In this paper, we present the first causal foundation model for the continuous treatment setting. Our model meta-learns the ability to predict causal effects across a wide variety of unseen tasks without additional training or fine-tuning. First, we design a novel prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatment variables in order to generate a rich causal training corpus. We then train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves given only observational data, leveraging in-context learning to amortize expensive Bayesian posterior inference. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models which are trained specifically for those tasks.

</details>


### [405] [Training ML Models with Predictable Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15134)
*Will Schwarzer,Scott Niekum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extrapolate deployment-scale failures from top-k evaluation failures; identify finite-k forecast error bias toward over-prediction; propose forecastability loss to handle rare high-failure modes; show two proof-of-concept fine-tuning experiments that reduce forecast error without harming main task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-deployment safety assessment is bottlenecked by small evaluation sets that rarely observe critical failures at deployment scale. A scalable method to estimate deployment failure rates from limited data is needed, including robustness to rare failure modes.

Method: Perform a finite-k decomposition of the forecast error when using the largest k failure scores to predict deployment-scale failure rates; analyze bias and when it under- or over-predicts. Introduce forecastability loss as a fine-tuning objective to mitigate the failure mode where rare high-failure modes in deployment are missed by the evaluation set. Validate in two proof-of-concept experiments (a language-model password game and a reinforcement learning gridworld) showing fine-tuning reduces held-out forecast error while retaining primary-task performance.

Result: The forecast-error bias tends to over-predict in typical cases, but can under-predict if a rare high-failure mode is present in deployment but missed in evaluation. Fine-tuning with forecastability loss substantially reduces held-out forecast error and preserves task performance, achieving safety comparable to supervised baselines in the two experiments.

Conclusion: Forecastability loss addresses a specific deployment risk: rare high-failure modes that are absent from limited evaluation sets. With finite-k error decomposition, practitioners can better calibrate deployment risk estimates and improve safety without sacrificing core capabilities, as demonstrated in two simple domains.

Abstract: Estimating how often an ML model will fail at deployment scale is central to pre-deployment safety assessment, but a feasible evaluation set is rarely large enough to observe the failures that matter. Jones et al. (2025) address this by extrapolating from the largest k failure scores in an evaluation set to predict deployment-scale failure rates. We give a finite-k decomposition of this estimator's forecast error and show that it has a built-in bias toward over-prediction in the typical case, which is the safety-favorable direction. This bias is offset when the evaluation set misses a rare high-failure mode that the deployment set contains, leaving the forecast to under-predict at deployment scale. We propose a fine-tuning objective, the forecastability loss, that addresses this failure mode. In two proof-of-concept experiments, a language-model password game and an RL gridworld, fine-tuning substantially reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability and achieving safety similar to that of supervised baselines.

</details>


### [406] [Forgetting That Sticks: Quantization-Permanent Unlearning via Circuit Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15138)
*Saisab Sadhu,Pratinav Seth,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows 4-bit post-training quantization (PTQ) can destroy many unlearning methods due to a sparsity-permanence tradeoff, and introduces MANSU—with circuit attribution, circuit-restricted null-space projection, and per-parameter magnitude floors—and CAD to verify true structural erasure. Together they enable meaningful forgetting under quantization, retain preservation, no PTQ gap, and structural erasure across models, outperforming gradient-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlearning evaluations are conducted in full precision, but deployed models are quantized. 4-bit PTQ can reverse forgetting, so there is a need for unlearning methods that survive quantization and truly erase the targeted knowledge—beyond behavioral suppression.

Method: - MANSU combines: (1) causal circuit attribution to identify the minimal forget-set subgraph; (2) circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher bound to retain important directions; (3) a per-parameter magnitude floor to ensure updates survive quantization and avoid bin-boundary locking. - CAD (Circuit Attribution Divergence) to mechanistically verify true structural erasure vs. behavioral suppression.

Result: Empirically, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy four properties (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, structural erasure) across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, with margin on each. Gradient-based methods degrade under compression, recovering only small accuracy (up to +0.05) under PTQ.

Conclusion: The observed root cause is a sparsity-permanence tradeoff: per-parameter updates are far smaller than NF4 quantization bins and, when spread across billions of parameters, cannot cross bin boundaries. MANSU overcomes this with aligned mechanistic constraints and a floor guarantee, and CAD provides a verification metric to distinguish structural erasure from mere behavioral suppression.

Abstract: Standard unlearning evaluations measure behavioral suppression in full precision, immediately after training, despite every deployed language model being quantized first. Recent work has shown that 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse machine unlearning; we show this is not a tuning artefact but a systematic dual failure: gradient-based methods that achieve meaningful forgetting lose it under compression, while methods that survive quantization barely change the model. Both failures trace to the same root cause: across all baselines, per-parameter updates lie 47-828x below the NF4 quantization bin width; updates diffused across billions of parameters cannot clear quantization bin boundaries, a consequence we formalize as a sparsity-permanence tradeoff. We present MANSU (Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning), which resolves both modes by combining causal circuit attribution to isolate the minimal forget-set subgraph, circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and a per-parameter magnitude floor guaranteeing quantization survival by construction. We additionally introduce Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD), a mechanistic verification metric distinguishing structural erasure from behavioral suppression, a distinction existing metrics cannot make. Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy all four properties with margin on each (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure), while gradient-based baselines recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression.

</details>


### [407] [Widening the Gap: Exploiting LLM Quantization via Outlier Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15152)
*Xiaohua Zhan,Kazuki Egashira,Robin Staab,Mark Vero,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel quantization-conditioned attack that reshapes weight distributions via injected outliers to trigger malicious behavior post-quantization across modern schemes, demonstrating broad applicability beyond simple quantizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess and demonstrate security risks of advanced LLM quantization schemes, showing that malicious behavior can emerge after quantization even when the full-precision model appears benign.

Method: Injects targeted outliers into specific weight blocks to induce weight collapse (nonzero weights rounding to zero) under quantization. This creates post-quantization models with predictable malicious behavior. The attack is demonstrated across modern schemes (AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF I-quants) and evaluated on multiple LLMs and three attack scenarios.

Result: High success rates across a broad range of quantization methods, including schemes where prior attacks failed, establishing that quantization security risks extend to complex, widely-used methods.

Conclusion: Quantization-related security risks are broad and practical across modern quantization techniques; defenses and mitigations are necessary as quantization becomes pervasive in deployment.

Abstract: LLM quantization has become essential for memory-efficient deployment. Recent work has shown that quantization schemes can pose critical security risks: an adversary may release a model that appears benign in full precision but exhibits malicious behavior once quantized by users. However, existing quantization-conditioned attacks have been limited to relatively simple quantization methods, where the attacker can estimate weight regions that remain invariant under the target quantization. Notably, prior attacks have consistently failed to compromise more popular and sophisticated schemes, limiting their practical impact. In this work, we introduce the first quantization-conditioned attack that consistently induces malicious behavior that can be triggered by a broad range of advanced quantization techniques, including AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF I-quants. Our attack exploits a simple property shared by many modern quantization methods: large outliers can cause other weights to be rounded to zero. Consequently, by injecting outliers into specific weight blocks, an adversary can therefore induce a targeted, predictable weight collapse in the model. This effect can be used to craft seemingly benign full-precision models that exhibit a wide range of malicious behaviors after quantization. Through extensive evaluation across three attack scenarios and LLMs, we show that our attack achieves high success rates against a broad range of quantization methods on which prior attacks fail. Our results demonstrate, for the first time, that the security risks of quantization are not restricted to simpler schemes but are broadly relevant across complex, widely-used quantization methods.

</details>


### [408] [Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15155)
*Zhengxi Lu,Zhiyuan Yao,Zhuowen Han,Zi-Han Wang,Jinyang Wu,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai,Weiming Lu,Jun Xiao,Yueting Zhuang,Yongliang Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDAR introduces a gated self-distillation objective to stabilize and improve multi-turn RL-based LLM agents by gating token-level distillation signals with a sigmoid based on teacher endorsement, treating OPSD as an auxiliary objective rather than the primary RL objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses instability and negative guidance in extending On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) to multi-turn agents, where token-level teacher signals can be misleading due to imperfect skill retrieval and asymmetric handling of rejections.

Method: SDAR maps detached token-level signals into a sigmoid gate to selectively amplify positives from the teacher while attenuating negative rejections, using OPSD as a gated auxiliary objective with RL as the main objective.

Result: Empirical gains across Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 on ALFWorld (+9.4%), WebShop (+10.2% in accuracy), and Search-QA (+7.0%), outperforming naive GRPO+OPSD and other hybrid RL–OPSD baselines across model scales.

Conclusion: SDAR effectively stabilizes multi-turn LLM training by combining gated token-level distillation with reinforcement learning, yielding consistent performance improvements and reduced instability compared to naive or purely OPSD-based approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a central paradigm for post-training LLM agents, yet its trajectory-level reward signal provides only coarse supervision for long-horizon interaction. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) complements RL by introducing dense token-level guidance from a teacher branch augmented with privileged context. However, transferring OPSD to multi-turn agents proves problematic: compounding multi-turn instability destabilizes supervision, while skill-conditioned privileged guidance requires asymmetric treatment for negative teacher rejections may arise from imperfect skills retrieval or utilization. We introduce SDAR (Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning), which treats OPSD as a gated auxiliary objective while keeping RL as the primary optimization backbone. SDAR maps detached token-level signals into a sigmoid gate, strengthening distillation on teacher-endorsed positive-gap tokens and softly attenuating negative teacher rejections. Across the Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 families on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, SDAR substantially improves over GRPO (+9.4% on ALFWorld, +7.0% on Search-QA, +10.2% on WebShop-Acc), avoids the instability of naive GRPO+OPSD, and consistently outperforms hybrid RL--OPSD baselines across model scales.

</details>


### [409] [Position: Behavioural Assurance Cannot Verify the Safety Claims Governance Now Demands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15164)
*Pratinav Seth,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Behavioral assurance cannot verify safety claims due to an audit gap; introduces fragile assurance; analyzes incentives across a 21-instrument audit; and advocates shifting regulatory emphasis toward mechanistic-evidence and limiting behavioral proxies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Governance frameworks demand verifiable properties (e.g., absence of hidden objectives, resistance to loss-of-control precursors, bounded catastrophic capability) but current assurance methods rely on observable outputs and fail to access latent representations or long-horizon agentic behaviors, creating a structural mismatch between what is required and what can be verified.

Method: Formalize the audit gap and the concept of fragile assurance; analyze a 21-instrument inventory to map incentive gradients; articulate a regulatory pivot that bounds behavioral evidence and augments pre-deployment access with mechanistic-evidence classes (linear probes, activation patching, before/after-training comparisons).

Result: A conceptual framework identifying a gap between required verification and achievable evidence, a taxonomy of fragile assurance, and an observed incentive gradient favoring surface behavioral proxies; concrete recommendations for a technical-regulatory pivot emphasizing mechanistic evidence and limiting behavioral proxies in governance.

Conclusion: To improve safety claims, regulatory practice should bound the weight of behavioral evidence in statutory texts and extend voluntary pre-deployment access to mechanistic evidence (e.g., linear probes, activation patching, before/after-training comparisons), thereby reducing the audit gap and fortifying assurance against latent, long-horizon risks.

Abstract: This position paper argues that behavioural assurance, even when carefully designed, is being asked to carry safety claims it cannot verify. AI governance frameworks enacted between 2019 and early 2026 require reviewable evidence of properties such as the absence of hidden objectives, resistance to loss-of-control precursors, and bounded catastrophic capability; current assurance methodologies (primarily behavioural evaluations and red-teaming) are epistemically limited to observable model outputs and cannot verify the latent representations or long-horizon agentic behaviours these frameworks presume to regulate. We formalize this structural mismatch as the audit gap, the divergence between required and achievable verification access, and introduce the concept of fragile assurance to describe cases where the evidential structure does not support the asserted safety claim. Through an analysis of a 21-instrument inventory, we identify an incentive gradient where geopolitical and industrial pressures systematically reward surface-level behavioral proxies over deep structural verification. Finally, we propose a technical pivot: bounding the weight of behavioral evidence in legal text and extending voluntary pre-deployment access with mechanistic-evidence classes, specifically linear probes, activation patching, and before/after-training comparisons.

</details>


### [410] [Eradicating Negative Transfer in Multi-Physics Foundation Models via Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15179)
*Ellwil Sharma,Arastu Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sparse-activated latent transformer (Shodh-MoE) for multi-physics transport achieves near-physical exact mass conservation and domain-specific specialization via a Top-1 router over compressed 3D latents, mitigating negative transfer across open-channel and porous-media PDE regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale SciML toward universal foundation models by addressing negative transfer when co-training heterogeneous PDE regimes, which impose conflicting spectral and geometric demands.

Method: A physics-informed autoencoder produces 16^3 compressed latent states on which a Helmholtz-inspired velocity parameterization enforces divergence-free flows. A Top-1 soft-semantic router routes latent patches to expert subnetworks (two experts for two regimes) while shared experts preserve universal symmetries. Training on 3D tensors (128^3) with 20k steps, distributed pretraining, and explicit mass-conservation constraints.

Result: Exact mass conservation achieved (~2.8e-10 divergence in FP64) on 128^3 grids. Latent validation MSEs: 2.46e-5 (Expert 0) and 9.76e-6 (Expert 1). Decoded physical MSEs: 2.48e-6 and 1.76e-6. Demonstrates autonomous domain bifurcation: regimes route to dedicated experts and simultaneous convergence across regimes.

Conclusion: Sparse expert routing is a practical architectural mechanism to mitigate multi-physics interference in universal neural operators, enabling specialized pathways while preserving shared physics and mass conservation.
